# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** List the six major components of a robotic system and briefly explain the function of any two.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six major components of a robotic system are:

1. **Manipulator (Mechanical Structure):** Provides the physical reach and mobility using rigid links connected by joints. It supports and positions the end effector in space.

2. **End Effector:** The tool or gripper attached to the wrist of the robot, used to interact with the workpiece (e.g., welding torch, two-finger gripper, vacuum cup).

3. **Actuators:** Convert stored energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic) into mechanical motion. Examples: servo motors, hydraulic cylinders, pneumatic pistons.

4. **Sensors:** Provide feedback about internal state (encoders, resolvers) and external environment (cameras, force sensors, LiDAR).

5. **Controller:** The digital brain that processes sensor data and generates command signals for actuators. Examples: PLC, microcontroller, industrial PC.

6. **Power Supply & Transmission:** Supplies and transmits regulated energy to all subsystems through gears, belts, ball screws, etc.

*(Award 1 mark for listing all six components; 1 mark each for explaining any two — total 3 marks)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Differentiate between **repeatability** and **accuracy** of an industrial robot with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Repeatability | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Ability to return to a previously taught position consistently | Ability to move to a position commanded by an absolute coordinate |
| **Measured As** | Spread of positions about the mean (statistical) | Deviation of mean position from the commanded position |
| **Typical Value** | ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm | ±0.5 mm to ±2 mm |
| **Importance** | Critical for high-volume assembly | Critical for offline programming and CAD-based tasks |

**Example:** A robot programmed to drill 10 holes at the same point may place each hole within ±0.05 mm of the others (excellent repeatability), but the mean hole position may be 1 mm away from the CAD-specified location (poor accuracy).

*(Award 1 mark each for definition of repeatability, definition of accuracy, and example — total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a robotic system. Also, classify the various types of joints used in robot manipulators.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the major components of a robotic system with a block diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

A robotic system is an integration of six major subsystems working in a closed loop:

1. **Manipulator:** The mechanical arm comprising rigid links connected by joints. It determines the reach, workspace, and DOF of the robot. Common materials are aluminum and steel alloys.

2. **End Effector:** Mounted on the wrist; it performs the actual task. Types include grippers (mechanical, vacuum, magnetic) and process tools (welding torch, spray gun, drill).

3. **Actuators:** Devices that provide the driving force/torque. Three principal types:
   - **Electric (Servo/Stepper motors):** Most common in modern robots — clean, precise, easy to control.
   - **Hydraulic cylinders:** For very heavy payloads (used in large manipulators, mining, construction).
   - **Pneumatic cylinders:** Fast-acting but limited precision — used in simple pick-and-place.

4. **Sensors:** Divided into:
   - **Internal (Proprioceptive):** Optical encoders, resolvers, tachometers — measure joint angle, velocity, and acceleration.
   - **External (Exteroceptive):** Vision cameras, ultrasonic, force/torque, tactile — perceive the environment.

5. **Controller:** The "brain" of the robot. It runs real-time algorithms (PID, feedforward, trajectory planning) and coordinates the perception-action loop. Examples: ABB IRC5, FANUC R-30iB, KUKA KR-C5.

6. **Power Supply:** Provides regulated DC/AC electrical power or pressurized hydraulic/pneumatic power to actuators and sensors. Includes transformers, rectifiers, filters, and pressure regulators.

*(Award ~1 mark per component × 6 = 6 marks + 1 mark for neat block diagram description = 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Classify the various types of joints used in robot manipulators.**

**Model Solution:**

Joints are classified into **lower pairs (1 DOF)** and **higher pairs (2 DOF)**:

**Lower-Pair Joints (1 DOF):**

1. **Revolute Joint (R):** Permits rotation about a single fixed axis. Contributes 1 DOF. Most common in articulated and SCARA robots.
2. **Prismatic Joint (P):** Permits linear sliding along a single axis. Contributes 1 DOF. Used in Cartesian and cylindrical robots.
3. **Helical (Screw) Joint:** Combines rotation and translation along the same axis at a fixed ratio. Contributes 1 DOF. Rare in industrial robots but used in lead screw drives.

**Higher-Pair Joints (2 DOF):**

4. **Cylindrical Joint:** Combines rotation and translation along the same axis independently. Contributes 2 DOF.
5. **Spherical (Ball-and-Socket) Joint:** Permits rotation about any of three perpendicular axes. Contributes 3 DOF.

**Joint Notation in Robotics:**
A robot is described by its joint sequence. For example:
- **RRR:** Three revolute joints → articulated wrist.
- **PPP:** Three prismatic joints → Cartesian robot.
- **RPP:** Revolute + two prismatic → cylindrical robot.

*(Award ~3 marks for lower-pair classification, ~2 marks for higher-pair classification, ~2 marks for joint notation with examples = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Compare the four basic robot configurations (Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar, and Articulated) with respect to their joint arrangement, degrees of freedom, workspace shape, and a real-world application.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the four basic robot configurations in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Cartesian Configuration (PPP):**
- Three prismatic joints arranged orthogonally along X, Y, Z axes.
- DOF = 3 (translational only).
- Workspace is a rectangular box of volume $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$.
- Excellent rigidity and accuracy; simple control.
- Applications: CNC machines, 3D printers, pick-and-place gantries.

**2. Cylindrical Configuration (RPP):**
- One revolute joint (base rotation) + two prismatic joints (radial and vertical).
- DOF = 3 (1 rotational + 2 translational).
- Workspace is an annular cylinder of volume $V = \pi(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \cdot H$.
- Better reach than Cartesian in the horizontal plane.
- Applications: Loading/unloading of CNC lathes, simple assembly tasks.

**3. Polar (Spherical) Configuration (RRP):**
- Two revolute joints + one prismatic joint.
- DOF = 3 (2 rotational + 1 translational).
- Workspace is a partial sphere of volume $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi(R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3)$.
- Excellent for operations requiring large reach around the base.
- Applications: Heavy welding, forging, casting handling.

**4. Articulated Configuration (RRR + Wrist RRR):**
- Six revolute joints — three for positioning + three for wrist orientation.
- DOF = 6 (full 3D pose control).
- Workspace is a near-complete sphere.
- Highest dexterity; complex kinematics.
- Applications: Automotive welding, painting, complex assembly.

*(Award ~1.5 marks per configuration = 6 marks + 1 mark for comparative perspective = 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Compare the configurations in a structured table and derive the workspace volume of a cylindrical robot.**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (refer Section 3.5 above):**

| Parameter | Cartesian | Cylindrical | Polar | Articulated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Joints | PPP | RPP | RRP | RRR + Wrist |
| DOF | 3 | 3 | 3 | 6 |
| Workspace | Box | Cylinder | Sphere | Sphere |
| Best Use | High-precision linear motion | Loading/unloading | Heavy reach | Complex 3D tasks |

**Derivation of Cylindrical Workspace Volume:**

The end-effector position in cylindrical coordinates is $(r, \theta, z)$ with limits:
- $R_{min} \leq r \leq R_{max}$
- $0 \leq \theta \leq 2\pi$
- $0 \leq z \leq H$

The annular cross-sectional area at any height $z$ is:

$$
A(z) = \pi(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2)
$$

The total volume is obtained by integrating the constant cross-section over the height:

$$
V = \int_{0}^{H} A(z) \, dz = \int_{0}^{H} \pi(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \, dz
$$

Since the integrand is constant with respect to $z$:

$$
V = \pi(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \cdot H
$$

**Numerical Example:** If $R_{min} = 0.3 \, \text{m}$, $R_{max} = 1.5 \, \text{m}$, $H = 2 \, \text{m}$:

$$
V = \pi \cdot (1.5^2 - 0.3^2) \cdot 2 = \pi \cdot (2.25 - 0.09) \cdot 2 = \pi \cdot 2.16 \cdot 2 \approx 13.57 \, \text{m}^3
$$

*(Award ~2 marks for comparison table, ~4 marks for derivation, ~1 mark for numerical example = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid These to Score Full Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing DOF with number of joints.** A robot with 6 joints may have only 3 DOF if some are coupled — state the independent movements clearly.
> 2. **Mixing up repeatability and accuracy.** Examiners specifically test this; using them interchangeably costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Forgetting units in volume derivation.** The final answer must explicitly state $\text{m}^3$ or $\text{mm}^3$.
> 4. **Skipping the Kutz-Gruebler formula justification.** When asked to compute DOF, simply writing the final number without the formula $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Listing sensors without categorizing** them into proprioceptive vs. exteroceptive. KTU Module 1 specifically emphasizes this classification.
> 6. **Drawing a hand-sketched block diagram without labeling all signal flows.** Always label arrows (control signal, feedback signal, power line) on your block diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ A robotic system = **6 subsystems**: Manipulator + End Effector + Actuators + Sensors + Controller + Power Supply.
- ☐ Maximum DOF in 3D space = **6** (3 translational + 3 rotational).
- ☐ **Revolute (R)** = rotational joint (1 DOF); **Prismatic (P)** = linear sliding joint (1 DOF).
- ☐ Kutz-Gruebler mobility formula: $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- ☐ Cartesian workspace volume: $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$.
- ☐ Cylindrical workspace volume: $V = \pi(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \cdot H$.
- ☐ Spherical workspace volume: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi(R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3)$.
- ☐ Five basic robot configurations: **Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Polar (RRP), SCARA (RRP), Articulated (6R)**.
- ☐ Actuator types: **Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic** — know one strength + one weakness of each.
- ☐ Sensors split into **Proprioceptive (internal)** and **Exteroceptive (external)** — be ready to give 3 examples of each.
- ☐ **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy.** Repeatability is statistical spread; accuracy is deviation from command.
- ☐ Repeatability values: industrial robots typically **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**.
- ☐ Standard acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.
- ☐ Payload force: $F = m \cdot g$.
- ☐ Joint velocity relation: $\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$ (Jacobian of forward kinematics).
- ☐ Controller examples: PLC, microcontroller (ARM/AVR), industrial PC, dedicated robot controllers (ABB, FANUC, KUKA).
- ☐ Transmission elements: gears, ball screws, harmonic drives, cycloidal reducers, belts, chains.

**End of Module 1 Notes — Robotic System Components**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Measures in Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safety Measures in Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Safety** is the disciplined application of engineering principles, regulatory standards, risk-assessment methodologies, and protective hardware/software measures intended to eliminate or reduce hazards arising from the design, installation, operation, programming, maintenance, and decommissioning of robotic systems, ensuring protection of human life, equipment integrity, and the surrounding environment.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OERAT612 – Basics of Robot Sensing)**, safety measures in robotics refer to the integrated suite of mechanical, electronic, control-software, and procedural safeguards that allow robots (especially those sharing workspace with humans) to operate without causing harm. The cornerstone of formal robot safety worldwide is the **ISO 10218-1 / ISO 10218-2** standard ("Robots and robotic devices – Safety requirements for industrial robots"), supplemented by **ISO/TS 15066** for collaborative robot operations and **ANSI/RIA R15.06** in North America.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a robot like a high-speed train sharing a track with pedestrians.**
> Without proper fencing, signals, speed limits, and emergency brakes, even a perfectly engineered machine becomes deadly. Robot safety is the "signalling, fencing, and braking system" of the industrial world — the rules, sensors, and protocols that keep the powerful machine and the fragile human from colliding in harmful ways.

Imagine a chef working alongside a heavy, fast-moving industrial robot arm. Just as a chef relies on a sharp knife being placed in a sheath, a clean floor to prevent slipping, and clear kitchen protocols to avoid burns, robot operators rely on **guards, sensors, interlocks, and standards** to prevent the robot from becoming a hazard. The robot itself is "blind" and "painless" — it does not know it has hurt someone. Safety measures give the robot *awareness* and *restraint*.

### 1.3 Why Robot Safety is Critical

A typical industrial robot can:
- Move at speeds up to **2 m/s** at the tool centre point (TCP).
- Generate static contact forces of **150 N to 250 N** (well above the human pain threshold of approximately **20–40 N**).
- Operate for thousands of cycles without rest, making human fatigue in the loop a major concern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Safety Metrics (Bolded Standard Values):**
> - **Human pain threshold:** ~**20 N** (quasi-static contact)
> - **Quasi-static contact limit (collaborative robots):** **140 N** (trunk), **65 N** (hand/wrist)
> - **Transient contact limit:** **280 N** (head), **160 N** (chest)
> - **Maximum robot speed during collaborative operation:** **250 mm/s** (ISO/TS 15066)
> - **Separation distance minimum (human-robot):** variable, computed using **S = K·T + C**
> - **Separation distance minimum (human-human):** **500 mm**

### 1.4 Classification of Robot Hazards

Robotic hazards are broadly classified into four families:

1. **Mechanical Hazards** – Crushing, shearing, cutting, entangling, striking, stabbing.
2. **Electrical Hazards** – Shock, arc flash, burns from high-voltage servo drives and capacitors.
3. **Thermal / Process Hazards** – Hot end-effectors, welding spatter, lasers, chemical exposure.
4. **Ergonomic / Psychosocial Hazards** – Repetitive stress, mental fatigue, sensorimotor stress from human-robot interaction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Robot Safety

| Pillar | What it Covers | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Engineering Controls (Design Safety)** | Intrinsic design, fail-safe architecture, power/force limiting, speed/ separation monitoring | Limiting motor torque via software |
| **Administrative Controls (Procedural Safety)** | Lockout/Tagout, training, maintenance protocols, signage | Permit-to-work system |
| **PPE (Protective Equipment)** | Personal protection as a last line of defence | Cut-resistant gloves, safety glasses |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **order these controls by priority** (Hierarchy of Controls). The correct answer is always **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**, with PPE being the *weakest* line of defence.

### 2.2 Safety Standards Landscape (KTU High-Yield)

| Standard | Domain | Key Concept Introduced |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 10218-1** | Industrial robot design (manufacturer) | Safety-rated stop, speed limits, force limits |
| **ISO 10218-2** | Robot system integration (integrator) | Cell layout, safeguarding, stop categories |
| **ISO/TS 15066** | Collaborative robots | Power & Force Limiting (PFL), pain thresholds |
| **ANSI/RIA R15.06** | North American adoption of ISO 10218 | US-compliant framework |
| **ISO 13849-1** | Safety-related control systems | Performance Levels (PL a → PL e) |
| **IEC 61508** | Functional safety of E/E/PE systems | Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 → SIL 4) |
| **EN 60204-1** | Electrical equipment of machines | Emergency stop, isolation, protective bonding |
| **ISO 12100** | General principles for design – Risk assessment | Three-step risk reduction process |

### 2.3 The Three-Step Risk Reduction Process (ISO 12100)

1. **Risk Assessment** – Identify hazards, estimate risk (Severity × Probability × Avoidance).
2. **Risk Reduction** – Apply inherent safe design, then safeguarding, then information for use.
3. **Documentation / Verification** – Prove residual risk is acceptable; document everything.

### 2.4 Risk Score (R) Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most commonly tested risk formula in KTU 2024 robotics papers is the **Risk Score Equation**:
>
> $$R = S \times P \times A$$
>
> Where:
> - **R** = Risk Score (numeric)
> - **S** = **Severity** of potential harm (1 = minor bruise, 5 = fatality)
> - **P** = **Probability** of occurrence (1 = rare, 5 = frequent)
> - **A** = **Avoidability** (1 = avoidable with means available, 5 = unavoidable)

Acceptability classification used in KTU-style questions:

| Risk Score (R) | Action Required |
|---|---|
| **1 – 4** | Acceptable – no action |
| **5 – 10** | Monitor – reduce if reasonably practicable |
| **11 – 15** | Substantial – reduce before commissioning |
| **16 – 25** | Unacceptable – stop operation immediately |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables & Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = S \times P \times A$ | Risk score | R, S, P, A → dimensionless | Risk assessment |
| 2 | $S_{\min} = K \cdot T + C$ | Minimum safety distance | $S_{\min}$ = mm, $K$ = mm/s, $T$ = ms, $C$ = mm | Safeguarding positioning |
| 3 | $T_{\text{total}} = t_r + t_b + t_m$ | Total stopping time | $t_r$ = reaction, $t_b$ = braking, $t_m$ = monitoring (s or ms) | Stop-circuit design |
| 4 | $F_{\text{max}} = m \cdot a_{\text{max}}$ | Worst-case contact force | $F_{\text{max}}$ = N, $m$ = kg, $a_{\text{max}}$ = m/s² | PFL design |
| 5 | $v_{\text{coll}} = \sqrt{v_0^{\,2} + 2 \cdot a \cdot d}$ | Velocity at collision (clamped) | $v_{\text{coll}}$ = m/s | Energy-transfer limit |
| 6 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of moving part | $E_k$ = J | Transient-contact analysis |
| 7 | $\text{MTTF}_d$ | Mean Time To Dangerous Failure | hours | ISO 13849 PL calculation |
| 8 | $\text{DC}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum \lambda_{dd}}{\sum (\lambda_{dd} + \lambda_{du})}$ | Diagnostic Coverage | dimensionless (0 to 1) | ISO 13849 PL calculation |
| 9 | $v_{\text{coll}} \le 250 \ \text{mm/s}$ | Collaborative speed cap | mm/s | ISO/TS 15066 |
| 10 | $F_{\text{stat}} \le 140 \ \text{N}$ | Quasi-static force limit (trunk) | N | ISO/TS 15066 |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing formulas in markdown tables, **never** use the bare pipe character `|` for absolute value (e.g., never write `|v|` inside a table row). Always replace it with `\vert` or `\mid` — for example, `$\vert v_0 \vert$` — otherwise the table layout will break in rendered KTU study materials.

### 2.6 Categories of Robot Stop Functions (ISO 10218-1)

- **Stop Category 0** – Immediate removal of power (uncontrolled). May be required for **functional safety** but not a *safeguarded* stop.
- **Stop Category 1** – Controlled stop with power maintained until stop achieved, then power removed.
- **Stop Category 2** – Stop achieved with power retained. Used in collaborative operations where the robot may resume automatically.
- **Protective Stop** – A stop initiated by a safeguard (e.g., light curtain, safety mat) — must be a *category 0 or 1* stop.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Safety measure theory is applied in:
- **Automotive assembly lines** (welding robots with light curtains).
- **Medical robotics** (da Vinci surgical system with force feedback).
- **Warehouse automation** (AMRs with 360° LiDAR safety zones).
- **Collaborative assembly cells** (Universal Robots, KUKA LBR iisy, FANUC CRX).
- **Space robotics** (NASA's Robonaut 2 — designed to be intrinsically safe for astronauts).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Safety Distance (Light Curtain / Electro-Sensitive Protective Equipment)

The **minimum safety distance $S_{\min}$** is the distance from the hazard zone boundary to the safeguard (e.g., light curtain) such that the robot can stop *before* a human reaches the danger point.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables**
- $K$ = approach speed parameter of the human body part (mm/s).
- $T$ = total stopping time of the robot system (ms).
- $C$ = intrusion distance (depth into the safeguarded space) of the body part or object (mm).

**Step 2 — State the governing equation** (ISO 13855):

$$
S_{\min} = K \cdot T + C
$$

**Step 3 — Select K from the standard**

For a *hand reaching at normal speed* → $K = 2000 \ \text{mm/s}$.
For a *hand reaching at high speed* (awkward postures) → $K = 1600 \ \text{mm/s}$ (general).
For *whole-body approach* → $K = 1600 \ \text{mm/s}$ with a $C \ge 850 \ \text{mm}$.

**Step 4 — Compute total stopping time**

$$
T = t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{react}} + t_{\text{brake}}
$$

where:
- $t_{\text{detect}}$ = time for safeguard to detect intrusion.
- $t_{\text{react}}$ = controller reaction time.
- $t_{\text{brake}}$ = mechanical braking time (often the largest).

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example**

> **Problem:** A light curtain protects a robotic palletizer. Detection time = 15 ms, controller reaction = 10 ms, brake time = 80 ms. The operator's hand can intrude up to 250 mm. Compute $S_{\min}$ using a *hand at high speed* assumption.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
T = 15 + 10 + 80 = 105 \ \text{ms}
$$

$$
S_{\min} = K \cdot T + C
$$

$$
S_{\min} = 1600 \ \text{mm/s} \times 0.105 \ \text{s} + 250 \ \text{mm}
$$

$$
S_{\min} = 168 \ \text{mm} + 250 \ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\min} = 418 \ \text{mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often give values in mixed units (e.g., T in ms, K in mm/s). Always convert **T to seconds** before multiplying. This single conversion mistake is responsible for ~30% of lost marks in KTU safety-numericals.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Worst-Case Contact Force (Quasi-Static Clamping)

In a collaborative robot, the worst-case quasi-static clamping force on a human body part is approximated by Newton's second law, integrated over the robot's stop distance.

**Step 1 — Free body equation of the robot TCP under emergency stop**

$$
F_{\text{TCP}} = m_{\text{TCP}} \cdot a_{\text{decel}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute constant deceleration $a_{\text{decel}} = v_0 / t_b$**

$$
F_{\text{TCP}} = m_{\text{TCP}} \cdot \frac{v_0}{t_b}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the velocity-squared relation from kinematics** to express $v_0$ in terms of stopping distance $d_s$:

$$
v_0^{\,2} = 2 \cdot a_{\text{decel}} \cdot d_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_{\text{decel}} = \frac{v_0^{\,2}}{2 d_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the force equation**

$$
F_{\text{TCP}} = m_{\text{TCP}} \cdot \frac{v_0^{\,2}}{2 d_s}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 2 to convert to effective clamping force** $F_{\text{clamp}}$ (a common empirical factor in safety testing):

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = 2 F_{\text{TCP}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = \frac{m_{\text{TCP}} \cdot v_0^{\,2}}{d_s}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation**

> **Problem:** A cobot TCP mass $m = 5 \ \text{kg}$ is moving at $v_0 = 0.25 \ \text{m/s}$ and the safety-rated monitored stop triggers with stopping distance $d_s = 0.04 \ \text{m}$. Find $F_{\text{clamp}}$ and compare to the trunk quasi-static limit of **140 N**.

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = \frac{5 \times (0.25)^2}{0.04}
$$

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = \frac{5 \times 0.0625}{0.04}
$$

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = \frac{0.3125}{0.04}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{clamp}} = 7.8125 \ \text{N}}
$$

**Conclusion:** 7.81 N ≪ 140 N ⇒ **SAFE for trunk contact**. The cobot can be classified as compliant with ISO/TS 15066 quasi-static thresholds.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Kinetic Energy and Transient Contact Limit

For *transient* (non-clamping) contacts, the energy absorbed is the limiting metric.

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy of moving part**

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

**Step 2 — For a head transient contact, the absorbed energy limit is** approximately $E_{\text{limit}} = 1.0 \ \text{J}$ (an empirical biomechanical threshold).

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum permissible velocity**

$$
v_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{\text{limit}}}{m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example**

> **Problem:** A small gripper of mass $m = 0.5 \ \text{kg}$ approaches a human head. Compute $v_{\max}$ for transient contact.

$$
v_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.0}{0.5}}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = \sqrt{4.0}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{\max} = 2.0 \ \text{m/s}}
$$

If the actual TCP velocity exceeds $v_{\max}$, additional safety measures (soft padding, active force limiting) must be applied.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Safeguard

| Hazard Type | Recommended Safeguard | Standard Reference | KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operator reaching into cell | Light curtain (ESPE) | ISO 13855 | Compute $S_{\min}$ |
| Operator stepping into zone | Safety mat | ISO 13856 | Pressure-sensitive floor |
| Operator near fixed point | Safety interlock on guard | ISO 14119 | Door switch + interlock |
| Slow collaborative task | Power & Force Limiting (PFL) | ISO/TS 15066 | Speed ≤ 250 mm/s |
| Dynamic collaborative task | Speed & Separation Monitoring (SSM) | ISO/TS 15066 | $S_{\min}$ with $K = 1600$ |
| Tool failure | Emergency stop (Cat 0 or 1) | ISO 10218-1 | Hardwired E-stop |
| Robot-to-robot collision | Zone-based safety controller | ISO 10218-2 | Shared zones, time-multiplexed access |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robot Safety System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Source Robot Motion] --> B[Sensor Layer]
    B --> B1[Light Curtain ESPE]
    B --> B2[Safety Mat]
    B --> B3[Emergency Stop Button]
    B --> B4[Force Torque Sensor]
    B --> B5[3D Vision / LiDAR]
    B --> C[Safety Controller]
    C --> C1[Logic Solver SIL2 PLd]
    C --> C2[Dual Channel Architecture]
    C --> C3[Diagnostic Coverage DCavg]
    C1 --> D[Actuator Layer]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Servo Drive Cutoff]
    D --> D2[Brake Engagement]
    D --> D3[Power Removal Cat 0 Stop]
    D --> E[Safeguarded Robot System]
    E --> F[Human Operator Safe Workspace]
    A --> G[Risk Assessment Module]
    G --> G1[ISO 12100 Three Step Process]
    G1 --> G
    G --> C
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Risk Reduction Decision Process (ISO 12100)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Risk Assessment] --> P2[Identify Hazards]
    P2 --> P3[Estimate Risk R = S x P x A]
    P3 --> P4{Is R Acceptable ?}
    P4 -- No --> P5[Apply Inherent Safe Design]
    P5 --> P6[Apply Safeguarding and Complementary Protective Measures]
    P6 --> P7[Provide Information for Use Training Signage]
    P7 --> P8[Recompute Residual Risk R]
    P8 --> P4
    P4 -- Yes --> P9[Document and Verify]
    P9 --> P10[Release Robot System for Operation]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Robot Safety Subsystem Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Hardware Level: Mechanical Guards Enclosures] --> L2[Sensor Level: Light Curtains Mats E Stops]
    L2 --> L3[Controller Level: Safety PLC SIL 2 or higher]
    L3 --> L4[Drive Level: STO Safe Torque Off]
    L4 --> L5[Software Level: Safety Rated Speed and Force Monitoring]
    L5 --> L6[Operational Level: Procedural Safety LOTO Training]
    L6 --> L7[Emergency Response Level: Emergency Stop Reset Manual Override]
```

### 4.4 State Diagram: Robot Operating States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SafeState
    SafeState --> AutomaticMode : Operator requests cycle
    AutomaticMode --> ProtectiveStop : Safeguard triggered
    AutomaticMode --> EmergencyStop : E stop pressed
    AutomaticStop --> AutomaticMode : Reset by authorised operator
    ProtectiveStop --> ManualMode : Operator intervention
    EmergencyStop --> SafeState : Manual reset after verification
    ManualMode --> SafeState : Teach mode exit
    SafeState --> [*] : Shutdown
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `B1`, `C2`) — never the reserved words `end`, `subgraph`, `style`, or `graph` as standalone names. Labels are plain uppercase text, with **no bold tags or HTML tables** inside double-quoted node text, in compliance with KTU diagram-rendering guidelines.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define robot safety. List any four major hazards associated with industrial robots.**

**Model Answer:**
Robot safety is the systematic application of engineering, regulatory, and procedural measures to eliminate or reduce hazards arising from the design, operation, and maintenance of robotic systems, ensuring the protection of human life, equipment, and the environment.

Four major hazards:
1. **Mechanical hazard** – crushing, shearing, entangling, or striking from robot motion.
2. **Electrical hazard** – shock or arc flash from high-voltage servo drives and stored capacitor energy.
3. **Thermal / process hazard** – burns from hot end-effectors, welding spatter, or laser emission.
4. **Ergonomic hazard** – repetitive stress and operator fatigue in human-robot interaction.

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is an Emergency Stop according to ISO 10218-1? Differentiate between Stop Category 0 and Stop Category 1.**

**Model Answer:**
An **Emergency Stop (E-stop)** is a manually actuated safeguard used to bring a robot to a safe state in the event of an imminent or actual hazardous situation. It must be hardwired, prominently located (red mushroom-head button on yellow background), and self-latching.

| Feature | Stop Category 0 | Stop Category 1 |
|---|---|---|
| Power removal | **Immediate uncontrolled** removal | **Controlled** removal after stop |
| Braking | Natural friction only | Active braking by drive |
| Use case | Uncontrolled hazard, fast cut-off | Most E-stops, controlled stop preferred |
| Reset | Manual reset after fault clear | Manual reset, then power removal |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**a) (7 marks)** Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** with a neat diagram. How does it apply to robotic safety design?

**b) (7 marks)** A collaborative robot is operating alongside a human operator. The robot TCP has a mass of 4 kg, and during a safety-rated monitored stop, the velocity drops from 0.5 m/s to 0 m/s in a stopping distance of 50 mm. Determine:
- (i) The deceleration experienced.
- (ii) The kinetic energy at the moment of stop initiation.
- (iii) The contact clamping force, and comment on its safety against the trunk quasi-static limit of 140 N.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
The Hierarchy of Controls (in descending order of effectiveness) is:
1. **Elimination** – Remove the hazard entirely (e.g., remove the need for human entry).
2. **Substitution** – Replace with a less hazardous process.
3. **Engineering Controls** – Guards, interlocks, light curtains, PFL.
4. **Administrative Controls** – Training, signage, lockout/tagout.
5. **PPE** – Gloves, goggles (last line of defence).

Applied to robotics, the designer first tries to **eliminate** the need for human presence (e.g., full automation), then **substitutes** hazardous processes (e.g., adhesive bonding instead of welding), then applies **engineering controls** (light curtains, force limiters), then **administrative** (training, lockout), and finally resorts to **PPE**.

**Part (b):**
- **(i) Deceleration:**
  Using $v_f^2 = v_0^2 - 2 a d_s$,
  $0 = (0.5)^2 - 2 a (0.05)$,
  $a = \dfrac{(0.5)^2}{2 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.10} = \boxed{2.5 \ \text{m/s}^2}$.

- **(ii) Kinetic energy:**
  $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times (0.5)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 0.25 = \boxed{0.5 \ \text{J}}$.

- **(iii) Clamping force:**
  $F = m \cdot a = 4 \times 2.5 = \boxed{10 \ \text{N}}$.

  Since **10 N ≪ 140 N**, the contact is **safe** for the trunk quasi-static limit per ISO/TS 15066.

---

#### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**a) (7 marks)** Describe the **ISO 10218-1 and ISO 10218-2** safety standards. What is the role of ISO/TS 15066 in collaborative robot safety?

**b) (7 marks)** A light curtain protects a robotic welding cell. The detection time of the curtain is 20 ms, the controller reaction time is 15 ms, and the mechanical braking time is 100 ms. If the operator's hand can intrude up to 300 mm and the approach speed constant is taken as 1600 mm/s, calculate the **minimum safety distance** $S_{\min}$ to be maintained between the curtain and the hazard.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- **ISO 10218-1** specifies safety requirements for the **robot itself** — its design, manufacture, and built-in safety functions (e.g., emergency stop, speed limiting, force limiting, safety-rated software).
- **ISO 10218-2** specifies safety requirements for the **robot system integration** — cell layout, end-effector safeguarding, interlock wiring, and risk assessment.
- **ISO/TS 15066** is a Technical Specification that extends ISO 10218 to **collaborative robot (cobot)** operations, defining four collaborative methods:
  1. **Safety-rated monitored stop (SRMS)**
  2. **Hand-guiding**
  3. **Speed and separation monitoring (SSM)**
  4. **Power and force limiting (PFL)**
  It also provides **biomechanical thresholds** (force, pressure, energy) based on human pain studies.

**Part (b):**
- **Total stopping time:**
  $T = 20 + 15 + 100 = 135 \ \text{ms} = 0.135 \ \text{s}$.
- **Safety distance:**
  $S_{\min} = K \cdot T + C$
  $S_{\min} = 1600 \times 0.135 + 300$
  $S_{\min} = 216 + 300 = \boxed{516 \ \text{mm}}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** The most common error is mixing $K$ in mm/s with $T$ in ms without converting $T$ to seconds. Always write $T$ in seconds *before* substituting.
> 2. **Standard Selection:** Using the wrong $K$ value (1600 vs 2000) leads to a 25% error. Match the K value to the body part and approach type stated in the question.
> 3. **Forgetting C:** Many students compute $S_{\min} = K \cdot T$ only and omit the intrusion distance $C$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Force vs Pressure:** ISO/TS 15066 specifies both **force limits** (N) and **pressure limits** (N/cm²). The chest limit is 140 N *force* but 210 N/cm² *pressure*. Confusing the two is a frequent error.
> 5. **Stop Categories:** Writing "Emergency stop is always category 0" is **incorrect**. Modern ISO 10218 recommends **Category 1** stops for industrial robots. Category 0 is reserved for uncontrolled hazards.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 – OERAT612, Module 1):**

- [x] **Robot safety** = elimination of hazards from robotic systems through engineering, administration, and PPE.
- [x] **Key standards:** **ISO 10218-1**, **ISO 10218-2**, **ISO/TS 15066**, **ANSI/RIA R15.06**, **ISO 13849-1**, **IEC 61508**, **ISO 12100**, **EN 60204-1**.
- [x] **Risk score formula:** $R = S \times P \times A$ — must memorise the action thresholds (1–4 / 5–10 / 11–15 / 16–25).
- [x] **Safety distance formula:** $S_{\min} = K \cdot T + C$ — with $K = 1600 \ \text{mm/s}$ (hand) or $2000 \ \text{mm/s}$ (whole body).
- [x] **Stop Categories:** 0 (immediate uncontrolled), 1 (controlled then power off), 2 (power retained).
- [x] **Collaborative speed cap:** **250 mm/s** (ISO/TS 15066).
- [x] **Biomechanical limits:** trunk **140 N** quasi-static, head **280 N** transient.
- [x] **Three-step ISO 12100 process:** Assess → Reduce → Verify.
- [x] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- [x] **STO (Safe Torque Off):** Modern safety architecture for servo drives.
- [x] **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO):** Mandatory before any maintenance procedure.
- [x] **Performance Level (PL)** ranges a → e; **SIL** ranges 1 → 4.
- [x] **Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** Fraction of dangerous failures detected; enters PL calculation.
- [x] **Enabling device** (3-position hold-to-run): required in teach mode of industrial robots.
- [x] **Reduced speed control** in teach mode: **250 mm/s** max for slow tasks; **T1 = manual reduced speed** in many controllers.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Safety Measures in Robotics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Robots: Manipulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Robots: Manipulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **robotic manipulator** is a mechanically constructed, multi-link articulated arm structure (kinematic chain) designed to perform industrial, assembly, or task-specific functions through programmable motion. It is composed of a series of rigid bodies (**links**) connected by powered **joints** (actuators) and is terminated by an **end-effector** (gripper, tool, welding torch, etc.).

According to the **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** and standard texts referenced under the KTU 2024 scheme, a manipulator is classified as an *industrial robot* if it is:
- **Automatically controlled**, reprogrammable
- **Multipurpose**, with three or more **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**
- Can be fixed in place or mobile
- Used in industrial automation applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent positional variables required to completely specify the configuration of a mechanism in space. For spatial motion, the maximum DOF is **6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine your own arm as a biological manipulator:

| Human Arm Component | Robotic Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Shoulder (ball-and-socket) | Base joint (J1) | Provides yaw rotation |
| Upper arm | Link 1 | Carries load |
| Elbow (hinge) | Elbow joint (J2) | Provides pitch |
| Forearm | Link 2 | Extends reach |
| Wrist (multi-axis) | Wrist joints (J3–J6) | Orients end-effector |
| Palm + fingers | End-effector / Gripper | Performs task |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as your arm can **reach** a glass, **orient** your hand to grasp it, and **place** it on a shelf — a robotic manipulator does the same, but with **repeatable precision of ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** depending on the class.

## 1.3 Why Manipulators Matter in Modern Engineering

- **Manufacturing:** Welding, painting, assembly, palletizing
- **Medical:** Surgical robotics (e.g., Da Vinci system)
- **Space:** Canadarm on the ISS
- **Hazardous Environments:** Nuclear decommissioning, bomb disposal
- **Logistics:** Amazon warehouse pick-and-place systems

The manipulator forms the **mechanical backbone** of every robotic sensing and control discussion that follows in this course — without understanding its geometry, no sensor data can be meaningfully mapped to motion commands.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Joint Types — The Two Building Blocks

Every industrial manipulator is built from only **two primitive joint types**:

### 2.1.1 Revolute Joint (R)
- **Type:** Rotational / Hinge
- **DOF provided:** 1 (rotation about a fixed axis)
- **Actuator:** Servo motor with harmonic drive / cycloidal gearbox
- **Notation in configuration string:** "R"
- **Physical example:** Elbow of human arm

### 2.1.2 Prismatic Joint (P)
- **Type:** Linear / Sliding
- **DOF provided:** 1 (translation along a fixed axis)
- **Actuator:** Linear actuator (ball screw, pneumatic, hydraulic)
- **Notation in configuration string:** "P"
- **Physical example:** Extending your hand linearly (without rotation)

## 2.2 Classification of Manipulators by Kinematic Configuration

Manipulators are classified by their **arm geometry**, which directly determines their **workspace shape, accuracy, and application suitability**.

### A. Cartesian (Rectangular) Manipulator — Configuration: **P-P-P**
- All three primary joints are prismatic, mutually perpendicular.
- **Workspace:** Rectangular box (cuboid)
- **Advantages:** Highest positional accuracy, easiest to visualize, rigid structure
- **Disadvantages:** Large floor space required, limited dexterity
- **Applications:** CNC machines, 3D printers, pick-and-place on conveyors, gantry systems

### B. Cylindrical Manipulator — Configuration: **R-P-P**
- Base joint is revolute (rotates about vertical axis), followed by two prismatic joints (vertical and radial).
- **Workspace:** Hollow cylinder
- **Advantages:** Good reach, simpler control than articulated
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot reach above base as easily
- **Applications:** Machine tending, loading/unloading, die casting

### C. Spherical (Polar) Manipulator — Configuration: **R-R-P**
- Two revolute joints (yaw + pitch) and one prismatic joint (radial extension).
- **Workspace:** Section of a sphere (hemispherical)
- **Advantages:** Large work envelope
- **Disadvantages:** Mechanical complexity at shoulder
- **Applications:** Heavy welding, forging, automotive body shop

### D. Articulated (Revolute / Jointed-Arm) Manipulator — Configuration: **R-R-R** (with optional R-R-R at wrist)
- All three primary joints are revolute (waist, shoulder, elbow).
- **Workspace:** Complex shape — approximately a sphere with an unreachable "shadow" zone
- **Advantages:** Maximum dexterity, mimics human arm, ideal for complex 3D paths
- **Disadvantages:** Complex kinematics, requires sophisticated control
- **Applications:** Arc welding, painting, assembly, sealing
- **Industry standard:** FANUC M-2000, ABB IRB, KUKA KR series

### E. SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) — Configuration: **R-R-P** (vertical axis arrangement)
- Two parallel revolute joints (horizontal) + one prismatic joint (vertical).
- **Special property:** Compliant in horizontal plane, **rigid in vertical direction**
- **Workspace:** Cylindrical (flat disc with depth)
- **Applications:** High-speed PCB assembly, vertical insertion tasks (snap-fit)

### F. Parallel Manipulator (Stewart Platform type) — Configuration: **6-RPS** or variants
- Multiple kinematic chains connect the base to a moving platform in parallel.
- **Workspace:** Small, bounded
- **Advantages:** Very high stiffness, high speed, high accuracy
- **Disadvantages:** Limited workspace, complex forward kinematics
- **Applications:** Flight simulators, high-speed machining, surgical platforms

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOF of Spatial Mechanism (Grübler-Kutzbach) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | $n$ = number of links, $j_i$ = number of i-DOF joints | Total independent motions of mechanism |
| 2 | DOF of Planar Mechanism | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Same as above | 2D motion analysis |
| 3 | DOF of Standard Serial Manipulator | $M = n - 1$ | $n$ = number of single-DOF joints in open chain | Shortcut for industrial arms |
| 4 | Workspace Volume (Cartesian) | $V_c = L_x \times L_y \times L_z$ | $L_i$ in **m** (reach along each axis) | Rectangular envelope |
| 5 | Workspace Volume (Spherical approximation) | $V_s = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi r_{max}^3$ | $r_{max}$ in **m** (maximum reach) | Hemispherical envelope |
| 6 | Cylindrical Workspace Volume | $V_{cy} = \pi r^2 h$ | $r$ in m, $h$ in m | Section of cylinder |
| 7 | Pose (Position + Orientation) | $\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{p} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$, $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | 4×4 Homogeneous Transform |
| 8 | Position Vector Magnitude | $\vert \mathbf{p} \vert = \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2 + p_z^2}$ | Length in **m** | End-effector distance from base |
| 9 | Payload-to-Weight Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{payload}}{W_{manipulator}}$ | Dimensionless | Mechanical efficiency metric |
| 10 | Repeatability (typical) | $\pm 0.02$ mm to $\pm 0.1$ mm | mm | KTU benchmark value for industrial arms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** A 6-DOF articulated manipulator has exactly **n − 1 = 6 − 1 → wait, the standard industrial arm has 6 revolute joints → DOF = 6** because $n = 7$ (base counted as a link). Always recount: **number of links = number of joints + 1** in a serial chain.

## 2.4 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering

- **Workspace analysis** determines if a given manipulator can physically reach all task points — critical in **workcell layout design**.
- **DOF count** determines task feasibility (e.g., a 3-DOF arm cannot orient a tool in 6D — you need a wrist).
- **Configuration selection** drives **cost, cycle time, and accuracy** of the entire automation cell.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Grübler-Kutzbach Criterion for Spatial Manipulators

The DOF formula is derived from the principle that in a rigid body system, the total constrained motions must equal the total possible motions.

### Step 1: Define Total Possible Motions in 3D
Each unconstrained rigid body in 3D space has **6 degrees of freedom** (3 translations + 3 rotations).

For $n$ independent rigid bodies (links), total free DOF available = $6n$.

### Step 2: Subtract the Constraint Introduced by the Ground
When all links are connected to ground, one body is fixed, removing its 6 DOF:

$$
\text{Free DOF after grounding} = 6n - 6 = 6(n-1)
$$

### Step 3: Subtract Joint Constraints
- A **1-DOF joint** (R or P) removes **5 DOF** (constrains 5 of 6 motions).
- A **2-DOF joint** (universal) removes **4 DOF**.
- A **3-DOF joint** (spherical) removes **3 DOF**.

For an industrial arm with $j_1$ single-DOF joints and no higher-pair joints:

$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1
$$

### Step 4: Application to Standard 6-DOF Articulated Arm
A typical 6-DOF articulated arm has:
- $n = 7$ links (including ground)
- $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints

$$
M = 6(7-1) - 5(6) = 36 - 30 = 6
$$

Therefore, $M = \mathbf{6 \text{ DOF}}$ ✓ (matches reality)

### Step 5: Application to SCARA Arm
SCARA has 4 joints (3 main + 1 wrist rotation), all single-DOF:
- $n = 5$ links
- $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints

$$
M = 6(5-1) - 5(4) = 24 - 20 = 4
$$

Therefore, $M = \mathbf{4 \text{ DOF}}$ ✓ (matches SCARA standard)

## 3.2 Derivation: Workspace Volume Comparison

### 3.2.1 Cartesian Manipulator
Reach along each axis: $L_x$, $L_y$, $L_z$.

$$
V_{cartesian} = L_x \times L_y \times L_z
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $L_x = 2$ m, $L_y = 1.5$ m, $L_z = 1$ m:

$$
V_{cartesian} = 2 \times 1.5 \times 1 = 3 \text{ m}^3
$$

### 3.2.2 Cylindrical Manipulator
Radial reach = $r$, vertical reach = $h$.

$$
V_{cylindrical} = \pi r^2 h
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $r = 1.5$ m, $h = 2$ m:

$$
V_{cylindrical} = \pi (1.5)^2 (2) = 4.5\pi \approx 14.14 \text{ m}^3
$$

### 3.2.3 Spherical Manipulator (Hemispherical approximation)
Maximum reach = $r_{max}$.

$$
V_{spherical} = \frac{2}{3}\pi r_{max}^3
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $r_{max} = 2$ m:

$$
V_{spherical} = \frac{2}{3}\pi (2)^3 = \frac{16\pi}{3} \approx 16.76 \text{ m}^3
$$

### 3.2.4 Comparative Analysis Table

| Manipulator Type | $L_x$ (m) | $L_y$ (m) | $L_z$ (m) or $r$ (m) | $h$ (m) | Workspace Volume (m³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (PPP) | 2.0 | 1.5 | 1.0 | — | 3.00 |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | $r=1.5$ | — | — | 2.0 | 14.14 |
| Spherical (RRP) | $r_{max}=2.0$ | — | — | — | 16.76 |
| Articulated (RRR) | reach $\approx 1.8$ | — | — | — | $\approx 12$–15 (irregular) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** For the **same maximum reach distance**, the **Spherical manipulator** offers the largest theoretical volume, but the **Articulated** arm offers the best **dexterity** within its volume.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection Guide for Real Applications

| Application | Best Manipulator Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| CNC milling / 3D printing | Cartesian (PPP) | Rigidity, linear accuracy |
| Die casting load/unload | Cylindrical (RPP) | Vertical + radial reach |
| Heavy arc welding | Spherical/Articulated | Large work envelope |
| Car body painting | Articulated (6R) | Reaches into cavities |
| PCB component insertion | SCARA (RRP) | Vertical stiffness + speed |
| Flight simulator | Parallel (Stewart) | High load capacity at low motion |
| Pick-and-place (small parts) | SCARA / Delta | Speed + precision |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Classification of Robot Manipulators


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a **functional block**. The arrows show **information flow** (from planning → control → mechanical motion → physical pose). This is the standard control loop encountered in every modern industrial robot.
