# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark and 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 3 Marks

**Q:** Define a *Wheeled Mobile Robot*. What is the difference between a *holonomic* and a *non-holonomic* wheeled robot? Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

A **Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)** is a ground-based autonomous or semi-autonomous vehicle that uses **rigid wheels** in pure rolling contact with the floor to achieve locomotion, with no legs, tracks, or articulated limbs.

A **holonomic** robot can instantaneously move in *any direction* in the 2D plane — including pure lateral (sideways) translation — without first re-orienting. Its *controllable DOF equals* the *configuration-space dimension* (3 DOF on a plane). **Example:** A robot equipped with three Swedish-90 wheels.

A **non-holonomic** robot has *fewer controllable DOF* than the configuration-space dimension. A differential-drive robot can move forward and rotate, but cannot move directly sideways — it must first perform a multi-step maneuver. **Example:** A TurtleBot-class differential-drive robot or an Ackermann-steered car.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** A complete 3-mark answer must (a) define WMR, (b) state the holonomic criterion, (c) state the non-holonomic criterion, and (d) give an example of each. Missing the example forfeits 1 mark.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks

**Q:** List the four main types of wheels used in mobile robotics. State one distinguishing property of each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Fixed (Standard) Wheel:** The rolling axis is rigidly fixed to the chassis; the wheel does not steer.
2. **Steered (Orientable) Wheel:** The wheel can rotate about a vertical steering axis in addition to rolling.
3. **Castor Wheel:** A steered wheel with a horizontal offset (the *castor offset*) between the contact point and the steering axis; aligns passively with the direction of motion.
4. **Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel:** A standard wheel augmented with passive rollers on its rim that allow *lateral* slip-free motion, enabling omnidirectional capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Students often confuse "Castor" and "Swedish." Remember: a *castor* wheel has the offset on the *chassis side* (passive steering); a *Swedish* wheel has rollers on the *rim* (no steering, only rolling + lateral freedom).

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **forward kinematic equations** of a differential-drive wheeled mobile robot. Clearly state the assumptions, the wheel-configuration geometry, and explain each step. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m, and initial pose $\xi_0 = (0, 0, 0)$. The right and left wheel angular velocities are held constant at $\omega_r = 4$ rad/s and $\omega_l = 2$ rad/s for 2 seconds. Compute the final pose $(x, y, \theta)$. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Assumptions:**
- The robot moves on a flat, horizontal 2D plane.
- The two driven wheels are identical, rigid, and roll without slipping.
- The wheelbase $L$ is the perpendicular distance between the two wheel contact points.
- A passive castor wheel provides static stability but does not affect the kinematic equations.
- Wheel angular velocities $\omega_r$ and $\omega_l$ are the inputs.

**Derivation (with the master-equation framework):**

Step 1 — Linear speed of each wheel's contact point:

$$
v_r = r\,\omega_r, \qquad v_l = r\,\omega_l
$$

Step 2 — Chassis translational velocity is the *average* of the two contact speeds (geometric mean of contact points):

$$
v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}
$$

Step 3 — Chassis angular velocity from the *difference* of wheel speeds, divided by the wheelbase:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}
$$

Step 4 — Project the chassis velocity into the world frame using the rotation matrix $R_z(\theta)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 5 — Heading rate equals the chassis angular velocity:

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

Final system (the canonical answer expected by KTU examiners):

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}\cos\theta \\[3pt] \dot{y} &= \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}\sin\theta \\[3pt] \dot{\theta} &= \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} \end{aligned}}
$$

**Brief explanation of physical meaning:** The first equation says the robot's *forward speed* is the average of the two wheel speeds. The second says the *angular rate* depends on how asymmetric the two wheel speeds are. The third and fourth project the forward speed into world coordinates, and the fifth is the heading rate.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

Given: $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\xi_0 = (0, 0, 0)$, $\omega_r = 4$ rad/s, $\omega_l = 2$ rad/s, $T = 2$ s.

**Step 1: Compute the linear and angular velocities (constants).**

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (4 + 2)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 6}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times (4 - 2)}{0.30} = \frac{0.05 \times 2}{0.30} = \frac{0.10}{0.30} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.3333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the change in heading.**

$$
\Delta\theta = \omega\,T = \frac{1}{3} \times 2 = \frac{2}{3} \text{ rad} \approx 1.0472 \text{ rad} \approx 60^\circ
$$

**Step 3: Compute the displacement along the average heading.**

Since the robot is turning at a constant rate, the trajectory is an **arc of a circle**, not a straight line. The ICR radius is:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.15}{1/3} = 0.45 \text{ m}
$$

The arc length traveled:

$$
s = v\,T = 0.15 \times 2 = 0.30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the final pose.**

The change in X and Y for an arc with $\xi_0 = (0,0,0)$ and constant $\omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= R\,\sin(\Delta\theta) - R\,\sin(0) = 0.45\,\sin(2/3) = 0.45 \times 0.6184 = 0.2783 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\Delta y &= -R\,\cos(\Delta\theta) + R\,\cos(0) = R\,(1 - \cos(\Delta\theta)) = 0.45\,(1 - 0.7859) = 0.45 \times 0.2141 = 0.0963 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Derivation of the exact arc-integration formulas:* For a differential drive, the equations $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ with $v$ and $\omega$ constant and $\theta(0) = 0$ integrate to:
> $$
> x(T) = \frac{v}{\omega}\,\sin(\omega T), \quad y(T) = \frac{v}{\omega}\,(1 - \cos(\omega T))
> $$

**Final Pose:**

$$
\boxed{\xi_f = (0.2783 \text{ m}, \; 0.0963 \text{ m}, \; 0.6667 \text{ rad})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Marks Lost Here):**
> 1. **Straight-line assumption:** The most common error is to assume the robot moves in a *straight line* and compute $x = v T \cos\theta_0$, $y = v T \sin\theta_0$. This is **wrong** for a turning robot. The trajectory is an *arc*, and you must use the exact arc-integration formulas or the small-angle Euler approximation with a very small $\Delta t$ summed over many steps.
> 2. **Sign errors in $\Delta x, \Delta y$:** The formula $\Delta y = R(1 - \cos\Delta\theta)$ assumes the robot is turning *left* (counter-clockwise). If the robot turns right, the sign of $\Delta y$ flips.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert rad to degrees (or vice versa):** When $\omega$ is in rad/s, the heading change is automatically in radians. Mixing radian and degree units in the trig functions is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — Internal Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Ackermann steering geometry** for a car-like wheeled robot. Derive the relation $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = \frac{W}{L_w}$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A car-like robot has wheelbase $L_w = 2.5$ m, track width $W = 1.5$ m, and is to negotiate a turn of radius $R = 10$ m. Compute the average steering angle $\delta$, and the inner and outer wheel angles $\delta_i$ and $\delta_o$. Verify the Ackermann identity. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Sketch Description (textual, since examiners accept either neat diagram or text description):**

- Draw a top-view of a four-wheeled vehicle.
- Label the **wheelbase** $L_w$ (longitudinal distance from front to rear axle).
- Label the **track width** $W$ (lateral distance between the two front wheels).
- Mark a point $O$ (the Instantaneous Center of Rotation) at perpendicular distance $R$ from the longitudinal centerline of the vehicle.
- The inner front wheel (closer to $O$) is steered to a larger angle $\delta_i$; the outer front wheel to a smaller angle $\delta_o$. The rear wheels are not steered.
- Connect the front and rear wheel centers with lines that intersect at $O$ to show that all four wheels are rolling on circles concentric with $O$.

**Derivation of the Ackermann identity:**

Step 1 — From the geometry, the average steering angle $\delta$ satisfies:

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{L_w}{R}
$$

This is because the front axle is at perpendicular distance $L_w$ from the rear axle, and the ICR is at distance $R$ from the longitudinal centerline.

Step 2 — The inner wheel is at distance $R - W/2$ from the ICR, and the outer wheel at $R + W/2$. Their individual steering angles are:

$$
\tan\delta_i = \frac{L_w}{R - W/2}, \qquad \tan\delta_o = \frac{L_w}{R + W/2}
$$

Step 3 — Take the cotangent of both expressions:

$$
\cot\delta_i = \frac{R - W/2}{L_w}, \qquad \cot\delta_o = \frac{R + W/2}{L_w}
$$

Step 4 — Subtract:

$$
\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = \frac{(R - W/2) - (R + W/2)}{L_w} = \frac{-W}{L_w}
$$

The absolute value of the difference is the standard form. By convention (using $\delta_i > \delta_o$), the KTU textbook identity is written as:

$$
\boxed{\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = \frac{W}{L_w}}
$$

> *Interpretation:* This identity is *independent of the turn radius* $R$. It depends only on the chassis dimensions. Hence, a mechanical **tie-rod** that enforces this geometry works for *all* turn radii.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:** $L_w = 2.5$ m, $W = 1.5$ m, $R = 10$ m.

**Step 1: Average steering angle.**

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{L_w}{R} = \frac{2.5}{10} = 0.25
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta = \arctan(0.25) = 14.04^\circ}
$$

**Step 2: Inner wheel angle.**

$$
\tan\delta_i = \frac{L_w}{R - W/2} = \frac{2.5}{10 - 0.75} = \frac{2.5}{9.25} = 0.2703
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_i = \arctan(0.2703) = 15.11^\circ}
$$

**Step 3: Outer wheel angle.**

$$
\tan\delta_o = \frac{L_w}{R + W/2} = \frac{2.5}{10 + 0.75} = \frac{2.5}{10.75} = 0.2326
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_o = \arctan(0.2326) = 13.10^\circ}
$$

**Step 4: Verify the Ackermann identity.**

$$
\cot\delta_i = \frac{1}{\tan\delta_i} = \frac{1}{0.2703} = 3.700
$$

$$
\cot\delta_o = \frac{1}{\tan\delta_o} = \frac{1}{0.2326} = 4.300
$$

$$
\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = 3.700 - 4.300 = -0.600
$$

The absolute value is $0.600$. Now compute the RHS:

$$
\frac{W}{L_w} = \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.600
$$

The identity holds. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $\delta_i > \delta > \delta_o$ (15.11° > 14.04° > 13.10°). The inner wheel turns sharper, exactly as expected for a clean rolling turn. The deviation of $\delta$ from $\delta_i$ and $\delta_o$ is small (≈ 1°) for a wide turn (large $R$), but becomes significant for tight turns (small $R$). This is why the Ackermann tie-rod is essential for *low-speed* maneuvering (parking); at high speeds, the kinematic constraint relaxes due to tire slip.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Marks Lost Here):**
> 1. **Sign error in the identity:** Writing $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = -W/L_w$ without explicitly noting that $\delta_i > \delta_o$ and the convention is to take the absolute value or swap the order. Examiners expect the boxed form: $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = W/L_w$, with $\delta_i$ on the left.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula for $\delta_i$ and $\delta_o$:** The inner wheel uses $(R - W/2)$, the outer uses $(R + W/2)$. Reversing these is a 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Calculator mode error:** Make sure the calculator is in **degree mode** for the final angle answers. Forgetting to convert radians back to degrees is a classic 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist — Read this before every KTU exam on this topic.**

- [x] **Wheeled Robot Definition:** Ground robot using rigid wheels in pure rolling contact; pose is $(x, y, \theta)$.
- [x] **Wheel Types:** Fixed, Steered, Castor, Swedish (45°/90°), Ball, Mecanum. Know *one* distinguishing property of each.
- [x] **Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic:** Holonomic = can move in any direction in 2D instantaneously. Non-holonomic = cannot.
- [x] **Gruebler Mobility:** $M = 3(N-1) - \sum(f_i - 1) - J$. Differential drive = 3-DOF. Ackermann = 2-DOF.
- [x] **Differential Drive Forward Kinematics:** $v = r(\omega_r + \omega_l)/2$, $\omega = r(\omega_r - \omega_l)/L$.
- [x] **Differential Drive Inverse Kinematics:** $\omega_r = (v + \omega L/2)/r$, $\omega_l = (v - \omega L/2)/r$.
- [x] **Forward Velocity in World Frame:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- [x] **Discrete Posture Update (Odometry):** Use $v\cos\theta_k \Delta t$ (not $\theta_{k+1}$) — Euler integration uses the *current* heading.
- [x] **ICR Radius:** $R = (L/2)(v_r + v_l)/(v_r - v_l)$.
- [x] **Ackermann Identity:** $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = W/L_w$. Independent of $R$.
- [x] **No-Slip Constraint (Master Equation):** $\dot{x}\sin(\alpha+\beta) - \dot{y}\cos(\alpha+\beta) - L\,\dot{\theta}\cos\beta = 0$.
- [x] **Kinematic Singularity:** Differential drive fails to produce $\omega$ when $v_r = v_l$ *and* we want $\omega \neq 0$… wait, the singularity is the *opposite*: we can have $\omega \neq 0$ only when $v_r \neq v_l$. Inverse Jacobian determinant $= -L/r^2 \neq 0$, so the system is invertible everywhere.
- [x] **Course-Context Link:** All these kinematics feed into the *sensing layer* of the robot — encoder odometry, IMU fusion, vision-based correction. This is why Module 1 of OERAT612 is foundational.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** If you are running out of time in the exam, write the *forward kinematics derivation* and the *Ackermann identity derivation* — between them, these two cover ~70% of the marks in any 14-mark question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aerial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerial Robots (Unmanned Aerial Vehicles - UAVs)** are autonomously or remotely operated robotic platforms that achieve lift, navigation, and mission execution through aerodynamic forces generated by airfoils, rotors, or hybrid propulsion systems, without an onboard human pilot.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: OERAT612), **Aerial Robots** are classified under **Mobile Robotics** and are defined as powered, aerial vehicles that use the lift generated by one or more rotors, fixed wings, or a hybrid combination, capable of performing **Sensing, Perception, and Actuation** tasks in three-dimensional (3D) space. They integrate embedded sensors, flight controllers, communication modules, and actuators into a unified mechatronic system.

**Key Standardized Metrics:**
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** Typically **6 DoF** (3 translational + 3 rotational) for full aerial mobility
- **Standard Mass Range:** **0.1 kg to 1500 kg** (from micro-UAVs to MALE-class UAVs)
- **Operating Altitude:** **< 120 m** (micro/mini), up to **> 20,000 m** (HALE)
- **Sensor Payload:** **0.05 kg to 200 kg** depending on platform class

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Mechanical Hummingbird with Super-Senses":**
> Imagine a hummingbird that can hover perfectly still in mid-air, dart forward, sideways, and backward, and never touches a branch unless it chooses to. Now imagine giving this hummingbird *superhero senses* — an "inner ear" that never lies (IMU), a built-in satellite compass (GPS), a brain that thinks 500 times per second (flight controller), and "eyes" that see in 3D, in the dark, and around corners (LiDAR / depth cameras). That is an **Aerial Robot**.

In simple terms:
- **Frame** = the bird's body/skeleton
- **Motors + Propellers** = the wings and muscles
- **Flight Controller** = the brain
- **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** = the inner ear (balance)
- **GPS** = the sense of where the Earth is
- **Sensors (Camera, LiDAR, Ultrasonic)** = the eyes
- **Battery** = the food/energy

---

### 1.3 Classification of Aerial Robots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Taxonomy** (must be memorized for direct 3-mark questions)

| Class | Type | Lift Mechanism | Hover Capability | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotor-based** | Multirotor (Quad, Hexa, Octa) | **4–8 rotors** | ✅ Yes | DJI Phantom, Parrot AR Drone |
| **Rotor-based** | Single Rotor (Helicopter) | **1 main + 1 tail** | ✅ Yes | Yamaha RMAX |
| **Fixed-wing** | Conventional Airplane | **Aerodynamic wings** | ❌ No | Predator, ScanEagle |
| **Hybrid** | VTOL (Vertical Take-Off & Landing) | **Tilt-rotor / tilt-wing** | ✅ (transitional) | V-22 Osprey (scaled) |
| **Flapping-wing** | Ornithopter / Entomopter | **Bio-inspired flapping** | ⚠ Limited | RoboBee, Festo SmartBird |

---

### 1.4 Why "Robot Sensing" Matters for Aerial Robots

The course **OERAT612 - Basics of Robot Sensing** emphasizes that an aerial robot is only as intelligent as its **sensors**. Without sensing:
- The drone cannot know its **orientation** (roll, pitch, yaw)
- It cannot know its **position** in 3D space
- It cannot **avoid obstacles**
- It cannot **map** the environment
- It cannot **stabilize** itself against wind gusts

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Sensing is the *perception* layer; without it, the *actuation* layer (motors/propellers) is blind and the robot cannot be autonomous.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Physics of Aerial Robot Flight

An aerial robot achieves flight by exploiting **Newton's Third Law** and **Bernoulli's Principle**. The vertical lift force must counteract gravitational weight for sustained hover.

---

#### 2.1.1 Thrust Generation (Multirotor)

Each rotor produces a thrust force **T** given by:

$$T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$

Where:
- $C_T$ = **Thrust coefficient** (dimensionless, ~0.1–0.2 for typical propellers)
- $\rho$ = **Air density** (kg/m³), standard value **1.225 kg/m³ at sea level**
- $n$ = **Rotational speed** (revolutions per second)
- $D$ = **Propeller diameter** (meters)

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** The thrust scales with the **fourth power of diameter** and **square of RPM**. Doubling the propeller size gives **16× more thrust** — this is why propeller diameter is the most critical design parameter.

---

#### 2.1.2 Hover Condition (Vertical Equilibrium)

For a multirotor with **N rotors** to hover, the total thrust must equal weight:

$$N \cdot T = m \cdot g$$

Substituting the thrust equation:

$$N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 = m \cdot g$$

Solving for the required hover RPM:

$$n_{hover} = \sqrt{\frac{m \cdot g}{N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}}$$

---

#### 2.1.3 Power Consumption in Hover

The power required to hover is:

$$P_{hover} = \frac{(m \cdot g)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot A_{disk}}}$$

Where $A_{disk} = \pi D^2 / 4$ is the total rotor disk area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Increasing total rotor disk area **reduces** hover power. This is why "big slow props" are more efficient than "small fast props" — a key engineering trade-off.

---

### 2.2 Coordinate Systems & Degrees of Freedom

> [!NOTE]
> **Body Frame vs. World Frame** — a 3-mark KTU favorite.

Aerial robots operate in two reference frames:
- **World (Inertial) Frame:** $X_E, Y_E, Z_E$ — fixed to Earth (e.g., NED: North-East-Down)
- **Body Frame:** $X_B, Y_B, Z_B$ — fixed to the vehicle

The **6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DoF)** are:

| DoF | Symbol | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x$ | Translational | Surge (forward/backward) |
| 2 | $y$ | Translational | Sway (left/right) |
| 3 | $z$ | Translational | Heave (up/down) |
| 4 | $\phi$ | Rotational | **Roll** (around X) |
| 5 | $\theta$ | Rotational | **Pitch** (around Y) |
| 6 | $\psi$ | Rotational | **Yaw** (around Z) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "**S**urge-**S**way-**H**eave" (translational) and "**R**oll-**P**itch-**Y**aw" (rotational) → **SSH-RPY**

---

### 2.3 Core Sensor Suite for Aerial Robots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the heart of Module 1 in OERAT612.** Expect a direct question on sensor classification.

| Sensor | Function | What it measures | Range / Spec |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IMU (Accelerometer + Gyroscope)** | Stabilization, attitude | Linear accel. + angular velocity | ±16g, ±2000°/s |
| **Magnetometer** | Heading reference | Earth's magnetic field | 3-axis, ±4800 µT |
| **Barometer** | Altitude hold | Atmospheric pressure | 10–1100 hPa |
| **GPS / GNSS** | Outdoor positioning | Lat, Lon, Alt via satellites | ±2.5 m (GPS), ±0.02 m (RTK) |
| **LiDAR** | 2D/3D mapping | Distance via laser pulses | 0.1–100 m |
| **Depth / RGB Camera** | Vision, SLAM | Visual features, depth | 30–120 FPS |
| **Ultrasonic** | Close-range altimetry | Distance via sound echo | 0.02–4 m |
| **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** | Proximity | Infrared light travel time | 0.1–5 m |
| **Optical Flow Sensor** | Indoor position hold | Pixel motion of ground texture | ±1 m/s velocity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensing Architecture Principle (KTU 2024):** Aerial robots use **Sensor Fusion** — combining IMU (high freq, drifts) with GPS (low freq, accurate) using **Kalman Filters** or **Extended Kalman Filters (EKF)** to get the best of both.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for OERAT612 Module 1.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = C_T \rho n^2 D^4$ | Thrust per rotor | N (Newtons) |
| 2 | $N \cdot T = mg$ | Hover equilibrium | N |
| 3 | $n_{hover} = \sqrt{mg / (N C_T \rho D^4)}$ | Required hover RPM | rev/s |
| 4 | $P_{hover} = (mg)^{3/2} / \sqrt{2 \rho A_{disk}}$ | Hover power | W (Watts) |
| 5 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2 C_D A$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 6 | $\tau = I \alpha$ | Rotational torque | N·m |
| 7 | $\mathbf{x}(k) = A \mathbf{x}(k-1) + B \mathbf{u}(k) + w$ | State-space model | — |
| 8 | $\mathbf{z}(k) = H \mathbf{x}(k) + v$ | Measurement model | — |
| 9 | $\omega_{roll} = g \tan(\phi) / v$ | Coordinated turn rate | rad/s |
| 10 | $f_{IMU} \geq 10 \cdot f_{control}$ | Sampling rule (Nyquist-based) | Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Notice the use of `\vert` or `\mid` in the formula sheet — **never** use `|` inside KTU table cells, as it breaks the markdown parser.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application | Key Sensors Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Precision Agriculture** | Crop health, spraying | Multispectral, GPS, LiDAR |
| **Search & Rescue** | Disaster zone mapping | Thermal cam, LiDAR, GPS |
| **Surveillance** | Border patrol | EO/IR cameras, radar |
| **Delivery Logistics** | Last-mile parcel delivery | GPS, ToF, vision |
| **Infrastructure Inspection** | Bridges, power lines, wind turbines | High-res cam, LiDAR, IMU |
| **Cinematography** | Aerial filming | Gimbal + stabilized cam |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Air quality, wildlife tracking | Gas sensors, GPS, cam |
| **Defense** | Reconnaissance, target tracking | Radar, IR, EO sensors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Hover RPM for a Quadcopter

**Problem Statement:**
A quadcopter (N = 4) has a total mass **m = 1.5 kg**, propeller diameter **D = 0.25 m**, thrust coefficient $C_T = 0.12$, and operates at sea level ($\rho = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$). Determine the required rotational speed (in RPM) for each rotor to maintain hover.

---

#### Step 1 — State the Hover Equilibrium Condition

For sustained hover, the total upward thrust must equal the total downward weight.

$$N \cdot T = m \cdot g$$

> **Logic:** This is Newton's First Law applied to the vertical axis. If upward thrust > weight, the drone accelerates upward; if less, it descends. For steady hover, they must be **exactly equal**.

---

#### Step 2 — Substitute the Thrust Equation

Replace $T$ with the actuator disc model $T = C_T \rho n^2 D^4$:

$$N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 = m \cdot g$$

> **Logic:** The thrust equation comes from blade-element theory. The $n^2$ term comes from the fact that blade tip speed scales linearly with $n$, and momentum transfer scales as the square of velocity. The $D^4$ emerges from integrating across the propeller disc area.

---

#### Step 3 — Solve Algebraically for $n$ (in rev/s)

Isolate $n^2$ on one side:

$$n^2 = \frac{m \cdot g}{N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}$$

Take the square root:

$$n = \sqrt{\frac{m \cdot g}{N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}}$$

> **Logic:** Mathematically valid since $n > 0$ in physical operation. We discard the negative root as it has no physical meaning for rotational speed.

---

#### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values

Plug in the given values:
- $m = 1.5 \, \text{kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- $N = 4$
- $C_T = 0.12$
- $\rho = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $D = 0.25 \, \text{m}$

First, compute $D^4$:

$$D^4 = (0.25)^4 = 0.00390625 \, \text{m}^4$$

Next, compute the numerator $m \cdot g$:

$$m \cdot g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \, \text{N}$$

Then, compute the denominator $N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4$:

$$N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4 = 4 \times 0.12 \times 1.225 \times 0.00390625$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 0.48 \times 1.225 \times 0.00390625 \\
&= 0.588 \times 0.00390625 \\
&= 0.002296875
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute the ratio:

$$n^2 = \frac{14.715}{0.002296875} = 6406.53 \, (\text{rev/s})^2$$

Take the square root:

$$n = \sqrt{6406.53} \approx 80.04 \, \text{rev/s}$$

---

#### Step 5 — Convert to RPM

$$n_{RPM} = n \times 60 = 80.04 \times 60 \approx 4802 \, \text{RPM}$$

> **Logic:** Multiply by 60 because 1 rev/s = 60 rev/min. The final answer is dimensionally consistent and physically realistic for a 1.5 kg quadcopter with 10-inch props.

---

#### Final Result

$$\boxed{n_{hover} \approx 4802 \, \text{RPM per motor}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** Typical hobby quadcopter motors operate at **4000–8000 RPM** in hover, so 4802 RPM is in the **valid engineering range**. ✔

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sensor-Centric Performance Comparison

The following matrix (commonly tested in KTU 14-mark questions) maps each sensor to its quantitative performance envelope:

| Sensor | Frequency (Hz) | Drift | Accuracy | Indoor/Outdoor | Cost (USD) | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accelerometer | 100–1000 | High (integrated drift) | ±0.01 g | Both | 5–20 | Saturation in shock |
| Gyroscope | 100–1000 | Low (short-term) | ±0.01°/s | Both | 10–50 | Bias instability |
| Magnetometer | 50–100 | Medium (EMI) | ±1° | Both | 5–15 | Disturbed near metal |
| Barometer | 50–200 | Low (thermal) | ±0.1 m | Both | 3–10 | Weather-sensitive |
| GPS (L1) | 1–10 | None (absolute) | ±2.5 m | Outdoor only | 10–30 | Loss in urban canyon |
| RTK-GPS | 1–20 | None (absolute) | ±0.02 m | Outdoor only | 500–5000 | Requires base station |
| LiDAR | 10–100 | None (range) | ±0.02 m | Both | 500–8000 | Sun interference |
| Vision (SLAM) | 30–60 | Low (loop closure) | ±0.05–0.5 m | Indoor good | 50–500 | Texture-less fails |
| Ultrasonic | 10–50 | None (range) | ±0.01 m | Indoor | 2–5 | Limited range |
| Optical Flow | 50–200 | Medium | ±0.05 m/s | Indoor | 10–30 | Needs textured floor |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always justify sensor choice with **three criteria**: (1) operating environment, (2) required accuracy, (3) update rate. Full marks require this triadic justification.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Sensor Fusion Update Rate Requirement

**Nyquist-based Sampling Rule for IMU in Control Loop:**

If a flight controller runs a PID stabilization loop at frequency $f_{control} = 500 \, \text{Hz}$, what is the minimum acceptable IMU sampling rate?

By the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency component. In practice, IMU sampling must be **5–10×** the control loop frequency to avoid phase lag and aliasing.

$$f_{IMU, \min} = k \cdot f_{control}, \quad k \in [5, 10]$$

Substituting $k = 10$:

$$f_{IMU, \min} = 10 \times 500 = 5000 \, \text{Hz}$$

> **Logic:** Higher oversampling allows digital filtering to reduce noise and ensures the inner-loop attitude estimator (EKF) receives fresh measurements before each control iteration. Industrial autopilots (Pixhawk, ArduPilot) typically run IMUs at **8 kHz** for this reason.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Aerial Robot System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the complete **sensing → processing → actuation** pipeline of an aerial robot. This is the most frequently drawn schematic in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source: Li-Po Battery 11.1V] --> B[Power Distribution Board PDB]
    B --> C1[ESC 1]
    B --> C2[ESC 2]
    B --> C3[ESC 3]
    B --> C4[ESC 4]
    C1 --> M1[Brushless Motor 1]
    C2 --> M2[Brushless Motor 2]
    C3 --> M3[Brushless Motor 3]
    C4 --> M4[Brushless Motor 4]
    M1 --> P1[Propeller CW]
    M2 --> P2[Propeller CCW]
    M3 --> P3[Propeller CW]
    M4 --> P4[Propeller CCW]
    P1 --> LIFT[Aerodynamic Lift Force]
    P2 --> LIFT
    P3 --> LIFT
    P4 --> LIFT
    FC[Flight Controller Pixhawk / APM] --> C1
    FC --> C2
    FC --> C3
    FC --> C4
    IMU[IMU: Accel + Gyro] --> FC
    MAG[Magnetometer] --> FC
    BARO[Barometer] --> FC
    GPS[GPS / GNSS Module] --> FC
    LIDAR[LiDAR Sensor] --> FC
    CAM[Camera / Optical Flow] --> FC
    FC --> TELE[Telemetry Radio 915 MHz]
    TELE --> GS[Ground Station Laptop]
    GS --> FC
    FC --> ATT[Attitude Estimator EKF]
    ATT --> CTRL[PID Controller]
    CTRL --> FC
```

---

### 4.2 Multirotor Movement Control — Motor Mixing Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic KTU Diagram:** How a quadcopter achieves 6-DoF motion with only 4 actuators (an *underactuated* system).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Desired Motion] --> MIX{Motor Mixing Logic}
    MIX --> M1A[Motor 1 Front CW: Speed = base + pitch - roll]
    MIX --> M2A[Motor 2 Right CCW: Speed = base - pitch - roll]
    MIX --> M3A[Motor 3 Rear CW: Speed = base - pitch + roll]
    MIX --> M4A[Motor 4 Left CCW: Speed = base + pitch + roll]
    M1A --> OUT1[Thrust + Yaw Reaction]
    M2A --> OUT2[Thrust + Yaw Reaction]
    M3A --> OUT3[Thrust + Yaw Reaction]
    M4A --> OUT4[Thrust + Yaw Reaction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CW/CCW alternation?** Adjacent propellers spin **opposite directions** so that reaction torques (gyroscopic) cancel out, preventing uncontrolled yaw spin. This is a **mandatory** design point for KTU.

---

### 4.3 Sensor Fusion Pipeline (EKF Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PRED[Prediction Step: IMU high-rate prediction] --> STATE[State Vector: Position, Velocity, Attitude, Bias]
    STATE --> PRED
    GPS_M[GPS Measurement] --> KF[Extended Kalman Filter Update]
    BARO_M[Barometer Measurement] --> KF
    MAG_M[Magnetometer Measurement] --> KF
    KF --> STATE
    STATE --> OUT[Corrected Pose Estimate: x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw]
    OUT --> CTRL[Flight Controller PID]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pipeline Logic:** IMU runs at **1–8 kHz** for *prediction* (high frequency, drifts). GPS / Baro / Mag run at **1–50 Hz** for *measurement update* (low frequency, absolute). EKF **fuses** both into a stable, drift-free estimate at the control loop rate.

---

### 4.4 Classification Tree of Aerial Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AR[Aerial Robots] --> ROT[Rotor-Based]
    AR --> FIX[Fixed-Wing]
    AR --> HYB[Hybrid VTOL]
    AR --> FLAP[Flapping-Wing Bio-Inspired]
    ROT --> SINGLE[Single Rotor Helicopter]
    ROT --> MULTI[Multirotor]
    MULTI --> TRI[Trirotor 3 rotors]
    MULTI --> QUAD[Quadrotor 4 rotors]
    MULTI --> HEXA[Hexarotor 6 rotors]
    MULTI --> OCTA[Octarotor 8 rotors]
    HYB --> TILT_R[Tilt-Rotor]
    HYB --> TILT_W[Tilt-Wing]
    HYB --> CONV[Convertible Tailsitter]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Modeled strictly on KTU University Exam pattern (Dec 2023, June 2024 series).**

---

#### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*
**Define an Aerial Robot. List any four commonly used sensors in an aerial robot with their primary function.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Aerial Robot** is an unmanned, powered aerial vehicle capable of autonomous or remotely-piloted flight, integrated with sensors and onboard computation for perception and decision-making.

**Four sensors:**
1. **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** Measures linear acceleration and angular velocity for attitude stabilization.
2. **GPS/GNSS:** Provides absolute global position (latitude, longitude, altitude).
3. **Barometer:** Measures atmospheric pressure to estimate altitude above ground.
4. **Magnetometer:** Detects Earth's magnetic field to provide absolute heading (yaw reference).

---

#### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
**Differentiate between a multirotor and a fixed-wing aerial robot. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Multirotor | Fixed-Wing |
|---|---|---|
| **Lift mechanism** | Multiple spinning rotors | Aerodynamic wings + forward motion |
| **Hover capability** | Yes | No (must maintain forward speed) |
| **Energy efficiency** | Low (always fighting gravity) | High (glide on wings) |
| **Maneuverability** | Very high (omni-directional) | Limited (forward flight dominant) |
| **Take-off/Landing** | Vertical (VTOL) | Runway or catapult needed |

- **Advantage of Multirotor:** Can hover stationary, ideal for inspection and surveillance tasks.
- **Advantage of Fixed-Wing:** Much longer endurance and range, suitable for mapping large areas.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer any **one** of the two choices.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
*[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a quadrotor aerial robot**, identifying the role of the **flight controller, ESC, motor, and propeller**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **expression for required hover RPM** of a quadrotor with N rotors of diameter D, mass m, operating in air of density $\rho$, with thrust coefficient $C_T$. Compute the numerical value for $m = 2$ kg, $N = 4$, $D = 0.25$ m, $C_T = 0.12$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **functional architecture** of a quadrotor consists of five major subsystems:

1. **Power Subsystem:** A Li-Po battery (typically 3S–6S, 11.1–22.2 V) supplies power to a **Power Distribution Board (PDB)**, which fans out the supply to all four Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs).

2. **Actuation Subsystem:** Each **ESC** receives a Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) signal (typically 50 Hz, 1000–2000 µs pulse width) from the flight controller. The ESC converts this low-power signal into a 3-phase AC waveform to drive a **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor**. The motor spins a **propeller**, which generates aerodynamic lift.

3. **Sensing Subsystem:** The **IMU (3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope)** measures linear acceleration and angular velocity at 1–8 kHz. A **magnetometer** provides yaw, a **barometer** provides altitude, and a **GPS** provides absolute position.

4. **Computation Subsystem:** The **Flight Controller (e.g., Pixhawk)** runs a real-time control loop at 400–1000 Hz. It performs **state estimation** (using EKF) and computes motor commands via **PID controllers** to track desired roll, pitch, yaw, and altitude setpoints.

5. **Communication Subsystem:** A **telemetry radio** (915 MHz / 433 MHz) exchanges data with a **ground station** for monitoring, mission planning, and manual override.

The control flow is: **Sensors → Flight Controller → ESC → Motor → Propeller → Lift**, in a closed loop.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Hover Condition:** Total upward thrust equals total weight.

$$N \cdot T = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Thrust Equation:** $T = C_T \rho n^2 D^4$, so

$$N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 = m \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Solve for n:**

$$n = \sqrt{\frac{m g}{N C_T \rho D^4}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values:**
- $m = 2$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $N = 4$, $C_T = 0.12$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $D = 0.25$ m
- $D^4 = 0.00390625$ m⁴
- Numerator: $2 \times 9.81 = 19.62$
- Denominator: $4 \times 0.12 \times 1.225 \times 0.00390625 = 0.002296875$

$$n = \sqrt{\frac{19.62}{0.002296875}} = \sqrt{8541.78} \approx 92.42 \, \text{rev/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to RPM:**

$$n_{RPM} = 92.42 \times 60 \approx 5545 \, \text{RPM}$$

$$\boxed{n_{hover} \approx 5545 \, \text{RPM per motor}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
*[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the **6 Degrees of Freedom** of an aerial robot, clearly distinguishing between **translational and rotational** motions. Define **Roll, Pitch, and Yaw** with reference to the body frame. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of **sensor fusion** in aerial robots. With a block diagram, explain how **GPS and IMU** are fused using an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** to obtain a stable pose estimate. Why is fusion necessary instead of using either sensor alone? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

An aerial robot possesses **6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DoF)** — three translational and three rotational.

**Translational DoF (linear motion along axes):**
1. **Surge** — motion along the body X-axis (forward / backward)
2. **Sway** — motion along the body Y-axis (left / right)
3. **Heave** — motion along the body Z-axis (up / down)

**Rotational DoF (angular motion about axes):**
4. **Roll ($\phi$)** — rotation about the body X-axis. Causes the drone to tilt **left or right**. Controlled by varying the speed of motors on opposite sides.
5. **Pitch ($\theta$)** — rotation about the body Y-axis. Causes the drone to tilt **forward or backward**. Controlled by varying the speed of front and rear motors.
6. **Yaw ($\psi$)** — rotation about the body Z-axis. Causes the drone to **rotate left or right** in the horizontal plane. Controlled by differential torque between CW and CCW propeller pairs.

The **body frame** is a right-handed coordinate system rigidly attached to the vehicle, with X pointing forward, Y pointing right, and Z pointing down (in NED convention).

> **Key Note:** A multirotor with N rotors has N actuators but 6 DoF → it is an **underactuated** system. Yaw cannot be controlled by varying total thrust (which would disturb altitude), so differential counter-torque is used.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Role of Sensor Fusion:**
Each sensor has **complementary strengths and weaknesses**:
- **IMU** runs at very high frequency (1–8 kHz) and provides smooth, short-term attitude and acceleration data, but **integrates drift** over time.
- **GPS** provides **absolute**, drift-free global position, but updates only at 1–10 Hz and fails indoors or in GPS-denied environments.

Neither alone is sufficient. **Sensor fusion** combines them to get **high-rate, drift-free, absolute state estimates**.

**EKF Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.3):**

- **Prediction Step:** IMU integrates acceleration → predicts next position/velocity/attitude.
- **Measurement Update Step:** GPS position measurement corrects the predicted state.
- **Output:** Smooth, drift-free pose estimate at the control loop rate.

**Mathematical model (for full marks, write both equations):**

State prediction:
$$\mathbf{x}(k) = f\bigl(\mathbf{x}(k-1), \mathbf{u}(k)\bigr) + w(k)$$

Measurement update:
$$\mathbf{z}(k) = H \cdot \mathbf{x}(k) + v(k)$$

Where $\mathbf{x}$ is the state vector (position, velocity, attitude, sensor bias), $\mathbf{u}$ is the IMU input, $w$ and $v$ are zero-mean Gaussian noise, and $H$ is the observation matrix mapping state to GPS measurement.

The EKF iteratively:
1. **Predicts** state from IMU
2. **Computes** the Kalman Gain $K$ (balances prediction vs. measurement trust)
3. **Updates** the state using the GPS measurement
4. **Propagates** covariance for the next cycle

This produces an estimate that is **far more accurate and robust** than either sensor could provide alone.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost 2–4 marks per question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Body Frame with World Frame** — Examiners deduct 1 full mark if you say "GPS measures body-frame position" (it doesn't — GPS gives world-frame coordinates).
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in derived formulas** — Always write $n$ in **rev/s** AND **RPM**. Half the marks go for the missing unit.
>
> 3. **Wrong direction of thrust** — Thrust is **upward**, weight is **downward**. Students often write $T = m \cdot g / N$ without showing the equilibrium equation.
>
> 4. **Vague sensor descriptions** — Saying "GPS gives position" earns only 1 of 3 marks. Say "GPS provides absolute geodetic coordinates (lat, lon, alt) at 1–10 Hz update rate with ±2.5 m horizontal accuracy under open sky."
>
> 5. **Missing "Why Fusion?"** — In sensor fusion questions, the **justification** (drift vs. absolute, low-freq vs. high-freq) carries 50% of the marks, not the diagram.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning underactuation** — A quadcopter has 4 motors but 6 DoF. Forgetting this loses a key conceptual mark.
>
> 7. **Mixing prop directions** — CW vs. CCW propellers are placed **diagonally opposite** in a quadcopter, not adjacently. Draw it correctly in diagrams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read this twice before the exam.**

✅ **Core Definition:** Aerial Robot = unmanned powered aerial vehicle with sensing + computation + actuation.

✅ **Classifications:** Multirotor (hover ✔), Fixed-wing (hover ✘), Hybrid VTOL (transitional), Flapping-wing (bio-inspired).

✅ **6-DoF:** Surge, Sway, Heave (translational) + Roll ($\phi$), Pitch ($\theta$), Yaw ($\psi$) (rotational).

✅ **Body vs. World Frame:** Body = moves with vehicle; World (NED) = fixed to Earth.

✅ **Hover Equation (Most Critical):** $N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 = m \cdot g$

✅ **Required Hover RPM:** $n_{hover} = \sqrt{mg / (N C_T \rho D^4)}$

✅ **Underactuation:** Quadcopter has 4 actuators but 6 DoF → yaw control via differential torque.

✅ **CW/CCW Propeller Alternation:** Adjacent motors spin opposite directions to cancel reaction torque.

✅ **Sensor Suite Must-Know:** IMU, GPS, Barometer, Magnetometer, LiDAR, Camera, Ultrasonic, Optical Flow.

✅ **Sensor Fusion = IMU (high-freq, drifts) + GPS (low-freq, absolute) via EKF** to get stable, drift-free estimate.

✅ **Nyquist Rule for IMU:** IMU sampling rate $\geq 5$–$10 \times$ control loop frequency.

✅ **Coordinate Convention:** NED (North-East-Down) is the KTU-standard world frame for aerial robots.

✅ **Power-Efficiency Principle:** Bigger propellers (larger $A_{disk}$) → lower hover power.

✅ **Key Material Constant:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ at sea level, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

✅ **Draw these in every answer:** (1) Block diagram, (2) Sensor suite table, (3) Motor mixing logic.

✅ **Always justify in exam answers:** *Why* this sensor, *Why* this formula, *Why* this architecture.

> **Final Tip:** In every 14-mark question, structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram → Derivation → Justification → Numerical Example**. This five-part pattern is what KTU board examiners reward with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Industrial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Industrial Robots

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

An **Industrial Robot** is officially defined by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** under **ISO 8373:2021** and by the **Robotic Industries Association (RIA)** as:

> "An **automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator**, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."

Breaking this definition down for KTU Board Examinations:

1. **Automatically Controlled** — The robot operates without continuous human intervention once programmed. It uses a dedicated controller (CPU-based) to execute the motion commands.
2. **Re-programmable** — The sequence of movements and tasks can be modified by reprogramming the controller. It is *not* a hard-wired or single-purpose machine.
3. **Multipurpose Manipulator** — A single robot can be re-tasked across different applications (welding, painting, assembly, pick-and-place) by changing the end-effector and the program.
4. **Three or More Axes (Programmable)** — A simple pick-and-place with 2 axes is not classified as a robot. The minimum programmable degrees of freedom is **three (3)**, though most industrial robots have **four (4) to six (6) DOF**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam High-Yield Note:** Any definition of an industrial robot that *omits* "re-programmable," "multipurpose," or "three or more axes" is considered **incomplete** by KTU evaluators and will attract a partial deduction of marks. Memorize the exact phrase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Human Arm" Comparison

Think of an industrial robot as a **mechanical equivalent of a human arm bolted to a stationary base**, equipped with muscles (actuators/motors), joints (revolute or prismatic), bones (rigid links), a hand (end-effector/gripper), and most importantly, a **brain (controller)**.

- **Human Arm → Robot Arm**: Your shoulder, elbow, and wrist are the robot's joints. Your upper arm and forearm are the robot's links.
- **Human Muscles → Servo Motors / Hydraulic / Pneumatic Actuators**: They produce the force for motion.
- **Human Eyes/Touch → Sensors**: Cameras, force sensors, proximity switches.
- **Human Brain → Microcontroller/PLC/Dedicated Robot Controller**: Plans, sequences, and executes the motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** A CNC milling machine is *not* a robot because it is **single-purpose** (only cutting). A robot arm fitted with a welding torch today and a paint spray gun tomorrow (with just a program change) is a robot because it is **multipurpose and re-programmable**.

## 1.3 Primary Components of an Industrial Robot (Mandatory for KTU 3-Mark Question)

A standard KTU 3-mark short-answer question asks: *List the main parts of an industrial robot.* Memorize these **five (5) blocks**:

| S.No | Component | Function | Analogy |
|------|-----------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Manipulator / Mechanical Arm** | Provides the physical motion through links and joints | Human arm skeleton |
| 2 | **End-Effector / Gripper / Tool** | Performs the actual task (gripping, welding, painting) | Human hand |
| 3 | **Controller** | The "brain" that stores and executes programs | Human brain |
| 4 | **Sensors** | Internal (encoders) and External (vision, force) feedback | Human senses |
| 5 | **Power Supply / Drive System** | Provides energy to actuators | Human metabolism |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** *Internal Sensors* (encoders, resolvers, tachometers) measure the **robot's own state** (joint position, velocity). *External Sensors* (cameras, force-torque, proximity) measure the **environment** (object position, obstacle detection).

## 1.4 Why Industrial Robots Matter in Modern Engineering

- **Repeatability**: Modern industrial robots offer a repeatability of **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** (precision in returning to a taught position).
- **Accuracy**: A separate, slightly looser metric indicating how close a commanded position is to the true target.
- **Endurance**: They can operate **24/7** in hazardous environments (foundries, paint booths, nuclear plants) without fatigue.
- **Productivity**: They consistently improve cycle time, throughput, and quality control in Mass Production and Industry 4.0 smart factories.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Industrial Robots (The "KTU Big Chart")

This is the single most important classification KTU examiners test. Industrial robots are classified based on **four (4) primary criteria**:

### Classification 1: By Coordinate System / Kinematic Structure (Most Frequently Asked)

The coordinate system of a robot is determined by the **type and sequence of its joints**. There are **five (5) major configurations**:

#### (a) Cartesian / Rectangular / Gantry Robot (PPP Configuration)

- **Joint Sequence**: Three **Prismatic (P)** joints along mutually perpendicular axes (X, Y, Z).
- **DOF**: 3 (plus wrist DOFs).
- **Work Envelope**: A rectangular box shape.
- **Advantages**: High accuracy, easy to visualize, rigid structure, large payload.
- **Disadvantages**: Requires a large volume of floor space, poor reach into confined spaces.
- **Applications**: Pick-and-place, loading CNC machines, palletizing, assembly of large parts.

#### (b) Cylindrical Robot (RPP Configuration)

- **Joint Sequence**: One **Revolute (R)** joint at the base (rotation about vertical Z), followed by two **Prismatic (P)** joints (radial and vertical).
- **DOF**: 3 (plus wrist).
- **Work Envelope**: A hollow cylinder.
- **Advantages**: Good reach around obstacles, simpler kinematics than articulated.
- **Disadvantages**: Limited to cylindrical workspace, prismatic joints are harder to seal.
- **Applications**: Machine tending, die casting, injection molding.

#### (c) Spherical / Polar Robot (RRP Configuration)

- **Joint Sequence**: Two **Revolute (R)** joints (waist rotation, shoulder pitch) + one **Prismatic (P)** joint (radial reach).
- **DOF**: 3 (plus wrist).
- **Work Envelope**: A section of a sphere.
- **Advantages**: Large workspace, good dexterity.
- **Disadvantages**: Complex inverse kinematics, lower accuracy at extended reach.
- **Applications**: Heavy-duty welding, forging, handling of large castings.

#### (d) SCARA Robot (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm — RRP variant)

- **Joint Sequence**: Two **Revolute (R)** joints for horizontal motion + one **Prismatic (P)** joint for vertical motion.
- **DOF**: 3 (plus wrist).
- **Work Envelope**: A flat disc-shaped region.
- **Key Feature**: "Selective Compliance" — **Rigid in the vertical (Z) direction**, **Compliant (flexible) in the horizontal (X-Y) plane**. This is achieved by parallelogram linkages.
- **Applications**: **PCB assembly**, small-parts insertion, vertical assembly tasks (e.g., inserting a pin into a hole without binding).

#### (e) Articulated / Jointed-Arm Robot (RRR Configuration)

- **Joint Sequence**: All three major axes are **Revolute (R)** — Waist (J1), Shoulder (J2), Elbow (J3).
- **DOF**: 3 to 6 (typical industrial 6-axis robot has 3 arm DOFs + 3 wrist DOFs).
- **Work Envelope**: A complex, near-spherical shape that resembles a partial sphere or "kidney bean."
- **Advantages**: Highest dexterity, can reach into confined spaces, mimics human arm most closely.
- **Disadvantages**: Most complex kinematics and dynamics, lowest stiffness, requires advanced controllers.
- **Applications**: **Arc welding, spot welding, painting, deburring, material handling** — *the most common industrial robot type*.

### Classification 2: By Control Method

| Control Type | Description | KTU Keyword |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Non-Servo / Open-Loop / Pick-and-Place** | Uses mechanical stops/limit switches. No feedback. Limited to point-to-point tasks. | "Limited Sequence Robot" |
| **Servo / Closed-Loop** | Uses position/velocity feedback (encoders). Continuous path control possible. | "Playback Robot" |
| **Point-to-Point (PTP)** | Moves between taught points without controlling the path between them. | Welding, loading |
| **Continuous Path (CP)** | Records and replays the entire trajectory, not just endpoints. | Spray painting, polishing |
| **Intelligent / Adaptive** | Uses AI / sensor feedback to modify behavior in real time. | Vision-guided assembly |

### Classification 3: By Power Source (Drive System)

- **Hydraulic** — High payload (hundreds of kg), used in heavy forging, mining. Drawbacks: leaks, noise.
- **Pneumatic** — Low payload, fast, low cost, clean. Used in food packaging, electronics.
- **Electric (Servo Motor)** — Most common today. High precision, clean, programmable. Used in 90% of modern industrial robots (e.g., FANUC, KUKA, ABB).

### Classification 4: By Physical Configuration / Generation

- **1st Generation** — Playback with teach pendant.
- **2nd Generation** — Sensor-equipped (vision, force).
- **3rd Generation** — Intelligent, AI-based, autonomous decision-making.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This formula sheet covers the **mandatory numerical and analytical questions** that KTU examiners set from the Industrial Robots topic in Module 1.

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Unit / Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------|---------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | $n = \text{Number of independent motions required to specify position/orientation}$ | Dimensionless | 6 DOF = full spatial pose (3 position + 3 orientation) |
| 2 | **Repeatability** | $R = \pm 3\sigma$ where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of position error | **mm** | How close the robot returns to a pre-taught point |
| 3 | **Accuracy** | $A = \text{Error between commanded and actual position}$ | **mm** | Difference between commanded and actual pose |
| 4 | **Resolution** | $\text{Res} = \frac{\text{Total travel}}{\text{Number of bits of encoder}}$ | **mm/bit** | Smallest incremental motion detectable |
| 5 | **Work Envelope / Workspace** | Depends on joint geometry (Box, Cylinder, Sphere) | **m³** | Total 3D volume the end-effector can reach |
| 6 | **Payload Capacity** | Maximum mass the robot can manipulate at rated performance | **kg** | Includes end-effector mass |
| 7 | **Joint Torque (Static)** | $\tau_i = \sum_{j} M_{ij} \ddot{q}_j + G_i(q)$ | **N·m** | Sum of inertial + gravitational torques at joint $i$ |
| 8 | **Robot Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = t_{move} + t_{process} + t_{return}$ | **seconds** | Time for one complete task cycle |
| 9 | **Reach (Spherical)** | $R = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$ (sum of link lengths) | **m** | Maximum distance from base to end-effector |
| 10 | **Reach (Cylindrical)** | $h_{min} \le z \le h_{max}, \quad 0 \le r \le r_{max}$ | **m** | Cylindrical work envelope bounds |

### 2.3 The "Why and How" Behind Joint Configurations

The **sequence** of joints defines the coordinate system. Why does this matter?

- **Prismatic joints** (linear sliding) provide **stiffness and accuracy** but limit orientation.
- **Revolute joints** (rotational) provide **dexterity and compactness** but accumulate error.
- A robot with all three major joints as revolute (Articulated) is the most dexterous, but each additional revolute joint multiplies the complexity of the **forward kinematics** (calculating the end-effector pose from joint angles) and the **inverse kinematics** (calculating required joint angles for a desired pose).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Keyword:** "Kinematic Chain" — the manipulator is a series of **rigid links connected by joints**, forming a chain from the base to the end-effector. Each link transforms the coordinate frame from one joint to the next via the **Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) parameters**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Industrial robots are the backbone of **Industry 4.0** and **smart manufacturing**. They are integrated with **IoT sensors**, **digital twins**, and **machine learning** for predictive maintenance, collaborative operation (cobots), and adaptive manufacturing. The selection of a robot's kinematic structure is dictated by the **task**, **workspace**, **payload**, and **cycle time** — the **four pillars of robot selection** as per KTU's problem-solving methodology.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-Link Planar Arm (RR Configuration)

This is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU University Examinations for Module 1. We derive the position of the end-effector as a function of the joint angles.

### Problem Setup

Consider a planar robot arm with:
- **Link 1 length** = $L_1$ (rotated by joint angle $\theta_1$ from the X-axis).
- **Link 2 length** = $L_2$ (rotated by joint angle $\theta_2$ relative to Link 1).
- The base of Link 1 is at the origin $O(0,0)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the position of Joint 2 (the elbow).**

The first link extends from the origin at angle $\theta_1$. Its end (the elbow) is at:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2(x, y) &= (L_1 \cos\theta_1, \; L_1 \sin\theta_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the absolute orientation of Link 2.**

Link 2 is at an angle of $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ relative to the global X-axis (since it is rotated by $\theta_2$ **relative to** Link 1).

**Step 3: Add the contribution of Link 2 to the elbow position.**

The end-effector position $P_E$ is the elbow position plus the projection of Link 2 along the absolute orientation of Link 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{E,x} &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
P_{E,y} &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Forward Kinematic Equation** of a 2-link planar arm.

**Step 4: Express in compact matrix form (Homogeneous Transformation).**

The position and orientation of the end-effector can be written as a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous transformation matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\theta_1, \theta_2) &= 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Work Envelope (Workspace) of the 2-Link Arm.**

- **Maximum reach** (both links aligned): $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$
- **Minimum reach** (links folded back): $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$

The workspace is an **annular ring** (region between two concentric circles of radii $R_{min}$ and $R_{max}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The forward kinematics has a **unique solution** (given $\theta_1, \theta_2$, there is exactly one end-effector pose). The **inverse kinematics** (given a desired $P_E$, find $\theta_1, \theta_2$) has **two solutions** (elbow-up and elbow-down) — a common source of exam questions.

> [!NOTE]

| Parameter | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | SCARA (RRP*) | Articulated (RRR) |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Joint Sequence** | 3 Prismatic | 1R + 2P | 2R + 1P | 2R + 1P (parallelogram) | 3 Revolute |
| **DOF (Major Axes)** | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 to 6 |
| **Work Envelope** | Rectangular Box | Hollow Cylinder | Section of a Sphere | Flat Disc | Complex Spherical |
| **Reach Formula** | $X_{max}, Y_{max}, Z_{max}$ (decoupled) | $r_{max}, h_{max}$ | $R = L_1 + L_2$ | $R = L_1 + L_2$ (XY plane) | $R = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$ |
| **Stiffness** | Very High | High | Medium | High (vertical) | Medium-Low |
| **Payload** | Up to several tons | Medium-High | High | Low-Medium | Medium |
| **Accuracy** | Very High | High | Medium | Very High (XY) | Medium |
| **Inverse Kinematics Complexity** | Trivial | Simple | Moderate | Moderate | Complex |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Medium | Low-Medium | High |
| **Floor Space Required** | Very High | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Typical Applications** | CNC loading, palletizing | Die casting, machine tending | Heavy welding, forging | PCB assembly, screw insertion | Arc welding, painting, deburring |
| **Example Manufacturers** | gantry systems, IBM | Unimate (legacy) | Unimation PUMA (early) | Epson, Yamaha, Adept | FANUC, KUKA, ABB, Yaskawa |

## 3.3 Derivation: Repeatability vs. Resolution vs. Accuracy (Conceptual + Mathematical)

These three terms are often confused. KTU frequently asks students to **distinguish** between them. Let us derive their mathematical relationship.

### Definitions

- **Resolution (Res)**: The **smallest increment of motion** that the control system can command and the robot can execute. It is determined by the **encoder bits** and the **gear ratio**.
- **Accuracy (Acc)**: The **closeness of the commanded (target) position** to the **true (actual) position** reached by the robot.
- **Repeatability (Rep)**: The **closeness of multiple attempts** to reach the **same commanded (taught) position** under identical conditions.

### Mathematical Expression for Resolution

For a revolute joint with an encoder of $n$ bits mounted on a link rotating through a total angular range of $\theta_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolution}_{angular} &= \frac{\theta_{max}}{2^n} \quad \text{[radians per count]}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a prismatic joint with a linear encoder of $n$ bits over a total linear range of $L_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolution}_{linear} &= \frac{L_{max}}{2^n} \quad \text{[mm per count]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Relationship

A robot's **accuracy** is always **worse (larger error)** than its **repeatability**, which in turn is **worse** than its **resolution**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolution} &\le \text{Repeatability} \le \text{Accuracy}
\end{aligned}
$$

- A high **resolution** means the robot can move in tiny steps, but it may not be **accurate** if there is backlash, gear wear, or thermal expansion.
- A high **repeatability** means the robot consistently returns to the **same** taught point, but the taught point may itself be far from the **true** target — meaning low accuracy.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Industrial robots are designed for **high repeatability** (e.g., FANUC M-2000iA has repeatability of $\pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$) but only **moderate accuracy**. This is why robots are taught (programmed by demonstration) rather than commanded by absolute coordinates.

## 3.4 Derivation: Workspace Volume of a Cylindrical Robot (Step-by-Step)

**Given** a cylindrical robot with:
- Vertical reach: $h_{min}$ to $h_{max}$ (so vertical extent $= h_{max} - h_{min}$)
- Radial reach: $0$ to $r_{max}$ (base to maximum extension)
- Angular sweep: $0$ to $360°$ (full rotation about base)

**The workspace is a hollow cylinder of height $H = h_{max} - h_{min}$ and outer radius $r_{max}$.**

### Volume Calculation

The **work envelope volume** $V$ of a full cylindrical robot workspace is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \pi r_{max}^2 \cdot H \\
  &= \pi r_{max}^2 (h_{max} - h_{min})
\end{aligned}
$$

**For a partial sweep (e.g., $\phi_{max}$ in radians instead of $2\pi$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{partial} &= \frac{1}{2} \phi_{max} r_{max}^2 \cdot H
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 7-Mark Question Pattern:** *"A cylindrical robot has a vertical travel of 1.2 m and a radial reach of 0.8 m. Calculate the work envelope volume if the base rotates through 270°."*

**Solution:**

- $H = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- $r_{max} = 0.8 \text{ m}$
- $\phi_{max} = 270° = \frac{3\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{partial} &= \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3\pi}{2} \times (0.8)^2 \times 1.2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 4.712 \times 0.64 \times 1.2 \\
&= 1.81 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Five Subsystems of an Industrial Robot

This block-level functional architecture shows the **interactions between the major subsystems**, mapped as a directed data-and-power flow diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PG1[POWER SUPPLY UNIT 220V AC or 480V AC or HYDRAULIC] --> CTRL1[ROBOT CONTROLLER MICROPROCESSOR BASED]
    PG1 --> ACT1[ACTUATOR BANK SERVO MOTORS PNEUMATIC HYDRAULIC]
    CTRL1 --> ACT1
    CTRL1 --> SENS1[INTERNAL SENSORS ENCODERS RESOLVERS TACHOMETERS]
    ACT1 --> MAN1[MANIPULATOR MECHANICAL ARM LINKS AND JOINTS]
    SENS1 --> CTRL1
    MAN1 --> EE1[END EFFECTOR GRIPPER WELDER SPRAYER TOOL]
    EXT1[EXTERNAL SENSORS VISION CAMERA FORCE TORQUE PROXIMITY] --> CTRL1
    UI1[TEACH PENDANT HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE] --> CTRL1
    PC1[OFFLINE PC OR NETWORK INDUSTRY 4 0] --> CTRL1
    CTRL1 --> OUT1[ROBOT MOTION AND TASK EXECUTION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Controller (center) is the **brain** that receives commands from the Teach Pendant and external sensors, and sends current/voltage signals to the actuators. The actuators move the manipulator, whose position is fed back through the internal sensors — closing the **servo loop**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INDUSTRIAL ROBOTS BY KINEMATIC STRUCTURE] --> B[PPP CARTESIAN GANTRY]
    A --> C[RPP CYLINDRICAL]

    A --> E[RRP SCARA]
    A --> F[RRR ARTICULATED JOINTED]

    B --> B1[WORK ENVELOPE RECTANGULAR BOX]
    B --> B2[APPLICATION CNC LOADING PALLETIZING]
    B --> B3[ADVANTAGE HIGH STIFFNESS HIGH ACCURACY]

    C --> C1[WORK ENVELOPE HOLLOW CYLINDER]
    C --> C2[APPLICATION DIE CASTING MACHINE TENDING]
    C --> C3[ADVANTAGE GOOD REACH RADIAL DIRECTION]

    D --> D1[WORK ENVELOPE PARTIAL SPHERE]
    D --> D2[APPLICATION HEAVY WELDING FORGING]
    D --> D3[ADVANTAGE LARGE WORKSPACE HIGH PAYLOAD]

    E --> E1[WORK ENVELOPE FLAT DISC XY]
    E --> E2[APPLICATION PCB ASSEMBLY SCREW INSERTION]
    E --> E3[ADVANTAGE SELECTIVE COMPLIANCE HIGH SPEED]

    F --> F1[WORK ENVELOPE COMPLEX SPHERICAL]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Servo Control Closed-Loop Architecture

This diagram illustrates the **closed-loop feedback mechanism** in a servo-controlled industrial robot joint, which is mandatory content for KTU's Intelligent/Sensor section.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CM1[COMMANDED POSITION FROM PROGRAM] --> SUM1[COMPARATOR SUMMING JUNCTION]
    FB1[FEEDBACK FROM ENCODER ACTUAL POSITION] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> ER1[ERROR SIGNAL E T]
    ER1 --> AMP1[SERVO AMPLIFIER PID CONTROLLER]
    AMP1 --> MOT1[SERVO MOTOR ACTUATOR]
    MOT1 --> LK1[ROBOT JOINT MECHANICAL LINK]
    LK1 --> ENC1[OPTICAL ENCODER RESOLVER]
    ENC1 --> FB1
    LK1 --> OUT1[PHYSICAL MOTION OF ROBOT]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** The **error signal** $e(t) = q_{commanded} - q_{actual}$ is continuously computed. The **PID controller** (Proportional + Integral + Derivative) drives the motor to reduce this error to zero. This is the essence of **closed-loop servo control** — the foundation of every modern industrial robot.


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions are **direct, definition/conceptual** questions. Answers must be in **2-3 sentences with key terms in bold**. Length: ~80-120 words.

### Question A1: Define an Industrial Robot. Mention its key components.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper - 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Industrial Robot** is an **automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator** programmable in **three or more axes** (ISO 8373:2021 definition). It is used in industrial automation applications.

The key components are:
1. **Manipulator** — the mechanical arm with links and joints that physically performs the motion.
2. **End-Effector** — the tool mounted at the wrist (gripper, welder, paint sprayer).
3. **Controller** — the microprocessor-based "brain" that stores and executes the program.
4. **Sensors** — internal (encoders for position feedback) and external (vision, force).
5. **Power Supply & Drive System** — provides energy to the actuators (servo, pneumatic, or hydraulic).

### Question A2: Distinguish between Repeatability and Accuracy of an industrial robot.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

**Repeatability** is the robot's ability to **return to the same pre-taught position** consistently over multiple cycles. It is measured as the **statistical spread** (typically $\pm 3\sigma$) of positions reached for the same command.

**Accuracy** is the robot's ability to **reach a commanded target position** that was not pre-taught. It is the deviation between the **commanded and the actual achieved position**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Repeatability: } R = \pm 3\sigma \quad &,\quad \text{Accuracy: } A = \vert q_{commanded} - q_{actual} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

Industrial robots generally have **high repeatability but only moderate accuracy**, which is why they are programmed by teach-pendant demonstration rather than by absolute coordinates.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are both complete 14-mark answers; only one will appear on the exam paper, and students answer the one they choose.

### Question A (14 Marks): Robot Classification and Forward Kinematics

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify industrial robots based on their kinematic configuration. For each type, state the joint sequence, work envelope shape, and one industrial application.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2-link planar robot arm has link lengths $L_1 = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and $L_2 = 0.4 \text{ m}$. The joint angles are $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $\theta_2 = 45°$. Derive and calculate the position of the end-effector.

#### Model Solution for (a):

Industrial robots are classified by their **kinematic configuration (joint sequence)** into **five types**:

| Type | Joint Sequence | Work Envelope | Application |
|------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Cartesian (PPP)** | 3 Prismatic | Rectangular box | CNC loading, palletizing |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | 1R + 2P | Hollow cylinder | Die casting, machine tending |
| **Spherical (RRP)** | 2R + 1P | Section of sphere | Heavy welding, forging |
| **SCARA (RRP)** | 2R + 1P (parallelogram) | Flat disc (XY) | PCB assembly, screw insertion |
| **Articulated (RRR)** | 3 Revolute | Complex spherical | Arc welding, painting |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Write the forward kinematic equations** for a 2-link planar arm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{E,x} &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
P_{E,y} &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values** $L_1 = 0.5 \text{ m}, L_2 = 0.4 \text{ m}, \theta_1 = 30°, \theta_2 = 45°$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{E,x} &= 0.5 \cos 30° + 0.4 \cos(30° + 45°) \\
        &= 0.5 \times 0.8660 + 0.4 \times \cos 75° \\
        &= 0.4330 + 0.4 \times 0.2588 \\
        &= 0.4330 + 0.1035 \\
        &= 0.5365 \text{ m} \\
\\
P_{E,y} &= 0.5 \sin 30° + 0.4 \sin(30° + 45°) \\
        &= 0.5 \times 0.5 + 0.4 \times \sin 75° \\
        &= 0.25 + 0.4 \times 0.9659 \\
        &= 0.25 + 0.3864 \\
        &= 0.6364 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: State the result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{P_E = (0.5365, \; 0.6364) \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The end-effector is at approximately **53.65 cm** along X and **63.64 cm** along Y from the base.

### Question B (14 Marks): Selection Methodology and Work Envelope Calculations

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step methodology for **selecting an industrial robot** for a given manufacturing application. List any **four key technical specifications** that must be evaluated.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **cylindrical robot** is to be installed in a machine-tending cell. The robot has a **vertical reach from 0.5 m to 2.0 m** and a **radial reach of 1.2 m**. The base can rotate through a **sweep angle of 240°**. Calculate the **work envelope volume** of this robot.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **step-by-step robot selection methodology** is:

1. **Define the Task** — Identify the operation: welding, painting, assembly, material handling, etc.
2. **Determine the Payload** — Calculate the total mass the robot must carry, including the end-effector, workpiece, and any fixtures.
3. **Define the Work Envelope** — Map the 3D region the robot must reach during its task.
4. **Determine the Required DOF** — Count the independent motions (typically 4 to 6 for complex tasks).
5. **Select the Kinematic Configuration** — Match PPP / RPP / RRP / SCARA / RRR to the task geometry.
6. **Verify Repeatability and Accuracy** — Compare against the part tolerances.
7. **Check Cycle Time** — Compute $T_{cycle} = t_{move} + t_{process} + t_{return}$ and compare to production rate.
8. **Select the Power Source** — Electric (most common), pneumatic (low payload), or hydraulic (heavy).
9. **Evaluate Cost and ROI** — Compare capital cost, maintenance, and labor savings.

**Four Key Technical Specifications:**

1. **Payload Capacity (kg)**
2. **Repeatability (mm)**
3. **Number of Axes / DOF**
4. **Maximum Reach / Work Envelope (m)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Extract the given parameters:**

- $H = h_{max} - h_{min} = 2.0 - 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $r_{max} = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- $\phi_{max} = 240° = \frac{240 \times \pi}{180} = \frac{4\pi}{3} \text{ rad}$

**Step 2: Apply the partial cylindrical work envelope formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot r_{max}^2 \cdot H \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4\pi}{3} \times (1.2)^2 \times 1.5 \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} \times 4.1888 \times 1.44 \times 1.5 \\
  &= 4.524 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: State the final result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{V_{work\_envelope} \approx 4.524 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert **degrees to radians** for $\phi_{max}$. Using 240° directly in the formula gives a wrong answer. Always convert: $\phi_{rad} = \phi_{deg} \times \frac{\pi}{180}$.
> 2. **Work Envelope vs. Workspace:** Students confuse **work envelope** (the geometric volume) with **workspace** (the practical reachable region accounting for joint limits and obstacles).
> 3. **Robot vs. CNC:** Many students describe CNC machines as robots in classification questions — a CNC is **single-purpose** and **not re-programmable across tasks**, so it is **not** a robot under the ISO definition.
> 4. **Forgetting "Three or More Axes":** A 2-axis machine is a pick-and-place device, not a robot. Always mention the 3+ axis criterion.
> 5. **Joint Sequence Notation:** Writing "PPP" for a Cylindrical robot (which is RPP) is a common error. Memorize the sequence as it appears in the **base-to-end-effector order**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as a **last-minute revision sheet** before entering the KTU examination hall.

### ✅ Must-Memorize Definitions

- **Industrial Robot** = *automatically controlled + re-programmable + multipurpose + 3+ axes* (ISO 8373:2021).
- **Manipulator** = the mechanical arm (links + joints).
- **End-Effector** = the tool at the wrist.
- **Controller** = the brain (microprocessor-based).
- **Kinematic Chain** = the series of rigid links connected by joints.
- **Work Envelope** = the total 3D volume reachable by the end-effector.
- **Repeatability** = $\pm 3\sigma$ spread for the same command.
- **Accuracy** = deviation between commanded and actual position.

### ✅ Must-Memorize Robot Configurations

| Acronym | Name | Joint Type | Envelope |
|---------|------|-----------|----------|
| **PPP** | Cartesian | Linear | Box |
| **RPP** | Cylindrical | Rotate + Linear | Cylinder |
| **RRP** | Spherical | Rotate + Linear | Sphere |
| **RRP-S** | SCARA | Rotate + Vertical | Disc |
| **RRR** | Articulated | All Rotate | Sphere |

### ✅ Must-Memorize Formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Repeatability: } R &= \pm 3\sigma \text{ (mm)} \\
\text{Resolution: } \text{Res} &= \frac{\text{Total range}}{2^n} \\
\text{2-Link End-Effector: } P_{E,x} &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
\text{2-Link End-Effector: } P_{E,y} &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
\text{Cylindrical Volume: } V &= \frac{1}{2} \phi_{max} r_{max}^2 H \\
\text{Cycle Time: } T &= t_{move} + t_{process} + t_{return} \\
\text{Max Reach (Articulated): } R_{max} &= L_1 + L_2 + L_3
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Must-Memorize Hierarchy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolution} \le \text{Repeatability} \le \text{Accuracy}
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Frequently Confused Pairs (Common KTU Pitfalls)

- **Robot vs. CNC** — Robot is re-programmable and multipurpose; CNC is not.
- **Repeatability vs. Accuracy** — Repeatability = same command, multiple tries. Accuracy = first-try correctness.
- **Internal vs. External Sensors** — Internal = robot's own state. External = environment.
- **PTP vs. CP Control** — PTP = point-to-point (welding). CP = continuous path (painting).
- **Servo vs. Non-Servo** — Servo = closed-loop (with feedback). Non-servo = open-loop (with stops).

### ✅ Most Probable KTU Exam Questions from this Topic

1. **Define an industrial robot and list its components.** (3 marks — every KTU cycle)
2. **Compare Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, SCARA, and Articulated robots.** (7 or 14 marks)
3. **Derive the forward kinematics of a 2-link arm.** (7 marks)
4. **Calculate the work envelope volume** of a cylindrical or spherical robot. (7 marks)
5. **Distinguish between repeatability, accuracy, and resolution.** (3 or 7 marks)
6. **Explain the step-by-step robot selection procedure.** (7 or 14 marks)
7. **Explain the closed-loop servo control of a robot joint with a block diagram.** (7 marks)

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Topper Strategy:** In the exam, **always draw a labeled diagram** for configuration questions (5-axis articulated robot, SCARA with parallelogram linkage, etc.) — diagrams fetch easy marks even if your text is incomplete. Use **bullet points** for 3-mark definitions. For 14-mark questions, structure the answer in **clear sub-headings (a), (b), (c)** matching the question parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Humanoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
