> - **Topic:** Humanoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Humanoid Robot** is a bipedal autonomous robotic platform that anthropomorphically mimics the mechanical structure, sensory apparatus, and cognitive behaviour of the human body. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, humanoids are formally defined as *"embodied intelligent agents possessing a head, a torso, two arms, and two legs, integrated with a heterogeneous sensor network enabling perception of self-state (proprioception) and environment (exteroception) to perform human-like tasks in human-centred environments."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The course OERAT612 treats humanoids as a **sensor-rich platform**. The emphasis is on the *sensor stack* — proprioceptive sensors (encoders, IMUs, force/torque), exteroceptive sensors (vision, audition, tactile), and the **sensor fusion** algorithms that bind them.

## 1.2 Key Sensor Categories in a Humanoid

| Sensory Class | Function | Human Counterpart | Example Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive** | Sense internal joint state | Muscle spindles, Golgi tendon | Rotary encoders, potentiometers |
| **Inertial** | Sense orientation & acceleration | Vestibular system (inner ear) | IMU, Gyroscope, Accelerometer |
| **Force / Tactile** | Sense contact & pressure | Skin mechanoreceptors | FSR, 6-axis F/T sensor |
| **Visual** | Sense shape, depth, motion | Eyes (retina) | Stereo CCD/CMOS cameras |
| **Auditory** | Sense sound localisation | Ears (cochlea) | Microphone arrays |
| **Thermal / Proximity** | Sense heat & distance | Thermoreceptors | IR sensors, LIDAR |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Humanoid as a "Sensing Athlete"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box**
> Imagine a blindfolded gymnast walking on a beam. She cannot see the beam, but she *feels* her ankle angle (proprioception), *senses* the pressure on her feet (tactile), and uses her inner-ear balance organs (vestibular = IMU) to stay upright. A humanoid robot does **exactly** the same — it has no biological intuition, so every sensation must be measured by a dedicated sensor and processed digitally. The robot's "brain" is its onboard processor running sensor-fusion algorithms.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Humanoid Design

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: Typical humanoid → **30 to 44 DOF**
- **Operating Cycle (sensing loop)**: **1 kHz** (1 ms real-time control loop)
- **Standard Gravity**: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- **Typical Humanoid Height**: **1.2 m – 1.8 m**
- **Sensor Sampling Rate**: Vision → **30–60 Hz**, IMU → **100–1000 Hz**, F/T → **1 kHz**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Humanoid — Operational Decomposition

A humanoid robot is decomposed into **five kinematic chains** branching from a central pelvis (the root link):

1. **Left Leg Chain** (6 DOF): Hip (3) → Knee (1) → Ankle (2)
2. **Right Leg Chain** (6 DOF): Hip (3) → Knee (1) → Ankle (2)
3. **Left Arm Chain** (7 DOF): Shoulder (3) → Elbow (1) → Wrist (3)
4. **Right Arm Chain** (7 DOF): Shoulder (3) → Elbow (1) → Wrist (3)
5. **Neck / Head Chain** (2–3 DOF): Pan-Tilt (± Roll)

**Total DOF** (standard humanoid such as ASIMO or Nao) $\approx 6 + 6 + 7 + 7 + 2 = \mathbf{28}$ to **34 DOF**.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Humanoid Sensing

### Pillar 1 — Proprioception (Knowing Itself)
Encoders at each revolute joint report $\theta_i$ and $\dot{\theta}_i$. From these, joint velocities and accelerations are derived for the **Forward Kinematics** chain.

### Pillar 2 — Equilibrium (Knowing Its Balance)
The **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** mounted at the pelvis reports:
- Roll angle $\phi$
- Pitch angle $\theta$
- Yaw angle $\psi$
- Linear accelerations $a_x, a_y, a_z$
- Angular velocities $\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z$

These are fused with **foot Force/Torque sensors** to compute the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)**, the cornerstone of bipedal stability.

### Pillar 3 — Exteroception (Knowing the World)
Stereo cameras provide depth maps via triangulation, microphone arrays perform sound-source localisation, and tactile skins on hands/feet detect contact and slip.

## 2.3 Sensor-Fusion Architecture

The humanoid fuses data in a hierarchical Kalman-filter / EKF cascade:

1. **Low-level fusion (1 kHz):** Joint encoders + IMU → body posture
2. **Mid-level fusion (100 Hz):** Posture + Foot F/T → ZMP, CoM
3. **High-level fusion (30 Hz):** Vision + LIDAR → world map, obstacle list

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics (Chain) | $T_{\text{end}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(\theta_i)$ | $A_i$ = DH transform | Compute end-effector pose |
| 2 | ZMP — x-axis | $x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} - \dfrac{z_{CoM} \cdot \ddot{x}_{CoM}}{g}$ | $z_{CoM}$ = CoM height | Stable walking |
| 3 | ZMP — y-axis | $y_{ZMP} = y_{CoM} - \dfrac{z_{CoM} \cdot \ddot{y}_{CoM}}{g}$ | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ | Stable walking |
| 4 | Stability Margin | $S_{margin} = d(\text{ZMP}, \text{Support Polygon edge})$ | Polygon = foot area | Fall prevention |
| 5 | Stereo Depth | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $f$ = focal length, $B$ = baseline, $d$ = disparity | 3D scene reconstruction |
| 6 | Linear Acceleration from IMU | $a_x = k_s \cdot V_{out,x}$ | $k_s$ = sensitivity (m/s²/V) | Pose estimation |
| 7 | Encoder Resolution | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{360°}{N_{ppr} \cdot Q}$ | $N_{ppr}$ = pulses/rev, $Q$ = quadrature | Joint precision |
| 8 | Total DOF (Humanoid) | $DOF_{total} = 2(6) + 2(7) + 2$ | Legs, arms, neck | Design specification |
| 9 | Camera Field of View | $FOV = 2 \arctan\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$ | $h$ = sensor height | Vision coverage |
| 10 | Support Polygon Area (rectangular foot) | $A = L \times W$ | $L, W$ = foot dims | Stability region |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Condition for ZMP Stability**
> For a humanoid to remain upright, the ZMP must lie **strictly within** the convex hull of the foot contact area (the *support polygon*). If $x_{ZMP}$ or $y_{ZMP}$ crosses the foot edge, the robot **falls**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Humanoid sensing pipelines are deployed in:
- **Disaster response** (e.g., Atlas by Boston Dynamics)
- **Healthcare assistance** (e.g., Pepper, NAO)
- **Space exploration** (NASA Valkyrie — Robonaut lineage)
- **Industrial cobots** with bipedal mobility (Agility Robotics Digit)
- **Social robotics** for elderly care and autism therapy
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Zero Moment Point (ZMP) from Newton's Laws

> This is the most important derivation for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise the logical steps.

### Step 1 — Define the System

Consider the humanoid as a single rigid body with:
- **Mass**: $m$
- **Centre of Mass (CoM)** at coordinates $(x, y, z)$
- **Ground reaction force** $\vec{F} = (F_x, F_y, F_z)$ acting at the ZMP point $(x_{ZMP}, y_{ZMP}, 0)$
- **Gravitational force**: $\vec{W} = (0, 0, -mg)$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law

The sum of forces on the body must equal mass × acceleration of CoM:

$$
\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}_{CoM}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_x = m \ddot{x}, \quad F_y = m \ddot{y}, \quad F_z - mg = m \ddot{z}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Newton's second law is vectorial; decompose it into its $x, y, z$ Cartesian components.

### Step 3 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about the ZMP

The ZMP is *defined* as the point where the net moment of all forces vanishes. So, taking moments about the ZMP and equating to zero:

$$
\sum \vec{M}_{ZMP} = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Moment Equation

The gravitational force $mg$ acting at CoM $(x, y, z)$ produces a moment about ZMP $(x_{ZMP}, y_{ZMP}, 0)$ given by the cross product:

$$
\vec{M}_{gravity} = (x - x_{ZMP})(-mg)\hat{j} \times \dots
$$

Writing the full moment about the x-axis (roll-moment) and y-axis (pitch-moment):

$$
M_x = -mg(x - x_{ZMP}) - m\ddot{z} \cdot x_{CoM} + m\ddot{x} \cdot z_{CoM} = 0
$$

$$
M_y = -mg(y - y_{ZMP}) - m\ddot{z} \cdot y_{CoM} + m\ddot{y} \cdot z_{CoM} = 0
$$

**Conversion logic:** The cross product $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ for gravitational force $\vec{F}=(0,0,-mg)$ and position offset $(x - x_{ZMP}, y - y_{ZMP}, z - 0)$ produces the moment components in the $x$ and $y$ directions.

### Step 5 — Simplify Using the Inverted Pendulum Approximation

For a flat ground, $z_{ZMP} = 0$, and assuming quasi-static walking where $\ddot{z} \approx 0$:

$$
-mg(x - x_{ZMP}) + m\ddot{x} \cdot z = 0
$$

Divide through by $-m$:

$$
g(x - x_{ZMP}) = \ddot{x} \cdot z
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $x_{ZMP}$

$$
x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} - \frac{z_{CoM} \cdot \ddot{x}_{CoM}}{g}
$$

### Step 7 — Repeat for the y-axis

By symmetry of the derivation:

$$
y_{ZMP} = y_{CoM} - \frac{z_{CoM} \cdot \ddot{y}_{CoM}}{g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**
> This pair of equations is the **heart of every bipedal walking controller**. A real humanoid measures $z_{CoM}$ from forward kinematics, $\ddot{x}_{CoM}$ from the IMU, and computes $x_{ZMP}$. If $x_{ZMP}$ leaves the foot polygon, an **ankle strategy** (corrective torque) is commanded instantly.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stereo Vision Depth Equation

A humanoid with two cameras separated by baseline $B$ computes depth from disparity $d$:

### Step 1 — Pinhole Projection for Left Camera

A 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ projects to image coordinate $(x_L, y_L)$:

$$
x_L = \frac{f \cdot X}{Z}, \quad y_L = \frac{f \cdot Y}{Z}
$$

### Step 2 — Same for Right Camera (shifted by $B$)

$$
x_R = \frac{f \cdot (X - B)}{Z}
$$

### Step 3 — Define Disparity

Disparity $d$ is the horizontal pixel difference between the two matched features:

$$
d = x_L - x_R = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Depth

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Disparity is inversely proportional to depth — far objects have near-zero disparity, near objects have large disparity.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Humanoids vs. Other Robots

| Parameter | Humanoid (e.g., ASIMO) | Wheeled Mobile (e.g., TurtleBot) | Industrial Arm (e.g., KUKA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Locomotion | Bipedal (ZMP-controlled) | Wheeled (Ackermann / differential) | Fixed base |
| DOF | 28 – 44 | 2 – 4 | 6 – 7 |
| Balance Required | **Yes (critical)** | No (statically stable) | No |
| Sensors | IMU + F/T + Vision + Tactile | LIDAR + Wheel encoders | Joint encoders only |
| Workspace | Full 3D human-like | Ground plane | Within arm reach |
| Control Loop | 1 kHz | 100 Hz | 1 kHz |
| Energy Efficiency | Low (gravity compensation) | High | Medium |
| Terrain Adaptability | High (stairs, rubble) | Low (smooth floors) | None |
| Sensing Complexity | **Very High** | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Humanoid Sensor-Fusion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Joint Encoders x 34] --> M1
    A2[IMU Pelvis Module] --> M1
    A3[Force Torque Sensors x 4] --> M1
    B1[Stereo Camera Pair] --> M2
    B2[Microphone Array] --> M2
    B3[Tactile Skin Arrays] --> M2
    M1[Low Level Fusion 1 kHz] --> C1[Body Posture Estimator]
    M2[High Level Fusion 30 Hz] --> C2[World Model Builder]
    C1 --> D1[ZMP Calculator]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Footstep Planner]
    D1 --> E2[Balance Controller]
    E1 --> F1[Actuator Commands to Joints]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Humanoid Motion Output]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sensor Hierarchy in a Humanoid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Proprioceptive Layer] --> P1a[Rotary Encoders]
    P1 --> P1b[Resolvers]
    P1 --> P1c[Hall Effect Sensors]
    E1[Inertial Layer] --> E1a[Gyroscope 3 axis]
    E1 --> E1b[Accelerometer 3 axis]
    E1 --> E1c[Magnetometer 3 axis]
    F1[Force Tactile Layer] --> F1a[6 axis F or T at ankle]
    F1 --> F1b[FSR arrays in soles]
    F1 --> F1c[Fingertip tactile pads]
    X1[Exteroceptive Layer] --> X1a[Stereo RGB cameras]
    X1 --> X1b[Depth or LIDAR]
    X1 --> X1c[Ultrasonic proximity]
    A1[Auditory Layer] --> A1a[Microphone array 4 ch]
    A1 --> A1b[Active sonar pinger]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — ZMP Stability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Read IMU and F or T sensors] --> S2[Compute CoM x y z]
    S2 --> S3[Compute ZMP x and y]
    S3 --> S4{Is ZMP inside support polygon?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Continue walking pattern]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Trigger recovery step]
    S6 --> S7[Recompute footstep]
    S7 --> S2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a humanoid robot. List any four categories of sensors used in humanoid robots. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A humanoid robot is a bipedal autonomous robot designed to mimic the human form, behaviour and sensory capability, comprising a head, torso, two arms and two legs.

**Four sensor categories:**

1. **Proprioceptive sensors** — rotary encoders at joints
2. **Inertial sensors** — IMU (gyroscope + accelerometer) at the pelvis
3. **Force/Torque sensors** — 6-axis F/T at ankles, FSR in soles
4. **Exteroceptive sensors** — stereo cameras, microphone arrays

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *list sensors but do not classify them*. The KTU 2024 marking scheme gives 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 marks *each* for properly categorised sensor names. A bare list of "camera, encoder, IMU" without classification loses marks.

---

### Q2. What is the Zero Moment Point (ZMP)? State its significance in humanoid locomotion. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the specific point on the ground (support surface) where the net moment of the gravitational and inertial forces acting on the robot equals zero.

**Significance:**

- It is the **stability criterion** for bipedal walking.
- For the robot to remain balanced, the ZMP must lie **inside the support polygon** formed by the feet in contact with the ground.
- It is computed in real-time from IMU and Force/Torque sensor data using:

$$
x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} - \frac{z_{CoM} \cdot \ddot{x}_{CoM}}{g}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the two choices. Each choice has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❑ Question A — *Sensing Architecture & Fusion* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensor systems used in humanoid robots with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Proprioceptive sensors** measure the *internal state* of the robot — its own joint angles, velocities and body posture.

| Sensor | Placement | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Optical Rotary Encoder** | Each of the 28–34 joints | Measures joint angle $\theta_i$ to 0.1° resolution |
| **Resolver** | High-precision joints | Measures absolute angle magnetically |
| **Hall-Effect Sensor** | Compact joints | Detects rotor position via magnetic field |
| **Current Sensor** | Motor drive | Indirectly estimates joint torque |
| **IMU (Gyro + Accelero)** | Pelvis / chest | Measures roll, pitch, yaw + linear acceleration |

**Exteroceptive sensors** measure the *external environment*.

| Sensor | Function |
|---|---|
| **Stereo RGB Camera** | Depth from disparity: $Z = fB/d$ |
| **Depth Camera (ToF)** | Real-time 3D point cloud |
| **LIDAR** | 360° range map for navigation |
| **Microphone Array** | Sound-source localisation via beamforming |
| **Tactile Skin** | Detects contact, pressure and slip on fingers |

> All sensor data is *time-stamped* and fed to the central processor at rates of 30 Hz to 1 kHz.

---

**(b) Describe sensor fusion in a humanoid robot with reference to Kalman filtering for bipedal balance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** Sensor fusion is the algorithmic combination of data from multiple sensors to produce a more accurate, reliable and complete estimate than any single sensor alone.

**Step 2 — Hierarchical Fusion Levels:**
- **Level 1 (1 kHz):** Joint encoders + IMU → body posture (EKF)
- **Level 2 (100 Hz):** Posture + F/T sensors → ZMP and CoM
- **Level 3 (30 Hz):** Vision + LIDAR → world map

**Step 3 — Kalman Filter Prediction Step:**

$$
\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + B u_{k-1}
$$

$$
P_{k \vert k-1} = A P_{k-1 \vert k-1} A^{T} + Q
$$

**Step 4 — Kalman Filter Update Step:**

$$
K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^{T} \left( H P_{k \vert k-1} H^{T} + R \right)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \left( z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} \right)
$$

$$
P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \vert k-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Application to Balance:** The IMU gives a fast but noisy estimate of tilt, while the F/T sensors give a slow but drift-free estimate of ZMP. The EKF optimally combines them to track the true CoM-ZMP relationship, ensuring the ZMP stays within the support polygon during walking.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write the Kalman equations but **fail to specify which sensor corresponds to which measurement vector $z_k$**. The KTU evaluator awards 2 marks *only* if the student explicitly maps: $z_k = [\text{IMU orientation}; \text{F/T force}]$. Always state this mapping.

---

### ❑ Question B — *Kinematics & Locomotion* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Discuss the degrees of freedom and kinematic structure of a humanoid robot. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of DOF:** The number of independent parameters that uniquely specify the configuration of a mechanism in 3D space.

**Kinematic Tree Structure:**
A humanoid is a **branching kinematic chain** rooted at the pelvis:

```
            Head (2 DOF)
              |
        Torso (0 DOF)
              |
   ___________________________
  |                           |
Left Arm (7)              Right Arm (7)
  |                           |
Left Leg (6)             Right Leg (6)
```

**Per-Limb Breakdown:**

- **Leg (6 DOF):** Hip yaw + pitch + roll (3) → Knee pitch (1) → Ankle pitch + roll (2)
- **Arm (7 DOF):** Shoulder yaw + pitch + roll (3) → Elbow pitch (1) → Wrist yaw + pitch + roll (3)
- **Head (2 DOF):** Pan + Tilt

**Total DOF:** $2(6) + 2(7) + 2 = \mathbf{28 \ DOF}$ (basic humanoid), expandable to **34 DOF** with wrist roll and additional neck axes.

**Forward Kinematics** of the right arm:

$$
T_{\text{hand}} = A_1(\theta_1) A_2(\theta_2) A_3(\theta_3) A_4(\theta_4) A_5(\theta_5) A_6(\theta_6) A_7(\theta_7)
$$

where each $A_i$ is a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transform (DH parameter matrix).

**Inverse Kinematics** is computed analytically (closed form) for the arm and numerically (Jacobian transpose / damped least-squares) for the legs during walking.

---

**(b) Explain the role of ZMP and foot Force/Torque sensors in achieving stable bipedal walking. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Stability Problem:**
Unlike a wheeled robot, a biped is **inherently unstable** — its narrow support polygon (one or two feet) and high CoM make it prone to tipping. Active control is mandatory.

**Step 2 — ZMP as the Control Variable:**
ZMP is *not* a physical sensor; it is a **computed control variable** that tells the controller *where the resultant ground reaction force effectively acts*.

$$
x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} - \frac{z_{CoM} \ddot{x}_{CoM}}{g}
$$

$$
y_{ZMP} = y_{CoM} - \frac{z_{CoM} \ddot{y}_{CoM}}{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Foot F/T Sensor Role:**
A **6-axis F/T sensor** is mounted between the ankle and the foot. It directly measures:
- $F_x, F_y, F_z$ (ground reaction forces)
- $M_x, M_y, M_z$ (ankle torques)

These measurements *bypass* the need to estimate the ZMP purely from IMU noise; the F/T sensor gives a **truth reference** that the ZMP controller tracks.

**Step 4 — Stability Criterion (Cart-table model):**
The robot's ZMP must remain inside the support polygon at all times. The *stability margin* is defined as:

$$
S_{margin} = \min_{(x_e, y_e) \in \text{Polygon edge}} \sqrt{(x_{ZMP} - x_e)^2 + (y_{ZMP} - y_e)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Control Loop:**
1. Read F/T sensors at **1 kHz**
2. Compute ZMP at **1 kHz**
3. If ZMP drifts toward polygon edge → ankle torque correction (ankle strategy)
4. If exceeded → **recovery step** (footstep adjustment)

**Step 6 — Engineering Outcome:**
This feedback loop enables humanoids like ASIMO and Atlas to walk on uneven terrain, climb stairs and recover from pushes — a feat impossible with open-loop (pre-programmed) walking patterns.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common mistake is to state that "ZMP is measured by a sensor." It is **NOT** — it is *computed* from sensor data. The KTU key specifically deducts 1 mark for this conceptual error. The 6-axis F/T sensor measures force and moment; the *ZMP is derived* from them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Humanoids**

- **Humanoid = bipedal + sensor-rich + anthropomorphic** form factor.
- **5 kinematic chains**: 2 legs (6 DOF each) + 2 arms (7 DOF each) + head (2 DOF) → **~28–34 DOF**.
- **Sensor pyramid**: Proprioception (encoders) → Inertial (IMU) → Force/Tactile (F/T, FSR) → Exteroception (vision, audio).
- **ZMP is a COMPUTED quantity, not a direct sensor reading.**
- **ZMP formula** (must memorise):

$$
x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} - \frac{z_{CoM} \cdot \ddot{x}_{CoM}}{g}
$$

- **Stability condition:** ZMP must lie *strictly within* the foot support polygon.
- **F/T sensors** (6-axis) at the ankle provide the *ground-truth* reaction force/torque for ZMP computation.
- **Kalman filter** is the canonical sensor-fusion algorithm — **predict** then **update** with measurement.
- **Stereo depth formula:** $Z = fB / d$.
- **IMU location:** Pelvis (centre of body) for best balance estimation.
- **Sampling rate hierarchy:** Encoders/IMU/F-T (1 kHz) → Vision/LIDAR (30 Hz) → Planning (10 Hz).
- **Famous humanoids for KTU context:** ASIMO (Honda), NAO (SoftBank), Atlas (Boston Dynamics), Pepper (SoftBank), Valkyrie (NASA).
- **Standard gravity constant:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ — appears in *every* ZMP computation.
- **Stability margin** = minimum distance from ZMP to support polygon edge — must be $> 0$ at all times.
- **Trade-off to remember:** More DOF → more dexterity BUT more sensors, more calibration, more control complexity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cobots
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cobots

# 🤖 COBOTS — Collaborative Robots

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cobot (Collaborative Robot)** is a robotic manipulator purposefully engineered to physically interact with human operators within a **shared, fenceless workspace**, while adhering to the safety, performance, and ergonomic requirements defined in **ISO 10218-1/-2** and the technical specification **ISO/TS 15066**.

Unlike traditional industrial robots that operate behind physical barriers (cages) at high speeds and payloads, cobots are designed using **inherently safe design principles**, **force-limiting actuators**, and **multimodal sensing** to ensure that any unintended contact remains within biomechanical tolerance limits of the human body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"A cobot is a robot whose deliberate design intent is to enable direct, simultaneous, and safe operation in a defined collaborative workspace shared with humans, employing one or more of the four collaborative operation modes defined by ISO/TS 15066."*

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Shop-Floor Colleague"

Imagine a senior craftsman and his apprentice working on the same wooden table. The apprentice (the cobot) **stops immediately** if the senior moves a saw toward his hand (Safety-Rated Monitored Stop). When the senior **physically guides** the apprentice's hand to a position, the apprentice remembers it (Hand-Guiding). If the senior walks closer, the apprentice **slows down or pauses** to maintain a safe distance (Speed and Separation Monitoring). And the apprentice is built with **soft, compliant joints** so that even if the senior's elbow bumps into it, no injury occurs (Power and Force Limiting).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction**
> - **Industrial Robot:** Cage, high speed, high payload, no human in workspace.
> - **Cobot:** No cage (or minimal), low-to-medium payload (typically **3 kg to 16 kg**), low inertia, intrinsically compliant, multi-modal sensors.

### 🧭 Historical Context

| Year | Milestone | Inventor / Body |
|:----:|:----------|:----------------|
| **1996** | Cobot concept patented | **Michael Peshkin & J. Edward Colgate**, Northwestern University |
| **2005** | UR5 — first commercial cobot | Universal Robots (Denmark) |
| **2011** | ISO 10218-1/-2 published | ISO |
| **2016** | ISO/TS 15066 published (biomechanical limits) | ISO |
| **2020+** | AI-augmented cobots with vision & LLMs | Multiple OEMs |

> [!TIP]
> KTU often tests the **origin year (1996)** and the **two founding names (Peshkin & Colgate)** as a 2-mark direct question.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 Anatomy of a Cobot — Operational Breakdown

A cobot system is composed of **three inter-locking subsystems**, each contributing to the safety-and-performance envelope:

1. **Mechanical Subsystem**
   - Lightweight, **round-edged** links (no pinch points).
   - **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** or **quasi-direct-drive (QDD)** motors for inherent compliance.
   - Joint torque sensors at every axis (typically **6-axis F/T** at the wrist).
   - **Backdrivable** drivetrains so external forces can back-propagate.

2. **Sensing Subsystem** *(Core focus of OERAT612)*
   - **Joint torque sensors** (current-based or strain-gauge) at each axis → estimate external contact force.
   - **Wrist 6-axis Force/Torque (F/T) sensor** → end-effector contact detection.
   - **Vision systems** (2D/3D cameras) → human detection, gesture recognition, hand-over.
   - **Proximity / capacitive skin sensors** → pre-collision warning.
   - **Tactile sensor arrays** (e.g., Tekscan, Xela uSkin) → fine-grained contact mapping.
   - **IMU / wearable trackers** on the human → trajectory prediction for SSM.

3. **Control Subsystem**
   - **Impedance / Admittance control** for soft contact.
   - **Collision detection & reaction** algorithms (e.g., Disturbance Observer, momentum-based observer).
   - **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** algorithms.
   - **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** controllers capping contact energy.

### 🎯 The Four Collaborative Operation Modes (ISO/TS 15066)

| Mode | Abbreviation | Working Principle | Typical Sensor |
|:-----|:-------------|:------------------|:----------------|
| **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop** | **SRMS** | Robot halts when human enters workspace | Safety scanner, area sensors |
| **Hand-Guiding** | **HG** | Human physically moves the robot end-effector; robot records the path | Wrist F/T sensor, enabling switch |
| **Speed and Separation Monitoring** | **SSM** | Robot speed = f(separating distance); lower distance → lower speed | Vision system, safety-rated camera |
| **Power and Force Limiting** | **PFL** | Robot's contact force/energy capped below biomechanical thresholds | Joint torque sensors, F/T wrist |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU **frequently** asks: *"List and explain the four modes of collaborative operation as per ISO/TS 15066."* This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question**.

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Critical Reminder:** All absolute-value bars inside table cells are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Symbol | Formula / Limit | Units | Domain / Note |
|:-:|:-------------------|:------:|:----------------|:------|:--------------|
| 1 | Quasi-Static Contact Force Limit (back/shoulder) | $F_{qs}$ | $F_{qs} \le \mathbf{140}$ | N | ISO/TS 15066, sustained contact |
| 2 | Transient Contact Force Limit (back/shoulder) | $F_{tr}$ | $F_{tr} \le \mathbf{280}$ | N | ISO/TS 15066, dynamic impact |
| 3 | Maximum Pressure Limit (quasi-static, soft tissue) | $p_{qs}$ | $p_{qs} \le \mathbf{50}$ | N/cm² | Per body-region table |
| 4 | Maximum Pressure Limit (transient) | $p_{tr}$ | $p_{tr} \le \mathbf{140}$ | N/cm² | Per body-region table |
| 5 | Biomechanical Energy Limit | $U_{max}$ | Body-region specific ($\mathbf{0.25}$ to $\mathbf{10}$ J) | Joule | Defines $F_{tr}$ curve |
| 6 | Separating Distance (SSM) | $S(t)$ | $S(t) = S_{h}(t) + S_{r}(t) + S_{s} + C + Z_{d} + Z_{r}$ | mm | $S_{h}$: human reach, $S_{r}$: robot reach, $C$: intrusion |
| 7 | Protective Stopping Distance (robot) | $S_{s}$ | $S_{s} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,v_{max}\,T_{r} + S_{b}$ | mm | $T_{r}$: reaction time |
| 8 | Equivalent Contact Energy | $U$ | $U = \tfrac{1}{2}\,k\,(\Delta x)^{2}$ | J | $k$: effective stiffness |
| 9 | Quasi-Static Max Force (energy model) | $F_{max}$ | $F_{max} = \sqrt{2\,k\,U_{lim}}$ | N | Used in PFL tuning |
| 10 | Operational Stop Time | $T_{r}$ | $T_{r} = T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{act}$ | s | Sensor + processor + actuator delay |
| 11 | Cobot Payload (typical commercial range) | $m_{p}$ | $\mathbf{3 \le m_{p} \le 16}$ | kg | UR3, UR5, UR10, UR16 |
| 12 | Repeatability of cobot | $r$ | $r \le \pm \mathbf{0.03}$ to $\pm \mathbf{0.1}$ | mm | Lower than industrial, by design |
| 13 | Inertia-scaled impact force (Raghavan model) | $F_{imp}$ | $F_{imp} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,E_{k}}{d}}$ | N | $E_{k}$: kinetic energy, $d$: deformation |

### 🛠️ Engineering Utility of Cobots

- **Automotive:** Final assembly where humans still need dexterity (wiring, trimming).
- **Medical:** Surgical-assist robots (e.g., da Vinci), rehabilitation exoskeletons.
- **Logistics:** Pick-and-place alongside pickers (Amazon, DHL).
- **Agri-Food:** Packing, palletising shoulder-to-shoulder with workers.
- **R&D / Education:** Teaching platforms (UR5e, Dobot, Franka Panda).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📘 Derivation 3.1 — Quasi-Static Contact Force from Energy Limit

In **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** mode, ISO/TS 15066 requires that the **energy transferred** to the human in any unintended contact must remain below a body-region specific threshold $U_{lim}$.

We model the contact using a **linear spring** of effective stiffness $k$ (N/m) representing the robot link + human tissue system. When the robot link compresses the human tissue by a deformation $\Delta x$ (m), the elastic energy stored is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \, k \, (\Delta x)^{2}$$

At the **maximum allowable deformation** $\Delta x_{max}$, the contact force $F_{max}$ is given by Hooke's law:

$$F_{max} = k \, \Delta x_{max}$$

**Step 1 — Eliminate the deformation variable:**

From the energy equation, solve for $\Delta x$:

$$\Delta x = \sqrt{\frac{2\,U}{k}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the force equation:**

$$F_{max} = k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\,U_{lim}}{k}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify algebraically:**

$$F_{max} = \sqrt{k^{2} \cdot \frac{2\,U_{lim}}{k}} = \sqrt{2\,k\,U_{lim}}$$

**Step 4 — Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\,F_{max} = \sqrt{2\,k\,U_{lim}}\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** To reduce peak contact force $F_{max}$, the designer can (a) **lower the effective stiffness $k$** (softer covers) or (b) **lower $U_{lim}$** (operate the cobot at lower speed/acceleration, since $U_{lim}$ is coupled to kinetic energy).

---

### 📘 Derivation 3.2 — Quasi-Static Force Limit from a Numerical Body-Region Case

**Problem Setup:** The cobot end-effector contacts a **human upper arm** (soft tissue). Given:
- Effective stiffness $k = \mathbf{75{,}000 \text{ N/m}}$.
- Energy limit (quasi-static) $U_{lim} = \mathbf{0.50 \text{ J}}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the derived formula directly:**

$$F_{max} = \sqrt{2 \cdot k \cdot U_{lim}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$F_{max} = \sqrt{2 \times 75{,}000 \times 0.50}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the product inside the root:**

$$F_{max} = \sqrt{75{,}000}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the square root:**

$$F_{max} \approx 273.86 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to the ISO/TS 15066 limit for the back/shoulder (quasi-static = 140 N):**

Since $273.86 \text{ N} > 140 \text{ N}$, the configuration is **NON-COMPLIANT**.

**Step 6 — Find the maximum allowable stiffness $k^*$ such that $F_{max} \le 140$ N:**

$$140 = \sqrt{2 \cdot k^{*} \cdot 0.50} \Rightarrow 140^{2} = k^{*} \Rightarrow k^{*} = 19{,}600 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{\,k^{*} = 19{,}600 \text{ N/m}\,}$$

**Conclusion:** The cobot link stiffness must be reduced to **at most 19.6 kN/m** (e.g., by adding a soft silicone cover) to satisfy the quasi-static contact limit for the back/shoulder region.

---

### 📘 Derivation 3.3 — SSM Protective Distance

**Given:**
- Human approach speed $v_{h} = \mathbf{1.6 \text{ m/s}}$ (typical walking).
- Robot approach speed $v_{r} = \mathbf{0.5 \text{ m/s}}$.
- Reaction time $T_{r} = \mathbf{0.1 \text{ s}}$.
- Intrusion distance $C = \mathbf{100 \text{ mm}}$.
- Sensor uncertainty $Z_{d} = Z_{r} = \mathbf{50 \text{ mm}}$ each.

**Step 1 — Human reach over reaction time:**

$$S_{h} = v_{h} \cdot T_{r} = 1.6 \times 0.1 = 0.16 \text{ m} = 160 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Robot reach over reaction time:**

$$S_{r} = v_{r} \cdot T_{r} = 0.5 \times 0.1 = 0.05 \text{ m} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Robot stopping distance:**

$$S_{s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot v_{r} \cdot T_{r} = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.1 = 0.025 \text{ m} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total minimum protective separation distance:**

$$\begin{aligned} S_{min} &= S_{h} + S_{r} + S_{s} + C + Z_{d} + Z_{r} \\ &= 160 + 50 + 25 + 100 + 50 + 50 \\ &= 435 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,S_{min} = 435 \text{ mm}\,}$$

> The human must stay at least **435 mm** away from any moving part of the cobot for it to safely maintain the $0.5$ m/s speed.

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Quick Reference

| Parameter | Traditional Industrial Robot | Cobot |
|:----------|:---------------------------:|:-----:|
| Workspace enclosure | **Mandatory (cage)** | **Not required** |
| Payload (typical) | 10 – 1000 kg | **3 – 16 kg** |
| Max TCP speed | 2 – 10 m/s | **0.25 – 1.0 m/s** |
| Repeatability | ± 0.02 mm | **± 0.05 – 0.1 mm** |
| Joint torque sensing | Rare | **Mandatory** |
| Force / pressure limit | Not applicable | **≤ 140 N (QS) / 280 N (TR)** |
| Cost (entry) | $50 K – $400 K | **$20 K – $50 K** |
| Safety standard | ISO 10218 | **ISO 10218 + ISO/TS 15066** |
| Programming | Teach pendant, RAPID | **Hand-guiding, graphical UI** |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 4.1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cobot Sensing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV["OPERATIONAL ENVIRONMENT"]
        HUM["HUMAN OPERATOR"]
        TASK["WORKPIECE / TASK"]
    end

    subgraph SENS["SENSING LAYER (OERAT612 CORE)"]
        JTS["JOINT TORQUE SENSORS 6x"]
        FTS["WRIST 6-AXIS F OR T SENSOR"]
        VIS["RGB-D VISION SYSTEM"]
        PROX["PROXIMITY SKIN SENSORS"]
        TAC["TACTILE ARRAY AT GRIPPER"]
        IMU["HUMAN WEARABLE IMU"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER"]
        OBS["DISTURBANCE OBSERVER"]
        IMP["IMPEDANCE CONTROLLER"]
        PFL["POWER AND FORCE LIMITER"]
        SSM["SPEED AND SEPARATION MONITOR"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        SEA["SERIES ELASTIC ACTUATORS 6x"]
        GRIP["GRIPPER / END EFFECTOR"]
    end

    HUM -- "exteroception" --> VIS
    HUM -- "bio-signals" --> IMU
    TASK -- "visual" --> VIS

    VIS --> SSM
    IMU --> SSM
    PROX --> OBS
    JTS --> OBS
    FTS --> PFL
    TAC --> PFL

    OBS --> IMP
    SSM --> IMP
    PFL --> IMP
    IMP --> SEA
    SEA --> GRIP
    GRIP --> HUM
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 4.2 — The Four ISO/TS 15066 Collaborative Modes (Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["HUMAN ENTERS COBOT WORKSPACE"] --> Q1{"IS HUMAN WITHIN\nREACH ENVELOPE?"}

    Q1 -- "NO" --> A1["SRMS\nSAFETY RATED MONITORED STOP\nRobot waits, all axes servo-hold"]

    Q1 -- "YES" --> Q2{"IS HUMAN\nACTIVELY GRIPPING\nEND EFFECTOR?"}

    Q2 -- "YES" --> A2["HG\nHAND GUIDING\nRobot records path, gravity-compensated"]

    Q2 -- "NO" --> Q3{"DISTANCE S(t)\nWITHIN SAFE THRESHOLD?"}

    Q3 -- "YES - VERY CLOSE" --> A3["PFL\nPOWER AND FORCE LIMITING\nF is less than 140 N QS, 280 N TR"]

    Q3 -- "NO - SEPARATED" --> A4["SSM\nSPEED AND SEPARATION MONITORING\nSpeed scaled to S(t)"]
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 4.3 — Cobot Sensing Loop with PFL Action

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CMD["USER COMMAND\nv_ref, x_ref"]
    CTRL["IMpedance\nCONTROLLER"]
    ACT["ACTUATOR\nSEA / QDD"]
    LINK["ROBOT LINK"]
    FEXT["EXTERNAL FORCE\nHUMAN CONTACT"]
    FTS2["F or T SENSOR\nAT WRIST"]
    PFL2["PFL\nCHECKER"]
    SAFE{"F MEASURED\nis less than F_LIMIT?"}
    STOP["PROTECTIVE STOP\nTRIGGERED"]

    CMD --> CTRL --> ACT --> LINK
    LINK --> FTS2
    FEXT -. "acts on" .-> LINK
    FTS2 --> PFL2
    PFL2 --> SAFE
    SAFE -- "YES" --> CTRL
    SAFE -- "NO" --> STOP
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### ✍️ PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2019 Scheme] *(3 marks)*
**Define a "Cobot." State the two international standards that govern its safe deployment.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Cobot (Collaborative Robot)** is a robot designed for direct, safe, and simultaneous operation in a shared workspace with a human operator, without the need for traditional guarding. It achieves this through inherent safe design, force-limiting, and multimodal sensing.

**Governing Standards:**
1. **ISO 10218-1 & ISO 10218-2** — Safety requirements for industrial robots (Part 1: Robot, Part 2: Integration).
2. **ISO/TS 15066** — Technical Specification providing **biomechanical thresholds** (force, pressure, energy) for collaborative operation.

*(Expected: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for ISO 10218, 1 mark for ISO/TS 15066.)*

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary] *(3 marks)*
**List the four collaborative operation modes defined in ISO/TS 15066 with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Mode | One-Line Description |
|:-:|:-----|:---------------------|
| 1 | **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** | The robot ceases all motion in a safety-rated manner whenever a human enters the collaborative workspace. |
| 2 | **Hand-Guiding (HG)** | The operator physically grasps and moves the end-effector; the robot records and replays the trajectory. |
| 3 | **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** | The robot's speed is dynamically reduced as the separating distance to the human decreases. |
| 4 | **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** | Inherent design and control cap the contact force/energy below biomechanical injury thresholds. |

*(Expected: 0.75 marks per correctly described mode.)*

---

### ✍️ PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 14 Marks each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **biomechanical limits** specified in **ISO/TS 15066** for collaborative robots. Discuss **quasi-static** and **transient** contact scenarios with their force and pressure limits for at least three body regions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **sensing architecture** of a cobot. How do **joint torque sensors** and **wrist F/T sensors** together enable **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)**? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **biomechanical limits** in **ISO/TS 15066** are derived from pain-onset and injury-onset studies on human volunteers and cadavers. The standard distinguishes between two contact regimes:

**1. Quasi-Static Contact (clamping/crushing):**
- Body part is trapped between the robot and a fixed surface.
- Limits are based on the **maximum pressure** and **force** sustained without injury over a few seconds.
- Reference limits:

| Body Region | $F_{qs}$ (N) | $p_{qs}$ (N/cm²) |
|:------------|:------------:|:----------------:|
| Back / Shoulder | 140 | 50 |
| Hand / Finger (palm) | 140 | 50 |
| Forearm | 140 | 50 |
| Chest | 110 | 50 |
| Abdomen | 110 | 30 |
| Thigh | 180 | 50 |
| Shin / Calf | 110 | 50 |

**2. Transient Contact (dynamic impact):**
- A non-clamping, brief collision. Energy absorption is allowed up to higher limits.
- Reference limits:

| Body Region | $F_{tr}$ (N) | $p_{tr}$ (N/cm²) | $U_{max}$ (J) |
|:------------|:------------:|:----------------:|:-------------:|
| Back / Shoulder | 280 | 140 | 10 |
| Hand / Finger (palm) | 280 | 140 | 10 |
| Forearm | 280 | 140 | 10 |
| Chest | 220 | 110 | 5 |
| Abdomen | 220 | 80 | 5 |
| Thigh | 360 | 140 | 25 |
| Shin / Calf | 220 | 110 | 5 |

**Key Insight:** Transient limits are roughly **2× the quasi-static limits** because the contact duration is too short for sustained tissue damage. Designers must specify which regime applies to a given application through **risk assessment**.

*(Mark split: 2 marks definition, 2 marks QS table, 2 marks TR table, 1 mark insight/comparison.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

A cobot's sensing architecture integrates **distributed joint-level** and **end-effector level** force estimation to enable Power and Force Limiting.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HUM2["HUMAN CONTACT FORCE F_ext"]
    LIN2["ROBOT LINK"]
    JTS2["6x JOINT TORQUE SENSORS"]
    FTS2["WRIST 6-AXIS F or T SENSOR"]
    EST["EXTERNAL FORCE ESTIMATOR\nGE + INERTIA COMPENSATION"]
    PFL3["PFL CHECKER\nF is less than F_lim"]
    DEC{"ACTION?"}
    SOFT["SOFT STOP / IMPEDANCE REFLEX"]
    HARD["PROTECTIVE STOP CATEGORY 1"]

    HUM2 -.-> LIN2
    LIN2 --> JTS2
    LIN2 --> FTS2
    JTS2 --> EST
    FTS2 --> EST
    EST --> PFL3
    PFL3 --> DEC
    DEC -- "F is less than F_lim" --> SOFT
    DEC -- "F is greater than F_lim" --> HARD
```

**Working Principle:**

- The **Generalized Momentum Observer (GMO)** or **Disturbance Observer (DOB)** estimates the **external joint torque** $\hat{\tau}_{ext}$ from motor current and link-side torque sensors.
- The external force at the **end-effector** $F_{ext,ee}$ is reconstructed from $\hat{\tau}_{ext}$ using the **geometric Jacobian** transpose $J(\theta)^{T}$:

$$F_{ext,ee} = (J^{T})^{+}\,\hat{\tau}_{ext} - F_{0}$$

- The **wrist F/T sensor** provides a **direct, calibrated measurement** of $F_{ext,ee}$ for verification and redundancy.
- The **PFL checker** compares the measured/estimated force to the threshold ($F_{lim} = 140$ N QS, $280$ N TR).
- If exceeded → **protective stop or impedance reflex** (compliance increase).

**Why both sensors?**
- Joint torque → **cobot-level** (the whole link can hit something, not just the wrist).
- Wrist F/T → **end-effector level** (precise for tool contact, calibrated).
- Together they provide **full-arm coverage** and **fault-tolerance** against single-sensor failure.

*(Mark split: 2 marks block diagram, 2 marks joint torque estimation, 2 marks wrist F/T role, 1 mark PFL logic.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Derive the **maximum allowable contact force** for a cobot in Power and Force Limiting mode using an **energy-based model**. State clearly all assumptions. Apply the derived formula to compute $F_{max}$ for: $k = 50{,}000$ N/m, $U_{lim} = 0.4$ J. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** mode in detail. Derive the expression for the **minimum protective separation distance** $S_{min}$ and apply it for the data: $v_{h} = 1.5$ m/s, $v_{r} = 0.4$ m/s, $T_{r} = 0.08$ s, $C = 80$ mm, $Z_{d} = Z_{r} = 40$ mm. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The robot link–human tissue contact is modelled as a **linear spring** with effective stiffness $k$ (N/m).
2. The contact is **inertialess** (slow enough that mass terms are negligible) — i.e., quasi-static.
3. The contact is **unidirectional** and **energy is conserved** in the spring (no dissipation considered; conservative estimate).
4. The only energy transfer is the **elastic potential energy** stored in the equivalent spring.

**Derivation:**

Elastic potential energy stored in a spring at deformation $\Delta x$:

$$U = \frac{1}{2}\,k\,(\Delta x)^{2}$$

Hooke's law for contact force at that deformation:

$$F = k \cdot \Delta x$$

Eliminate $\Delta x$:

$$\Delta x = \frac{F}{k}$$

Substitute:

$$U = \frac{1}{2}\,k\,\left(\frac{F}{k}\right)^{2} = \frac{F^{2}}{2\,k}$$

Solve for $F$:

$$F = \sqrt{2\,k\,U}$$

At the limit:

$$\boxed{\,F_{max} = \sqrt{2\,k\,U_{lim}}\,}$$

**Numerical Application:**

Given $k = 50{,}000$ N/m, $U_{lim} = 0.4$ J:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{max} &= \sqrt{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.4} \\ &= \sqrt{40{,}000} \\ &= 200 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** Since $F_{max} = 200 \text{ N}$ **exceeds the ISO/TS 15066 quasi-static limit of 140 N** for the back/shoulder, the system is **non-compliant**. The designer must either:
- Soften the cover (reduce $k$ to $\le 24{,}500$ N/m), or
- Operate the cobot at lower kinetic energy such that $U_{lim} \le 0.196$ J (e.g., reduce joint speed by $\sqrt{0.196/0.4} \approx 0.7\times$).

*(Mark split: 2 marks assumptions, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks numerical evaluation.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**SSM Mode Explanation:**
In Speed and Separation Monitoring, the cobot continuously senses the **separating distance** $S(t)$ between itself and the human using a **safety-rated vision system** (e.g., safetyEYE, Pilz) or laser scanners. The cobot's **maximum allowed speed** $v_{r,max}$ is a **monotonically increasing function** of $S(t)$: as the human approaches, the robot slows down; if the human crosses the **protective separation distance** $S_{p}$, the robot performs a **protective stop**.

**Derivation of $S_{min}$:**

The minimum protective separation distance must account for **all movements** during the **system reaction time** $T_{r}$ (time from human-detection to robot-halt) plus the **robot stopping distance** plus **uncertainties** plus a **human intrusion distance**:

$$S_{min} = S_{h}(t) + S_{r}(t) + S_{s} + C + Z_{d} + Z_{r}$$

Where:
- $S_{h}(t) = v_{h} \cdot T_{r}$ — human travels toward robot.
- $S_{r}(t) = v_{r} \cdot T_{r}$ — robot travels toward human.
- $S_{s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot v_{r} \cdot T_{r}$ — robot stopping distance (assuming linear deceleration).
- $C$ — intrusion distance (toward the hazard, based on safety standard).
- $Z_{d}, Z_{r}$ — position uncertainties of detection system and robot.

**Numerical Application:**
Given $v_{h} = 1.5$ m/s, $v_{r} = 0.4$ m/s, $T_{r} = 0.08$ s, $C = 80$ mm, $Z_{d} = Z_{r} = 40$ mm:

$$\begin{aligned} S_{h} &= 1.5 \times 0.08 \times 1000 = 120 \text{ mm} \\ S_{r} &= 0.4 \times 0.08 \times 1000 = 32 \text{ mm} \\ S_{s} &= 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.08 \times 1000 = 16 \text{ mm} \\ S_{min} &= 120 + 32 + 16 + 80 + 40 + 40 \\ &= 328 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,S_{min} = 328 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**Operational Implication:** If the human comes within **328 mm** of the moving part, the cobot must immediately command a protective stop. If the human is at exactly the threshold, the cobot's maximum allowed speed becomes zero. This ensures no contact occurs.

*(Mark split: 2 marks SSM explanation, 2 marks derivation, 3 marks numerical evaluation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating ISO 10218 with ISO/TS 15066** — ISO 10218 is the *mandatory* standard; ISO/TS 15066 is a *technical specification* with biomechanical numbers. Marks lost if not distinguished.
> 2. **Forgetting the units of pressure** — ISO/TS 15066 uses **N/cm²** (not Pa or N/m²). Wrong units = −1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing QS and TR limits** — Students often write 140 N for everything. Remember: **140 N (QS) and 280 N (TR)**.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of $F_{max} = \sqrt{2kU_{lim}}$** — If a derivation is asked, the **3-step algebra (energy → deformation → force)** must appear. Skipping directly to the final equation costs **2 of 7 marks**.
> 5. **Peshkin & Colgate vs. Colgate & Peshkin** — Both names are required. Losing either = −0.5 mark.
> 6. **Cobot ≠ Service robot** — A cobot is a *manipulator*; a service robot is a *mobile platform*. Don't confuse.
> 7. **SSM distance units** — Always convert m/s × s to **mm** at the end. Mixing units = −1 mark.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🧠 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this!)**
> - ✅ **Cobot** = Collaborative Robot; concept patent 1996 by **Peshkin & Colgate**.
> - ✅ **Standards:** **ISO 10218-1/-2** + **ISO/TS 15066**.
> - ✅ **Four Modes:** **SRMS, HG, SSM, PFL** (mnemonic: *"S-H-S-P"*).
> - ✅ **Quasi-Static Force Limit (back/shoulder):** **140 N**, **50 N/cm²**.
> - ✅ **Transient Force Limit (back/shoulder):** **280 N**, **140 N/cm²**, **10 J**.
> - ✅ **Key Formula:** $F_{max} = \sqrt{2\,k\,U_{lim}}$ (energy-based PFL design).
> - ✅ **SSM Formula:** $S_{min} = S_{h} + S_{r} + S_{s} + C + Z_{d} + Z_{r}$.
> - ✅ **Sensors (for OERAT612):** Joint torque sensors, wrist F/T, vision, proximity skin, tactile, IMU.
> - ✅ **Cobot payload range:** **3 – 16 kg** (UR3, UR5, UR10, UR16).
> - ✅ **Repeatability:** ±0.05 – ±0.1 mm (intentionally lower than industrial to enable compliance).
> - ✅ **Distinction:** Industrial robot = **caged + high speed + high payload**; Cobot = **fenceless + low speed + force-limited + sensor-rich**.
> - ✅ **External force estimation:** $\hat{\tau}_{ext} \rightarrow F_{ext} = (J^{T})^{+}\,\hat{\tau}_{ext}$.
> - ✅ **Typical reaction time budget:** $T_{r} = T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{act} \le 100$ ms.
> - ✅ **Energy conservation assumption** in $F_{max}$ derivation = conservative (worst-case).
> - ✅ **Soft tissue regions** (palm, belly) have *lower* pressure limits than *hard* regions (back, thigh).
> - ✅ **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** + **impedance control** = the engineering "magic" behind PFL.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Hack:** If a question asks *"Name the four modes"*, always write them in the **ISO/TS 15066 order**: **SRMS → HG → SSM → PFL**. Examiners award full marks only when the order is preserved. 🎯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Robots and Swarm Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Autonomous Robots and Swarm Robots # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Autonomous Robots — Formal Definition An **Autonomous Robot** is a mechatronic system capable of performing intended tasks in unstructured environments **without continuous human intervention or explicit programming for every situation**, by relying on its own sensing, decision-making, and actuation capabilities. Autonomy, in the context of KTU robotics, refers to the robot's ability to perceive its environment, reason about the perceived data, plan a sequence of actions, and execute those actions to accomplish a goal — all in real-time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Autonomy is **NOT** the same as automation. An *automated* robot repeats a pre-defined task in a structured environment (e.g., a car assembly arm). An *autonomous* robot must decide *what to do* when the environment changes unexpectedly. ### Levels of Autonomy (KTU Classification) The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with the widely accepted six-level autonomy framework, adapted from the SAE and Sheridan scales: | Level | Name | Human Involvement | Sensing Need | |-------|------|-------------------|---------------| | **0** | No Autonomy | Full teleoperation | None | | **1** | Assisted | Operator handles most decisions | Basic feedback | | **2** | Partial Automation | Human supervises & may override | Moderate sensing | | **3** | Conditional Autonomy | Robot decides within a defined scope | Multi-sensor fusion | | **4** | High Autonomy | Robot decides fully in known zones | Full perception stack | | **5** | Full Autonomy | Zero human intervention | **General-purpose sensing + learning** | > [!NOTE] > As per KTU Module 1, the course focuses primarily on **Levels 3 to 5**, where sensing is the *core* enabler of autonomy. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Self-Driving Car vs. The Automatic Washing Machine" Imagine two vehicles: - **Automatic Washing Machine on Wheels** → It does one thing, repeatedly, with no awareness of traffic, pedestrians, or weather. This is **automated**. - **Self-Driving Car (Waymo / Tesla FSD)** → It perceives lanes, pedestrians, traffic signals, decides to brake, overtake, or wait, and adapts to new conditions never pre-programmed. This is **autonomous**. The difference is **sensing + reasoning + adaptability**. A robot becomes autonomous the moment it must *think* before it *acts*, using real-time data from sensors. --- ## 1.2 Swarm Robots — Formal Definition A **Swarm Robot** is a member of a large-scale decentralized multi-robot system in which the **collective behavior emerges from local interactions** between individual robots and between robots and their environment. Swarm robotics is the study of how to coordinate large numbers of relatively simple robots using only **local perception, local communication, and local decision-making**, with **no central controller**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A swarm is defined by **four pillars** — (i) large number of agents, (ii) homogeneity, (iii) local sensing/communication, (iv) absence of global coordination. Failure of any pillar disqualifies the system from being a "swarm." ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Ant Colony" A single ant is nearly blind, fragile, and unintelligent. Yet, a colony of ants: - Finds the shortest path to food (without a map). - Builds complex ventilation chambers (without an architect). - Forms living bridges across gaps (without an engineer). **No ant supervises another.** Each ant follows simple rules (follow pheromones, avoid obstacles, stay close to nest-mates). The **intelligence is in the group, not the individual**. This is the foundational principle of swarm robotics. > [!NOTE] > The formal term for this is **emergent behavior** — complex global patterns arising from simple local rules. This is the heart of KTU Module 1's swarm robotics section. --- ## 1.3 Why These Topics Matter in Robot Sensing Both autonomous robots and swarm robots depend critically on **sensing** because: 1. Without sensing, there is no perception of the environment. 2. Without perception, decision-making becomes blind. 3. In swarms, local sensing replaces global planning, making each sensor a node of collective intelligence. The rest of this module builds on these two foundational concepts to introduce how robots *see*, *hear*, *touch*, and *navigate* the world. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) Architecture of Autonomous Robots Every autonomous robot, regardless of application, operates through a **closed-loop architecture** known as the Sense–Plan–Act paradigm. Modern variants (deliberative, reactive, hybrid) all inherit this fundamental cycle. ### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown 1. **Sense Stage (Perception Layer)** - The robot gathers raw data from onboard sensors (LiDAR, cameras, IMU, ultrasonic, GPS). - Sensor fusion algorithms combine heterogeneous data into a coherent internal world model. - Key metric: **sensor update rate** (Hz) and **field of view (FOV)**. 2. **Plan Stage (Cognition Layer)** - The robot reasons over the world model to generate a sequence of actions. - Techniques: A* / Dijkstra for global path planning, RRT* for motion planning, MDPs / POMDPs for decision-making under uncertainty. - Key metric: **planning horizon** (seconds) and **re-planning frequency**. 3. **Act Stage (Actuation Layer)** - Commands are sent to motors, grippers, wheels, or effectors. - Feedback from encoders and force sensors closes the loop. - Key metric: **actuator response time** (ms). 4. **Continuous Loop** - The cycle repeats at the **control loop frequency** $f_c$, typically **10 Hz – 100 Hz** for ground robots and up to **1 kHz** for high-speed manipulators. > [!NOTE] > The **deliberative** architecture plans first then acts (slow but smart). The **reactive** architecture skips planning and maps sensors directly to actuators (fast but short-sighted). The **hybrid architecture** (e.g., 3T) combines a slow deliberative layer with a fast reactive layer — this is what most modern autonomous robots use. --- ## 2.2 The Stiffness–Compliance Trade-off in Autonomy Autonomous robots must choose between **rigid execution** and **adaptive compliance**: $$ \text{Autonomy Index (AI)} = \frac{\text{Perception Coverage} \times \text{Decision Quality}}{\text{Human Intervention Frequency}} $$ A higher AI indicates greater independence. Note that **decision quality** itself depends on sensor accuracy and algorithm robustness. --- ## 2.3 Swarm Robotics — Core Properties (KTU High-Yield) | Property | Definition | Engineering Implication | |----------|------------|------------------------| | **Homogeneity** | All robots share the same hardware/software | Cheaper mass production, simpler maintenance | | **Decentralization** | No global coordinator | Single-point-of-failure eliminated | | **Local Sensing** | Each robot senses only its vicinity | Reduced bandwidth & power needs | | **Local Communication** | Robots exchange info only with neighbors | Scalability to thousands of units | | **Emergent Behavior** | Global patterns from local rules | Robustness and fault tolerance | | **Scalability** | Performance is invariant to swarm size | Industrial deployment flexibility | | **Fault Tolerance** | Failure of $k$ robots does not collapse the swarm | Mission reliability | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU exams, remember the **"No Central Control"** principle. If you describe a swarm with a central base station issuing commands, it is a **multi-robot system**, NOT a swarm. --- ## 2.4 Mathematical Foundations of Swarm Behavior ### 2.4.1 Aggregation & Dispersion Models The density of robots $\rho(x, y, t)$ in a 2D space evolves according to a **reaction–diffusion equation**: $$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = D \nabla^{2} \rho + f(\rho) $$ where: - $D$ = diffusion coefficient (how freely robots spread) - $\nabla^{2} \rho$ = Laplacian of robot density - $f(\rho)$ = local interaction term (e.g., repulsion if too close, attraction if too far) ### 2.4.2 Reynolds' Boids Model (1986) The classic bird-flocking algorithm, foundational to swarm robotics: $$ \vec{v}_{\text{desired}} = w_1 \vec{v}_{\text{separation}} + w_2 \vec{v}_{\text{alignment}} + w_3 \vec{v}_{\text{cohesion}} $$ where: - $\vec{v}_{\text{separation}}$ = vector away from nearest neighbors - $\vec{v}_{\text{alignment}}$ = velocity matching with neighbors - $\vec{v}_{\text{cohesion}}$ = vector toward local centroid - $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = tunable weights ### 2.4.3 Communication Range Constraint For a swarm of $N$ robots in a 2D area $A$ with communication radius $r$: $$ \text{Coverage Ratio} = \frac{N \cdot \pi r^{2}}{A} $$ For a connected swarm, this ratio should be $\geq 1$ (each robot has at least one neighbor on average). --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Description | Units / Range | |---------|-------------|---------------| | $\text{AI} = \dfrac{P_c \cdot Q_d}{f_{HI}}$ | Autonomy Index | Dimensionless | | $f_c$ | Control loop frequency | **10 – 1000 Hz** | | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = D \nabla^{2} \rho + f(\rho)$ | Swarm density evolution | $\rho$ in robots/m² | | $\vec{v}_{\text{desired}} = w_1 \vec{v}_{\text{sep}} + w_2 \vec{v}_{\text{aln}} + w_3 \vec{v}_{\text{coh}}$ | Boids flocking rule | Vector (m/s) | | $\dfrac{N \pi r^{2}}{A} \geq 1$ | Swarm connectivity | Dimensionless | | $d_{\text{min}} = r_{\text{robot}} \cdot (1 + k_{\text{safety}})$ | Inter-robot collision avoidance | meters | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Autonomous Robots**: Used in self-driving cars (Waymo, Tesla), planetary exploration rovers (NASA Perseverance, ISRO Chandrayaan), underwater ROVs, surgical robots (da Vinci), and warehouse automation (Amazon Kiva). - **Swarm Robots**: Used in agricultural monitoring, disaster response (search & rescue), military reconnaissance, environmental cleanup, and large-scale terrain mapping (e.g., MIT's Kilobots, Harvard's Ambulatory Microrobots). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Swarm Size for Area Coverage **Problem Statement:** Given a square area of side $L$ meters and a robot with sensing radius $r$ meters, derive the minimum number of robots $N_{\min}$ required to ensure full coverage with at least **two-fold redundancy** for fault tolerance. ### Step 1 — Total Area to be Covered $$ A_{\text{total}} = L^{2} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{)} $$ ### Step 2 — Coverage Provided by One Robot A single robot with a circular sensing footprint covers: $$ A_{\text{robot}} = \pi r^{2} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{)} $$ ### Step 3 — Minimum Robots for Single Coverage To cover the entire area without redundancy: $$ N_{\text{single}} = \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{robot}}} = \frac{L^{2}}{\pi r^{2}} $$ ### Step 4 — Introduce Redundancy Factor $k$ For fault tolerance, every region must be covered by **at least $k$ robots**: $$ N_{\min} = k \cdot N_{\text{single}} = \frac{k L^{2}}{\pi r^{2}} $$ ### Step 5 — Adjust for Overlap Losses In practice, perfect packing of circles inside a square achieves only $\approx 0.9069$ efficiency. So: $$ N_{\text{real}} = \frac{k L^{2}}{\eta \, \pi r^{2}}, \quad \eta \approx 0.9069 $$ $$ N_{\text{real}} = \frac{k L^{2}}{0.9069 \, \pi r^{2}} \approx \frac{1.103 \, k L^{2}}{r^{2}} $$ ### Numerical Example For $L = 100$ m, $r = 5$ m, and $k = 2$: $$ N_{\text{real}} = \frac{1.103 \times 2 \times (100)^{2}}{(5)^{2}} = \frac{1.103 \times 2 \times 10000}{25} = 882.4 $$ Therefore, **883 robots** are required for 2-fold redundant full coverage of a $100 \times 100$ m area with 5 m sensing radius. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Autonomous vs. Swarm Robots | Parameter | Autonomous Robot | Swarm Robot | |-----------|------------------|-------------| | **Number of agents** | Typically 1 (sometimes a small team) | Large (10 – 10,000+) | | **Central control** | Yes (onboard processor) | **None** | | **Sensing scope** | Global (or near-global) | **Strictly local** | | **Decision-making** | Centralized AI | Distributed / emergent | | **Communication** | Wi-Fi / 5G (long-range) | Bluetooth / IR / RF (short-range) | | **Cost per unit** | High (₹ lakhs – crores) | Low (target: <₹10,000) | | **Sensors used** | LiDAR, GPS, stereo cameras, IMU | IR, proximity, low-res cameras, beacons | | **Failure tolerance** | Single failure = mission failure | Up to 30–50% failure tolerable | | **Power autonomy** | Hours to days | Minutes to hours | | **Computational load** | Heavy (GPU often required) | Tiny (microcontroller) | | **Example** | Mars Rover, Self-driving car | Kilobot, Crazyflie, Ants | --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Reynolds' Boids Alignment Term Consider a robot $i$ with neighbors within a sensing sphere of radius $R$. The alignment velocity vector is: ### Step 1 — Identify Neighbors Let $\mathcal{N}_i = \{j : \vert \vec{p}_j - \vec{p}_i \vert \leq R\}$ be the set of neighbors of robot $i$. ### Step 2 — Compute Average Neighbor Velocity $$ \vec{v}_{\text{alignment}}^{(i)} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j $$ ### Step 3 — Normalize to Maximum Speed $$ \vec{u}_{\text{alignment}}^{(i)} = \frac{\vec{v}_{\text{alignment}}^{(i)}}{\vert \vec{v}_{\text{alignment}}^{(i)} \vert + \epsilon} \cdot v_{\max} $$ where $\epsilon$ is a small constant to prevent division by zero and $v_{\max}$ is the maximum linear velocity of the robot. ### Step 4 — Combine with Final Velocity Command The robot's final velocity vector becomes: $$ \vec{v}_{\text{cmd}}^{(i)} = v_{\max} \cdot \frac{w_1 \vec{u}_{\text{sep}} + w_2 \vec{u}_{\text{aln}} + w_3 \vec{u}_{\text{coh}}}{\vert w_1 \vec{u}_{\text{sep}} + w_2 \vec{u}_{\text{aln}} + w_3 \vec{u}_{\text{coh}} \vert} $$ This derivation is **frequently asked** in KTU Module 1 exams. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Computing Connectivity Ratio **Given:** 50 swarm robots, each with communication radius $r = 2$ m, deployed in a $20 \times 20$ m area. **Find:** Connectivity ratio and decide if the swarm is connected. ### Step 1 — Compute Total Area $$ A = 20 \times 20 = 400 \quad \text{m}^2 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Robot Communication Area $$ A_{\text{comm}} = \pi r^{2} = \pi (2)^{2} = 4\pi \approx 12.566 \quad \text{m}^2 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Connectivity Ratio $$ \text{CR} = \frac{N \cdot A_{\text{comm}}}{A} = \frac{50 \times 12.566}{400} = \frac{628.3}{400} = 1.571 $$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation Since $\text{CR} = 1.571 \geq 1$, the swarm is **connected** on average. Each robot has approximately **1.5 neighbors**, sufficient for basic flocking but marginal for complex coordination. For robust fault tolerance, a target $\text{CR} \geq 3$ is recommended. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Autonomous Robot — Sense–Plan–Act Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR ENV[Environment] -->|Raw Stimuli| SENS[Sensors Layer
LiDAR, Camera, IMU, GPS] SENS -->|Preprocessed Data| FUS[Sensor Fusion
Kalman Filter] FUS -->|World Model| PER[Perception Module
Object Detection SLAM] PER -->|Semantic Map| PLAN[Planning Module
Path + Behavior] PLAN -->|Action Sequence| DEC[Decision Module
MDP / Rule Engine] DEC -->|Motor Commands| ACT[Actuators Layer
Motors, Grippers] ACT -->|Physical Effect| ENV FB[Encoder Feedback] --> ACT OBS[Obstacles and Dynamics] --> ENV ``` **Description:** The diagram maps the closed-loop SPA architecture. Sensors extract raw environmental data; fusion builds a coherent world model; perception extracts semantics; planning and decision layers reason over goals; actuators execute; encoder feedback closes the inner loop. Obstacles and environmental dynamics continuously perturb the external environment, keeping the loop alive. --- ## 4.2 Swarm Robot — Local Interaction Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AGENT_A[Robot A] SA[Local Sensors] DA[Decision Unit
Simple Rules] AA[Actuators] CA[Comm Module] end subgraph AGENT_B[Robot B] SB[Local Sensors] DB[Decision Unit] AB[Actuators] CB[Comm Module] end subgraph AGENT_C[Robot C] SC[Local Sensors] DC[Decision Unit] AC[Actuators] CC[Comm Module] end SA --> DA DA --> AA CA <-->|Local RF IR| CB CB <-->|Local RF IR| CC DA <--> DB DB <--> DC AA --> EMERG[Emergent Global Behavior] AB --> EMERG AC --> EMERG EMERG -.->|No Central Control| AGENT_A EMERG -.->|No Central Control| AGENT_B EMERG -.->|No Central Control| AGENT_C ``` **Description:** Each robot is identical. They sense locally, decide locally, and exchange data with immediate neighbors only. The dotted feedback from emergent behavior is conceptual — there is no global signal returning to any individual robot. --- ## 4.3 Autonomy Level Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD L0[Level 0 No Autonomy] --> L1[Level 1 Assisted] L1 --> L2[Level 2 Partial Automation] L2 --> L3[Level 3 Conditional Autonomy] L3 --> L4[Level 4 High Autonomy] L4 --> L5[Level 5 Full Autonomy] L0 -.->|Human does everything| H0[Operator] L3 -.->|Robot decides in scope| H3[Supervisor] L5 -.->|Robot handles all| H5[None] ``` --- ## 4.4 Swarm vs. Multi-Robot System Distinction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SWARM[True Swarm] S1[R1] S2[R2] S3[R3] S4[R4] S1 <-. Local Comm .-> S2 S2 <-. Local Comm .-> S3 S3 <-. Local Comm .-> S4 end subgraph MRS[Multi-Robot System Not Swarm] M1[R1] M2[R2] M3[R3] CTRL[Central Controller] CTRL -->|Command| M1 CTRL -->|Command| M2 CTRL -->|Command| M3 end ``` **Key Visual Cue:** The swarm has **no central block**. The multi-robot system has an explicit central controller box. This single diagram is a high-yield recall tool for KTU exams. --- ## 4.5 Reynolds Boids — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Position of Self] --> N[Identify Neighbors
within radius R] N --> S[Compute Separation Vector] N --> AL[Compute Alignment Vector] N --> C[Compute Cohesion Vector] S --> W[Weighted Sum] AL --> W C --> W W --> NORM[Normalize to vmax] NORM --> OUT[Final Velocity Command] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define an autonomous robot. Differentiate it from an automated robot with one suitable example.** **Model Answer:** An **autonomous robot** is a robot that can perform its tasks without continuous human guidance by perceiving its environment through sensors, reasoning over the perceived data, and adapting its actions to changing conditions. The key distinction is **decision-making in unstructured environments**. An **automated robot** performs pre-programmed, repetitive tasks in a controlled environment without requiring intelligence; it cannot adapt to unexpected changes. **Example:** A robotic arm welding car bodies on an assembly line is **automated**. A self-driving car navigating through traffic is **autonomous**. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List any four defining properties of a swarm robotic system.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Decentralization** — No central controller exists. 2. **Local sensing and communication** — Each robot only perceives its immediate vicinity. 3. **Homogeneity** — All robots are functionally identical. 4. **Emergent behavior** — Complex global patterns arise from simple local rules. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) architecture** of an autonomous robot in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of sensor fusion in this architecture. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With neat diagrams, describe the **three levels of robot architecture**: deliberative, reactive, and hybrid. Justify why hybrid architectures dominate modern autonomous systems. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The **SPA architecture** is a foundational paradigm for autonomous robots. It models intelligence as a continuous closed-loop cycle: 1. **Sense Stage:** The robot collects raw data from heterogeneous sensors — cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic rangefinders, IMU, and GPS. The raw data is noisy, asynchronous, and in different coordinate frames. 2. **Sensor Fusion:** Algorithms such as the **Kalman Filter**, **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, or **Particle Filter** combine multi-sensor data into a unified, low-noise world model. This is critical because a single sensor can fail (e.g., camera in fog, GPS indoors), but fusion maintains robustness. 3. **Plan Stage:** The world model is fed to path-planning algorithms (A*, RRT*, D*) and behavior engines (state machines, MDPs) to generate a sequence of desired actions. 4. **Act Stage:** Commands are dispatched to actuators with closed-loop feedback from wheel encoders and force sensors. **Block Diagram (described verbally):** > Environment → Sensors → Sensor Fusion → Perception → Planning → Decision → Actuators → Environment (loop closes) **Role of Sensor Fusion:** - Compensates for individual sensor noise. - Provides redundancy (fault tolerance). - Enables perception in adverse conditions. - Increases update rate beyond any single sensor. #### Model Solution for (b): - **Deliberative (Horizon):** Plans fully before acting. Uses a global world model. Slow but globally optimal. Example: Early Shakey the Robot. - **Reactive (Brooks' Subsumption):** Maps sensors directly to actuators using finite-state machines. Fast but myopic. Example: Roomba. - **Hybrid (3T - Three Tier):** Combines both — a slow deliberative planner, a fast reactive layer, and a sequencer between them. Example: NASA Mars Rovers. Hybrid dominance is justified because: - Pure deliberative is too slow for dynamic obstacles. - Pure reactive cannot achieve long-horizon goals. - Hybrid offers **real-time responsiveness + strategic planning**. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Define **swarm robotics**. Explain the **Reynolds Boids model** with its three governing rules. Show the mathematical formulation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A team of 80 homogeneous swarm robots is deployed in a $25 \times 25$ m square area. Each robot has a circular sensing and communication radius of 1.5 m. Compute the **connectivity ratio** and comment on the swarm's ability to maintain fault-tolerant coordination. If the requirement is to achieve a connectivity ratio of at least 3, how many additional robots must be added? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Swarm Robotics:** A decentralized multi-robot system where collective behavior emerges from local interactions among simple, identical agents. **Reynolds' Boids Model (1986)** describes flocking using three rules: 1. **Separation** — Steer to avoid crowding neighbors. 2. **Alignment** — Steer toward the average heading of neighbors. 3. **Cohesion** — Steer toward the average position of neighbors. **Mathematical Formulation:** $$ \vec{v}_{\text{cmd}}^{(i)} = v_{\max} \cdot \frac{w_1 \vec{u}_{\text{sep}} + w_2 \vec{u}_{\text{aln}} + w_3 \vec{u}_{\text{coh}}}{\vert w_1 \vec{u}_{\text{sep}} + w_2 \vec{u}_{\text{aln}} + w_3 \vec{u}_{\text{coh}} \vert} $$ where: - $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are tunable weights (typically $w_1 \geq w_2 \geq w_3$). - $\vec{u}$ vectors are unit vectors computed from neighbor information. #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Compute Total Area:** $$ A = 25 \times 25 = 625 \quad \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Communication Footprint per Robot:** $$ A_{\text{comm}} = \pi r^{2} = \pi (1.5)^{2} = 2.25 \pi \approx 7.069 \quad \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Connectivity Ratio for $N = 80$:** $$ \text{CR} = \frac{N \cdot A_{\text{comm}}}{A} = \frac{80 \times 7.069}{625} = \frac{565.49}{625} = 0.905 $$ **Comment:** Since $\text{CR} \approx 0.905 < 1$, the swarm is **NOT fully connected** on average. Many robots will be isolated, breaking coordination. **Step 4 — Required Number of Robots for $\text{CR} \geq 3$:** $$ N_{\text{needed}} = \frac{3 \times A}{A_{\text{comm}}} = \frac{3 \times 625}{7.069} = \frac{1875}{7.069} \approx 265.3 $$ **Step 5 — Additional Robots Required:** $$ \Delta N = 266 - 80 = 186 \text{ additional robots} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating "automation" with "autonomy"** — A pre-programmed arm is *not* autonomous. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion. > 2. **Adding a central controller to swarm diagrams** — A system with a central computer is a *multi-robot system*, NOT a swarm. Lose 3 marks. > 3. **Forgetting to normalize the Reynolds vector** — Always divide by the magnitude of the weighted sum. Unnormalized outputs cause dimensional errors. > 4. **Using `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables** — Causes table-rendering failure. Use `\vert` instead. > 5. **Skipping the units in final numerical answers** — Always write "meters", "robots", "Hz" explicitly. > 6. **Confusing "fault tolerance" with "redundancy"** — Redundancy is the *means*; fault tolerance is the *property*. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist - **Autonomy** = Sensing + Reasoning + Adaptability (Levels 0 to 5). - **Automation** = Repetition without intelligence. - **Sense–Plan–Act** = Universal closed-loop pattern for all autonomous robots. - **Hybrid architecture** = Industry standard combining deliberative + reactive layers. - **Sensor fusion** (Kalman / Particle filter) is the *backbone* of perception. ### 🐜 Swarm Robotics Pillars - Decentralization, Homogeneity, Local sensing, Local communication, Emergence. - **No central control** — distinguishes swarms from multi-robot systems. - **Reynolds Boids** = Separation + Alignment + Cohesion. - Connectivity ratio $\dfrac{N \pi r^{2}}{A} \geq 1$ for basic, $\geq 3$ for robust. ### 🧮 Must-Know Formulas - Autonomy Index: $\text{AI} = \dfrac{P_c \cdot Q_d}{f_{HI}}$ - Boids velocity: $\vec{v}_{\text{desired}} = w_1 \vec{v}_{\text{sep}} + w_2 \vec{v}_{\text{aln}} + w_3 \vec{v}_{\text{coh}}$ - Swarm density PDE: $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = D \nabla^{2} \rho + f(\rho)$ - Coverage: $N_{\text{real}} = \dfrac{1.103 \, k L^{2}}{r^{2}}$ - Connectivity: $\text{CR} = \dfrac{N \pi r^{2}}{A}$ ### 🎯 Exam Day Strategy - Always draw **Mermaid block diagrams** for SPA and Boids questions. - Mention **at least two real-world examples** in every definition question. - Always state **assumptions** before numerical derivations. - End long answers with a **practical application** line for full marks.

topics.title: Robotics Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robotics Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotics Applications** refers to the systematic deployment of autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic systems across diverse operational domains to perform tasks that are **repetitive**, **hazardous**, **precision-critical**, or **beyond human physical capability**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme OBE framework (aligned with ISO 8373:2021 — Robots and Robotic Devices — Vocabulary), a robotic application is characterised by the triad of:

1. A **manipulator / mobile platform** (mechanical structure)
2. A **sensing subsystem** (perception of the environment)
3. An **actuation & control subsystem** (decision + action loop)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Robotics Applications is a *foundational descriptive topic*. The examiner expects students to (a) classify robots by *application domain*, (b) state *real-world use-cases* with sensor types, and (c) justify *why a robot* is preferred over a human worker for a given task.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot as a Specialised Worker"

Imagine a factory where every worker is a **specialist**:

- A **welder** who never tires, never blinks, and lays the exact same bead 10,000 times a day → that is the **industrial welding robot** (e.g., on a Toyota assembly line).
- A **surgeon** whose hands never tremble, magnified 15× through a screen → that is the **Da Vinci surgical robot**.
- A **mars geologist** sent 225 million km away to test rocks → that is the **NASA Perseverance rover**.

Each of these is a *robot* customised for a *specific application*. The application dictates the **sensors used**, the **degree of freedom (DOF)** required, the **payload**, and the **control architecture**. The unifying theme is **sensing → decision → action**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Robotic Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Vocabulary (ISO 8373 / KTU Reference):**
> - **Payload** — Maximum mass the robot can manipulate, typically **1 kg to 1000 kg**.
> - **Reach** — Maximum radial distance from the robot base to the end-effector.
> - **Repeatability** — Position-return accuracy, typically **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** for industrial arms.
