# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between active and passive sensors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — distribute as follows):**

| Feature | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Generates output without external excitation | Requires external excitation signal |
| **Energy Source** | Measurand itself | External power supply |
| **Working Principle** | Direct energy conversion (energy harvesting) | Modulation of excitation signal |
| **Example** | Thermocouple (Seebeck effect), Piezoelectric accelerometer | Thermistor, Strain gauge, LDR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks for each sensor — 0.5 for definition, 0.5 for example, 0.5 for principle.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: What are proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors? Give two examples of each in a robotic system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Proprioceptive Sensors (1.5 marks):**
- Measure the **internal state** of the robot (joint angles, motor speeds, orientation).
- Examples: (i) **Optical encoder** for joint position, (ii) **IMU** for orientation and acceleration.

**Exteroceptive Sensors (1.5 marks):**
- Measure the **external environment** surrounding the robot.
- Examples: (i) **Ultrasonic sensor** for obstacle distance, (ii) **Camera** for visual perception.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model — 14 Marks]

**Q: Classify sensors based on (a) power source and measurement domain with suitable robotic examples. (b) Explain in detail how ultrasonic and piezoelectric sensors work, highlighting their classification under multiple axes.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Classification by Power Source (3.5 marks):**

**Active Sensors** generate electrical output directly from the measurand without external power. Examples in robotics:
- **Piezoelectric force sensor** in a robotic gripper → converts mechanical pressure directly into voltage.
- **Thermocouple** in a high-temperature industrial robot → generates EMF via Seebeck effect.

**Passive Sensors** require an external excitation signal. The measurand modulates this signal. Examples in robotics:
- **Strain gauge** on a robot arm → requires bridge excitation voltage to measure deflection.
- **Hall effect sensor** for joint position → requires excitation current.

**Classification by Measurement Domain (3.5 marks):**

| Domain | Definition | Robotic Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive** | Internal state | Encoders (joint angle), IMU (orientation), Tachometer (motor speed), Potentiometer (position) |
| **Exteroceptive** | External environment | Camera (vision), LiDAR (mapping), Ultrasonic (obstacle), GPS (global position), Tactile (contact) |

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on whether the robot needs to know its own state or perceive its surroundings — most modern robots use **a fusion of both classes**.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Ultrasonic Sensor (3.5 marks):**

**Working Principle:** An ultrasonic sensor emits a high-frequency sound pulse (typically **40 kHz**) using a piezoelectric transmitter. The pulse travels through air, reflects off an obstacle, and returns to the receiver. The time-of-flight $\Delta t$ is measured.

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $c \approx$ **343 m/s** is the speed of sound in air at 20°C.

**Multi-axis classification:**
- **Active** (requires driving pulse, but generates its own acoustic energy)
- **Exteroceptive** (measures external obstacle)
- **Non-contact**
- **Analog output** (time-of-flight is continuous)
- **Acoustic detection principle**

**Piezoelectric Sensor (3.5 marks):**

**Working Principle:** Certain crystals (quartz, PZT — lead zirconate titanate) generate a charge when subjected to mechanical stress (direct piezoelectric effect). The voltage across the crystal is:
$$V_{out} = d_{33} \cdot \sigma \cdot \frac{A}{C}$$
where $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric coefficient, $\sigma$ = applied stress, $A$ = electrode area, $C$ = capacitance.

**Multi-axis classification:**
- **Active** (self-generating, no excitation needed)
- **Proprioceptive** (typically mounted on robot joints to measure force/vibration)
- **Contact** (requires mechanical contact)
- **Analog output**
- **Mechanical detection principle**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly confuse piezoelectric as a passive sensor. **Remember:** Piezoelectric is **ACTIVE** because the measurand itself (mechanical stress) directly produces the output voltage — no external excitation is required. Conversely, an ultrasonic transducer is sometimes classified as active because it actively emits a pulse — but for classification purposes, the **emission** makes it active and the **detection** is exteroceptive.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — 14 Marks]

**Q: (a) Explain the classification of sensors based on output signal type, contact characteristics, and detection mechanism. Provide a tabulated summary with at least three examples per class. (b) A robot uses a quadrature incremental encoder with N = 500 PPR on each wheel. The wheel diameter is 10 cm. If the robot moves forward and the encoder registers 12,000 counts in one direction, calculate: (i) the rotation angle of the wheel, (ii) the linear distance traveled.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Classification by Output Signal Type (2 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Analog** | Continuous voltage/current proportional to measurand | Thermistor, strain gauge, LDR |
| **Digital** | Discrete binary or coded output | Digital encoder, I2C IMU (MPU6050), SPI pressure sensor |

**Classification by Contact (2 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | Requires physical touch with target | Tactile sensor, limit switch, contact thermometer |
| **Non-contact** | Measures without physical touch | Camera, LiDAR, ultrasonic, IR proximity, Hall effect |

**Classification by Detection Mechanism (3 marks):**

| Mechanism | Energy Conversion | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Mechanical → Electrical | Strain gauge, accelerometer, piezoelectric |
| **Electrical** | Electrical property change | Potentiometer (resistance), capacitive sensor |
| **Magnetic** | Magnetic field sensing | Hall sensor, magnetometer |
| **Optical** | Light → Electrical signal | Photodiode, camera, LiDAR |
| **Thermal** | Temperature → EMF/Resistance | Thermocouple, RTD, thermistor |
| **Acoustic** | Sound wave detection | Microphone, ultrasonic |
| **Chemical** | Chemical reaction → signal | pH electrode, gas sensor |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Encoder PPR: $N = 500$ pulses per revolution
- Wheel diameter: $D = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- Registered counts: $C = 12{,}000$ in one direction

**Step 1 — Effective counts per revolution (quadrature decoding):**
A quadrature encoder gives **4 effective counts per pulse cycle** (both edges of both channels A and B):
$$N_{eff} = 4 \times N = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Rotation angle $\theta$:**
$$\theta = \frac{C}{N_{eff}} \times 360° = \frac{12{,}000}{2000} \times 360° = 6 \times 360° = 2160°$$

In revolutions:
$$\theta_{rev} = \frac{12{,}000}{2000} = 6 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Linear distance $s$:**
Wheel circumference:
$$C_{wheel} = \pi \cdot D = \pi \cdot 0.10 = 0.3142 \text{ m}$$

Linear distance traveled:
$$s = \theta_{rev} \cdot C_{wheel} = 6 \times 0.3142 = 1.885 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Rotation angle: $\theta = 2160°$ (or **6 revolutions**)
- Linear distance: $s \approx \mathbf{1.885 \text{ m}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the quadrature factor of 4** — students often compute $\theta = (12000/500) \times 360° = 8640°$, which is **4× too large**. Always check whether the problem states "quadrature" decoding.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — converting cm to m is critical. Mixing units will cost 1 mark.
> 3. **No final unit** — always write m, cm, °, or rad explicitly.
> 4. **Confusion between PPR and CPR** — PPR = pulses per revolution; CPR = counts per revolution = 4 × PPR for quadrature.
> 5. **Direction sign** — if the robot moves in reverse, the answer should be negative.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ☐ **Active vs Passive:** Active sensors generate their own signal (no excitation); passive sensors modulate an external excitation.
- ☐ **Proprioceptive vs Exteroceptive:** Proprioceptive = internal state (encoders, IMU); Exteroceptive = external environment (camera, LiDAR).
- ☐ **Analog vs Digital:** Analog output is continuous and needs ADC; digital output is discrete and interfaces directly with MCU.
- ☐ **Contact vs Non-Contact:** Contact sensors require physical touch (tactile, limit switches); non-contact use EM/acoustic waves (camera, sonar, IR).
- ☐ **Absolute vs Relative:** Absolute sensors retain reference after power-off (absolute encoder, GPS); relative sensors drift (incremental encoder, gyroscope).
- ☐ **Time-of-flight formula:** $d = (c \cdot \Delta t) / 2$ — applies to both ultrasonic ($c = 343$ m/s) and LiDAR ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s).
- ☐ **Encoder resolution:** Quadrature decoding gives $4N$ counts/rev for $N$ PPR.
- ☐ **Strain gauge equation:** $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$, with bridge output $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon / 4$.
- ☐ **Thermocouple Seebeck:** $E = K \cdot \Delta T$ — Type K has $K \approx 41 \, \mu V/°C$.
- ☐ **Hall effect:** $V_H = (R_H / t) \cdot I \cdot B$ — used for contactless position and current sensing.
- ☐ **Cross-classification is mandatory:** Every sensor belongs to **multiple classes simultaneously** — a good answer must address **all relevant axes**.
- ☐ **Always state the working principle** along with classification in KTU answers — part (a) without working principle loses 1–2 marks.
- ☐ **The detection mechanism must be matched to the measurand** — for example, optical sensors cannot measure magnetic fields directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question asks "Classify the following sensor", structure your answer using a **tabular format with classification axis as rows and sensor class as columns**. This visual presentation typically scores higher marks in KTU ESE evaluations because it demonstrates structured thinking aligned with CO (Course Outcome) mapping.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robotic Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **Robotic Sensor** is a transducer or device that converts a physical, chemical, or environmental stimulus from the robot's internal state or its external operating environment into a measurable electrical signal that can be processed by the robot's controller for perception, decision-making, and closed-loop feedback control.

In the context of the KTU 2024 OERAT612 syllabus, robotic sensors are broadly classified along two primary axes:

| Classification Axis | Category | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin of Signal** | **Proprioceptive (Internal)** | Measures the robot's *own* internal state (joint angle, wheel speed, motor current, battery voltage). |
| | **Exteroceptive (External)** | Measures the *external* environment (distance, light, sound, temperature, force from objects). |
| **Actuation Principle** | **Active Sensor** | Emits energy (e.g., laser, ultrasound) and measures the reflection. |
| | **Passive Sensor** | Merely receives ambient energy (e.g., camera, microphone, thermocouple). |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy: The Robot as a Living Organism**
> Imagine a human driving a car in dense fog. The driver uses **eyes** (vision/camera) to see the road, **ears** (microphones/ultrasonic) to hear other vehicles, **skin on hands** (tactile/force sensors) to feel the steering wheel, and **proprioception** (joint encoders/IMUs) to know the angle of the arms without looking. A robot functions identically: sensors are the "five senses" of a machine. Without sensors, a robot is *blind, deaf, and numb* — it cannot perceive, adapt, or react to its workspace.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following standard metrics are universally applied when evaluating any robotic sensor in the KTU examination framework:

- **Accuracy**: Closeness of the measured value to the *true* value.
- **Precision (Repeatability)**: Ability to produce the *same* output for the same input repeatedly.
- **Resolution**: The *smallest* detectable change in the measurand.
- **Sensitivity ($S$)**: Ratio of output signal change to input stimulus change.
- **Dynamic Range**: Ratio between the largest and smallest detectable stimuli.
- **Bandwidth**: Range of frequencies over which the sensor operates reliably.
- **Linearity**: How closely the output follows a straight line vs. input.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Define a sensor," always state both the **transduction function** (what it converts) and the **purpose** (feedback to controller). Examiners award the second mark specifically for mentioning *feedback/control*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step

The signal flow inside any robotic sensor system follows a deterministic, well-defined pipeline:

1. **Stimulus (Measurand)**: The physical quantity of interest ($x$) interacts with the sensing element (e.g., light photons striking a photodiode).
2. **Transduction**: The sensing element converts $x$ into an intermediate signal (typically electrical: voltage $V$ or current $I$).
3. **Signal Conditioning**: Amplification, filtering, and linearization are applied to make the raw signal usable.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)**: The conditioned analog signal is digitized into a discrete numerical value for the controller.
5. **Processing & Fusion**: The robot's CPU/MCU interprets the digital reading, often combining it with other sensor inputs (sensor fusion).
6. **Actuation Command**: Based on the perception, the controller drives motors/joints, closing the loop.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Sensor Selection

Robotic sensors are selected based on the **task requirements** of the robot:

- **Industrial Welding Robot** → Needs **temperature sensors** and **proximity sensors** for safety.
- **Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR)** → Needs **LiDAR**, **encoders**, and **IMUs** for navigation.
- **Surgical Robot** → Needs **high-precision force/torque sensors** for haptic feedback.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations — they appear in nearly every Part B question.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | V/unit of $x$ |
| 2 | **Resolution** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Full Scale Output}}{2^{n}}$ where $n$ = ADC bits | unit of $x$ |
| 3 | **Quantization Error** | $Q_{err} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 5 | **Hysteresis Error** | $\epsilon_h = \dfrac{\max(x_{up} - x_{down})}{\text{Full Scale}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) |
| 6 | **Non-linearity Error** | $\epsilon_{nl} = \dfrac{\max \vert y_{actual} - y_{ideal} \vert}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) |
| 7 | **Dynamic Range** | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{x_{max}}{x_{min}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 8 | **First-Order Sensor Response** | $y(t) = K\,x\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Output unit |
| 9 | **Time Constant** | $\tau = RC$ (for electrical sensors) | Seconds (s) |
| 10 | **Range / Span** | $Range = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Same as $x$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern industry, robotic sensors form the backbone of:

- **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing**: Sensor-fused cobots (collaborative robots) work safely alongside humans.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: LiDAR + camera + radar fusion enables real-time obstacle avoidance.
- **Medical Robotics**: Force sensors in the da Vinci Surgical System provide surgeons with haptic feedback.
- **Space Exploration**: NASA's Mars rovers (Perseverance, Curiosity) rely on stereo vision, IMUs, and tactile sensors for autonomous traversal of unknown terrain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The single most important principle in KTU valuation is that a sensor is *never* useful in isolation. Its value lies in the **closed-loop control system** it enables. Always answer with the sensor *and* the feedback loop context.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Error of an ADC-Interfaced Sensor

When a robotic sensor's analog output is digitized by an $n$-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), the output is partitioned into discrete steps. The maximum inherent error due to this discretization is the *quantization error*.

**Step 1: Define the size of one Least Significant Bit (LSB).**
Each step of the ADC represents a voltage increment equal to:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}
$$

where $V_{ref}$ is the reference voltage of the ADC and $n$ is the number of bits.

**Step 2: Recognize that the maximum error occurs halfway between two adjacent codes.**
The worst-case quantization error is therefore *half* of one LSB, because the true analog value can lie anywhere within a quantization interval of width $\Delta V$, and the maximum deviation from the chosen code is $\Delta V / 2$.

$$
Q_{err} = \frac{\Delta V}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the expression for $\Delta V$ into the equation.**

$$
Q_{err} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}
$$

**Step 4: Final compact form (board exam ready).**

$$
\boxed{Q_{err} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Evaluation — Worked Example.**

Given: $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $n = 10$ bits. Compute the maximum quantization error.

$$
Q_{err} = \frac{5}{2^{10+1}} = \frac{5}{2048}
$$

Evaluating the division:

$$
Q_{err} \approx 0.00244\,\text{V} = 2.44\,\text{mV}
$$

Thus, any reading from this 10-bit ADC can be off by at most **2.44 mV** — a critical figure when selecting sensors for high-precision robotics.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Order Dynamic Response of a Sensor

Many robotic sensors (thermistors, accelerometers, photodiodes) behave as **first-order systems** with a single dominant time constant $\tau$.

**Step 1: Write the standard first-order differential equation** relating output $y(t)$ to input $x(t)$ with static sensitivity $K$:

$$
\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K\,x(t)
$$

**Step 2: Apply a step input** $x(t) = X_0\,u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the unit step). Assume zero initial conditions $y(0) = 0$.

**Step 3: Take the Laplace transform** of both sides:

$$
\tau s Y(s) + Y(s) = K \cdot \frac{X_0}{s}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $Y(s)$**:

$$
Y(s) (\tau s + 1) = \frac{K X_0}{s}
$$

$$
Y(s) = \frac{K X_0}{s(\tau s + 1)}
$$

**Step 5: Apply partial fraction decomposition**:

$$
Y(s) = K X_0 \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{\tau}{\tau s + 1} \right] = K X_0 \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s + 1/\tau} \right]
$$

**Step 6: Take the inverse Laplace transform** to obtain the time-domain response:

$$
\boxed{y(t) = K X_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the 63.2% rise time (the hallmark of a first-order system):**

At $t = \tau$:

$$
y(\tau) = K X_0 \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = K X_0 (1 - 0.3679) \approx 0.632\,K X_0
$$

This means that the sensor's output reaches **63.2% of its final value** after one time constant — a standard result used in KTU derivations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the zero-initial-condition assumption. If the sensor was previously at a non-zero state, the total response is the superposition of the natural response and forced response. Always state $y(0) = 0$ in such derivations.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Trade-Offs

| Sensor Type | Measurand | Active/Passive | Typical Range | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoder (Optical)** | Angular position | Active | 0°–360° | High resolution, digital output | Needs shaft access |
| **Potentiometer** | Linear/angular position | Passive | Few mm to m | Cheap, simple | Contact wear, low precision |
| **Ultrasonic (HC-SR04)** | Distance | Active | 2 cm – 400 cm | Low cost | Poor angular resolution, specular reflections |
| **LiDAR** | Distance (2D/3D) | Active | Up to 100 m | High accuracy, long range | Expensive |
| **Camera (CCD/CMOS)** | Light intensity/image | Passive | Variable | Rich information | Heavy processing, lighting dependent |
| **IMU (Accelerometer + Gyro)** | Acceleration, angular rate | Passive | $\pm 2g$ to $\pm 16g$ | Compact, fast | Drift over time |
| **Force/Torque Sensor** | Mechanical force | Passive | Few N to kN | Direct measurement | Expensive, mounting complexity |
| **Thermistor / RTD** | Temperature | Passive | $-55°$C to $150°$C | High sensitivity | Non-linear (thermistor) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Robotic Sensor Signal Chain

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture from physical stimulus to robotic actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Stimulus x] --> B[Sensing Element]
    B --> C[Raw Electrical Signal]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning]
    D --> E[Amplification]
    E --> F[Filtering]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    G --> H[Microcontroller or Processor]
    H --> I[Sensor Fusion and Decision Logic]
    I --> J[Controller]
    J --> K[Actuator]
    K --> L[Robot Motion or Action]
    L --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for Students:** The block labeled "A" represents the *measurand* (e.g., distance to an obstacle). The closed loop back from "L" to "A" represents the **real-time feedback nature** of robotic control — the action of the robot continuously alters the stimulus, which is sensed again, forming a closed perception-action loop.

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Robotic Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Robotic Sensors]
    S --> P[Proprioceptive Internal]
    S --> E[Exteroceptive External]
    P --> P1[Encoders]
    P --> P2[Potentiometers]
    P --> P3[Inertial Measurement Unit IMU]
    P --> P4[Tachometers]
    P --> P5[Current and Voltage Sensors]
    E --> E1[Range and Distance Sensors]
    E --> E2[Vision Sensors]
    E --> E3[Tactile and Force Sensors]
    E --> E4[Acoustic Sensors]
    E --> E5[Thermal Sensors]
    E1 --> R1[Ultrasonic]
    E1 --> R2[LiDAR]
    E1 --> R3[Infrared]
    E2 --> V1[CCD Camera]
    E2 --> V2[CMOS Camera]
    E3 --> T1[Force Torque Sensor]
    E3 --> T2[Tactile Skin Array]
```

## 4.3 Sensor Fusion Block Architecture

In advanced robots (e.g., autonomous vehicles), multiple sensors feed into a fusion block. Below is the block-level functional architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LIDAR[LiDAR Sensor] --> FUSE[Central Sensor Fusion Engine]
    CAM[Camera Sensor] --> FUSE
    IMU[IMU Sensor] --> FUSE
    ENC[Wheel Encoders] --> FUSE
    GPS[GPS Receiver] --> FUSE
    FUSE --> EST[State Estimator Kalman Filter]
    EST --> MAP[World Map Update]
    EST --> PLAN[Path Planner]
    PLAN --> CTRL[Motion Controller]
    CTRL --> ACT[Robot Actuators]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** Physical drawings of robots and sensors are hard to render in text. The KTU board examiner will accept a well-labeled **block-level functional architecture** as a valid substitute — it demonstrates the same conceptual understanding of signal flow and interconnections.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 1]**

**Q1. Define a robotic sensor. Distinguish clearly between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors, giving two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A robotic sensor is a device that converts a physical stimulus from the robot's internal state or its external environment into a measurable electrical signal for the controller.

- **Proprioceptive Sensors** measure the *internal* state of the robot.
  - *Examples*: Optical encoder (measures joint angle), tachometer (measures motor speed).
- **Exteroceptive Sensors** measure the *external* environment.
  - *Examples*: Ultrasonic distance sensor, CCD camera.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**

**Q2. List and briefly explain any four key performance characteristics used to evaluate a robotic sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Accuracy**: The closeness of the measured value to the *true* value of the measurand.
2. **Precision / Repeatability**: The ability of the sensor to produce the *same* output for the same input on repeated trials.
3. **Resolution**: The *smallest* change in the measurand that the sensor can detect.
4. **Sensitivity**: The ratio of the change in output to the change in input, denoted $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$.

*(A fifth acceptable answer: Dynamic Range, Linearity, or Bandwidth.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Full Module Question]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a generic robotic sensor system. Describe in detail the function of each block from the physical stimulus to the controller. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **quantization error** of a robotic sensor connected to an $n$-bit ADC. A 12-bit ADC is used to digitize the output of a temperature sensor with a full-scale output of 5 V. Compute the resolution and the maximum quantization error of the system. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Explanation (7 Marks)**

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the block diagram. The explanation for each block:

1. **Physical Stimulus ($x$)**: The environmental quantity to be measured (force, light, distance, temperature).
2. **Sensing Element**: Transduces $x$ into an electrical signal (e.g., photodiode, thermistor).
3. **Signal Conditioning**: Modifies the raw signal — amplification increases amplitude, filtering removes noise.
4. **ADC**: Converts the analog signal into a discrete digital code.
5. **Microcontroller / Processor**: Interprets the digital value using software.
6. **Decision Logic**: Compares the value with thresholds, fuses with other sensors, and decides on the next action.
7. **Controller and Actuator**: Executes the chosen action (motor movement, alarm, etc.).

*Valuation Note*: Award **2 marks** for the diagram, **3 marks** for block-wise description, and **2 marks** for connecting it to closed-loop control.

**Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Derivation** (full marks: 3):

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

$$
Q_{err} = \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Numerical** (full marks: 4):

Given: $n = 12$, $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$.

Resolution (size of 1 LSB):

$$
\Delta V = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} \approx 1.22\,\text{mV} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

Quantization error:

$$
Q_{err} = \frac{5}{2^{13}} = \frac{5}{8192} \approx 0.61\,\text{mV} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often confuse *resolution* with *quantization error*. **Resolution** is *one full LSB* ($\Delta V$), while **quantization error** is *half an LSB*. Writing $5/4096$ when the question asks for quantization error will cost you 1 mark.
> 2. Always include the **unit (V or mV)** in the final answer. Numerical answers without units are penalized by 0.5 mark per occurrence.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the following performance characteristics of a robotic sensor with suitable formulas:
   (i) Sensitivity
   (ii) Hysteresis error
   (iii) Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in dB
   (iv) Dynamic range **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A first-order temperature sensor has a static sensitivity $K = 0.1\,\text{V/°C}$ and a time constant $\tau = 5\,\text{s}$. The sensor is initially at 25 °C and is suddenly placed in an environment at 100 °C. Derive and plot (qualitatively) the time-domain response. Find the sensor output at $t = 5\,\text{s}$ and at $t = 10\,\text{s}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Performance Characteristics (7 Marks)**

(i) **Sensitivity** (1.5 marks):

$$
S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}
$$

It is the slope of the calibration curve. Higher sensitivity means a larger output change per unit input.

(ii) **Hysteresis Error** (1.5 marks):

$$
\epsilon_h = \frac{\max(x_{up} - x_{down})}{\text{Full Scale}} \times 100\%
$$

It is the maximum difference between the upscale and downscale readings of the sensor, expressed as a percentage of full scale.

(iii) **SNR in dB** (2 marks):

$$
SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)
$$

It quantifies the strength of the desired signal relative to background noise. Higher SNR indicates a cleaner measurement.

(iv) **Dynamic Range** (2 marks):

$$
DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{x_{max}}{x_{min}}\right)
$$

It is the ratio (in dB) of the largest to the smallest measurable stimulus.

**Part (b) — First-Order Sensor Response (7 Marks)**

**Derivation** (3 marks): From Section 3.2,

$$
y(t) = K X_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

For our problem:
- Static sensitivity: $K = 0.1\,\text{V/°C}$
- Step input magnitude: $X_0 = 100 - 25 = 75\,°\text{C}$
- Time constant: $\tau = 5\,\text{s}$

The final (steady-state) output:

$$
y(\infty) = K X_0 = 0.1 \times 75 = 7.5\,\text{V} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Output at $t = 5\,\text{s}$ (i.e., one time constant)**:

$$
y(5) = 7.5 \left(1 - e^{-5/5}\right) = 7.5 (1 - e^{-1}) = 7.5 (1 - 0.3679)
$$

$$
\boxed{y(5) \approx 7.5 \times 0.6321 \approx 4.74\,\text{V}} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**Output at $t = 10\,\text{s}$ (i.e., two time constants)**:

$$
y(10) = 7.5 \left(1 - e^{-10/5}\right) = 7.5 (1 - e^{-2}) = 7.5 (1 - 0.1353)
$$

$$
\boxed{y(10) \approx 7.5 \times 0.8647 \approx 6.49\,\text{V}} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**Qualitative Plot**: The output is an exponential rise from 0 V at $t = 0$ (since the initial state is at 25 °C with output $K \times 25 = 2.5\,\text{V}$; the deviation from initial is what we plot) asymptotically approaching 7.5 V.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. The total sensor output voltage is $y_{total} = y_{initial} + \Delta y$. If the initial output was $0.1 \times 25 = 2.5\,\text{V}$, then at $t = 5\,\text{s}$, the *total* output is $2.5 + 4.74 = 7.24\,\text{V}$. Many students forget the initial offset.
> 2. Do not confuse $K X_0$ with the *output voltage*. The expression $K X_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ gives the *change* in output due to the step input. Always clarify whether you are computing change or total.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robotic Sensors**

- ✅ A **robotic sensor** is a *transducer* that converts a physical stimulus into an electrical signal for the robot's controller.
- ✅ **Proprioceptive** sensors measure *internal* state (encoders, IMUs, current sensors).
- ✅ **Exteroceptive** sensors measure *external* environment (cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic).
- ✅ **Active** sensors emit energy; **passive** sensors only receive energy.
- ✅ The signal chain is: **Stimulus → Sensing element → Conditioning → ADC → Processor → Controller → Actuator**.
- ✅ **Sensitivity** $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$ is the slope of the input-output curve.
- ✅ **Resolution** is the *smallest detectable* change; for ADC, $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$.
- ✅ **Quantization error** is *half* of resolution: $Q_{err} = V_{ref}/2^{n+1}$.
- ✅ **Hysteresis** is the difference between upscale and downscale readings.
- ✅ **SNR in dB** uses the formula $20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ — not $10 \log_{10}$ (that is for power).
- ✅ **First-order sensor response**: $y(t) = K X_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- ✅ At $t = \tau$, output reaches **63.2%** of final value; at $t = 5\tau$, it is essentially at steady state (>99%).
- ✅ Always **state units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
- ✅ Always connect sensor descriptions to **closed-loop feedback** to earn the "application" mark.
- ✅ The KTU board exam typically tests: (1) definitions, (2) classification, (3) derivation of $Q_{err}$ or first-order response, and (4) numerical evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proximity Sensor: Eddy Current Proximity Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proximity Sensor: Eddy Current Proximity Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eddy Current Proximity Sensor (ECPS):** A non-contact, inductive displacement measurement device that detects the presence, position, or distance of a *conductive target* by exploiting the **electromagnetic induction** phenomenon. The sensor excites a high-frequency alternating current (typically **1 MHz to 10 MHz**) through a coil, generating a primary alternating magnetic field. When a conductive (preferably ferrous or non-ferrous metal) target enters this field, **Foucault (eddy) currents** are induced on the target's surface. These circulating currents, in accordance with **Lenz's Law**, generate a secondary magnetic field that **opposes** the primary field, thereby reducing the effective inductance and increasing the resistive losses of the sensor coil. The resulting change in coil impedance is processed and calibrated to yield a highly precise measurement of the **lift-off distance** (the gap between the sensor face and the target surface).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OERAT612 - Module 2):** The Eddy Current Proximity Sensor is a *non-contact* sensor classified under *electromagnetic proximity sensors*. It is fundamentally different from inductive proximity sensors that rely on a **ferromagnetic target altering the inductance of a coil**; an ECPS works on the **eddy current loss** mechanism, making it sensitive to *all conductive materials* (aluminium, copper, brass, stainless steel, titanium), not just ferrous metals.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Magnetic "Braking" Effect

Imagine you are pushing a heavy **swing** (the sensor's magnetic field) and a small, smooth **aluminium plate** (the conductive target) suddenly enters the swing's path. The swing doesn't just pass through — it gets **dragged and slowed down** because the plate is constantly being pushed by invisible currents that fight back.

In electrical terms:
- **The swing** = the alternating magnetic field from the sensor coil.
- **The aluminium plate** = the conductive target.
- **The invisible drag** = the eddy currents that oppose the field.
- **The faster you push (higher frequency), the stronger the drag** — this is the *skin effect* and the basis of the sensor's frequency-dependent response.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Unlike optical or ultrasonic sensors, an Eddy Current sensor is **unaffected by dust, oil, water, plastic, or non-conductive contaminants** on the target surface. This makes it the *go-to sensor* in harsh industrial environments such as jet engine turbine blade tip-clearance measurement, cryogenic valve position sensing, and high-speed spindle vibration monitoring.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Operating Frequency Range:** **1 MHz – 10 MHz** (typical: 2–4 MHz for general industrial use).
- **Typical Sensing Range (Lift-off):** **0.1 mm – 10 mm** (industrial grade: up to 25 mm with extended-range probes).
- **Resolution:** As fine as **0.01 µm (10 nm)** in laboratory-grade systems.
- **Linearity:** **±0.5% to ±1%** of full scale.
- **Operating Temperature:** **−55 °C to +250 °C** (probe-head dependent; electronics separately rated).
- **Response Frequency/Bandwidth:** Up to **100 kHz** (for dynamic vibration measurement).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The working of an Eddy Current Proximity Sensor can be broken down into the following structured physical and electrical stages:

- **Step 1 — Excitation of the Probe Coil:**
  The oscillator circuit inside the sensor drives a stable high-frequency alternating current (typically sinusoidal) through a precisely wound **air-core (or ferrite-core-backed) coil**. This produces a primary alternating magnetic field concentrated at the tip of the probe.

- **Step 2 — Field Interaction with the Conductive Target:**
  When a conductive target enters this alternating flux region, the time-varying magnetic flux $\Phi(t)$ passing perpendicularly through the target's surface **induces a circulating electromotive force (emf)** within the conductor, in accordance with **Faraday's Law of Induction**.

- **Step 3 — Generation of Eddy Currents:**
  This induced emf drives free electrons of the conductor in closed, swirling loops — known as **Foucault currents or Eddy currents** — primarily concentrated on the *target's surface* (skin effect).

- **Step 4 — Lenz's Law Opposition:**
  By **Lenz's Law**, the eddy currents flow in a direction such that their *self-induced secondary magnetic field* **opposes the change in the primary flux**. This effectively **reflects a counter-flux** back into the sensor coil.

- **Step 5 — Impedance Modulation of the Sensor Coil:**
  The reflected counter-flux **reduces the effective inductance** of the probe coil (due to magnetic field cancellation) and simultaneously introduces **additional resistive losses** (since eddy current circulation dissipates real power as $I^2R$ heat in the target).

- **Step 6 — Signal Conditioning and Linearization:**
  The resulting change in coil impedance is detected by a **bridge circuit** (often a Wheatstone bridge with the probe as one arm) or a **LC oscillator** whose output frequency or amplitude shifts in proportion to the lift-off distance. After demodulation, filtering, and linearization, a calibrated **voltage output proportional to distance** is produced.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the impedance change?** The sensor coil and the conductive target form a loosely coupled **transformer** — the sensor coil acts as the *primary*, and the eddy-current loop in the target acts as a *shorted secondary*. As the target approaches, the *mutual coupling coefficient $k$* increases, drawing more energy from the primary and loading the coil, which manifests as a change in the coil's equivalent impedance $Z_{eq}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **How is the target material identified?** Different materials have different **electrical conductivity $\sigma$** and **magnetic permeability $\mu$**, leading to different eddy current magnitudes for a given excitation frequency. The sensor must be **calibrated to a specific target material** for highest accuracy.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are repeatedly tested in KTU Module 2 questions.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inductance of a Solenoid | $L = \dfrac{N^{2}\,\mu\,A}{\ell}$ | $N$ = turns, $\mu$ = permeability (H/m), $A$ = area (m²), $\ell$ = length (m) | Foundation of probe coil design |
| 2 | Coil Impedance | $Z = R_{\text{coil}} + j\omega L$ | $R_{\text{coil}}$ = coil resistance ($\Omega$), $\omega = 2\pi f$ (rad/s) | AC analysis of the probe |
| 3 | Skin Depth in Target | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\,\mu\,\sigma}}$ | $\sigma$ = target conductivity (S/m), $\mu$ = target permeability (H/m) | Determines effective eddy current penetration depth |
| 4 | Quality Factor of Probe | $Q = \dfrac{\omega L}{R_{\text{coil}} + R_{\text{eddy}}}$ | $R_{\text{eddy}}$ = equivalent eddy current loss resistance | Sensitivity indicator |
| 5 | Mutual Inductance Coupling | $M = k\sqrt{L_{1}\,L_{2}}$ | $k$ = coupling coefficient, $0 \leq k \leq 1$ | Transformer model of sensor-target system |
| 6 | Equivalent Impedance (Ladder/Rosine Model) | $Z_{eq} = R_{1} + j\omega L_{1} + \dfrac{\omega^{2}M^{2}}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}$ | Subscript 1 = sensor coil, 2 = target eddy current loop | Predicts the actual impedance change observed |
| 7 | Voltage Output (Linearized) | $V_{\text{out}} = A - B \cdot \ln(d)$ | $d$ = lift-off distance, $A,B$ = calibration constants | Empirical distance-voltage relationship (logarithmic) |
| 8 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta Z}{\Delta d}$ | $S$ = sensitivity ($\Omega$/m or V/mm) | Defines resolution and range |
| 9 | Induced emf (Faraday's Law) | $\varepsilon = -N\,\dfrac{d\Phi_{B}}{dt}$ | $\Phi_{B}$ = magnetic flux (Wb) | Origin of eddy current generation |
| 10 | Target Resistance (eddy loop) | $R_{2} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\sigma\,\delta\,w}$ | $w$ = effective width of eddy path (m) | Used in impedance modelling |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** The output voltage $V_{\text{out}}$ is **NOT linearly proportional to distance $d$** — it follows an **inverse (logarithmic) characteristic**. Students who assume linear proportionality in numerical problems lose full marks. The sensor is *linearized* electronically for specific range windows.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace:** Blade-tip clearance measurement in jet engines (sub-millimetre accuracy at temperatures exceeding 800 °C).
- **Manufacturing:** Shaft vibration, displacement, and runout monitoring on CNC spindles.
- **Metallurgy:** Detection of cracks, corrosion, and surface defects in conductive structures (Eddy Current Testing — ECT).
- **Robotics:** End-effector positioning, weld seam tracking, and proximity detection in pick-and-place systems.
- **Semiconductor:** Wafer positioning and thickness measurement (with dual-coil configuration).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This section provides *complete, line-by-line derivations* of the foundational equations governing the Eddy Current Proximity Sensor. Every algebraic transition is explicitly justified.

### 3.1 Derivation of Skin Depth ($\delta$) in the Conductive Target

**Starting Point — The Electromagnetic Wave Equation in a Conductor:**

When an EM wave penetrates a good conductor (such as a metal target), the displacement current is negligible compared to the conduction current. The wave equation reduces to a **diffusion equation** whose solution is exponentially decaying.

**Step 1:** Write the 1D diffusion equation for the magnetic field amplitude $H(x)$ in the conductor:

$$\frac{d^{2}H}{dx^{2}} = j\,\omega\,\mu\,\sigma\,H$$

**Step 2:** Assume a trial solution of the form $H(x) = H_{0}\,e^{\gamma x}$, where $\gamma$ is the complex propagation constant.

Substituting:

$$\gamma^{2} = j\,\omega\,\mu\,\sigma$$

**Step 3:** Extract the square root. Use the identity $\sqrt{j} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\omega\,\mu\,\sigma}\;\cdot\;\sqrt{j} = \sqrt{\omega\,\mu\,\sigma}\;\cdot\;\frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\,\mu\,\sigma}{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Write the field in explicit real and imaginary form:

$$H(x) = H_{0}\,e^{-x\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}}\;\cdot\;e^{-j\,x\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}}$$

**Step 5:** The first exponential governs **attenuation**. By definition, the **skin depth $\delta$** is the distance at which the amplitude falls to $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ of its surface value.

Setting the exponent equal to $-1$:

$$\delta\,\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}$$

**Step 6:** Express angular frequency in terms of ordinary frequency using $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2\pi f\,\mu\,\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f\,\mu\,\sigma}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Higher excitation frequency $f$ → smaller skin depth → eddy currents are more tightly confined to the surface → *more sensitive* to surface defects but *less penetration* into the bulk. Lower frequency → deeper penetration → better for thicker targets. This is the **frequency-tuning principle** of the sensor.

### 3.2 Derivation of Equivalent Coil Impedance with Eddy Current Loading

**Setup:** Model the sensor coil and the target as a **two-coil mutually-coupled transformer** with one shorted secondary (the eddy current loop).

- Primary (sensor coil) parameters: $L_{1}$, $R_{1}$
- Secondary (target eddy loop) parameters: $L_{2}$, $R_{2}$
- Mutual inductance: $M = k\sqrt{L_{1}L_{2}}$
- Primary excitation: $V_{1} = V_{0}e^{j\omega t}$

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the **primary loop** (mesh 1):

$$V_{1} = R_{1}I_{1} + j\omega L_{1}I_{1} - j\omega M I_{2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply KVL to the **secondary loop** (mesh 2, with no source, only induced emf):

$$0 = R_{2}I_{2} + j\omega L_{2}I_{2} - j\omega M I_{1}$$

**Step 3:** Solve the secondary equation for $I_{2}$:

$$I_{2}\,(R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}) = j\omega M I_{1}$$

$$I_{2} = \frac{j\omega M}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}\,I_{1}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $I_{2}$ into the primary KVL equation:

$$V_{1} = \left(R_{1} + j\omega L_{1}\right)I_{1} - j\omega M \cdot \frac{j\omega M}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}\,I_{1}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the coupling term. Note that $j\omega M \cdot j\omega M = -\omega^{2}M^{2}$, so the negative signs combine to $+1$:

$$V_{1} = \left[\,R_{1} + j\omega L_{1} + \frac{\omega^{2}M^{2}}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}\,\right] I_{1}$$

**Step 6:** Identify the equivalent impedance of the primary coil as seen by the source:

$$\boxed{\;Z_{eq} = R_{1} + j\omega L_{1} + \frac{\omega^{2}M^{2}}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The third term $\dfrac{\omega^{2}M^{2}}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}$ is the **reflected impedance** of the target's eddy current loop back into the sensor coil. As the target approaches, the coupling coefficient $k$ (and hence $M$) **increases**, increasing the magnitude of this term, which **reduces the net inductive reactance** and **adds a lossy resistive component** — this is the measurable change exploited by the sensor.

### 3.3 Rationalization of Reflected Impedance Term

To extract real and imaginary parts (useful for understanding the energy dissipation), rationalize the denominator:

**Step 1:** Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate $(R_{2} - j\omega L_{2})$:

$$\frac{\omega^{2}M^{2}}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}} = \frac{\omega^{2}M^{2}\,(R_{2} - j\omega L_{2})}{R_{2}^{2} + \omega^{2}L_{2}^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Split into real (resistive) and imaginary (reactive) parts:

$$\text{Reflected Resistance: } \Delta R = \frac{\omega^{2}M^{2}\,R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + \omega^{2}L_{2}^{2}}$$

$$\text{Reflected Reactance: } \Delta X = -\frac{\omega^{3}M^{2}\,L_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + \omega^{2}L_{2}^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** The total equivalent impedance becomes:

$$Z_{eq} = \left(R_{1} + \frac{\omega^{2}M^{2}\,R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + \omega^{2}L_{2}^{2}}\right) + j\left(\omega L_{1} - \frac{\omega^{3}M^{2}\,L_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + \omega^{2}L_{2}^{2}}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** $\Delta R$ represents **energy dissipated in the target** as eddy current heating (real power loss). $\Delta X$ represents the **net reduction in coil inductance** (since the imaginary part is *negative*, the effective inductance decreases). Both effects are monotonic functions of $M$ — i.e., monotonic with the lift-off distance $d$.

### 3.4 Sensitivity and Lift-off Distance Relationship

The mutual inductance $M$ between the probe and target decreases approximately as a **cubic function of distance** for small lift-offs and as a **higher-order polynomial** for larger gaps. A commonly used empirical model is:

$$M(d) \;\approx\; \frac{M_{0}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{d}{d_{0}}\right)^{n}}\quad\text{where } n \in [1.5,\, 3.0]$$

- $M_{0}$ = mutual inductance at zero lift-off (theoretical maximum).
- $d$ = lift-off distance.
- $d_{0}$ = characteristic distance constant (probe geometry dependent).
- $n$ = empirical fall-off exponent.

The sensitivity is the derivative of the measurable quantity with respect to $d$:

$$S = \left\lvert \frac{dZ_{eq}}{dd} \right\rvert = \left\lvert \frac{\partial Z_{eq}}{\partial M} \cdot \frac{dM}{dd} \right\rvert$$

This is maximum at **small lift-off distances**, which is why ECPS are most accurate in the **0–2 mm range** and progressively less sensitive at larger gaps.

### 3.5 Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A 3 MHz eddy current probe has a coil of 50 turns wound on a 4 mm diameter form, length 6 mm, operating in air. Calculate its nominal inductance. If the target is aluminium ($\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^{7}$ S/m, $\mu_{r} = 1$), calculate the skin depth in the target. Comment on the choice of operating frequency.

**Solution:**

Inductance:

$$L = \frac{N^{2}\mu_{0}A}{\ell} = \frac{(50)^{2} \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times \pi (2\times 10^{-3})^{2}}{6 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$L = \frac{2500 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-5}}{6 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 6.58\;\mu\text{H}}$$

Skin depth:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times 3\times 10^{6} \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 3.5 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4.15 \times 10^{8}}} \approx 4.91 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} \approx 49.1\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Comment:** A 3 MHz excitation produces a skin depth of ~49 µm in aluminium — adequate for *surface defect detection* and *lift-off measurement* up to a few millimetres. The choice balances sensitivity (high frequency = small $\delta$ = strong eddy currents) and penetration depth (sufficient to register on thin foils).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an Eddy Current Proximity Sensor. State any two distinguishing features that differentiate it from a conventional inductive proximity sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Eddy Current Proximity Sensor** is a non-contact electromagnetic sensor that detects the distance to a *conductive target* by measuring the change in impedance of an excited coil caused by **eddy currents induced in the target** via electromagnetic induction.

**Distinguishing features:**

1. **Target Material Sensitivity:** A conventional inductive proximity sensor requires a **ferromagnetic target** (e.g., iron/steel) to alter the coil's reluctance. An ECPS can detect **any electrically conductive material** — both ferrous (steel) and non-ferrous (aluminium, copper, brass, titanium) — because it senses *eddy current losses*, not magnetic flux concentration.

2. **Operating Principle:** A conventional inductive sensor changes its inductance via a **change in magnetic circuit reluctance** when a ferromagnetic slug enters. An ECPS changes its coil impedance via **Lenz's Law counter-flux** generated by induced eddy currents, with no change in reluctance geometry.

3. **Operating Frequency:** Conventional inductive sensors operate at low frequencies (a few kHz). ECPS operate at high frequencies (1–10 MHz) to ensure strong eddy current generation in the target.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "inductive sensor" and "eddy current sensor" interchangeably. They are **different families**. The inductive sensor is part of Module 2 — *both* are in your syllabus, but their underlying physics is *not* the same. Marks are deducted for conflating the two.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the term "skin effect" and state its significance in the operation of an Eddy Current Proximity Sensor. Write the expression for skin depth.**

**Model Answer:**

**Skin Effect** is the phenomenon whereby an alternating current (and the associated alternating magnetic field) within a conductor does *not* distribute uniformly across the cross-section, but instead **concentrates near the surface** of the conductor, with the current density falling off exponentially with depth.

**Significance in ECPS:**

- It determines the **effective depth** at which eddy currents are induced in the conductive target.
- The eddy currents (and therefore the measurable impedance change) are confined to a thin layer of thickness $\delta$ from the target's surface.
- A smaller skin depth (achieved at **higher operating frequencies**) makes the sensor **more sensitive to surface features and smaller lift-off distances**, but limits the useful range.

**Expression for skin depth:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\,\mu\,\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f\,\mu\,\sigma}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is writing $\delta \propto f$ or $\delta \propto \sqrt{f}$. The correct dependency is $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$ — as frequency **increases**, skin depth **decreases**. Writing the wrong proportionality is an instant 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of an **Eddy Current Proximity Sensor**. Clearly state the role of the high-frequency oscillator, the probe coil, and the demodulation stage.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle — Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete Mermaid block diagram.)

**Stage 1 — High-Frequency Oscillator (1.5 marks):**
A crystal-stabilized or LC oscillator generates a stable sinusoidal AC excitation in the range of **1 MHz to 10 MHz**. This high frequency is essential because the magnitude of induced eddy currents is proportional to the rate of change of flux ($d\Phi/dt$), and the skin depth $\delta$ must be small enough that the eddy current effect is strong.

**Stage 2 — Probe Coil and Electromagnetic Interaction (2.5 marks):**
The high-frequency current is fed to the probe coil wound around a ferrite core inside a non-conductive bobbin. The coil radiates an alternating magnetic field from its tip. When a conductive target enters this field:

- **Faraday's Law** induces circulating eddy currents in the target's surface layer.
- **Lenz's Law** causes these eddy currents to oppose the primary flux, generating a counter-flux that links back with the probe coil.
- The mutual coupling between coil and target effectively loads the probe — **reducing its inductance** and **increasing its equivalent resistance**.

**Stage 3 — Impedance Detection (1.5 marks):**
The probe is connected as one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge** (or forms part of an LC oscillator tank). The bridge becomes unbalanced by the impedance change, producing a differential AC voltage proportional to the lift-off.

**Stage 4 — Demodulation, Filtering, and Linearization (1.5 marks):**
The differential AC signal is **demodulated** (using a synchronous detector or envelope detector) into a DC voltage, **filtered** to remove high-frequency noise, and **linearized** by a look-up table or polynomial correction circuit. The final output is a calibrated DC voltage (e.g., 0–10 V) or 4–20 mA current proportional to distance.

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **equivalent impedance** of the sensor coil when loaded by a conductive target, modelled as a mutually coupled shorted secondary. State the physical meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Model Assumptions:** Sensor coil (primary) with self-inductance $L_{1}$ and resistance $R_{1}$. Target eddy-current loop (secondary) with $L_{2}$ and $R_{2}$, short-circuited. Mutual inductance $M = k\sqrt{L_{1}L_{2}}$.

**Step 1 — KVL at primary:** (1 mark)

$$V_{1} = R_{1}I_{1} + j\omega L_{1}I_{1} - j\omega M I_{2}$$

**Step 2 — KVL at secondary (shorted, no source):** (1 mark)

$$0 = R_{2}I_{2} + j\omega L_{2}I_{2} - j\omega M I_{1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $I_{2}$:** (1.5 marks)

$$I_{2} = \frac{j\omega M}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}\,I_{1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into primary equation:** (1.5 marks)

$$V_{1} = \left(R_{1} + j\omega L_{1}\right)I_{1} + \frac{\omega^{2}M^{2}}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}\,I_{1}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the equivalent impedance:** (1 mark)

$$\boxed{\;Z_{eq} = R_{1} + j\omega L_{1} + \frac{\omega^{2}M^{2}}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Physical meaning of each term (1 mark):**

- $R_{1} + j\omega L_{1}$: the *unperturbed* impedance of the sensor coil alone (when no target is present or the target is infinitely far).
- $\dfrac{\omega^{2}M^{2}}{R_{2} + j\omega L_{2}}$: the **reflected impedance** of the target's eddy-current loop. Its real part represents the *eddy-current power loss* (energy dissipated in the target as heat), and its imaginary part represents the *mutual demagnetization effect* that reduces the net inductance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget the *negative sign* in the mutual coupling terms. The minus sign arises from the **dotted-end convention** of mutual inductance and Lenz's Law. A missing sign leads to a wrong sign in the reflected impedance and a 2-mark deduction.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **skin effect** in a conductor. Derive the expression for skin depth. Discuss how the choice of operating frequency affects the performance of an Eddy Current Proximity Sensor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Skin Effect (1 mark):**
When high-frequency alternating current flows through a conductor, the current density is not uniform — it is **maximum at the surface** and **decreases exponentially with depth** into the conductor.

**Step 2 — Derivation (4 marks):** (See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation.)

Starting from the diffusion equation $\dfrac{d^{2}H}{dx^{2}} = j\omega\mu\sigma H$, the trial solution $H(x) = H_{0}e^{\gamma x}$ yields:

$$\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$

The amplitude of the field at depth $x$ is $H_{0}\,e^{-x/\delta}$, where the **skin depth** is:

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f\mu\sigma}}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of Frequency on Performance (2 marks):**

- **Higher frequency** → smaller skin depth → eddy currents are confined to a thinner surface layer → **stronger surface current density** → **larger impedance change** in the probe → **higher sensitivity** but **reduced measurement range** and **greater susceptibility to surface roughness**.
- **Lower frequency** → larger skin depth → deeper eddy penetration → **lower sensitivity** but **larger lift-off range** and **reduced sensitivity to surface finish**.

A practical engineering compromise is **2–4 MHz** for general industrial ECPS.

---

**(b)** A 2 MHz Eddy Current probe has 80 turns of wire on a cylindrical form of diameter 5 mm and length 4 mm. Calculate:
- (i) The inductance of the coil in air.
- (ii) The skin depth in an aluminium target ($\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^{7}$ S/m, $\mu_{r} = 1$).
- (iii) Comment on the suitability of this frequency for detecting sub-surface defects at 0.5 mm depth.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Inductance in air (2.5 marks):**

$$L = \frac{N^{2}\mu_{0}A}{\ell} = \frac{(80)^{2} \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times \pi (2.5\times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$L = \frac{6400 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$L = \frac{1.5786 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 3.95 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{H} \approx \boxed{39.5\;\mu\text{H}}$$

**(ii) Skin depth in aluminium (2.5 marks):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{6} \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 3.5 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2.77 \times 10^{8}}} \approx 6.01 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} \approx \boxed{60.1\;\mu\text{m}}$$

**(iii) Comment on suitability for 0.5 mm sub-surface defect (2 marks):**

The skin depth is only **60.1 µm**, which is **much smaller than 0.5 mm**. At 2 MHz, the eddy currents are confined to a thin surface layer (0.06 mm), so a defect located at 0.5 mm depth would produce **negligible disturbance** in the eddy current pattern. Therefore, **2 MHz is NOT suitable** for detecting sub-surface defects at 0.5 mm depth in aluminium.

**Recommendation:** A *lower* excitation frequency (in the kHz range) would be required to increase the skin depth to ≥ 0.5 mm, at the cost of reduced surface sensitivity. For example, at $f = 30$ kHz:

$$\delta \approx \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times 3 \times 10^{4} \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.5 \times 10^{7}}} \approx 0.49\;\text{mm}$$

This is borderline; for reliable sub-surface defect detection at 0.5 mm in aluminium, the frequency should be reduced to **15–20 kHz**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones — Eddy Current Proximity Sensor (Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing inductive and eddy-current sensors** (loses 2–3 marks in descriptive questions).
> 2. **Writing the wrong proportionality for skin depth** — it is $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$, not $\delta \propto \sqrt{f}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign** in mutual-coupling KVL equations — the reflection impedance sign is critical.
> 4. **Assuming linear $V_{out}$ vs. distance** — the actual response is logarithmic; the linearized output is only valid within the calibrated range.
> 5. **Not specifying target material** in numerical problems — different materials yield different $\delta$, $R_{2}$, and $L_{2}$; a "bare" numerical answer is incomplete.
> 6. **Missing units** in the final answer — the KTU evaluator deducts 0.5 marks per quantity without units.
> 7. **Confusing the role of Litz wire** — it is used to *minimize* the coil's own skin-effect loss, not the target's.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Eddy Current Proximity Sensor (Module 2):**
> - ⭐ The sensor operates on the principle of **eddy current induction** in conductive targets — *not* on reluctance change.
> - ⭐ Operating frequency range: **1 MHz – 10 MHz**.
> - ⭐ Skin depth formula: $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$ — smaller for higher frequency and higher conductivity.
> - ⭐ The sensor can detect *all* conductive materials — ferrous **and** non-ferrous.
> - ⭐ Equivalent circuit: **transformer model** with shorted secondary → $Z_{eq} = R_{1} + j\omega L_{1} + \omega^{2}M^{2}/(R_{2} + j\omega L_{2})$.
> - ⭐ The reflected impedance term has *two physical effects*: (a) eddy current power loss (real), and (b) demagnetizing reactance (imaginary).
> - ⭐ Output voltage is **logarithmically** related to lift-off distance — the sensor is electronically linearized.
> - ⭐ Sensitivity is highest at **small lift-off distances**; falls off with $d^{1.5}$ to $d^{3}$.
> - ⭐ The probe uses **Litz wire** windings and a **ferrite core** for field concentration.
> - ⭐ Advantages: non-contact, no contamination sensitivity (immune to oil, dust, water), sub-µm resolution, high bandwidth.
> - ⭐ Limitations: only works on *conductive* targets; requires target-specific calibration; affected by temperature drift of conductivity.
> - ⭐ Applications: blade-tip clearance, vibration monitoring, NDT (non-destructive testing), robotic proximity sensing, wafer metrology.
> - ⭐ **Law to remember in derivations:** Faraday's Law ($\varepsilon = -N\,d\Phi/dt$) + Lenz's Law (counter-flux direction).
> - ⭐ **KTU keyword in answers:** always mention *"mutual coupling"*, *"Lenz's Law opposition"*, and *"skin effect"* — these are examiner triggers for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Proximity Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inductive Proximity Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Inductive Proximity Sensor (IPS)** is a non-contact solid-state electromagnetic transducer that detects the presence, absence, or position of a metallic (ferrous or non-ferrous) target object by exploiting the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and **Lenz's Law**. The sensor generates a high-frequency alternating electromagnetic field from an energized coil (typically an LC tank oscillator); when a conductive target enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced on the target's surface, which in turn load the oscillator circuit, causing a measurable change in its amplitude or frequency that is converted into a binary switching output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag — OERAT612 / Module 2**
> Inductive sensors are listed under *Contactless / Proximity Sensors* and are commonly clubbed with capacitive and magnetic sensors as the three pillars of industrial non-contact detection.

### 1.2 Intuition — The "Doorbell Buzzer" Analogy

Imagine a **burglar-alarm magnetic loop** buried under your doormat. When no one steps on it, the loop is quiet. The instant a metallic toolbox is placed near the loop, an invisible "electrical drag" pulls energy out of the loop — almost as if the toolbox were a silent sponge soaking up magnetic energy. The sensor's electronics detect this energy theft and flip an internal switch, exactly like a relay clicking "ON".

In an inductive proximity sensor:
- The **coil** = the doormat loop (energy source).
- The **metallic target** = the toolbox (energy sink via eddy currents).
- The **detector electronics** = the burglar alarm listener.
- The **output transistor** = the relay that clicks ON or OFF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Inductive sensors detect **metallic targets only**. They are *blind* to plastic, wood, ceramics, and liquids — a critical distinction often tested in KTU multiple-choice questions.

### 1.3 Operating Boundary Conditions

- **Standard Target (ST)** = **1 mm thick**, square mild-steel (Fe 360) plate, side equal to the sensor's active face diameter.
- **Operating Frequency** = typically **100 kHz to 1 MHz** (sensing coil).
- **Rated Switching Distance (sₙ)** = a theoretical, normalized reference distance; the real usable distance is **0 ≤ s ≤ sₙ**.
- **Hysteresis (H)** = typically **1 % to 20 % of sᵣ** (the real switching distance).
- **Repeat Accuracy (R)** = typically **0.01·sᵣ** to **0.15·sᵣ**.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Class | Activation Mechanism | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|
| **Inductive (IPS)** | Eddy-current damping of HF field | All metals (Fe, Cu, Al, Brass) |
| **Capacitive (CPS)** | Dielectric change in RC field | Metals + non-metals |
| **Magnetic (Reed/Hall)** | Flux-density change | Magnets / soft-iron only |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Stack — Five Pillars

1. **Pillar 1 — High-Frequency Oscillator**: A stable LC tank (Hartley or Colpitts topology) sustains a sinusoidal current in the sensing coil at frequency **f₀** in the **100 kHz–1 MHz** band.
2. **Pillar 2 — Active Electromagnetic Field**: The coil radiates a localized alternating flux Φ(t) = Φₘ sin(ωt) that extends a few millimeters into free space.
3. **Pillar 3 — Faraday + Lenz Action on the Target**: As per Faraday's Law, the alternating flux cutting a nearby conductor induces an EMF ε = −N(dΦ/dt). Per Lenz's Law, the induced (eddy) current circulates so as to *oppose* the cause.
4. **Pillar 4 — Energy Extraction (Loading Effect)**: The eddy-current loop dissipates real power **Pₑ = Iₑ²·R_target**, draining energy from the tank circuit. The oscillator amplitude drops by ΔU.
5. **Pillar 5 — Threshold Detection & Output**: A Schmitt-trigger / demodulator compares the oscillator amplitude against a fixed reference. When ΔU exceeds the threshold, the output transistor (PNP / NPN / NO / NC) switches.

### 2.2 The "WHY" Behind Each Step

- **Why HF?** — Higher f gives larger dΦ/dt, which boosts ε and produces stronger eddy currents even at millimeter-scale distances. The skin depth δ also shrinks, confining currents to a thin surface layer where they can be detected reliably.
- **Why a coil?** — A multi-turn air-core or ferrite-core coil concentrates flux into a unipolar, well-defined lobe normal to the sensor face — perfect for short-range robotic end-effectors.
- **Why demodulate?** — The raw AC amplitude is too noisy to compare; a peak-detector / rectifier extracts the envelope.
- **Why a Schmitt trigger?** — It provides clean snap-action switching with built-in hysteresis to prevent output chattering.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid — "FLIPS"**: **F**araday, **L**enz, **I**nductance, **P**ower-loss, **S**kin-depth. These five are the highest-yield formulas for KTU Board problems.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's induced EMF | Volt (V) |
| 2 | $\Phi = B \cdot A \cdot \cos\theta$ | Magnetic flux linkage | Weber (Wb) |
| 3 | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, \mu_r \, N^{2} \, A}{\ell}$ | Inductance of a long solenoid | Henry (H) |
| 4 | $X_L = 2 \pi f L$ | Inductive reactance | Ohm (Ω) |
| 5 | $Z = \sqrt{R^{2} + X_L^{2}}$ | Coil impedance magnitude | Ohm (Ω) |
| 6 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$ | Skin / penetration depth | Meter (m) |
| 7 | $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \, B_p^{2} \, d^{2} \, f^{2}}{6 \rho} \cdot V$ | Eddy-current power loss | Watt (W) |
| 8 | $s_r = s_n \cdot C_f$ | Real switching distance | mm |
| 9 | $H\,[\%] = \dfrac{s_r - s_d}{s_r} \cdot 100$ | Hysteresis percentage | % |
| 10 | $C_f = \dfrac{\text{actual target factor}}{1}$ | Correction factor (Cu ≈ 0.4, Al ≈ 0.35, Brass ≈ 0.5) | dimensionless |

> **Where:**
> $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,H/m$, $\omega = 2\pi f$, $\sigma$ = conductivity of target (S/m), $\rho$ = target resistivity (Ω·m), $V$ = effective target volume, $s_n$ = nominal switching distance, $s_r$ = real switching distance, $s_d$ = release distance, $d$ = target thickness.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In robotics and factory automation, inductive sensors are used as:
- **End-effector homing switches** on CNC tool changers.
- **Gear-tooth counters** (using the frequency-shift mode instead of amplitude-shift).
- **Weld-nut detection** in automotive body shops.
- **Conveyor position flags** in pick-and-place cells.
- **Tool-breakage detectors** in machining centres.

Because they are sealed (IP67 / IP69K), contactless (no wear), and immune to dust, oil, and vibration, they outlast mechanical limit switches by **10:1 to 100:1**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mutual Coupling Between Sensor Coil and Target

Consider a sensor coil of inductance $L_0$ and resistance $R_0$ in series with a tuning capacitor $C$. When a conductive target approaches, mutual inductance $M$ appears between the sensor coil and the eddy-current loop in the target. The target loop itself has self-inductance $L_t$ and resistance $R_t$.

#### Step 1 — Write the target-side KVL

The induced EMF in the target loop must equal the voltage drop across its internal impedance:

$$
\varepsilon_t = -\dfrac{d}{dt}(M i_1) = R_t \, i_2 + L_t \dfrac{di_2}{dt}
$$

where $i_1$ is the sensor coil current and $i_2$ is the induced eddy current.

#### Step 2 — Solve for the eddy current in steady-state sinusoidal regime

Replace $\dfrac{d}{dt}$ with $j\omega$ for phasor analysis:

$$
j\omega M I_1 = (R_t + j\omega L_t) \, I_2
$$

Rearrange to isolate the eddy current:

$$
I_2 = \dfrac{j\omega M}{R_t + j\omega L_t} \, I_1
$$

This is the magnitude of the induced current — directly proportional to frequency $f$ and mutual coupling $M$.

#### Step 3 — Reflect the target impedance into the sensor side

The reflected impedance seen by the sensor coil is:

$$
Z_{ref} = \dfrac{\omega^{2} M^{2}}{R_t + j\omega L_t}
$$

Rationalise the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate $(R_t - j\omega L_t)$:

$$
Z_{ref} = \dfrac{\omega^{2} M^{2} \, (R_t - j\omega L_t)}{R_t^{2} + \omega^{2} L_t^{2}}
$$

#### Step 4 — Separate real and imaginary parts

$$
Z_{ref} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\omega^{2} M^{2} R_t}{R_t^{2} + \omega^{2} L_t^{2}}}_{\text{Real part } R_{ref}} \; - \; j \underbrace{\dfrac{\omega^{3} M^{2} L_t}{R_t^{2} + \omega^{2} L_t^{2}}}_{\text{Imaginary part } X_{ref}}
$$

#### Step 5 — Physical interpretation

- The **real part $R_{ref}$** represents **energy dissipated by eddy currents** — this is what loads the oscillator and reduces its amplitude.
- The **imaginary part $X_{ref}$** represents a **frequency shift** of the LC tank — useful in the *frequency-shift variant* of inductive sensors.
- The total apparent inductance of the sensor coil becomes:

$$
L_{app} = L_0 - \dfrac{\omega^{2} M^{2} L_t}{R_t^{2} + \omega^{2} L_t^{2}}
$$

As the target approaches, $M$ increases, **$L_{app}$ drops**, detuning the LC tank and shifting its resonant frequency downward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip — Examiner's Favourite Derivation**
> Most 14-mark "explain the working" questions award **3 to 4 marks** to this reflected-impedance argument alone. Memorise Steps 4 and 5 verbatim.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Skin Depth (Penetration Depth) of Eddy Currents

Eddy currents in a thick conductor are not uniform; they concentrate near the surface and decay exponentially inward.

#### Step 1 — Maxwell's curl equation inside the conductor

Inside a conductor with conductivity $\sigma$ and permeability $\mu$, an alternating field obeys:

$$
\nabla^{2} \mathbf{H} = j \omega \mu \sigma \, \mathbf{H}
$$

#### Step 2 — 1-D form assuming planar field decay

For a plane wave penetrating the +z direction:

$$
\dfrac{d^{2} H_x}{dz^{2}} = j \omega \mu \sigma \, H_x
$$

#### Step 3 — Assume exponential solution

Let $H_x(z) = H_0 \, e^{-\gamma z}$. Substituting:

$$
\gamma^{2} = j \omega \mu \sigma
$$

#### Step 4 — Take the square root using $j = e^{j\pi/2}$

$$
\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}
$$

#### Step 5 — Write the field as decaying sinusoid

$$
H_x(z) = H_0 \, e^{-z/\delta} \, e^{-jz/\delta}
$$

where the penetration depth $\delta$ is:

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}
$$

#### Step 6 — Numerical sanity check

For copper at $f = 500\,kHz$, $\rho = 1.7 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\!\cdot\!m$, $\mu \approx \mu_0$:

$$
\delta \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 5\times 10^{5} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}}
$$

Computing numerator: $\rho = 1.7\times10^{-8}$.
Computing denominator: $\pi \times 5\times10^{5} \times 4\pi\times10^{-7} = 5\pi^{2}\times10^{-1} \approx 4.934$.
Therefore $\delta \approx \sqrt{3.45 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.86\times10^{-4}\,m \approx 0.186\,mm$.

> This confirms that eddy currents are confined to a **sub-millimetre surface skin**, which is why an inductive sensor is sensitive to a *very thin* metal target — typically **1 mm** is the standard thickness.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Inductance Change with Target Distance

For a circular target of diameter $D$ placed at axial distance $r$ from a circular coil of diameter $d_c$, the fractional inductance change follows an empirical closed form:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{k}{1 + \left(\dfrac{2r}{D}\right)^{2}}
$$

where $k$ is the *coupling coefficient* (typically 0.4–0.6 for an optimally sized target). This shows:

- $\Delta L / L_0$ is maximum when $r \ll D$ (target fully covers the coil footprint).
- $\Delta L / L_0 \to 0$ when $r \gg D$ (target too small or too far).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sensor Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[DC Supply 10 to 30 V] --> OSC[LC Oscillator 100 kHz to 1 MHz]
    OSC --> COIL[Ferritic Core Sensing Coil]
    COIL -.EM Field.-> TGT[Conductive Target]
    TGT -.Eddy Currents.-> COIL
    COIL --> DEMOD[Peak Detector and Demodulator]
    DEMOD --> SCHM[Schmitt Trigger with Hysteresis]
    SCHM --> OUT[Open Collector Output Transistor]
    OUT --> LD[External Load Relay or PLC Input]
    SCHM -.Feedback Threshold.-> DEMOD
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Amplitude-Shift vs Frequency-Shift Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Amplitude Shift Variant]
        A1[LC Tank] --> A2[Rectifier]
        A2 --> A3[Schmitt Trigger]
        A3 --> A4[Output]
    end
    subgraph B[Frequency Shift Variant]
        B1[LC Tank with PLL] --> B2[Frequency to Voltage Converter]
        B2 --> B3[Comparator]
        B3 --> B4[Output]
    end
    COIL2[Common Sensing Coil] --> A1
    COIL2 --> B1
```

### 4.3 Shielded vs Unshielded Sensing Field Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SH[Shielded Barrel Type]
        S1[Coil Core] --> S2[Metal Ring Shield]
        S2 -.Field Lines Axial.-> S3[Target Must Approach Face On]
    end
    subgraph US[Unshielded Smooth Barrel]
        U1[Coil Core] --> U2[No Metal Ring Shield]
        U2 -.Field Lines Lateral.-> U3[Target Detected From Side Too]
    end
```

### 4.4 Signal Conditioning and Output Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAW[Raw Sensor Output Transistor] --> PU[Pull Up or Pull Down Resistor]
    PU --> ISOL[Galvanic Isolation Optional]
    ISOL --> MCU[Robot Controller GPIO Input]
    MCU --> ACTION[Decision Logic Actuation]
```

### 4.5 Hysteresis Loop Characteristic (Textual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STATE1[Output OFF - Target Far] -->|Target Approaches sr| STATE2[Output ON]
    STATE2 -->|Target Recedes to sd| STATE1
    NOTE[Hysteresis H = sr - sd Prevents Chattering]
    STATE1 -.- NOTE
    STATE2 -.- NOTE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define an inductive proximity sensor and state **two** materials it cannot detect.

**Model Answer:**
An inductive proximity sensor is a non-contact electromagnetic device that detects the presence of metallic targets by sensing the change in amplitude of a high-frequency LC oscillator caused by eddy currents induced on the target. It detects only conductive materials and is blind to **(i) plastics / polymers** and **(ii) wood / ceramics / non-conductive liquids** (any two).

*(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
