| 3 | $E_{actual} = E_{measured}(T_{hot},T_{ref}) + E_{lookup}(T_{ref},0°C)$ | Cold Junction Compensation equation | V |
| 4 | $T_{hot} = T_{ref} + \Delta T_{measured}$ | True temperature from compensated EMF | K or °C |
| 5 | $P_{thermoelectric} = \alpha^2 \cdot \sigma \cdot T \cdot A / L$ | Thermoelectric power figure-of-merit (advanced) | W |
| 6 | $S_{sensitivity} \approx \Delta E / \Delta T$ | Practical sensitivity (look-up from ITS-90 tables) | μV/°C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Note:** In the table above, vertical bars and absolute-value operators have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to maintain markdown table integrity. Always render absolute values as $\vert x \vert$ in handwritten or LaTeX responses.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In Robotics:** Embedded in **BLDC motor housings** to predict bearing/winding failure via temperature trends.
- **In Industrial Automation:** Furnace and extrusion-line thermal profiling.
- **In Aerospace Robotics:** Skin and engine-exhaust temperature monitoring of unmanned aerial systems.
- **In Medical Robotics:** Sterilizer temperature validation in surgical instrument handling.
- **In Space Robotics (NASA/JAXA rovers):** Extreme-environment thermal mapping on planetary surfaces.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Seebeck Voltage (Linearized)

The fundamental thermoelectric relation is:

$$E = \int_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}} \left[ \alpha_A(T) - \alpha_B(T) \right] \, dT$$

**Step 1:** Define individual material Seebeck coefficients.
Let $\alpha_A(T)$ be the absolute Seebeck coefficient of metal A, and $\alpha_B(T)$ that of metal B.

**Step 2:** Recognize the net EMF is the integral of the *difference* of Seebeck coefficients across the temperature span.
The reason: each conductor, exposed to a temperature gradient, internally develops an opposing potential; the net external voltage is the difference of the two internal potentials.

**Step 3:** For engineering simplification, we approximate the integral as a *mean* Seebeck coefficient $\bar{\alpha}_{AB}$ over the operating range.

$$\bar{\alpha}_{AB} = \frac{1}{T_{hot} - T_{cold}} \int_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}} \left[ \alpha_A(T) - \alpha_B(T) \right] dT$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the mean into the EMF equation.

$$E = \bar{\alpha}_{AB} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example (Type K thermocouple, $\bar{\alpha}_{AB} \approx 41 \,\mu V/°C$).
Suppose $T_{hot} = 250\,°C$ and $T_{cold} = 25\,°C$:

$$E = (41 \times 10^{-6}) \times (250 - 25)$$

$$E = (41 \times 10^{-6}) \times 225$$

$$E = 9225 \times 10^{-6} \; V = 9.225 \; mV$$

This matches the **ITS-90 Type K reference table** (within typical 1–2% tolerance for linear approximation).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)

A real-world scenario: The thermocouple wires terminate at the robot's data-acquisition (DAQ) board at terminal block temperature $T_{ref}$ (which is *not* 0 °C).

**Step 1:** The voltage measured by the DAQ is:

$$E_{measured} = \bar{\alpha}_{AB} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{ref})$$

**Step 2:** To obtain the true voltage that would exist if the reference were at 0 °C, we *add* the EMF corresponding to $T_{ref} \to 0°C$:

$$E_{true} = E_{measured} + E_{ref \to 0}$$

By the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**

$$E_{true} = \bar{\alpha}_{AB} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{ref}) + \bar{\alpha}_{AB} \cdot (T_{ref} - 0)$$

**Step 3:** Algebraically combine:

$$E_{true} = \bar{\alpha}_{AB} \cdot T_{hot} - \bar{\alpha}_{AB} \cdot T_{ref} + \bar{\alpha}_{AB} \cdot T_{ref}$$

$$E_{true} = \bar{\alpha}_{AB} \cdot T_{hot}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the true hot-junction temperature:

$$T_{hot} = \frac{E_{true}}{\bar{\alpha}_{AB}}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example (Type K, $\bar{\alpha} = 41 \mu V/°C$, $T_{ref} = 30 °C$, $E_{measured} = 8.61 mV$).

$$E_{true} = 8.61 \, mV + (41 \, \mu V/°C \times 30 °C)$$

$$E_{true} = 8.61 \, mV + 1.23 \, mV = 9.84 \, mV$$

$$T_{hot} = \frac{9.84 \, mV}{41 \, \mu V/°C} = \frac{9840 \, \mu V}{41 \, \mu V/°C} = 240\,°C$$

This is the **true measuring-junction temperature**, fully compensated.

---

### 3.3 Sensitivity Comparison Matrix (High-Yield KTU Table-Memory)

| Type | + Conductor | − Conductor | Sensitivity (μV/°C) | Range (°C) | Robotic Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **K** | NiCr (Chromel) | NiAl (Alumel) | **41** | −270 to 1370 | General-purpose motor/housing |
| **J** | Iron (Fe) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | **50** | −210 to 1200 | Plastic-extruder monitoring |
| **T** | Copper (Cu) | Constantan | **40** | −270 to 400 | Cryogenic robot coolers |
| **E** | NiCr | Constantan | **68** | −270 to 1000 | High-output lab instruments |
| **R** | Platinum (Pt) | Pt-13% Rh | **10** | −50 to 1768 | Precision furnace robotic arms |
| **S** | Pt | Pt-10% Rh | **10** | −50 to 1768 | Laboratory-grade assays |
| **B** | Pt-30% Rh | Pt-6% Rh | **9** | 0 to 1820 | High-temp casting lines |

> [!TIP]
> **Type K is the most commonly used in robotics** — memorize its 41 μV/°C sensitivity for KTU numerical problems.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Robotic Thermocouple Sensing Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Heat Source] --> B[Measuring Hot Junction]
    B --> C[Thermocouple Wires: Metal A and Metal B]
    C --> D[Extension or Compensating Lead Wires]
    D --> E[Cold Reference Junction at DAQ Terminal Block]
    E --> F[Low-Pass Noise Filter]
    F --> G[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    G --> H[ADC of Microcontroller]
    H --> I[Software Cold Junction Compensation]
    I --> J[Linearization via ITS-90 LUT]
    J --> K[Robot Decision Module: Trip or Continue]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Process Flow of EMF Generation & Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Two Dissimilar Metals Joined] --> stepB[Heat Applied to Hot Junction]
    stepB --> stepC[Seebeck Effect Activated]
    stepC --> stepD[Charge Diffusion Across Metals]
    stepD --> stepE[Net EMF E alpha times Delta T Generated]
    stepE --> stepF[Signal Routed to Cold Reference]
    stepF --> stepG[Reference Temperature Tref Measured]
    stepG --> stepH{CJC Algorithm Active?}
    stepH -- Yes --> stepI[Compensate Using ITS-90 Lookup]
    stepH -- No --> stepJ[Raw Voltage Only]
    stepI --> stepK[Linearization Curve Applied]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL[Final Hot Junction Temperature Output]
    stepL --> stepM[Robot Control Logic Updates]
```

### 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Source Vthermocouple] --> R1[Lead Resistance of Metal A]
    S1 --> R2[Lead Resistance of Metal B]
    R1 --> N1[Reference Node at DAQ Terminal]
    R2 --> N1
    N1 --> AMP[High CMRR Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MCU[Robot MCU: Compute and Act]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `stepA`, `R1`, `MCU`); no reserved keywords are used as node names; labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text without bold or HTML formatting.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State and explain the Seebeck Effect. How is it utilized in a thermocouple for robotic temperature sensing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The **Seebeck Effect** states that when two dissimilar metals are joined at one end (hot junction) and the other ends are kept at a different temperature (cold junction), a small DC EMF is generated that is proportional to the temperature difference.
- (1 mark) The **Seebeck Coefficient (α)** quantifies this conversion: $E = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$.
- (1 mark) In robotics, this EMF is amplified, digitized, and cold-junction compensated using ITS-90 tables to deliver accurate temperature feedback for **motor protection, battery management, and end-effector control**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*List any three laws of thermocouples. Why is cold-junction compensation necessary in robotic data-acquisition systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Law of Homogeneous Circuit** — no EMF in a single metal; **Law of Intermediate Metals** — third metal does not alter EMF if its junctions are isothermal; **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** — EMF is additive across temperature intervals.
- (1 mark) In DAQ boards, the reference junction is at ambient electronics temperature, **not 0 °C**.
- (1 mark) Without CJC, the inferred $T_{hot}$ will be erroneous by the magnitude of the reference temperature offset, leading to false thermal alarms in robotic safety systems.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choose either A or B)*

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **Type K thermocouple**. Discuss the role of **cold-junction compensation (CJC)** in obtaining the true hot-junction temperature. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A Type K thermocouple has a mean Seebeck coefficient of **41 μV/°C**. The hot junction is at an unknown temperature, the cold (reference) junction sits at **30 °C**, and the measured EMF is **8.61 mV**. Compute the true hot-junction temperature, with a clear stepwise CJC procedure. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to A(a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Construction (2 marks):**
   - *Positive leg:* NiCr (Chromel) — nickel-chromium alloy.
   - *Negative leg:* NiAl (Alumel) — nickel-aluminium alloy.
   - Wires joined at the **measuring (hot) junction**; free ends terminate at the cold/reference junction in the DAQ terminal block.
2. **Working (2 marks):**
   - Heating the hot junction creates a thermal gradient.
   - Due to differing Seebeck coefficients of the two alloys, a net EMF of order $\mu$V/°C is generated.
   - Voltage is amplified, digitized, and linearized via ITS-90 tables.
3. **Cold-Junction Compensation (3 marks):**
   - The reference junction is at $T_{ref}$ (DAQ board temperature, typically 25–50 °C), not 0 °C.
   - Measured voltage: $E_{measured} = \alpha \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{ref})$.
   - True voltage: $E_{true} = E_{measured} + \alpha \cdot T_{ref}$.
   - True temperature: $T_{hot} = E_{true}/\alpha$.
   - Practical implementation: a **semiconductor temperature sensor** (e.g., LM35) measures $T_{ref}$ locally; the MCU adds the offset using the ITS-90 lookup.

---

**Solution to A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify $\bar{\alpha}_{K} = 41 \times 10^{-6} \; V/°C$, $T_{ref} = 30 °C$, $E_{measured} = 8.61 \times 10^{-3} \; V$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the compensation EMF for the reference.

$$E_{ref \to 0} = \bar{\alpha}_{K} \cdot T_{ref} = (41 \times 10^{-6}) \times 30 = 1230 \times 10^{-6} \; V = 1.23 \; mV$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the Law of Intermediate Temperatures to get the true EMF.

$$E_{true} = E_{measured} + E_{ref \to 0} = 8.61 \; mV + 1.23 \; mV = 9.84 \; mV$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for true hot-junction temperature.

$$T_{hot} = \frac{E_{true}}{\bar{\alpha}_{K}} = \frac{9.84 \times 10^{-3}}{41 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Perform the division.

$$T_{hot} = \frac{9840 \, \mu V}{41 \, \mu V/°C} = 240 \, °C$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Interpret: *The actual measuring-junction temperature is **240 °C**, not 210 °C, which would have been the naïve (uncompensated) reading. This 30 °C offset is precisely the reference-junction temperature that the CJC algorithm has corrected for.*

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Concluding remark for robotic relevance: *A 30 °C under-read in a BLDC motor winding would falsely pass a 240 °C hotspot as 210 °C, risking insulation failure. CJC is therefore a safety-critical step in robotic thermal management.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Question A*

**(a)** Explain the **three laws of thermocouples** with one engineering application of each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare the **construction, sensitivity, range, and robotic application** of at least **four standard thermocouple types** in a comparative matrix. Justify why **Type K is preferred** for general-purpose robotic temperature sensing. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to B(a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Law of Homogeneous Circuit (2 marks):**
   - *Statement:* No EMF can be generated in a circuit composed of a single homogeneous metal, even with a temperature gradient.
   - *Application:* Validates that **two distinct metals are essential** for thermocouple operation; explains why a pure copper loop with a temperature gradient reads 0 V.

2. **Law of Intermediate Metals (2 marks):**
   - *Statement:* Inserting a third metal into the circuit does not affect the net EMF, provided its two junctions are at the same temperature.
   - *Application:* Permits use of **copper lead wires, solder joints, and the voltmeter** between the thermocouple and DAQ without introducing measurement error.

3. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures (2 marks):**
   - *Statement:* The EMF of a thermocouple with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs for $T_1 \to T_2$ and $T_2 \to T_3$.
   - *Application:* Foundation of **Cold-Junction Compensation** — software can compute true EMF by adding the reference offset.

4. **Synthesis (1 mark):** Together, these three laws form the analytical backbone of any thermocouple-based temperature-sensing channel in robotics.

---

**Solution to B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Matrix (4 marks):**

| Type | + Leg / − Leg | Sensitivity (μV/°C) | Range (°C) | Robotic Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **K** | NiCr / NiAl | **41** | −270 to 1370 | General motor & battery thermal monitoring |
| **J** | Fe / Constantan | **50** | −210 to 1200 | Plastic-processing robotic cells |
| **T** | Cu / Constantan | **40** | −270 to 400 | Cryogenic gripper cooling |
| **E** | NiCr / Constantan | **68** | −270 to 1000 | Lab-grade analytical instruments |

**Justification for Type K Preference (3 marks):**

1. **Wide operating range** (up to 1370 °C) covers virtually all robotic thermal scenarios — motor windings, battery packs, weld tips, and food-handling grippers.
2. **Good sensitivity** (41 μV/°C) provides adequate signal-to-noise ratio for typical robotic ADC resolutions.
3. **Cost-effectiveness, oxidation resistance, and standardization** make Type K the de-facto choice for OEM robotic platforms, with readily available ITS-90 calibration tables.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Skipping the CJC step in numerical problems** — marks lost on the *compensation term*; examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Writing `|` (vertical bar) inside table cells** breaks markdown — examiners may deduct for non-renderable answer scripts.
> 3. **Confusing Seebeck with Peltier** — Seebeck is temperature→EMF; Peltier is current→heat. This is a frequent *2-mark killer* in Part A.
> 4. **Forgetting the Law of Intermediate Metals** when justifying copper lead wires — examiners specifically look for this in 7-mark answers.
> 5. **Using °K (Kelvin symbol) instead of K** — a small but recurring presentation penalty in KTU scripts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **thermocouple** converts a temperature gradient into a small DC voltage via the **Seebeck Effect**.
- ✅ The **Seebeck Coefficient α** (μV/°C) is unique to each thermocouple *type* (combination of metals).
- ✅ **Three Laws of Thermocouples:** Homogeneous Circuit, Intermediate Metals, Intermediate Temperatures.
- ✅ **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** is mandatory in all real robotic DAQ systems because the reference terminal is never at 0 °C.
- ✅ **Type K** is the *industry and robotics default* — memorize its 41 μV/°C sensitivity and 1370 °C ceiling.
- ✅ The governing linearized equation is: $E = \alpha \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$.
- ✅ **ITS-90** is the standard reference table for converting EMF → temperature.
- ✅ **Law of Intermediate Metals** permits use of connectors, copper wires, and the voltmeter without EMF corruption.
- ✅ **Polarity matters** in thermocouples: reversing legs inverts the voltage sign.
- ✅ **Advantages** in robotics: rugged, wide range, fast response, inexpensive, self-powered.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** low output (μV), nonlinear, requires CJC, susceptible to electromagnetic interference in robot EMI-rich environments.
- ✅ **Robotic-specific use cases:** motor winding thermal trip, battery pack safety, weld-tip feedback, end-effector safety interlocks, planetary rover skin monitoring.
- ✅ In **KTU 14-mark problems**, always show: (i) CJC offset, (ii) compensated EMF, (iii) division by α, (iv) interpretation.
- ✅ In **KTU 3-mark answers**, always define both *Seebeck Effect* and *CJC* — the examiner expects two crisp definitions, not a single long paragraph.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoder Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Encoder Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoder Sensor:** An electro-mechanical transducer that converts the **angular position** (rotary encoder) or **linear displacement** (linear encoder) of a shaft/slider into an electrical signal — typically a train of digital pulses or a unique multi-bit code word — that can be interpreted by a robot controller to determine position, speed, and direction of motion.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OERAT612 – Basics of Robot Sensing)**, an encoder is classified as an **internal-state (proprioceptive) position sensor**. It is *the* workhorse feedback device inside the joint servo-loop of almost every industrial manipulator, mobile robot wheel, and CNC machine because it provides **high resolution (often > 10,000 counts/rev)**, **non-contacting operation**, and **digital output** that is intrinsically immune to analog drift.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Car's Odometer:**
> Imagine the small rolling digit-counter in an old car. Every time the wheel completes one revolution, a mechanical pin pushes the rightmost digit by 1. After 10 revolutions, it rolls over to the next digit. A rotary encoder does *exactly the same thing*, except instead of a mechanical pin, a beam of light is interrupted (or a magnet's field is sensed) once per slit on a disc — and instead of visible digits, the controller receives electrical pulses it can count.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Computer Mouse Scroll Wheel:**
> Your mouse's scroll wheel spins a slotted disc. An LED shines through the slots onto a photodiode. Every slot passing the LED = one "tick" the computer registers. That is essentially an **incremental optical encoder**.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

Robot encoders fall along **two orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|------|------------|
| **Output Type** | Incremental (relative) **vs.** Absolute |
| **Transduction Principle** | Optical **vs.** Magnetic **vs.** Capacitive **vs.** Inductive (LVDT/RVDT style) |

The two most-important KTU high-yield categories are:

- **Incremental Encoder** — produces a continuous stream of pulses; position is obtained by *counting from a known reference (home)*.
- **Absolute Encoder** — produces a *unique binary (or Gray) code word for every shaft angle*, so position is known **immediately after power-up** without homing.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU One-Liner to Memorize:** *Incremental = count pulses, need homing. Absolute = read code, no homing needed.*

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded Physical Constants / Parameters)

- **CPR — Counts Per Revolution** of the disc. Typical industrial range: **100 to 10,000 CPR**.
- **PPR — Pulses Per Revolution** = CPR for a single channel (often 1×, 2×, or **4× quadrature decoding**).
- **Resolution** $\theta_{min}$ = **360° / N**, where N = effective counts after quadrature.
- **Maximum Frequency** $f_{max}$ (often **20 kHz – 1 MHz**) sets the upper speed limit.
- **Operating Temperature** industrial range: **−10 °C to +70 °C**.
- **Supply Voltage**: standard **5 V DC** (TTL) or **24 V DC** (HTL).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Optical Incremental Encoder (Most Common)

The optical encoder is the **dominant type in robotics**; understanding it unlocks all others.

**Structured Logic Steps:**

1. **Light Source:** An LED (typically infrared, $\lambda \approx$ **880 nm**) emits a steady collimated beam.
2. **Code Disc:** A glass or plastic disc attached to the shaft has **N opaque lines** (slats) photolithographically etched, alternating with transparent gaps.
3. **Slotted Mask (Collimating Slit):** A stationary mask with a single radial slit ensures only one disc line is being read at a time — this *sharpens the pulse edges* and is the secret to high resolution.
4. **Photodetector:** A photodiode or phototransistor receives the chopped light and converts it into a square-wave voltage.
5. **Output Stage:** A comparator (Schmitt trigger) cleans the signal to **5 V TTL levels**.
6. **Dual-Channel Layout:** A *second* LED-detector pair is offset by **¼ of a slat pitch (90° electrical)** to produce channel B. This offset is what gives **direction discrimination** (Section 2.3).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two channels?** A single channel tells you *how much* the shaft moved. Two channels in quadrature tell you *how much AND which direction*.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Absolute Optical Encoder

Instead of slits, the disc has **concentric tracks**. Track 0 is the MSB (coarsest, one transparent/one opaque sector); Track $n-1$ is the LSB (finest, many sectors).

- A **binary-coded disc** can produce ambiguous readings at sector boundaries (multiple bits change simultaneously → glitches).
- Therefore, KTU-standard absolute encoders use the **Gray code**, in which **only ONE bit changes between adjacent sectors**. The controller decodes Gray → binary in software (Section 3.4).

## 2.3 Quadrature Decoding — The Heart of Direction Sensing

When channel A leads channel B, rotation is **CW**; when B leads A, rotation is **CCW**. Quadrature decoding multiplies the effective resolution by **4×** by counting edges:

- **1× mode:** count rising edges of A only → **CPR = N**.
- **2× mode:** count rising + falling edges of A → **2 × CPR**.
- **4× mode:** count all four edges (A↑, A↓, B↑, B↓) → **4 × CPR**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Angular Resolution | $\theta_{min} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{eff}}$ | degrees | $N_{eff}$ = effective counts per rev |
| 2 | Linear Resolution | $d_{min} = \dfrac{\pi D}{N_{eff}}$ | mm | D = wheel/screw pitch diameter |
| 3 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{\Delta C \cdot \theta_{min}}{\Delta t}$ | rad/s | $\Delta C$ = counts in $\Delta t$ |
| 4 | Linear Velocity | $v = \omega \cdot r = \dfrac{\Delta C \cdot \pi D}{N_{eff} \cdot \Delta t}$ | m/s | r = D/2 |
| 5 | Max Shaft Speed | $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{2\pi f_{max}}{N_{eff}}$ | rad/s | $f_{max}$ = encoder bandwidth |
| 6 | Effective Counts | $N_{eff} = k \cdot CPR$ | counts/rev | k = 1, 2, or 4 (quadrature) |
| 7 | Gray → Binary MSB | $b_{n-1} = g_{n-1}$ | — | Copy most significant bit |
| 8 | Gray → Binary (general) | $b_{i} = b_{i+1} \oplus g_{i}$ | — | XOR current gray bit with already-decoded MSB |
| 9 | Static Accuracy (incremental) | $\epsilon \approx \pm \dfrac{1}{2}\theta_{min}$ | degrees | Quantisation limit |
| 10 | Output Frequency | $f_{out} = \dfrac{CPR \cdot \omega}{60}$ | Hz | $\omega$ in RPM |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Robots (FANUC, KUKA, ABB):** each joint has an *incremental encoder on the motor* and often an *absolute encoder on the link side* (dual-loop control).
- **AGVs / Mobile Robots:** wheel odometry uses encoders to estimate pose $(x, y, \theta)$ via dead-reckoning.
- **3D Printers / CNC:** lead-screw encoders close the position loop — required by KTU control-systems modules.
- **Servo Motors:** modern BLDC servos integrate the encoder *inside* the motor housing (e.g., 23-bit absolute, 8 million counts/rev).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Angular Resolution from CPR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Show that angular resolution $\theta_{min}$ is inversely proportional to the number of counts per revolution.

**Step 1 — State the definition.**
A full mechanical revolution subtends exactly **$360°$** (or $2\pi$ rad).

**Step 2 — Distribute the full revolution among all counts.**
If the encoder produces $N_{eff}$ equally spaced counts per revolution, each count represents an equal angular slice. The size of one slice is therefore the *total* angle divided by the *number of slices*:

$$
\theta_{min} = \frac{360°}{N_{eff}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to radians (KTU requires both units in derivations).**

$$
\theta_{min} = \frac{2\pi}{N_{eff}} \;\; \text{rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply quadrature multiplication.**

$$
N_{eff} = k \cdot CPR, \quad k \in \{1,\, 2,\, 4\}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{min} = \frac{360°}{k \cdot CPR}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Velocity from Pulse Counts

**Step 1 — Counts observed in a sampling window.**
Let the controller read $\Delta C$ counts during a sampling interval $\Delta t$.

**Step 2 — Convert counts to angle.**

$$
\Delta \theta = \Delta C \cdot \theta_{min} = \Delta C \cdot \frac{360°}{N_{eff}}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by time to obtain angular velocity.**

$$
\omega = \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t} = \frac{\Delta C \cdot 360°}{N_{eff} \cdot \Delta t}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to rad/s.**

$$
\boxed{\omega = \frac{2\pi \, \Delta C}{N_{eff} \cdot \Delta t} \;\; \text{rad/s}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to RPM (often required in KTU numericals).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{RPM} &= \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{60 \cdot \Delta C}{N_{eff} \cdot \Delta t}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Mobile Robot Wheel Odometry

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Question:** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheels of diameter $D = 0.20$ m. Each wheel carries an incremental encoder of $CPR = 500$, decoded in **4× quadrature**. The left wheel counts $C_L = 1240$ and the right wheel counts $C_R = 1280$ in a sampling interval $\Delta t = 0.1$ s. Find (a) the linear velocity of each wheel, (b) the robot's linear velocity $v$, (c) the robot's angular velocity $\omega_{robot}$, and (d) the distance travelled in 5 seconds.

**Step 1 — Effective counts per revolution.**

$$
N_{eff} = k \cdot CPR = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \;\; \text{counts/rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear resolution per count.**

$$
d_{min} = \frac{\pi D}{N_{eff}} = \frac{\pi \times 0.20}{2000} = \frac{0.6283}{2000} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m/count}
$$

This is exactly $\pi / 10000$ m — note that this is the *same* as the wheel circumference divided by 2000.

**Step 3 — Velocity of left wheel.**

$$
v_L = \frac{C_L \cdot d_{min}}{\Delta t} = \frac{1240 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}{0.1} = \frac{0.3896}{0.1} = 3.896 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity of right wheel.**

$$
v_R = \frac{C_R \cdot d_{min}}{\Delta t} = \frac{1280 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}{0.1} = \frac{0.4021}{0.1} = 4.021 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Robot linear velocity (mid-point of wheelbase).**

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{3.896 + 4.021}{2} = 3.9585 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Robot angular velocity.** Let wheelbase $L$ be given as $L = 0.50$ m.

$$
\omega_{robot} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{4.021 - 3.896}{0.50} = \frac{0.125}{0.50} = 0.25 \;\; \text{rad/s} \;\; (\text{CCW})
$$

The sign (positive) tells us the robot is turning to its *left* because the right wheel is faster.

**Step 7 — Distance in 5 s.**

$$
s = v \cdot t = 3.9585 \times 5 = 19.79 \;\; \text{m}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Gray Code to Binary Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Show that a position read as a Gray word $g_{n-1}g_{n-2}\dots g_1 g_0$ can be unambiguously converted to binary $b_{n-1}b_{n-2}\dots b_1 b_0$.

**Step 1 — MSB rule.**
In Gray code, the most-significant bit is identical to the MSB of the binary code:

$$
b_{n-1} = g_{n-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Recursive XOR rule.**
Every subsequent binary bit is the XOR of the previous (already-decoded) binary bit and the current Gray bit:

$$
b_i = b_{i+1} \oplus g_i, \quad i = n-2, n-3, \dots, 0
$$

**Step 3 — Worked 4-bit example.** Suppose the disc gives Gray word $g = 1010$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_3 &= g_3 = 1 \\
b_2 &= b_3 \oplus g_2 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
b_1 &= b_2 \oplus g_1 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
b_0 &= b_1 \oplus g_0 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So the Gray word **1010** corresponds to the binary word **1100**, which is decimal **12**. The decoder correctly maps the disc angle to position number 12, *with no ambiguous intermediate readings* because at every transition only one bit changes in Gray space.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Encoder Types (Exam Quick-Reference)

| Property | Incremental Optical | Absolute Optical | Magnetic | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output after power-off | Lost → must home | Retained | Retained (if abs.) | Retained (if abs.) |
| Resolution | Very high (4× CPR) | Limited by tracks (≤ 13-bit typical) | Medium | Medium |
| Robustness to dust/oil | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Cost | Low–Medium | High | Medium | Low |
| Typical use | Motor feedback | Robot joint absolute | Harsh environment | Consumer / hobby |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Optical Incremental Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["LED Source A"]
    srcB["LED Source B"]
    mask["Collimating Slit Mask"]
    disc["Rotating Code Disc N Slits"]
    pdA["Photodiode A"]
    pdB["Photodiode B"]
    cmpA["Comparator Schmitt A"]
    cmpB["Comparator Schmitt B"]
    outA["Channel A Square Wave"]
    outB["Channel B Square Wave 90 deg Shifted"]
    ctrl["Robot Controller Quadrature Decoder"]

    srcA --> mask
    srcB --> mask
    mask --> disc
    disc --> pdA
    disc --> pdB
    pdA --> cmpA
    pdB --> cmpB
    cmpA --> outA
    cmpB --> outB
    outA --> ctrl
    outB --> ctrl
```

**Reading the diagram:** Light from two LEDs is collimated, chopped by the rotating disc (slits), and detected by photodiodes. The comparators square-up the signals. Channel B is mechanically offset by 90° on the disc, so the controller receives two square waves that are 90° out of phase. The decoder extracts (a) position by counting edges and (b) direction from the phase order.

## 4.2 Quadrature Timing — Direction Discrimination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["State A is 1 B is 0"]
    s2["State A is 1 B is 1"]
    s3["State A is 0 B is 1"]
    s4["State A is 0 B is 0"]
    s1 -->|CW rotation| s2
    s2 -->|CW rotation| s3
    s3 -->|CW rotation| s4
    s4 -->|CW rotation| s1
    s1 -->|CCW rotation| s4
    s4 -->|CCW rotation| s3
    s3 -->|CCW rotation| s2
    s2 -->|CCW rotation| s1
```

**Interpretation:** The decoder walks the state machine clockwise for one rotation direction and counter-clockwise for the opposite direction — exactly the same state graph used in KTU microcontroller quadrature-counter peripherals (e.g., STM32 TIM encoder mode, Arduino `attachInterrupt` pairs).

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Encoders

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    enc["Robot Encoders"]
    enc --> inc["Incremental Relative"]
    enc --> abs["Absolute"]

    inc --> optI["Optical"]
    inc --> magI["Magnetic Hall Effect"]
    inc --> capI["Capacitive"]

    abs --> optA["Optical Gray Code"]
    abs --> magA["Magnetic Multi Pole"]
    abs --> resA["Resolvers AC"]

    optI --> qd["Quadrature 1x 2x 4x"]
    optI --> idx["With Index Z Pulse Once per Rev"]

    optA --> nbit["N bit Parallel Output"]
    optA --> ser["Serial SSI BiSS EnDat"]
```

## 4.4 Gray-Code-to-Binary Decoder (Functional Blocks)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g3["g3 MSB"] --> b3["b3 = g3"]
    b3 --> xor2["XOR Gate"]
    g2["g2"] --> xor2
    xor2 --> b2["b2"]
    b2 --> xor1["XOR Gate"]
    g1["g1"] --> xor1
    xor1 --> b1["b1"]
    b1 --> xor0["XOR Gate"]
    g0["g0"] --> xor0
    xor0 --> b0["b0 LSB"]
```

**How to read:** Each XOR gate takes the *already-decoded* higher-order binary bit and the *next* Gray bit. The cascade implements the recurrence $b_i = b_{i+1} \oplus g_i$ exactly as derived in Section 3.4. This is the hardware mirror of the software routine.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Mark Convention:** Part A is short-answer — definition + 1 supporting fact/diagram. Three crisp lines are ideal.

### Question A1 — Define Encoder. Differentiate between incremental and absolute encoders. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** An encoder is a digital position transducer that converts the angular or linear displacement of a shaft/slider into electrical pulses or a unique binary code.
2. **Incremental (1 mark):** Outputs a pulse train whose count is proportional to displacement; position is relative to a reference and is **lost on power-off** — re-homing is required.
3. **Absolute (1 mark):** Outputs a **unique N-bit code word for every shaft angle** (typically Gray-coded); the absolute position is **retained after power-off** without homing.

### Question A2 — What is quadrature decoding? Why is 4× decoding preferred in robotics? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Quadrature decoding uses two square waves (Channel A and Channel B) that are **90° electrically out of phase** to extract both *displacement* and *direction*.
2. **Direction sensing (1 mark):** If A leads B, rotation is CW; if B leads A, rotation is CCW — this is impossible with a single channel.
3. **4× advantage (1 mark):** Counting **all four edges** (rising/falling of A and B) **quadruples** the effective resolution: $N_{eff} = 4 \times CPR$, allowing slower shaft speeds without losing low-speed accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Convention:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided *between* two full 14-mark questions.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **optical incremental encoder**. Define CPR, PPR, and quadrature decoding.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Use the diagram from Section 4.1 — LED → slit → disc → photodiode → comparator → channel A/B output.
2. **Construction (2 marks):** Glass/plastic disc with N etched radial lines, two IR LED + photodiode pairs, stationary collimating mask, comparator output stage. Channels A and B are spatially offset by **¼ slit pitch (90° electrical)**.
3. **CPR vs PPR (1 mark):** *Counts per revolution* = total transitions (edges) per revolution in 4× mode; *pulses per revolution* = number of full pulse cycles per revolution (one cycle = one high + one low). For a single-channel incremental encoder **PPR = CPR/4** in 4× decoding.
4. **Quadrature decoding (2 marks):** State the three modes (1×, 2×, 4×); tabulate the four states (A,B) = (1,0), (1,1), (0,1), (0,0); explain that the *order* of state transitions determines direction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An incremental encoder has **CPR = 1024**, decoded in **4× quadrature**. It is mounted on the lead screw (pitch = 5 mm) of a CNC axis. Compute (i) the linear resolution per count, (ii) the maximum linear travel speed if the encoder bandwidth is **100 kHz**, (iii) the angle rotated when the count register reads **4500**.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Effective counts (1 mark):**

$$
N_{eff} = 4 \times 1024 = 4096 \;\; \text{counts/rev}
$$

2. **Linear resolution per revolution (1 mark):** Lead-screw pitch $p = 5$ mm/rev.

$$
d_{min} = \frac{p}{N_{eff}} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{4096} = 1.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm/count} \approx 1.22 \;\; \mu\text{m/count}
$$

3. **Maximum linear speed (3 marks):** The encoder produces $f_{max} = 100{,}000$ counts/sec. Since 4096 counts = 5 mm:

$$
v_{max} = f_{max} \times d_{min} = 100{,}000 \times 1.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm/s} = 122.1 \;\; \text{mm/s}
$$

4. **Angle for 4500 counts (2 marks):**

$$
\theta = 4500 \times \theta_{min} = 4500 \times \frac{360°}{4096} = 4500 \times 0.08789° = 395.5°
$$

Equivalently, **395.5° − 360° = 35.5°** past one full revolution (the screw has advanced 1 full pitch + a further 1.22 µm × 500 counts ≈ 0.61 mm).

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction of an **absolute optical encoder** using **Gray code**. Why is Gray code preferred over straight binary code for the disc pattern?

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):** The disc has **N concentric tracks**, each with a different angular pattern of transparent and opaque sectors. Track 0 is the MSB (only one transition per rev — coarse), Track $n-1$ is the LSB (finest, $2^{n-1}$ transitions per rev). A separate LED + photodetector is placed on each track. The parallel outputs form an N-bit word read by the controller.
2. **Gray code rule (2 marks):** In Gray code, **exactly ONE bit changes** between any two adjacent sectors, whereas in straight binary up to **n bits can change simultaneously** at sector boundaries (e.g., 0111 → 1000 changes 4 bits at once).
3. **Why Gray wins (2 marks):** Because of finite LED/photodiode alignment tolerance, when the shaft sits *on* a sector boundary the photodetectors may not all switch simultaneously → straight binary can produce *any* of $2^k$ spurious intermediate codes for k changing bits, causing huge position errors. Gray code limits the ambiguity to **±1 LSB**, which is trivial to correct.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mobile robot's drive wheel (diameter **15 cm**) carries a **500-CPR** quadrature-decoded encoder. In a **0.05 s** interval the controller counts **250 pulses**. Calculate (i) the wheel's linear velocity, (ii) the angular velocity in rad/s, (iii) the linear distance covered in **3 seconds**.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Effective counts per revolution (1 mark):**

$$
N_{eff} = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \;\; \text{counts/rev}
$$

2. **Linear resolution per count (1 mark):**

$$
d_{min} = \frac{\pi D}{N_{eff}} = \frac{\pi \times 0.15}{2000} = 2.356 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m/count} \;\; (\approx 0.236 \text{ mm})
$$

3. **Linear velocity (2 marks):**

$$
v = \frac{\Delta C \cdot d_{min}}{\Delta t} = \frac{250 \times 2.356 \times 10^{-4}}{0.05} = \frac{0.0589}{0.05} = 1.178 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

4. **Angular velocity (2 marks):**

$$
\omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{1.178}{0.075} = 15.71 \;\; \text{rad/s}
$$

Cross-check: $\omega = 2\pi \Delta C / (N_{eff} \Delta t) = 2\pi \times 250 / (2000 \times 0.05) = 1570.8 / 100 = 15.71$ rad/s ✓

5. **Distance in 3 seconds (1 mark):**

$$
s = v \cdot t = 1.178 \times 3 = 3.534 \;\; \text{m}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ×4 in quadrature.** Always write $N_{eff} = 4 \times CPR$ *before* plugging into any formula. Half the cohort forgets this and loses 1–2 marks per sub-question.
> 2. **Using $D$ instead of $r$.** The formula $v = \omega r$ uses *radius*, but $d_{min} = \pi D / N_{eff}$ uses *diameter*. Mixing these is the #1 numerical error.
> 3. **Stating "absolute encoder needs no homing" without qualifying.** Examiners want the reason: it produces a unique code per angle so the controller knows position immediately on power-up.
> 4. **Drawing a Gray-code disc without labelling tracks.** Always label MSB (coarsest) and LSB (finest) tracks in any diagram — a 1-mark deduction otherwise.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final numerical answer.** KTU answer keys always carry units; missing units ⇒ −½ mark per sub-part even if the number is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read before entering the exam hall)**

- ✅ **Encoder** = electromechanical position-to-electrical-signal transducer.
- ✅ **Incremental** → pulse train → needs homing → lost on power-off.
- ✅ **Absolute** → unique N-bit Gray code → no homing → survives power-off.
- ✅ **Optical** is most accurate; **Magnetic** is most rugged; **Capacitive** is cheapest.
- ✅ **CPR** = counts/rev (after 4× decoding, multiply by 4).
- ✅ **Angular resolution** $\theta_{min} = 360° / N_{eff}$.
- ✅ **Linear resolution** $d_{min} = \pi D / N_{eff}$.
- ✅ **Velocity** $v = \Delta C \cdot d_{min} / \Delta t$ — the universal KTU formula.
- ✅ **Direction** determined by **phase order** of A and B; A-leads-B = CW.
- ✅ **4× quadrature** multiplies resolution by 4 and gives direction.
- ✅ **Gray code** ensures only **1 bit changes** per sector transition → no spurious codes at boundaries.
- ✅ **Gray → Binary**: MSB copies; every other bit is XOR of the decoded MSB above and the Gray bit at that position.
- ✅ **Max shaft speed** $\omega_{max} = 2\pi f_{max} / N_{eff}$ — choose CPR so this exceeds the application speed.
- ✅ **Index (Z) pulse** = once-per-revolution reference for homing an incremental encoder.
- ✅ **Mobile robot odometry** uses the dual formula: $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.
- ✅ Always carry **units** in every numerical step — KTU penalises unit-less final answers.
- ✅ Draw the **block diagram of optical encoder** from memory (LED → mask → disc → photodiode → comparator) — it appears in almost every KTU exam under this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** LVDT # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of LVDT ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive transducer (electromechanical sensor) that converts **linear displacement** (position) of a movable ferromagnetic core into a corresponding **differential AC voltage output**. It operates on the principle of mutual inductance variation between a primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings. The LVDT is a **contactless, frictionless** displacement sensor widely used in robotics for: - End-effector position feedback - Joint displacement measurement - Proximity and vibration sensing - Force/pressure estimation (via auxiliary mechanisms) > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** LVDT is categorized under **passive transducers** because it requires an **external AC excitation** (carrier signal) to function. It does not generate electricity on its own. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English) Imagine a **seesaw** balanced on a triangular pivot (fulcrum). The seesaw has two baskets on each end. - When you place a **heavy ball in the left basket**, the left side goes down, the right side goes up. - The **tilt angle** of the seesaw tells you *how much* and *which side* the ball is on. - If the ball is exactly in the center, the seesaw is level — no tilt, no output. **Mapping to LVDT:** | Seesaw Analogy | LVDT Component | |----------------|----------------| | Heavy ball | Movable ferromagnetic core | | Two baskets | Two secondary coils (S₁ and S₂) | | Pivot / Fulcrum | Center tap of secondary | | Tilt angle | Differential output voltage (E_s1 − E_s2) | | Person pushing the ball | Linear displacement to be measured | So, as the core moves left, the left secondary's voltage increases and the right decreases — the **difference** between them gives you both the **magnitude** AND the **direction** of displacement. Brilliant, isn't it? --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Operating Parameters of a Standard Industrial LVDT:** > - **Excitation Frequency:** Typically **1 kHz to 10 kHz** (commonly 5 kHz) > - **Excitation Voltage:** Typically **1 V to 24 V RMS** > - **Operating Range:** ±0.005 inches to ±20 inches > - **Linearity:** Up to **±0.25%** of full range (high-grade LVDTs) > - **Resolution:** Essentially **infinite** (limited only by readout electronics) > - **Output:** **Differential AC voltage** (must be demodulated to DC) --- ## 1.4 Block Overview of LVDT System in a Robot ``` [Physical Displacement] → [LVDT Sensor] → [AC Output Signal] → [Phase-Sensitive Demodulator / Signal Conditioner] → [DC Voltage] → [Robot Controller / ADC] ``` > [!NOTE] > The LVDT alone produces an **AC signal**. A **phase-sensitive detector (PSD)** is mandatory to convert this into a **bipolar DC voltage** (positive for one direction, negative for the other). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction of LVDT (Structural Anatomy) The LVDT consists of **three main components** arranged coaxially: 1. **Primary Winding (P):** A single coil wound on a cylindrical former, located at the **center** of the assembly. It is energized by an **AC excitation voltage (E_p)**. 2. **Two Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂):** Identical coils wound on the same former, placed **symmetrically on either side** of the primary. They are connected in **series opposition** (anti-series). 3. **Movable Ferromagnetic Core (Armature):** A high-permeability nickel-iron core that slides inside the hollow former. Its position determines the magnetic coupling between P and the secondaries. > [!NOTE] > The core is **NOT physically attached** to any winding — it moves freely inside a non-contacting bore. This makes the LVDT **frictionless and wear-free**. --- ## 2.2 Working Principle — The 'Why' and 'How' The LVDT works on the principle of **mutual inductance variation**. Let's break it down step by step: - **Step 1 — Magnetic Flux Generation:** When AC excitation is applied to primary P, an alternating magnetic flux is established in the core and surrounding space. - **Step 2 — Coupling to Secondaries:** This flux links with S₁ and S₂ by mutual induction, inducing EMFs **E_s1** and **E_s2** respectively. - **Step 3 — Core Position Effect:** The position of the movable core changes the **reluctance** of the magnetic path for each secondary: - Core moves **toward S₁** → coupling with S₁ increases → **E_s1 increases**, E_s2 decreases. - Core moves **toward S₂** → coupling with S₂ increases → **E_s2 increases**, E_s1 decreases. - Core at **null (center) position** → **E_s1 = E_s2** → differential output = 0. - **Step 4 — Differential Output:** Because S₁ and S₂ are connected in **series opposition**, the net output is: $$E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$ - **Step 5 — Direction Detection:** The **phase** of E_o relative to E_p indicates displacement direction (after PSD processing). --- ## 2.3 Phases of LVDT Operation ### Case 1: Core at Null Position (Center) - Core is equidistant from S₁ and S₂. - $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ - **Net output $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 0$** - Theoretically zero (practically a small residual "null voltage" exists due to imperfections). ### Case 2: Core Displaced Toward S₁ (Left) - Flux linkage with S₁ increases, with S₂ decreases. - $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ - $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} > 0$ (and in phase with $E_p$) ### Case 3: Core Displaced Toward S₂ (Right) - Flux linkage with S₂ increases, with S₁ decreases. - $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ - $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} < 0$ (and 180° out of phase with $E_p$) --- ## 2.4 Output Characteristics Curve The LVDT output $E_o$ is a **linear function** of core displacement $x$ within its working range: $$E_o = k \cdot x$$ where: - $k$ = sensitivity (V/mm or V/inch), a constant for a given LVDT and excitation - $x$ = displacement of core from null position (signed quantity) > [!NOTE] > The output is **linear over a wide range** (typically ±5 mm around null for a standard LVDT). Beyond this range, the curve flattens as the core fully couples with one secondary. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam-Ready Formula Table for LVDT** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $E_{s1} = M_1 \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ | EMF induced in secondary S₁ | $M_1$ = mutual inductance with S₁ | | 2 | $E_{s2} = M_2 \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ | EMF induced in secondary S₂ | $M_2$ = mutual inductance with S₂ | | 3 | $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ | Differential output (series-opposed) | Volts (V) | | 4 | $E_o = (M_1 - M_2)\dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ | Output in terms of mutual inductances | At null, $M_1 = M_2$ so $E_o = 0$ | | 5 | $E_o = k \cdot x$ | Linear output-displacement relation | $k$ in V/mm, $x$ in mm | | 6 | $k = \dfrac{E_o}{x}$ | Sensitivity of LVDT | V per unit displacement | | 7 | $\text{Sensitivity} \propto f$ | Sensitivity increases with excitation frequency | $f$ = excitation frequency | | 8 | $M = \dfrac{N_1 N_2 \mu A}{l}$ | Mutual inductance formula | $\mu$ = permeability of core, $A$ = area, $l$ = length | | 9 | $E_o = \dfrac{2 \pi f \cdot I_p \cdot \Delta M}{}$ | AC output magnitude | $f$ = frequency, $I_p$ = primary current | | 10 | $\%\text{ Linearity} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Linearity specification | $\Delta y_{max}$ = max deviation, $y_{FS}$ = full scale | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** Always use $\vert M_1 - M_2 \vert$ in table cells as $\vert M_1 - M_2 \vert$ — never the pipe symbol `|`, which breaks markdown table syntax. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering LVDTs are the **workhorse sensors** in many industrial and robotic applications: - **Robotics:** Joint angle/position feedback, gripper position sensing - **Aerospace:** Flight control surface position (actuators), landing gear position - **Hydraulics:** Piston position in hydraulic cylinders - **Nuclear Plants:** Valve position in high-radiation environments (since LVDT is radiation-tolerant) - **CNC Machines:** Tool position verification - **Civil Engineering:** Structural displacement and strain monitoring > [!NOTE] > The **infinite resolution** of LVDT (no friction → no stiction → no quantization noise from mechanical contact) makes it superior to potentiometers for high-precision robotic applications. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Output Voltage of LVDT ### Given Setup - Primary winding excited by AC voltage $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$ - Primary current: $I_p = \dfrac{E_p}{Z_p}$ where $Z_p$ is primary impedance - Mutual inductances with secondaries: $M_1$ and $M_2$ (both functions of core position $x$) - Secondaries connected in **series opposition** ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Express the induced EMFs in the two secondaries using Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction: $$e_{s1} = -M_1 \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$$ $$e_{s2} = -M_2 \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$$ The negative sign indicates Lenz's law (induced EMF opposes the change). For magnitude analysis, we drop the sign. **Step 2:** Compute the time derivative of primary current. Since $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t)$: $$\dfrac{di_p}{dt} = \omega I_p \cos(\omega t) = 2\pi f \cdot I_p \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the EMF expressions: $$e_{s1} = M_1 \cdot \omega I_p \cos(\omega t) = 2\pi f M_1 I_p \cos(\omega t)$$ $$e_{s2} = M_2 \cdot \omega I_p \cos(\omega t) = 2\pi f M_2 I_p \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4:** Since the secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, the net output voltage is the **algebraic difference** (subtraction): $$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$$ $$e_o = 2\pi f I_p \cos(\omega t) \cdot (M_1 - M_2)$$ **Step 5:** Define the differential mutual inductance: $$\Delta M = M_1 - M_2$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{e_o = 2\pi f \cdot I_p \cdot \Delta M \cdot \cos(\omega t)}$$ **Step 6:** The **peak (amplitude)** of the output is: $$E_o = 2\pi f \cdot I_p \cdot \vert M_1 - M_2 \vert$$ **Step 7:** At the **null position**, the core is centered, so $M_1 = M_2$, giving: $$\Delta M = 0 \implies E_o = 0 \quad \text{(verified)}$$ **Step 8:** For small displacements $x$ from null, $\Delta M$ is proportional to $x$ (linear region): $$\Delta M \approx \left(\dfrac{dM}{dx}\right)_0 \cdot x$$ **Step 9:** Substituting back: $$E_o = 2\pi f \cdot I_p \cdot \left(\dfrac{dM}{dx}\right)_0 \cdot x$$ **Step 10:** Define sensitivity constant $k$: $$k = 2\pi f \cdot I_p \cdot \left(\dfrac{dM}{dx}\right)_0$$ **Step 11:** Final linear output equation: $$\boxed{E_o = k \cdot x}$$ This is the **working equation of LVDT** in the linear region. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Mutual Inductance in Terms of Core Position For a simple magnetic circuit, the mutual inductance between primary and a secondary depends on the **overlap length** between the core and that secondary winding. **Step 1:** Let $l$ = total length of one secondary winding, $x$ = core displacement from null. **Step 2:** For secondary S₁ (left), the effective overlap is $l/2 + x$. For secondary S₂ (right), the effective overlap is $l/2 - x$. **Step 3:** The mutual inductances are proportional to overlap: $$M_1 = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{l}{2} + x\right)$$ $$M_2 = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{l}{2} - x\right)$$ where $K$ is a constant depending on core permeability and winding geometry. **Step 4:** Compute the difference: $$\Delta M = M_1 - M_2 = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{l}{2} + x\right) - K \cdot \left(\dfrac{l}{2} - x\right) = 2Kx$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$\Delta M = 2Kx$$ This confirms the **linearity** of the LVDT in the working range. **Step 6:** Output voltage becomes: $$E_o = 2\pi f \cdot I_p \cdot 2K \cdot x = 4\pi f K I_p \cdot x$$ Comparing with $E_o = kx$: $$k = 4\pi f K I_p$$ This shows sensitivity is **directly proportional to excitation frequency** and primary current. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** An LVDT has a sensitivity of $k = 0.5$ V/mm and is excited by a 5 kHz AC signal. The primary current is 10 mA. The core is displaced by 4 mm to the right of null. > **(a)** Find the output voltage. > **(b)** What is the direction of the output voltage relative to the primary? > **(c)** If the excitation frequency is doubled, what is the new sensitivity? ### Solution **Part (a): Output voltage** $$E_o = k \cdot x = 0.5 \text{ V/mm} \times 4 \text{ mm} = \boxed{2.0 \text{ V}}$$ **Part (b): Direction** The core moved to the **right** (toward S₂), so $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$, and $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ is **negative** in the standard convention. After phase-sensitive demodulation, this produces a **negative DC voltage**, signaling rightward displacement. In AC terms, $e_o$ is **180° out of phase** with the primary excitation $e_p$. **Part (c): New sensitivity with doubled frequency** Since $k \propto f$ (linear proportionality): $$k_{new} = 2 \times k_{old} = 2 \times 0.5 = \boxed{1.0 \text{ V/mm}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This shows why **higher excitation frequency** is used in high-precision LVDTs — it boosts sensitivity without increasing the physical size of the device. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics of LVDT ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LVDT Internal Architecture and Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR ACsrc[AC EXCITATION SOURCE
5 to 10 kHz] --> PrimP[PRIMARY WINDING P
Center coil] PrimP -->|mutual induction| Sec1[SECONDARY S1
Left coil] PrimP -->|mutual induction| Sec2[SECONDARY S2
Right coil] Core{MOVABLE FERROMAGNETIC CORE
Position x determines coupling} -.->|alters coupling| PrimP Core -.->|alters coupling| Sec1 Core -.->|alters coupling| Sec2 Sec1 -->|Es1| DiffOp[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT
ES1 minus ES2] Sec2 -->|Es2| DiffOp DiffOp -->|AC differential voltage| PSD[PHASE SENSITIVE
DEMODULATOR] PSD -->|Bipolar DC| ADC[ADC AND CONTROLLER] ADC --> RobotOut[ROBOT POSITION
FEEDBACK] style ACsrc fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style PrimP fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style Sec1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style Sec2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style Core fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style DiffOp fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px style PSD fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px style ADC fill:#e8d5f2,stroke:#660099,stroke-width:2px style RobotOut fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — LVDT Operational States ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NullPos : Core centered NullPos : NULL POSITION NullPos : Output Eo equals 0 NullPos : M1 equals M2 NullPos --> LeftDisplace : Core moves toward S1 LeftDisplace : LEFT DISPLACEMENT LeftDisplace : Es1 greater than Es2 LeftDisplace : Eo is in phase with Ep LeftDisplace : DC output is POSITIVE LeftDisplace --> NullPos : Core returns to center NullPos --> RightDisplace : Core moves toward S2 RightDisplace : RIGHT DISPLACEMENT RightDisplace : Es2 greater than Es1 RightDisplace : Eo is 180 deg out of phase with Ep RightDisplace : DC output is NEGATIVE RightDisplace --> NullPos : Core returns to center LeftDisplace --> Saturation : Beyond linear range RightDisplace --> Saturation : Beyond linear range Saturation : SATURATION REGION Saturation : Output flattens Saturation : Not usable ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — LVDT Signal Conditioning Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD Input[Physical Linear Displacement x] --> LVDT1[LVDT SENSOR] LVDT1 -->|Raw AC Eo| Amp[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER
Gain stage] Amp -->|Amplified AC| Filter[ACTIVE BANDPASS FILTER
Removes noise harmonics] Filter -->|Clean AC| PSD1[PHASE SENSITIVE DETECTOR
Multiplies by reference] Osc[OSCILLATOR] -->|Reference sine| PSD1 PSD1 -->|Rectified bipolar| LPF[LOW PASS FILTER
Extracts DC envelope] LPF -->|Smooth DC voltage| Out[DC OUTPUT
Proportional to x with sign] style Input fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style LVDT1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style Amp fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px style Filter fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px style PSD1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px style Osc fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style LPF fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px style Out fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Component Identification Legend | Block | Real Component in Robot | |-------|-------------------------| | AC EXCITATION SOURCE | Function generator or oscillator IC (e.g., ICL8038) | | PRIMARY WINDING P | Center coil of LVDT bobbin | | SECONDARY S1, S2 | Symmetric end coils of LVDT bobbin | | MOVABLE CORE | Nickel-iron armature connected to robot joint | | DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT | Series-opposed connection at LVDT output leads | | PHASE SENSITIVE DEMODULATOR | AD598 / AD698 IC (industry standard) | | ADC AND CONTROLLER | Microcontroller / DSP in robot control unit | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define LVDT. List any four of its advantages.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear displacement into a proportional differential AC voltage output, using the principle of variation in mutual inductance between a primary winding and two secondary windings due to movement of a ferromagnetic core. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Infinite resolution** — being frictionless, it has no mechanical granularity. 2. **High sensitivity** — output is directly proportional to displacement over a wide linear range. 3. **No friction or hysteresis** — no mechanical contact between core and windings. 4. **Robust and reliable** — no moving electrical contacts, hence long operational life. 5. *(Additional)*: Bidirectional output — single sensor measures both magnitude and direction. 6. *(Additional)*: Can operate in harsh environments (high temperature, radiation, pressure). --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: What is the significance of connecting the two secondary windings of an LVDT in series opposition?** **Model Answer:** The two secondary windings of an LVDT are connected in **series opposition** (anti-series / subtractive polarity) so that the **induced EMFs subtract** from each other to produce a **differential output**: $$E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$ **Significance:** 1. **Null Position Detection:** When the core is at the center, $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$, giving $E_o = 0$. This serves as the natural zero-reference point. 2. **Direction Sensing:** The sign of $E_o$ (or its phase relative to the primary) indicates the direction of displacement — toward S₁ or S₂. 3. **Common-Mode Rejection:** External disturbances (temperature drift, common noise) affect both secondaries equally, and subtraction cancels them out, improving accuracy. 4. **Linearity:** The differential output is linear with core displacement in the working range. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Module internal choice is mandatory — students choose ONE of the two alternatives offered. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question] **Q: (a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an LVDT. (7 Marks)** **(b) An LVDT has a sensitivity of 0.4 V/mm and is excited by a 4 kHz sinusoidal source. The core is displaced by 6 mm. Calculate: (i) the output voltage, (ii) the output voltage if the excitation frequency is increased to 8 kHz, and (iii) comment on the linearity assumption if the displacement is increased to 15 mm. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Construction (4 marks):** The LVDT consists of: - A hollow cylindrical former (non-magnetic) - One **primary winding (P)** wound at the center - Two identical **secondary windings (S₁ and S₂)** wound symmetrically on either side of P - A movable **ferromagnetic core** (Ni-Fe alloy) that slides coaxially inside the former - The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** **Working (3 marks):** 1. Primary P is energized by AC excitation $E_p$, producing an alternating flux. 2. This flux links with S₁ and S₂, inducing EMFs $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ respectively. 3. When the core is at the **null position** (center), $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$, so net output is zero. 4. When the core moves toward S₁, flux coupling with S₁ increases → $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ → output is positive. 5. When the core moves toward S₂, flux coupling with S₂ increases → $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ → output is negative (180° phase shift). 6. The magnitude of the differential output is proportional to the displacement. **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the standard LVDT architecture. #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $k = 0.4$ V/mm, $f_1 = 4$ kHz, $x = 6$ mm **(i) Output voltage at 4 kHz:** $$E_o = k \cdot x = 0.4 \times 6 = \boxed{2.4 \text{ V}}$$ **(ii) Output voltage at 8 kHz:** Since $k \propto f$, the new sensitivity: $$k_2 = k_1 \times \dfrac{f_2}{f_1} = 0.4 \times \dfrac{8}{4} = 0.8 \text{ V/mm}$$ New output: $$E_{o,new} = 0.8 \times 6 = \boxed{4.8 \text{ V}}$$ **(iii) Comment on linearity at 15 mm:** At $x = 15$ mm, the displacement is likely **beyond the linear range** of a standard LVDT (typically ±5 mm to ±10 mm). The output will **saturate** and no longer obey $E_o = kx$. The relationship becomes **non-linear** (curve flattens) because the core is fully within one secondary winding, and further movement does not proportionally change the mutual inductance. Hence the linearity assumption fails. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question] **Q: (a) Derive the expression for the output voltage of an LVDT in terms of displacement. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 Marks)** **(b) Explain any four applications of LVDT in robotics with a brief description of each. Also draw the output characteristics curve showing the linear and non-linear regions. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Assumptions:** 1. The two secondaries are perfectly identical and symmetric. 2. Operating point is within the linear range (small displacement from null). 3. Excitation is a pure sinusoidal AC of constant amplitude and frequency. 4. Magnetic coupling is ideal with no leakage flux. 5. Core permeability is constant and high. **Derivation:** Let the primary excitation be $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$. Primary current: $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t)$, where $I_p = E_p / Z_p$. EMF induced in secondary S₁: $$e_{s1} = M_1 \dfrac{di_p}{dt} = M_1 \cdot \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)$$ EMF induced in secondary S₂: $$e_{s2} = M_2 \dfrac{di_p}{dt} = M_2 \cdot \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)$$ Differential output (series opposition): $$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = \omega I_p (M_1 - M_2) \cos(\omega t)$$ Define $\Delta M = M_1 - M_2$. For small displacement $x$ from null: $$\Delta M = 2Kx \quad (\text{where } K = \text{geometry constant})$$ Therefore: $$E_o = 2\pi f I_p \cdot 2Kx = 4\pi f K I_p \cdot x$$ Define sensitivity: $k = 4\pi f K I_p$ $$\boxed{E_o = k \cdot x}$$ #### Model Solution for (b): **Four Applications of LVDT in Robotics:** 1. **Joint Position Feedback in Robotic Arms:** LVDTs mounted at revolute joints measure the linear extension produced by a cam or linear actuator, providing precise angular position feedback for closed-loop servo control. 2. **Gripper / End-Effector Position Sensing:** LVDTs embedded in robotic grippers measure finger separation and clamping displacement, enabling force-controlled grasping of delicate objects. 3. **Pneumatic / Hydraulic Cylinder Position:** In hydraulic robot actuators, the LVDT measures piston displacement, enabling accurate velocity and position control of heavy robotic manipulators. 4. **Proximity and Collision Detection:** LVDTs mounted on the robot's skin or bumper detect approach distance to obstacles, providing analog feedback for collision-avoidance algorithms. 5. *(Bonus — Mobile Robots)* **Suspension Travel Monitoring:** In legged/wheeled mobile robots, LVDTs measure suspension compression, providing terrain-adaptive feedback. **Output Characteristics Curve:** ``` Eo | +Ve | / | / (Linear region) | / 0 |-----X------ (Null position) | \ | \ (Linear region) -Ve | \ |_________\_____________ x (displacement) -ve 0 +ve ⌐⌐⌐⌐⌐⌐⌐⌐⌐⌐⌐ (Saturation / non-linear) ``` - **Linear region:** roughly ±5 mm around null — output is proportional to displacement. - **Non-linear region (saturation):** beyond this range, output flattens. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in LVDT Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to mention "series opposition":** Many students describe the LVDT but forget to state that S₁ and S₂ are connected in *opposition* (anti-series). This is essential to justify the **differential** nature of the output. **Penalty: 1-2 marks.** > > 2. **Confusing "null voltage" with "null position":** Null position is where the core is at the center; null voltage is the small residual output (a few mV) at that position due to winding imperfections. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > > 3. **Skipping the phase-sensitive demodulator:** LVDT output is AC. If the question asks for "voltage proportional to displacement," you **must mention PSD/rectifier/ demodulator** to obtain DC. **Penalty: 1-2 marks.** > > 4. **Wrong formula for sensitivity proportionality:** Students often write $k \propto f$ but should be more precise: $k \propto f$ AND $k \propto I_p$ (primary current). > > 5. **Drawing mistakes:** The core should be shown *inside* the former, not outside. The secondaries should be on either side of the primary, not stacked. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > > 6. **Unit errors:** Sensitivity units are V/mm or V/inch — not V/m or V/cm unless specifically converted. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for LVDT (KTU Module 3)** - ☐ **LVDT** = Linear Variable Differential Transformer — a passive inductive displacement sensor. - ☐ **Type:** Passive transducer (requires external AC excitation). - ☐ **Principle:** Variation of **mutual inductance** between primary and two secondaries. - ☐ **Three key components:** Primary winding, two identical secondary windings, movable ferromagnetic core. - ☐ **Secondaries are connected in series opposition** → differential output $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$. - ☐ **Null position:** Core centered → $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ → $E_o = 0$. - ☐ **Direction of displacement** is encoded in the **phase** of the output relative to primary excitation. - ☐ **Working equation:** $E_o = k \cdot x$ (linear region). - ☐ **Sensitivity formula:** $k = 4\pi f K I_p$ — directly proportional to frequency $f$ and primary current $I_p$. - ☐ **AC output requires phase-sensitive demodulation (PSD)** to obtain bipolar DC for ADC input. - ☐ **Infinite resolution** — because there is no friction or contact. - ☐ **Advantages:** Frictionless, infinite resolution, bidirectional, robust, high linearity, long life. - ☐ **Disadvantages:** Requires AC excitation + signal conditioning; sensitive to external magnetic fields; limited dynamic response. - ☐ **Typical excitation:** 1–10 kHz, 1–24 V RMS. - ☐ **Key applications in robotics:** joint position feedback, gripper sensing, hydraulic piston position, proximity detection. - ☐ **Mutual inductance relation:** $M_1 - M_2 = 2Kx$ (linear with displacement within working range). - ☐ **Remember:** LVDT measures *linear* displacement only (the RVDT variant measures rotary displacement). - ☐ **Mathematical link:** $\Delta M \propto x$ → $E_o \propto x$ → output-displacement linearity. - ☐ **The phase-sensitive detector (PSD)** is what makes LVDT truly bidirectional. --- *End of Module 3 — LVDT Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | OERAT612 Basics of Robot Sensing*

topics.title: Accelerometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accelerometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accelerometer (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):** An accelerometer is a **dynamic electro-mechanical transducer** that measures **proper acceleration** — the rate of change of velocity of a body relative to a free-fall (inertial) reference frame, expressed along one or more orthogonal axes. The output is an analog or digital electrical signal proportional to the acceleration vector acting on the sensor's proof mass.

In the context of **robot sensing**, an accelerometer is a fundamental **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** sub-component that allows a robot to perceive its own motion, tilt, vibration, and shock without requiring external references. The measured quantity has SI units of **m/s²**, although the sensor output is often normalized in terms of the standard gravitational constant **g = 9.80665 m/s²**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** An accelerometer does **NOT** measure velocity, and it does **NOT** measure the coordinate acceleration in an arbitrary frame. It specifically measures *proper* (specific) acceleration, which is acceleration *relative to free-fall*. When the device is at rest on Earth, it reads approximately **1 g** along the vertical axis, not zero.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ball in a Box"

Imagine a small steel ball suspended inside a sealed box by a soft spring, with a sensor attached that measures how far the spring stretches:

- **When the box is still** on a table → the ball is pulled down by gravity, stretching the spring by a fixed amount. Sensor reads **1 g**.
- **When the box is pushed forward** → the ball lags behind due to inertia, the spring stretches *more* in the opposite direction. Sensor reads forward acceleration.
- **When the box is in free fall** → both box and ball fall together at the same rate, the spring is unstretched. Sensor reads **0 g**.

This "ball + spring + sensor" mental picture is the **foundation of every accelerometer ever built**, from the macroscopic seismograph to a silicon MEMS chip the size of a grain of sand.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard gravity | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| Acceleration SI unit | $a$ | **m/s²** |
| Frequency reference | $g$ (g-force) | 1 g = 9.80665 m/s² |
| Common range | $\pm a_{FS}$ | $\pm 2g, \pm 4g, \pm 8g, \pm 16g$ |

## 1.4 Classification Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping:** For Module 3, the student must be able to distinguish between the following accelerometer families used in robotics:

- **Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer** — dominant in modern robots and smartphones.
- **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** — used for high-frequency vibration sensing in industrial robots.
- **Piezoresistive Accelerometer** — used in high-shock applications (e.g., airbag deployment).
- **Servo (Force-Balance) Accelerometer** — high-precision laboratory / navigation grade.
- **Bulk Micro-machined vs. Surface Micro-machined** — manufacturing taxonomy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — Mass-Spring-Damper Model

Every accelerometer, regardless of its transduction principle, can be abstracted as a **single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) second-order mass-spring-damper system**. This is the *most important* theoretical concept for KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 The Three Constituents

1. **Proof Mass ($m$)** — a small inertial reference that resists acceleration via Newton's Second Law.
2. **Suspension Spring ($k$)** — supports the proof mass and provides a restoring force.
3. **Damper ($b$)** — introduces velocity-proportional viscous damping to control the dynamic response.

### 2.1.2 The Governing Differential Equation

Let $x(t)$ be the displacement of the **housing** (frame) of the accelerometer and $z(t)$ be the absolute displacement of the **proof mass** with respect to an inertial frame. The *relative* displacement of the mass with respect to the housing is:

$$y(t) = z(t) - x(t)$$

Applying Newton's Second Law to the proof mass, the forces acting on it are:
- The inertial reaction of the mass: $-m\ddot{z}$
- The spring restoring force: $-ky$
- The damping force: $-b\dot{y}$

The equation of motion is therefore:

$$m\ddot{z} + b\dot{y} + ky = 0$$

Substituting $z = x + y$ gives:

$$m\ddot{x} + m\ddot{y} + b\dot{y} + ky = 0$$

Rearranging for the input acceleration $\ddot{x}$ of the housing:

$$m\ddot{y} + b\dot{y} + ky = -m\ddot{x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The right-hand side $-m\ddot{x}$ is the inertial force per unit mass** — this is what the sensor ultimately transduces. The displacement $y$ of the proof mass relative to the housing is directly proportional to the acceleration being measured.

## 2.2 Transfer Function Derivation (Second-Order System)

Taking the Laplace transform of the equation of motion (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$ms^2 Y(s) + bs Y(s) + k Y(s) = -ms^2 X(s)$$

Factoring $Y(s)$:

$$Y(s)\,[ms^2 + bs + k] = -ms^2 X(s)$$

The transfer function from input acceleration $\ddot{x}(t)$ (or $s^2 X(s)$) to relative displacement $y$ is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{-ms^2}{ms^2 + bs + k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the negative sign?** The proof mass moves *opposite* to the acceleration of the housing due to inertia. The negative sign is often dropped in practice since most transducers (capacitive, piezoelectric) only care about magnitude.

Defining the **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ and **damping ratio** $\zeta$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad,\quad \zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

The transfer function becomes the canonical second-order form:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{s^2 X(s)} = \frac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams:** The student is expected to recognize that the accelerometer behaves as a **second-order low-pass filter** for the input acceleration. Below $\omega_n$, displacement is proportional to acceleration; above $\omega_n$, the response rolls off at $-40$ dB/decade.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — All Key Equations for the Accelerometer Topic**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law on mass | $F = ma$ | Force = mass × acceleration (N) |
| 2 | Spring force | $F_s = ky$ | Hooke's Law (N) |
| 3 | Damper force | $F_d = b\dot{y}$ | Viscous damping (N) |
| 4 | EOM (input accel) | $m\ddot{y} + b\dot{y} + ky = -m\ddot{x}$ | Governing ODE |
| 5 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Damped frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s (underdamped) |
| 8 | Static sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}$ | m per (m/s²) |
| 9 | Sensitivity (capacitive) | $S_C = \dfrac{\Delta C}{a} = \dfrac{2 C_0 \, y}{d \, a}$ | F·s²/m |
| 10 | Charge sensitivity (piezo) | $S_q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | C / (m/s²) |
| 11 | Frequency response | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ |
| 12 | Resolution | $a_{\min} = \dfrac{\sigma_{noise}}{S}$ | m/s² |
| 13 | Cross-axis sensitivity | $S_{xy} = \dfrac{a_{x,\text{measured when } a_y \text{ applied}}}{a_y}$ | dimensionless ratio |
| 14 | Bias stability | $a_b$ | m/s² (drift over time) |
| 15 | Sensor output voltage | $V_{out} = V_{ref} + S_V \cdot a$ | V (linear model) |

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Robotics

The accelerometer is the **backbone of modern robot localization, stabilization, and control**:

- **Tilt / Inclination sensing:** By measuring the gravity vector projected on each axis, a robot can compute roll and pitch angles (essential for humanoid balance, drone flight, and legged locomotion).
- **Vibration monitoring:** Industrial robotic arms use accelerometers for predictive maintenance — a sudden rise in high-frequency vibration indicates bearing wear.
- **Dead-reckoning / IMU fusion:** Combined with a gyroscope (and filtered via a Kalman filter), the accelerometer provides short-term translational estimates in GPS-denied environments.
- **Collision detection:** A sudden spike in acceleration along an unexpected axis triggers an emergency stop in collaborative robots (cobots).
- **End-effector force estimation:** Wrist-mounted accelerometers help reconstruct contact forces during assembly tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Accelerometer Transfer Function

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Diagram Setup

Consider a proof mass $m$ suspended inside the accelerometer housing by a spring of stiffness $k$ and a damper with damping coefficient $b$. The housing is subjected to an external acceleration $a(t) = \ddot{x}(t)$. The relative displacement of the mass with respect to the housing is $y(t)$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Algebraic Development

**Step 1 — Identify the absolute displacement of the mass.**

The absolute position of the mass in the inertial frame is the housing displacement plus the relative displacement:

$$z(t) = x(t) + y(t)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate twice to obtain absolute acceleration.**

$$\dot{z}(t) = \dot{x}(t) + \dot{y}(t)$$
