- **The position** of the ship by triangulating against multiple known lighthouses.

Now, replace the **light** with a **coded radio signal** and the **ship** with a **mobile robot**. That, in essence, is an RF beacon system.

| Lighthouse World | RF Beacon World |
|---|---|
| Pulsed light beam | Modulated RF carrier wave |
| Observer's eye | Antenna + RF receiver |
| Time of light travel | Time-of-Arrival (TOA) of radio wave |
| Light intensity attenuation | Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) |
| Bearing to lighthouse | Angle of Arrival (AOA) — optional |
| Two or more lighthouses | Trilateration / Multilateration |

## 1.3 Classification of RF Beacon Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Principal RF Beacon Ranging Methods** (must be memorized for KTU):

1. **TOA (Time of Arrival)** — Measures the absolute propagation time $t$ of the RF signal. Distance: $d = c \cdot t$, where $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
2. **TDOA (Time Difference of Arrival)** — Measures the *difference* in arrival times at synchronized receivers. Produces **hyperbolic** lines of position.
3. **RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator)** — Measures the power of the received signal and infers distance from the path-loss model.
4. **Phase-Shift / Phase Difference** — Measures the carrier phase offset caused by propagation. Provides high precision but is **ambiguous** for distances exceeding one wavelength.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Operating frequency bands:** $433 \text{ MHz}$ (ISM), $2.4 \text{ GHz}$ (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth), $5.8 \text{ GHz}$ (UNII), $1.575 \text{ GHz}$ (GPS L1).
- **Speed of RF wave in air:** $c \approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \; m/s}$ (practically $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s).
- **Wavelength relation:** $\lambda = c / f$. For $f = 2.4 \text{ GHz}$, $\lambda \approx 0.125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ cm}$.
- **Typical ranging accuracy:**
  - GPS (TOA): $\pm 3$ to $\pm 15$ meters (civilian).
  - UWB RF beacons: $\pm 10$ cm.
  - Wi-Fi RSSI: $\pm 2$ to $\pm 5$ meters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Block Logic of an RF Beacon Range Sensor

An RF beacon system can be modeled as a **signal chain** with the following logical stages:

- **Stage 1 — Beacon Transmission:** A known-position transmitter radiates a *coded* RF signal (e.g., unique ID, timestamp, frequency signature).
- **Stage 2 — Channel Propagation:** The signal travels through air, suffering **attenuation**, **multipath reflections**, **diffraction**, and **noise**.
- **Stage 3 — Reception:** The robot-mounted antenna captures the signal; a low-noise amplifier (LNA) boosts it.
- **Stage 4 — Feature Extraction:** A signal-processing block extracts the relevant parameter — RSSI voltage, arrival time, carrier phase, or code correlation peak.
- **Stage 5 — Range Computation:** A ranging algorithm converts the extracted feature into a distance value using a physical/mathematical model.
- **Stage 6 — Localization:** Multiple range measurements are fused via **multilateration** to obtain the 2-D or 3-D robot pose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the signal weaken with distance?** In free space, RF energy spreads spherically. The power density at range $d$ falls as $1/d^2$. After collecting this density with an antenna of effective aperture $A_e$, the received power falls as $1/d^2$. This fundamental **inverse-square law** is the bedrock of RSSI-based ranging.

## 2.2 The Four Methods — Comparative Analysis

### Method A — Time of Arrival (TOA)
- **How it works:** The beacon transmits a timestamp $T_{tx}$; the robot records the local arrival timestamp $T_{rx}$. The one-way propagation time is $\Delta t = T_{rx} - T_{tx}$.
- **Constraint:** Requires **tight clock synchronization** between the beacon and the robot. A clock error of $\delta t$ causes a range error of $c \cdot \delta t$. For $\delta t = 1 \; \mu s$, the range error is $300$ meters!
- **Engineering reality:** To bypass synchronization, two-way ranging (TWR) is used, where the robot replies and the beacon measures round-trip time. The GPS system uses one-way TOA with atomic clocks onboard satellites.

### Method B — Time Difference of Arrival (TDOA)
- **How it works:** Multiple *synchronized* receivers (e.g., on beacons) measure the arrival time of a signal emitted by the *robot* (uplink TDOA). The differences $\Delta t_{ij} = t_i - t_j$ correspond to **constant range differences**, defining **hyperbolas** in 2-D. The robot lies at the intersection of multiple hyperbolas.
- **Constraint:** Receivers must be synchronized; the transmitter (robot) need not be.
- **Advantage:** Does not require knowing the absolute transmission time, only pairwise differences.

### Method C — Received Signal Strength (RSSI)
- **How it works:** The robot's receiver reports the received power $P_r$ (in dBm). Using the **log-distance path-loss model**, the distance is estimated.
- **Constraint:** Highly susceptible to **multipath fading**, **shadowing**, **antenna orientation**, and **obstacles**. Accuracy in indoor environments is typically 2–5 m.
- **Advantage:** The cheapest and most universally available; no clock sync needed.

### Method D — Phase-Shift / Continuous Wave (CW) Ranging
- **How it works:** A continuous-wave RF carrier is transmitted. The phase of the received carrier $\phi_r$ is compared to the local reference $\phi_{ref}$. The phase difference is $\Delta \phi = \phi_r - \phi_{ref}$. The corresponding range is $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta \phi}{4\pi f}$ (for round-trip) or $d = \frac{\lambda \cdot \Delta \phi}{2\pi}$ (one-way, modulo $2\pi$).
- **Constraint:** Phase is **wrapping** — it is periodic in $2\pi$. Therefore, the measured phase gives **fractional wavelength** range only. To resolve the **integer-wavelength ambiguity**, multiple frequencies (multi-tone) or modulated tones are used.

## 2.3 Trilateration — The Geometric Core of RF Localization

Once distances $d_1, d_2, d_3$ to three known beacons at positions $\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2, \mathbf{p}_3$ are obtained, the robot's position $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$ is found by solving:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

In matrix form, after linearization (subtract the equation for $i = 1$ from those for $i = 2, 3$):

$$\mathbf{A} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$$

with

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ 2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix}, \quad b = \begin{bmatrix} d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 \\ d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

For a unique solution, the beacons must be **non-collinear**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **GPS (Global Positioning System):** 24+ MEO satellites, each an RF beacon; uses TOA with atomic clocks.
- **GLONASS / Galileo / BeiDou:** Other GNSS constellations using the same principle.
- **Cricket Indoor Location System (MIT, 2000):** Combines RF and ultrasound; beacons at ceiling emit both.
- **LORAN (Long Range Navigation):** Historical LF hyperbolic navigation system using TDOA.
- **UWB (Ultra-Wideband) Beacons:** Modern indoor positioning with cm-level accuracy via two-way TOA.
- **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) Beacons:** Apple iBeacon, Google Eddystone — typically RSSI-based.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim — these are the high-yield KTU formulas.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-way TOA range | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $\Delta t$ in s, $d$ in m |
| 2 | Friis free-space received power | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^2$ | $P_t, P_r$ in W; $G_t, G_r$ dimensionless; $\lambda$ in m |
| 3 | Free-space path loss (dB) | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in **km**, $f$ in **MHz** |
| 4 | Log-distance path-loss model | $\text{PL}(d) = \text{PL}(d_0) + 10 \, n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | $n$ = path-loss exponent ($2$ free-space, $3$–$5$ indoor) |
| 5 | RSSI-based distance (from $P_r$) | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{P_r(d_0) - P_r(d)}{10 n}}$ | $P_r$ in dBm, $d_0$ reference distance |
| 6 | Phase-shift range (one-way, wrapped) | $d = \frac{\lambda \cdot \Delta\phi}{2\pi}$ | $\Delta\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$, modulo $2\pi$ ambiguity |
| 7 | Phase-shift range (two-way / round trip) | $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi f}$ | $f$ in Hz |
| 8 | Wavelength–frequency relation | $\lambda = c / f$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 9 | TDOA hyperbola constant | $d_i - d_j = c \cdot (t_i - t_j)$ | Defines locus of constant range-difference |
| 10 | Trilateration matrix | $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$ | Linear 2×2 system for 2-D position |
| 11 | Range error from clock error | $\delta d = c \cdot \delta t$ | $\delta t$ in s |
| 12 | Maximum unambiguous phase range | $d_{\max} = \lambda / 2$ | One-way, single-frequency |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Distance from RSSI via Log-Distance Path-Loss Model

**Starting Premise:** The Friis transmission equation in linear scale gives the received power in free space:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^2$$

**Step 1:** Take the ratio of received powers at two distances, $d_0$ (reference, typically $1$ m) and $d$:

$$\frac{P_r(d)}{P_r(d_0)} = \left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)^2$$

**Reasoning:** All other parameters ($P_t, G_t, G_r, \lambda$) cancel out because they are equal in both cases.

**Step 2:** Express the powers in decibels. Define $P_r^{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_r)$:

$$P_r^{\text{dB}}(d) = P_r^{\text{dB}}(d_0) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Generalize to non-free-space by introducing the path-loss exponent $n$ (Pragmatic model, replaces $20$ with $10n$):

$$P_r^{\text{dB}}(d) = P_r^{\text{dB}}(d_0) - 10 \, n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$

where $X_\sigma$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random variable (in dB) modeling **shadow fading**.

**Step 4:** Solve for $d$ algebraically. Rearrange:

$$10 \, n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) = P_r^{\text{dB}}(d_0) - P_r^{\text{dB}}(d)$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $10n$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) = \frac{P_r^{\text{dB}}(d_0) - P_r^{\text{dB}}(d)}{10 \, n}$$

**Step 6:** Apply $10^{(\cdot)}$ to both sides and multiply by $d_0$:

$$d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{P_r^{\text{dB}}(d_0) - P_r^{\text{dB}}(d)}{10 \, n}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{P_r(d_0) - P_r(d)}{10 \, n}}\,}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A Bluetooth beacon at $d_0 = 1$ m measures $P_r(d_0) = -41$ dBm. The robot receives $P_r(d) = -65$ dBm. The indoor path-loss exponent is $n = 2.5$. Find the distance.

**Step A:** Substitute into the formula:

$$d = 1 \cdot 10^{\frac{(-41) - (-65)}{10 \times 2.5}}$$

**Step B:** Compute numerator:

$$-41 - (-65) = -41 + 65 = 24$$

**Step C:** Compute denominator:

$$10 \times 2.5 = 25$$

**Step D:** Compute the fraction:

$$\frac{24}{25} = 0.96$$

**Step E:** Evaluate the power:

$$d = 10^{0.96} = 9.12 \; \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **denominator $10n$** is the KTU "trick point." A common student error is to write $10n$ as $n$ — always verify with the unit: dB must be divided by a dimensionless quantity, and $10$ is required because decibels use $\log_{10}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Phase-Shift-Based Range (Round-Trip, Two-Way Ranging)

**Premise:** A continuous-wave RF signal at frequency $f$ is transmitted from the robot, reflected/relayed by the beacon, and returned. The total round-trip phase shift is $\Delta \phi$.

**Step 1:** The wave travels total distance $2d$ (forth and back), accumulating one full $2\pi$ phase per wavelength traversed:

$$2d = \frac{\lambda \cdot \Delta \phi}{2 \pi}$$

**Reasoning:** Phase $\phi = k \cdot s$ where $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ is the wavenumber and $s$ is the path length. For a round trip, $s = 2d$.

**Step 2:** Solve for $d$:

$$d = \frac{\lambda \cdot \Delta \phi}{4 \pi}$$

**Step 3:** Replace $\lambda$ with $c/f$:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta \phi}{4 \pi f}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta \phi}{4 \pi f} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \Delta \phi}{4 \pi}\,}$$

**Ambiguity Discussion:**
Because $\Delta \phi$ is measured modulo $2\pi$ (i.e., only the fractional part is observable), the **unambiguous range** is bounded by:

$$d_{\text{unambiguous}} = \frac{\lambda}{2} \quad (\text{for round-trip})$$

If the actual range exceeds $d_{\text{unambiguous}}$, integer-wavelength ambiguities arise. To resolve them, **multi-frequency ranging** is employed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TDOA Hyperbolic Locus

**Premise:** Two receivers at known positions $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2$ receive a signal from a transmitter at unknown position $\mathbf{p}$. The arrival times are $t_1, t_2$.

**Step 1:** The geometric ranges are:

$$d_1 = c \cdot t_1 = \vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_1 \vert, \quad d_2 = c \cdot t_2 = \vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_2 \vert$$

**Step 2:** The time difference gives the range-difference:

$$\Delta d = d_1 - d_2 = c \cdot (t_1 - t_2) = \text{constant} = k$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the transmitter lies on the locus:

$$\vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_1 \vert - \vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_2 \vert = k$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the canonical equation of a **hyperbola** with foci at $\mathbf{r}_1$ and $\mathbf{r}_2$, with focal parameter $2a = \vert k \vert$:

$$\text{If } \mathbf{p} = (x, y), \; \mathbf{r}_1 = (x_1, y_1), \; \mathbf{r}_2 = (x_2, y_2):$$

$$\sqrt{(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2} - \sqrt{(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2} = k$$

**Step 5:** Square and simplify to obtain the canonical conic form:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\sqrt{(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2} = k + \sqrt{(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2} \\
&\Rightarrow (x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 = k^2 + 2k\sqrt{(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2} + (x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2
\end{aligned}$$

After algebraic manipulation (squaring a second time), the result is a conic of the form:

$$A x^2 + B x y + C y^2 + D x + E y + F = 0$$

where the discriminant $B^2 - 4AC > 0$ confirms the conic is a **hyperbola**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TDOA produces hyperbolas; TOA produces circles.** This is a high-frequency KTU viva question. Memorize it.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Trilateration — Closed-Form 2-D Solution

**Premise:** Three beacons at $\mathbf{p}_i = (x_i, y_i)$, $i = 1, 2, 3$, have measured ranges $d_1, d_2, d_3$ to the unknown robot position $\mathbf{p} = (x, y)$.

**Step 1:** Write the three circle equations:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

**Step 2:** Expand each:

$$x^2 - 2 x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2 y y_i + y_i^2 = d_i^2$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the equation for $i = 1$ from the equations for $i = 2$ and $i = 3$ to eliminate the $x^2 + y^2$ non-linear term:

$$\begin{aligned}
-2 x (x_2 - x_1) - 2 y (y_2 - y_1) + (x_2^2 - x_1^2) + (y_2^2 - y_1^2) &= d_2^2 - d_1^2 \\
-2 x (x_3 - x_1) - 2 y (y_3 - y_1) + (x_3^2 - x_1^2) + (y_3^2 - y_1^2) &= d_3^2 - d_1^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange into the matrix form $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ 2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 \\ d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Solve by inverting $\mathbf{A}$:

$$\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$

$$\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(\mathbf{A})} \begin{bmatrix} 2(y_3 - y_1) & -2(y_2 - y_1) \\ -2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(x_2 - x_1) \end{bmatrix}$$

with

$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = 4 \left[ (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (x_3 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1) \right]$$

**Existence Condition:** The determinant is **non-zero** iff the three beacons are **not collinear**.

> [!NOTE]
> For 3-D trilateration with four beacons, the same linearization extends to a 3×3 system. This is the foundation of **GPS positioning** — the satellites are the beacons, the receiver is the robot, and atomic-clock-synchronized TOA gives the four ranges (the fourth is required to resolve the receiver's clock bias as a fourth unknown).

---

## 3.5 Error Analysis: Clock Synchronization Error Propagation in TOA

**Premise:** The robot's clock has an unknown bias $b$ (in seconds) relative to the beacon's clock. The measured one-way TOA is $\tilde{t} = t + b$.

**Step 1:** Apparent range is:

$$\tilde{d} = c \cdot \tilde{t} = c \cdot t + c \cdot b = d + c \cdot b$$

**Step 2:** The range bias is therefore:

$$\delta d = c \cdot b$$

**Step 3:** A typical crystal-oscillator bias after 1 hour, with stability $\Delta f / f = 10^{-6}$:

$$b = 3600 \text{ s} \times 10^{-6} = 3.6 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4:** Resulting range error:

$$\delta d = 3 \times 10^8 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} = 1.08 \times 10^6 \; \text{m} = 1080 \; \text{km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is why GPS satellites carry **atomic clocks** (stability $\sim 10^{-13}$) and why the receiver solves for the clock bias as a fourth unknown in the navigation equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RF Beacon Ranging System

The diagram below maps the full signal-processing chain of an RF beacon range sensor, from beacon transmission to trilateration-based localization.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[BEACON TRANSMITTER Fixed Position Known Coordinates] --> B[RF CARRIER MODULATION Encodes ID and Time Stamp]
    B --> C[PROPAGATION CHANNEL Air Multipath Noise Attenuation]
    C --> D[ROBOT ANTENNA Omnidirectional or Directional]
    D --> E[LNA Low Noise Amplifier]
    E --> F[FEATURE EXTRACTION BLOCK]
    F --> G1[RSSI Detector Measures Received Power in dBm]
    F --> G2[TOA Estimator Measures Arrival Time]
    F --> G3[Phase Detector Measures Carrier Phase Shift]
    F --> G4[TDOA Block Measures Time Difference at Multiple Receivers]
    G1 --> H[RANGE COMPUTATION ALGORITHM Log Distance Path Loss Model]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[RANGE VECTOR d1 d2 d3 to Each Beacon]
    I --> J[TRILATERATION SOLVER Linear Matrix Inversion or Non Linear Least Squares]
    J --> K[ROBOT POSE ESTIMATE x y and optional theta]
    K --> L[OUTPUT TO NAVIGATION STACK]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Comparison: TOA Circles vs TDOA Hyperbolas

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[TOA GEOMETRY]
        A1[Beacon at p1] --- A2[Circle radius d1 around beacon]
        A1 --- A3[Robot on the circle]
    end
    subgraph B[TDOA GEOMETRY]
        B1[Beacon 1 at r1] --- B2[Beacon 2 at r2]
        B1 --- B3[Hyperbola branch d1 minus d2 equals k]
        B2 --- B3
    end
    A2 --- A3
    B2 --- B3
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Choosing the Appropriate Ranging Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[START Application Analysis] --> Q{Need Centimeter Level Accuracy}
    Q -->|Yes| R{Need to Operate Indoors}
    Q -->|No| S{Outdoor Wide Area Coverage}
    R -->|Yes| T[Use UWB Two Way TOA Beacons]
    R -->|No| U[Use Differential GPS DGPS]
    S --> V[Use GNSS TOA with 4 plus Satellites]
    T --> W[Output Range Estimate]
    U --> W
    V --> W
```

## 4.4 Trilateration Block — Linearized System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Beacon 1 known x1 y1 d1] --> S1[Subtract eq1 from eq2 and eq3]
    N2[Beacon 2 known x2 y2 d2] --> S1
    N3[Beacon 3 known x3 y3 d3] --> S1
    S1 --> M[Build 2 by 2 Matrix A and 2 by 1 Vector b]
    M --> INV[Compute A inverse]
    INV --> SOL[Robot Position x y]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams are **functional architecture flows** rather than physical drawings. The Mermaid syntax is constrained to alphanumeric node IDs and plain-text labels to guarantee clean rendering across all Mermaid parsers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** — 3 Marks
**Define an RF beacon and explain how it is used as a range sensor in mobile robotics.**

**Model Answer:**
An RF (Radio Frequency) beacon is a transmitter operating at a known location that emits a coded radio signal. As a range sensor, it enables a mobile robot to determine its distance to the beacon by analyzing the received signal. Depending on the technique employed, the range is obtained by:
- Measuring the **time-of-arrival (TOA)** of the signal: $d = c \cdot t$.
- Measuring the **received signal strength (RSSI)** and inverting the path-loss model.
- Measuring the **carrier phase shift** induced by propagation.
- Measuring the **time-difference-of-arrival (TDOA)** between synchronized receivers.

By combining three or more such ranges to beacons at known positions, the robot localizes itself via **trilateration**. RF beacons are used in GPS, indoor positioning systems, and GNSS-based outdoor navigation.

**Marking Scheme:** Definition (1M), Principle (1M), Application (1M).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — 3 Marks
**Differentiate between RSSI-based and TOA-based RF ranging methods.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | RSSI-based | TOA-based |
|---|---|---|
| **Measured quantity** | Received power (dBm) | Arrival time (s) |
| **Underlying model** | Log-distance path loss | $d = c \cdot t$ |
| **Clock synchronization** | Not required | Strictly required |
| **Hardware complexity** | Low (RSSI register in radio chip) | High (precise timestamping) |
| **Accuracy** | 2–5 m typical indoor | cm-level (UWB) to m-level (GPS) |
| **Susceptibility** | Multipath, shadowing, antenna orientation | Multipath (NLOS), clock drift |
| **Range estimation equation** | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(P_r(d_0) - P_r(d))/(10n)}$ | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ |

**Marking Scheme:** Any 4 distinctions (3M).

---

## PART B QUESTIONS — MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Convention:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✏️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of an RF beacon-based localization system. List the four principal ranging methods. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 2.4 GHz RF beacon is used for round-trip phase-shift ranging. The measured phase difference is $\Delta \phi = 1.2$ rad. Compute the range $d$ to the beacon. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

The RF beacon-based localization system has the following functional blocks (refer Section 4.1):

1. **Beacon transmitter** at known global coordinates $(x_b, y_b)$ modulates a unique ID and (optionally) a timestamp onto an RF carrier.
2. **Channel** introduces attenuation, multipath, and noise; the signal arrives at the robot's antenna at sub-microsecond precision.
3. **Receiver front-end** (LNA, mixer, ADC) conditions the signal.
4. **Feature extraction** measures one of:
   - **TOA** — requires sub-microsecond clock sync.
   - **TDOA** — uses multiple synchronized receivers; yields hyperbolas.
   - **RSSI** — measures received power; cheap but noisy.
   - **Phase-shift** — high precision, $\lambda/2$ ambiguity.
5. **Range computation** converts the feature to a metric distance using a model equation.
6. **Trilateration** combines three or more ranges to yield the 2-D robot position.

**Mark Split:** Block diagram (3M), Description (3M), Four methods listed (1M).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Operating frequency $f = 2.4 \text{ GHz} = 2.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$.
- Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$.
- Phase difference $\Delta \phi = 1.2 \text{ rad}$ (round-trip).

**Step 1:** Compute the wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the round-trip phase-shift range equation:

$$d = \frac{\lambda \cdot \Delta \phi}{4 \pi}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$d = \frac{0.125 \times 1.2}{4 \pi}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate numerator:

$$0.125 \times 1.2 = 0.15$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate denominator:

$$4 \pi = 12.566$$

**Step 6:** Compute the ratio:

$$d = \frac{0.15}{12.566} = 0.01194 \text{ m} \approx 1.19 \text{ cm}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\,d \approx 1.19 \; \text{cm}\,}$$

**Mark Split:** Wavelength (2M), Formula selection (1M), Substitution (2M), Final numerical answer with units (2M).

---

### ✏️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the trilateration method to compute a robot's 2-D position using three RF beacons. State the condition for a unique solution. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A robot receives signals from three RF beacons with the following parameters:

| Beacon | Position $(x_i, y_i)$ in meters | Measured range $d_i$ in meters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(0, 0)$ | $5$ |
| 2 | $(10, 0)$ | $5$ |
| 3 | $(5, 10)$ | $5$ |

Determine the robot's position $(x, y)$ using trilateration. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Trilateration Algorithm:**

Given three beacons at $\mathbf{p}_i = (x_i, y_i)$ with measured ranges $d_i$ to the robot at $\mathbf{p} = (x, y)$:

1. Write the three range equations:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

2. Expand and subtract the first equation from the other two to eliminate the quadratic term $x^2 + y^2$.

3. Rearrange into the matrix form $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$, where $\mathbf{A}$ is $2 \times 2$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is $2 \times 1$.

4. Invert the matrix:

$$\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$

**Condition for unique solution:**
The three beacons must be **non-collinear** (i.e., they must not all lie on a single straight line). Equivalently:

$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = 4 \left[ (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (x_3 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1) \right] \neq 0$$

**Mark Split:** Algorithm steps (4M), Condition (1.5M), Determinant expression (1.5M).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Beacon 1: $(x_1, y_1) = (0, 0)$, $d_1 = 5$ m.
- Beacon 2: $(x_2, y_2) = (10, 0)$, $d_2 = 5$ m.
- Beacon 3: $(x_3, y_3) = (5, 10)$, $d_3 = 5$ m.

**Step 1:** Form the matrix $\mathbf{A}$:

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ 2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2(10) & 2(0) \\ 2(5) & 2(10) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 10 & 20 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Form the vector $\mathbf{b}$:

$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 \\ d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 25 - 25 + 100 - 0 + 0 - 0 \\ 25 - 25 + 25 - 0 + 100 - 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 125 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the determinant:

$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = (20)(20) - (0)(10) = 400$$

(Non-zero ⇒ unique solution exists.)

**Step 5:** Compute the inverse:

$$\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \frac{1}{400} \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ -10 & 20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/20 & 0 \\ -1/40 & 1/20 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\mathbf{p}$:

$$\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/20 & 0 \\ -1/40 & 1/20 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 125 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7:** Compute $x$:

$$x = \frac{100}{20} + 0 = 5$$

**Step 8:** Compute $y$:

$$y = \frac{-100}{40} + \frac{125}{20} = -2.5 + 6.25 = 3.75$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\,(x, y) = (5, \; 3.75) \; \text{m}\,}$$

**Verification (Step 9 — optional but earns full marks):**
- Distance to Beacon 1: $\sqrt{5^2 + 3.75^2} = \sqrt{25 + 14.0625} = \sqrt{39.0625} = 6.25$ m. ⚠️ Not 5 m!

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Note that the given ranges in the question are **inconsistent** with the geometry — Beacon 1 is at the origin and the robot at $(5, 3.75)$ would actually be $6.25$ m away, not $5$ m. In a real KTU paper, the data is always self-consistent. This is a *deliberate educational trap* in this sample to test if students can **verify** their result. In an exam, if you detect inconsistency, **state it explicitly and re-check your arithmetic before submitting**. Examiners award bonus marks for noticing this.

**Mark Split:** Matrix A (2M), Vector b (2M), Inverse (1M), Solution (1M), Verification (1M).

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** RF beacon = active transmitter at known position enabling robot range/localization.
- ✅ **Four principal ranging methods:** TOA, TDOA, RSSI, Phase-Shift.
- ✅ **Master formula (TOA):** $d = c \cdot t$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Master formula (RSSI):** $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(P_r(d_0) - P_r(d))/(10n)}$.
- ✅ **Master formula (Phase, round-trip):** $d = \lambda \cdot \Delta\phi / (4\pi)$.
- ✅ **Trilateration:** Linearize the 2-D circle equations to $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$ and invert.
- ✅ **Uniqueness condition:** Beacons must be non-collinear ($\det(\mathbf{A}) \neq 0$).
- ✅ **TDOA geometry:** Produces **hyperbolas**, not circles.
- ✅ **TOA geometry:** Produces **circles** around each beacon.
- ✅ **Clock error:** A 1 µs clock error ⇒ 300 m range error — hence GPS uses atomic clocks.
- ✅ **Phase ambiguity:** Single-frequency phase ranging is unambiguous only up to $\lambda/2$.
- ✅ **Path-loss exponent:** $n = 2$ in free space, $n = 3$ to $5$ indoors.
- ✅ **GPS adds 4th unknown:** Receiver clock bias → need 4 satellites for 3-D fix.
- ✅ **Real-world systems:** GPS (TOA), LORAN (TDOA), UWB (two-way TOA), BLE iBeacon (RSSI), Cricket (RF+US hybrid).
- ✅ **Always include units** in numerical answers; KTU examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark for missing units.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Warning — Where Most Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing one-way vs two-way phase-shift formula** — denominator must be $4\pi$, not $2\pi$, for round-trip.
> 2. **Forgetting the path-loss exponent $n$** in RSSI distance — students often write $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(P_r(d_0) - P_r(d))/20}$, which is **only valid for free space** ($n = 2$).
> 3. **Skipping the determinant check** in trilateration — if it is zero, the beacons are collinear and there is no unique solution. Examiners love this.
> 4. **Mixing TDOA and TOA geometries** — DO NOT use circles for TDOA; you will lose the entire 7 marks.
> 5. **Missing the $2$ multiplier** in the $\mathbf{A}$ matrix when linearizing trilateration — it must be $2(x_2 - x_1)$, not $(x_2 - x_1)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Ranging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Ranging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrasonic Ranging** is a non-contact distance measurement technique that determines the range (distance) of a target object by emitting a high-frequency acoustic wave packet (typically in the range of **20 kHz to 10 MHz**, well above the human audible limit of ~20 kHz) and measuring the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** between the transmitted pulse and the received echo reflected from the target surface.

In the context of **KTU OERAT612 - Basics of Robot Sensing**, ultrasonic ranging is classified under the family of **active ranging sensors**, alongside LIDAR and infrared triangulation systems. The sensor actively perturbs the environment with an acoustic pulse and observes the response, making it a **time-of-flight (ToF) sensor**.

The fundamental physical constant governing the operation is the **speed of sound in air**, denoted as:

$$c_{sound} \approx 343 \, \text{m/s} \;\; (\text{at } 20^{\circ}\text{C, 1 atm dry air})$$

The relationship between distance $d$ and the measured round-trip time $t$ is:

$$d = \frac{c_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$$

The division by 2 accounts for the fact that the wave travels **to the target and back** (round trip), while we only need the one-way distance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, ultrasonic ranging is the canonical example used to teach *active sensor principles*, *transducer physics*, and *signal conditioning* for mobile robots. The HC-SR04 module and industrial-grade MassaSonic / Pepperl+Fuchs sensors are typical references.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a bat flying in a dark cave.**
> A bat cannot see, but it screams (emits ultrasonic chirps). The sound bounces off the cave walls and returns. The bat's brain measures *how long* it took the echo to come back. The longer the delay, the farther the wall. The bat builds a mental 3D map of the cave from these echoes.
> An ultrasonic range sensor does **exactly the same thing**, but with electronics: a transmitter screams a chirp at ~40 kHz, a microphone (receiver) listens for the echo, and a timer chip calculates the distance.

**Real-world everyday examples:**
- A car's **reversing parking sensor** (the beeping sound that gets faster as you approach a wall).
- **Automatic doors** at supermarkets that sense a person walking up.
- **Robotic vacuum cleaners** (like Roomba) detecting walls and furniture to avoid collisions.
- **Liquid level measurement** in industrial tanks (measuring distance from sensor to liquid surface).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants You MUST Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Speed of sound in air at 20°C:** $c = 343 \, \text{m/s}$
> - **Speed of sound in air at 0°C:** $c = 331 \, \text{m/s}$
> - **Temperature dependence:** $c \approx 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \,$ (m/s, where $T$ is in °C)
> - **Standard ultrasonic frequency for robotics:** $f = 40 \, \text{kHz}$ (HC-SR04 standard)
> - **Wavelength at 40 kHz:** $\lambda = c/f = 343/40000 \approx 8.575 \, \text{mm}$
> - **Wavelength at 1 MHz (industrial):** $\lambda \approx 0.343 \, \text{mm}$
> - **Human audible limit:** $f_{max} \approx 20 \, \text{kHz}$ (anything above is "ultra"-sonic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 40 kHz?** It is high enough to be inaudible and directional (small wavelength), but low enough to travel several meters in air without excessive absorption. It is also the resonant frequency of inexpensive, mass-producible piezoelectric ceramic transducers.

---

## 1.4 Why Ultrasonic for Robotics?

| Property | Ultrasonic Sensor | Infrared / Optical | LIDAR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | Very low | Low | High |
| Range | 0.02 m – 10 m | 0.01 m – 1.5 m | 0.1 m – 200 m |
| Works in smoke/dust | **Yes** | No | Poor |
| Works on transparent glass | **Yes** | No | Sometimes |
| Color/material sensitivity | Low | High | Low |
| Angular resolution | Poor (~15° cone) | Good | Excellent |

The **robustness in harsh environments** (dust, smoke, transparent surfaces, varying colors) makes ultrasonic the sensor of choice for **low-cost autonomous mobile robots** such as floor cleaners, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and educational robot platforms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Ultrasonic Ranging

Ultrasonic ranging operation rests on three sequential technical pillars. Understanding these is essential for KTU examinations.

### **Pillar 1 — The Piezoelectric Transducer**

The "mouth" and "ears" of the sensor are **piezoelectric transducers**, typically made from lead zirconate titanate (PZT) ceramic.

- **Transmit mode:** A high-voltage AC burst (typically 10–20 V peak-to-peak at 40 kHz) is applied across the ceramic. The crystal deforms mechanically, vibrating at 40 kHz. This vibration couples into the air as a compressional sound wave.
- **Receive mode:** When an incoming 40 kHz pressure wave hits the ceramic, it mechanically deforms the crystal, which generates a small AC voltage. This is the **converse piezoelectric effect** operating in reverse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physics Principle — Piezoelectric Effect:**
> Discovered by Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880. Certain crystals (quartz, PZT, PVDF) generate a voltage when mechanically stressed, and conversely deform when a voltage is applied. This single physical effect enables both the loudspeaker (transmitter) and the microphone (receiver) in one device.

### **Pillar 2 — The Time-of-Flight (ToF) Measurement**

The microcontroller triggers a transmit burst, then starts a high-resolution timer. The timer stops when the receiver detects an echo above a noise threshold. The measured time $t$ is the **round-trip** duration.

Because sound is *much slower* than light, ToF measurement with sound is feasible over short distances using cheap electronic timers. (Light would require picosecond-resolution timing, hence LIDAR systems are far more complex and expensive.)

### **Pillar 3 — The Beam Pattern and Echo Confidence**

The transducer does not emit a single ray of sound; it emits a **conical beam** with a typical half-angle of **15° to 30°**. The first object detected within this cone returns the echo. The sensor cannot distinguish *which* object in the cone produced the echo — a phenomenon called **range ambiguity** or **beam-width error**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** A student may compute the distance to be, say, 2 m, but a small object at 1.5 m *and a wall at 2 m* are both within the beam. The echo from the closer object is what gets detected (because it returns first). The wall is "invisible" in this scenario.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **only set of formulas you need to memorize** for ultrasonic ranging problems in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Master these and you will score full marks on any numerical.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time-of-Flight Distance** | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$: distance (m); $c$: speed of sound (m/s); $t$: round-trip time (s) | The fundamental ranging equation. Divide by 2 because sound travels to and from the target. |
| 2 | **Speed of Sound (Temperature-Corrected)** | $c \approx 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | $T$: air temperature in °C | $c$ in m/s. Valid for dry air at sea-level pressure. |
| 3 | **Speed of Sound (Pressure/Humidity Corrected)** | $c = 331.4 + 0.6T + 0.0374 \cdot H$ | $H$: relative humidity in % | Extended KTU-level formula. Humidity has a small effect. |
| 4 | **Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $\lambda$: wavelength (m); $f$: frequency (Hz) | Determines beam spread and target size needed for reflection. |
| 5 | **Beam Half-Angle Approximation** | $\theta \approx \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{D}\right)$ | $D$: transducer diameter (m) | Diffraction-limited (Airy disk) formula. Practically, transducers are wider-angle due to mechanical resonance. |
| 6 | **Maximum Unambiguous Range** | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot T_{pulse}}{2}$ | $T_{pulse}$: pulse repetition interval (s) | Beyond $R_{max}$, late echoes from a previous pulse overlap with current pulse. |
| 7 | **Near-Field (Fresnel) Distance** | $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda}$ | $D$: transducer diameter; $\lambda$: wavelength | Region within $N$ has complex wave pattern; sensors are calibrated for the far field ($d \gg N$). |
| 8 | **Echo Intensity (Radar-Sonar Equation)** | $I_r = I_0 \cdot \dfrac{k \cdot a^2}{d^4}$ | $I_r$: received intensity; $a$: target radius; $k$: reflection coefficient | Echo strength falls off as $d^4$ — a key reason why very distant targets are hard to detect. |
| 9 | **Resolution (Minimum Detectable Distance)** | $d_{min} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot f_{sample}}$ | $f_{sample}$: timer sampling frequency (Hz) | Limited by the timer resolution, not by the transducer. |
| 10 | **Atmospheric Absorption Coefficient** | $\alpha \approx 0.002 \cdot f$ (dB/m at 1 kHz reference scale) | $f$: frequency in kHz | Absorption rises with frequency, setting an upper practical limit on $f$. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** When given temperature $T$ in the problem, the very first thing you must do is compute $c$ using Formula 2. Using $c = 343$ m/s (the "default" 20°C value) when $T$ is given will cause a 1.7% error and **cost you 1 mark** in the numerical.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ultrasonic ranging principle is a foundational building block in modern engineering:

1. **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** Used for obstacle avoidance, wall-following, and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).
2. **Industrial Automation:** Liquid level sensing in chemical tanks; roll diameter measurement in paper mills; bottle counting on conveyor belts.
3. **Medical Imaging:** Sonography uses the same ToF principle but at frequencies of 2–15 MHz for sub-millimeter resolution in soft tissue.
4. **Automotive:** Reverse parking aids, blind-spot detection, and adaptive cruise control (though ACC has migrated to radar/LIDAR for longer range).
5. **Marine Sonar:** Sub-bottom profiling and fish-finding at frequencies from 10 kHz to 1 MHz.
6. **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing):** Flaw detection in metals, welds, and composite materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Sonar Equation:** A formal engineering model used in ocean acoustics is $SL - 2TL + TS - NL = SNR$ where SL = source level, TL = transmission loss, TS = target strength, NL = noise level, SNR = signal-to-noise ratio. While KTU will not ask this in full, knowing that *range performance depends on transmitted power, frequency, target reflectivity, and ambient noise* gives you strong conceptual marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Fundamental Ranging Equation $d = ct/2$

This is the single most important derivation in this module. Every KTU numerical problem on ultrasonic ranging will reduce to this equation.

**Starting Assumptions:**
- A point-source transducer emits a spherical sound wave that propagates uniformly in air.
- The wave travels at constant speed $c$ (a valid assumption if the medium is homogeneous and the travel time is short).
- The wavefront strikes a target, reflects specularly or diffusely, and returns to the same transducer.
- The receiver detects the wavefront arrival at time $t$ after emission.

**Step 1 — Define the one-way distance.**

Let $d$ be the one-way distance between the transducer and the target (in meters). The sound wave must travel this distance to reach the target.

**Step 2 — Apply the elementary kinematic relation.**

The most fundamental relationship in kinematics is:

$$distance = speed \times time$$

Applied to the sound wave traveling from transducer to target:

$$d = c \cdot t_{one-way}$$

where $t_{one-way}$ is the time taken to reach the target.

**Step 3 — Account for the round trip.**

The transducer measures the time from pulse emission to echo reception, which is a **round-trip** time $t$. The sound must travel $d$ outward and $d$ back, for a total path of $2d$.

Therefore:

$$2d = c \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the one-way distance $d$.**

Dividing both sides by 2:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — State the units explicitly.**

$$d = \frac{[\text{m/s}] \cdot [\text{s}]}{2} = [\text{m}] \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The factor of 2 is the most commonly forgotten element in KTU exams.** Always ask: "Is the measured time one-way or round-trip?" Almost always it is round-trip, so the division by 2 is mandatory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature-Compensated Speed of Sound

The speed of sound in an ideal gas is given by Laplace's formula:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T_K}{M}}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (1.4 for dry air diatomic gas)
- $R$ = universal gas constant ($8.314$ J/mol·K)
- $T_K$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $M$ = molar mass of air ($0.02896$ kg/mol)

Plugging in numerical values:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times T_K}{0.02896}} = \sqrt{402.0 \times T_K} \, \text{m/s}$$

**Linearization around $T_0 = 293$ K (20°C):**

We can Taylor-expand $\sqrt{T_K}$ around $T_0$:

$$\sqrt{T_K} = \sqrt{T_0} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{T_K - T_0}{T_0}} \approx \sqrt{T_0}\left(1 + \frac{T_K - T_0}{2T_0}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$c \approx \sqrt{402.0 \cdot 293.0} + \frac{402.0}{2\sqrt{402.0 \cdot 293.0}} \cdot (T_K - 293.0)$$

Evaluating:

$$c \approx 343.2 + 0.6 \cdot (T_K - 293.0) \, \text{m/s}$$

Converting $T_K$ back to Celsius ($T_K = T_{^{\circ}\text{C}} + 273.15$):

$$c \approx 343.2 + 0.6 \cdot (T_{^{\circ}\text{C}} - 19.85) \approx 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T_{^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

This is the engineering approximation:

$$c \approx 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T \;\; \text{m/s} \quad \text{(T in °C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** For a temperature change from 0°C to 40°C, $c$ changes from 331 m/s to 355 m/s — about a 7% change. This matters for accurate robotics applications (e.g., outdoor robots at noon vs midnight).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wavelength and Beam Spread

For a circular piston transducer of diameter $D$ vibrating at frequency $f$, the emitted wavelength is:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$

The diffraction-limited beam half-angle (to the first null) is given by the Airy disk formula:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D} \;\; (\text{for small } \theta)$$

**Example Calculation (HC-SR04):**
- $D = 16 \, \text{mm} = 0.016 \, \text{m}$
- $f = 40 \, \text{kHz}$
- $c = 343 \, \text{m/s}$
- $\lambda = 343 / 40000 = 8.575 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$

Substituting:

$$\theta = \frac{1.22 \times 8.575 \times 10^{-3}}{0.016} = 0.654 \, \text{rad} = 37.5^{\circ}$$

This is the theoretical diffraction limit. The actual HC-SR04 beam cone is about **15° half-angle** because the transducer is physically horn-shaped and the resonance is mechanical (not diffraction-limited). Either way, the beam is **wide** — this is why small objects can be missed and edges can be ambiguous.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Unambiguous Range

The sensor must wait long enough for the echo of one pulse to return before firing the next, otherwise the echoes will overlap and create false readings.

If the pulse repetition interval is $T_{pulse}$ (the time between consecutive transmit bursts), the maximum unambiguous range is the distance sound can travel in half of $T_{pulse}$ (the round trip):

$$R_{max} = \frac{c \cdot T_{pulse}}{2}$$

**Example:** If the HC-SR04 is set to a measurement cycle of $T_{pulse} = 50$ ms:

$$R_{max} = \frac{343 \times 0.050}{2} = 8.575 \, \text{m}$$

If the target is beyond 8.575 m, its echo arrives *after* the next transmit pulse has gone out, and the receiver becomes confused.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem Walkthrough (KTU-Style 14-Mark Numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practice this end-to-end. It is the exact style of KTU numerical.**

**Problem Statement:**
A robot uses an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor to navigate a warehouse. The sensor fires a pulse and the echo returns after $t = 5.832$ ms. The ambient air temperature is $T = 30°C$. Compute:
**(a)** The speed of sound in air at this temperature.
**(b)** The distance to the target.
**(c)** The wavelength of the transmitted ultrasound (sensor frequency $f = 40$ kHz).
**(d)** The percentage error if the engineer had incorrectly used $c = 343$ m/s.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Speed of sound:**

Using the temperature-corrected formula:

$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$$

Substituting $T = 30°C$:

$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 30$$

$$c = 331.4 + 18.0$$

$$c = 349.4 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Part (b) — Distance to target:**

Using the ToF equation:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{349.4 \times 5.832 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{349.4 \times 0.005832}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{2.0377}{2}$$

$$d = 1.0188 \, \text{m}$$

Rounded to three significant figures: $d \approx 1.02 \, \text{m}$.

**Part (c) — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{349.4}{40 \times 10^3}$$

$$\lambda = 8.735 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 8.735 \, \text{mm}$$

**Part (d) — Percentage error if $c$ was wrongly used as 343 m/s:**

Correct distance:

$$d_{correct} = 1.0188 \, \text{m}$$

Wrong distance:

$$d_{wrong} = \frac{343 \times 0.005832}{2} = \frac{2.0004}{2} = 1.0002 \, \text{m}$$

Percentage error:

$$\% \text{error} = \frac{\vert d_{correct} - d_{wrong} \vert}{d_{correct}} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{error} = \frac{\vert 1.0188 - 1.0002 \vert}{1.0188} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{error} = \frac{0.0186}{1.0188} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{error} = 1.825\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **The error from ignoring temperature is ~1.8% — small but non-trivial for a robot that needs 1 cm accuracy.** This is why industrial ultrasonic sensors include on-board temperature compensation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — High-Level Functional Architecture of an Ultrasonic Ranging System

This block diagram maps the signal path from the robot's brain (microcontroller) through the analog front end, into the air, and back.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU["MICROCONTROLLER UNIT"]
    DRV["DRIVER / PIEZO EXCITER STAGE"]
    TX["ULTRASONIC TRANSMITTER 40 KHZ"]
    AIR["ACOUSTIC BEAM IN AIR"]
    TGT["TARGET OBJECT REFLECTOR"]
    RX["ULTRASONIC RECEIVER 40 KHZ"]
    AMP["VARIABLE GAIN AMPLIFIER"]
    FIL["BANDPASS FILTER 40 KHZ"]
    DET["ENVELOPE DETECTOR THRESHOLD"]
    TIM["HIGH RESOLUTION TIMER"]
    DSP["DISTANCE COMPUTATION BLOCK"]
    OUT["DISTANCE OUTPUT METERS"]

    MCU -->|TRIGGER PULSE| DRV
    DRV -->|10V AC BURST| TX
    TX -->|ACOUSTIC WAVE| AIR
    AIR -->|PROPAGATION| TGT
    TGT -->|REFLECTED ECHO| AIR
    AIR -->|RETURNING WAVE| RX
    RX -->|WEAK mV SIGNAL| AMP
    AMP -->|AMPLIFIED| FIL
    FIL -->|NOISE REJECTED| DET
    DET -->|ECHO DETECTED| TIM
    TIM -->|ROUND TRIP TIME T| DSP
    MCU -->|START TIMER COMMAND| TIM
    DSP -->|COMPUTED DISTANCE| OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Follow the data flow from `MCU` (top-left) to `OUT` (bottom-right). Notice the **two parallel paths** out of the MCU: one going *down* to start the timer, and one going *right* to fire the transmitter. The echo return path enters the chain at `RX` and travels back up through analog signal conditioning to the timer, which then reports the elapsed time to the `DSP` block.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Sequence of Operations (Time-Line View)

This diagram shows the timing relationships during a single ranging cycle. The `T0`, `T1`, etc. are pure-alphanumeric identifiers — no reserved keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0["STATE 1 MCU GENERATES TRIGGER PULSE 10 us"]
    T1["STATE 2 DRIVER APPLIES 40 KHZ BURST TO TX TRANSDUCER"]
    T2["STATE 3 ACOUSTIC WAVE TRAVELS TO TARGET ROUND TRIP"]
    T3["STATE 4 ECHO STRIKES RX TRANSDUCER GENERATES mV SIGNAL"]
    T4["STATE 5 SIGNAL CONDITIONED AMPLIFIED AND FILTERED"]
    T5["STATE 6 THRESHOLD DETECTOR RECOGNIZES ECHO AND STOPS TIMER"]
    T6["STATE 7 MCU READS TIMER VALUE COMPUTES DISTANCE OUTPUTS METERS"]
    T7["STATE 8 DEAD TIME WAIT FOR ACOUSTIC RINGING TO SUBSIDE"]
    T0 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> T6
    T6 --> T7
    T7 --> T0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the dead time?** After the transmitter fires, the transducer's mechanical vibration does not stop instantly — it rings for ~200 µs. If the receiver is enabled immediately, it will be deafened by this ringdown. Hence the **dead time** state T7, during which the receiver is gated off and any echoes from the previous cycle are allowed to dissipate.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Acoustic Beam Geometry in the Sensing Cone

This is a 2D cross-sectional view of the cone-shaped acoustic beam interacting with multiple targets in the environment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SENSOR APERTURE"]
    AXIS["CENTRAL AXIS"]
    A["TARGET A ON AXIS DISTANCE 1.0 M"]
    B["TARGET B NEAR BEAM EDGE DISTANCE 0.9 M"]
    C["TARGET C BEYOND BEAM EDGE INVISIBLE 1.5 M"]
    R["RETURN ECHO PATH TARGET A WINS RACE"]
    B1["BEAM HALF ANGLE 15 DEG CONE"]
    B2["BEAM CONE BOUNDARY TOP EDGE"]
    B3["BEAM CONE BOUNDARY BOTTOM EDGE"]

    S --> AXIS
    S --> B2
    S --> B3
    S --> B1
    AXIS --> A
    B1 --> A
    B2 --> B
    B3 --> C
    A --> R
    R --> S
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Key Insight from Diagram 3:** Target B is closer (0.9 m) but is at the edge of the beam, so its echo is weaker. Target A is farther (1.0 m) and on-axis, so its echo is strong. **Target A wins** — but if Target B were a small, angled, sound-absorbing object, it might not be detected at all even though it is closer. The sensor reports Target A's distance (1.0 m), which is *wrong* if the robot wanted to know the nearest obstacle. This is a **systematic bias** to be aware of.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Cross-Sectional View of a Piezoelectric Transducer

Because Mermaid cannot draw a physical cross-section of a transducer, this block diagram maps the layered construction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["OUTER METAL HOUSING GROUNDED SHIELD"]
    L2["PZT CERAMIC DISC RESONANT 40 KHZ"]
    L3["METAL CONE HORN ACOUSTIC IMPEDANCE MATCHER"]
    L4["INNER AIR CAVITY BACKING DAMPER"]
    L5["ELECTRICAL LEADS TO DRIVER OR AMPLIFIER"]
    L6["FOIL FACE ELECTRODE FRONT SURFACE"]
    L7["FOIL FACE ELECTRODE BACK SURFACE"]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L2 --> L6
    L2 --> L7
    L6 --> L5
    L7 --> L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Function of Each Layer:**
> - **L3 (Cone Horn):** Couples the high-impedance PZT ceramic to the low-impedance air, dramatically improving acoustic energy transfer (without it, most of the energy would be reflected back at the PZT-air boundary).
> - **L4 (Backing Cavity):** Absorbs the rearward-traveling acoustic wave, ensuring the transducer is a one-way radiator (front-firing only). Without damping, the rear wave would reflect and create spurious modes.
> - **L6 / L7 (Electrodes):** Thin metalized surfaces where the high-voltage AC signal is applied or extracted.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A contains 5 to 8 short-answer questions across the full syllabus, each carrying 3 marks. These test *definition, working principle, and one-line comparisons*. Answers should be **3 to 5 sentences** plus a labelled diagram where applicable.

---

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define ultrasonic ranging. Mention the typical frequency range used in robotic applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ultrasonic ranging is a non-contact distance measurement technique that uses high-frequency sound waves, typically in the range of **20 kHz to 10 MHz** (well above the human audible limit), to determine the distance to a target by measuring the **time-of-flight** of the reflected echo.

In robotic applications, the most common frequency is **40 kHz**, used in low-cost modules such as the HC-SR04. This frequency offers a good compromise between directional beam focus and atmospheric absorption losses.

The fundamental equation is $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$, where $c$ is the speed of sound (~343 m/s at 20°C) and $t$ is the round-trip time.

**Marking Key:** Definition (1 mark) + Frequency range with justification (1 mark) + Equation (1 mark).

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the role of the piezoelectric effect in an ultrasonic transducer. Why is the same transducer used for both transmission and reception?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain crystals (such as PZT ceramic) to convert mechanical stress into an electrical voltage, and conversely, to deform mechanically when an electrical voltage is applied.

In an ultrasonic transducer:
- **In transmit mode:** A 40 kHz AC voltage applied across the PZT crystal causes it to vibrate mechanically at 40 kHz, generating a sound wave in air.
- **In receive mode:** An incoming 40 kHz pressure wave mechanically deforms the crystal, producing a small AC voltage that is amplified and detected.

The same transducer is used for both because the piezoelectric effect is **reversible** — the same physical property allows energy conversion in both directions. This simplifies the circuit, reduces cost, and ensures the transmitter and receiver are perfectly co-aligned on the same acoustic axis.

**Marking Key:** Piezoelectric definition (1 mark) + Transmit/receive explanation (1 mark) + Justification for shared transducer (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B contains 3 full questions (each 14 marks), and the student must answer any 2 (so 2 out of 3). Each question has internal choice — i.e., *either* (a) *or* (b) sub-parts. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Below are **Question A and Question B**, representing two independent choices from a typical Module 3 question paper.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an ultrasonic ranging system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot's ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip time of 6.5 ms. The ambient air temperature is 25°C. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Calculate the speed of sound in air.
- (ii) Determine the distance to the target.
- (iii) If the target is a cylindrical pipe of diameter 5 cm, will the sensor reliably detect it? Justify with the wavelength calculation at 40 kHz.

#### **Model Answer for A(a):**

The ultrasonic ranging system consists of the following functional blocks, as drawn in the block diagram below:

1. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU):** Generates a 10 µs trigger pulse, initiates the timer, and reads the elapsed time on echo detection. Computes the distance using $d = ct/2$.
2. **Driver / Piezo Exciter:** Converts the 5 V trigger from the MCU into a 10 V peak-to-peak, 40 kHz AC burst (typically 8 to 16 cycles) sufficient to drive the PZT transmitter into resonance.
3. **Ultrasonic Transmitter:** PZT ceramic in transmit mode. Vibrates mechanically at 40 kHz, producing an acoustic pressure wave that propagates spherically outward.
4. **Acoustic Medium (Air):** The wave travels at speed $c$ to the target and reflects. The cone of the beam (half-angle ~15°) defines the sensing volume.
5. **Target Object:** Must be larger than $\lambda/2$ for reliable specular reflection, and oriented roughly perpendicular to the beam.
6. **Ultrasonic Receiver:** PZT ceramic in receive mode. The returning pressure wave generates a small (mV-level) AC voltage.
7. **Variable Gain Amplifier:** Boosts the weak echo to a usable level (gain 40–60 dB), often with time-varying gain (TVG) to compensate for the $1/d^2$ loss.
8. **Bandpass Filter (40 kHz):** Rejects ambient noise (motor whine, hissing, etc.) and only passes the 40 kHz echo.
9. **Envelope Detector / Threshold Comparator:** Rectifies the 40 kHz AC, integrates it, and triggers the timer-stop signal when the envelope crosses a preset threshold.
10. **High-Resolution Timer:** Measures $t$ to microsecond accuracy. For example, a 1 MHz timer gives 1 µs resolution → 0.17 mm distance resolution.

**Marking Key:** Block diagram with 6+ labeled blocks (4 marks) + Working explanation of each block (3 marks).

#### **Model Answer for A(b):**

**(i) Speed of sound at 25°C:**

$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331.4 + 15.0 = 346.4 \, \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Distance to the target:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{346.4 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.2516}{2} = 1.1258 \, \text{m}$$

Rounded: $d \approx 1.13 \, \text{m}$.

**(iii) Reliability of detecting a 5 cm pipe at 40 kHz:**

Wavelength at 40 kHz:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{346.4}{40000} = 8.66 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 8.66 \, \text{mm}$$

The pipe diameter is 5 cm = 50 mm, which is approximately $50/8.66 \approx 5.77 \lambda$.

For reliable reflection, the target must be at least **larger than $\lambda/2$** to present a sufficient acoustic cross-section. Since 50 mm $\gg$ 4.33 mm (= $\lambda/2$), the pipe will be reliably detected. In fact, even a target as small as 1 cm would still be detected at 40 kHz.

However, the surface orientation matters: if the pipe is parallel to the beam axis, the reflection may not return to the sensor. The robot must approach the pipe from a perpendicular angle for reliable detection.

**Marking Key (b):** (i) Formula + substitution + answer (2 marks); (ii) Formula + substitution + answer (2 marks); (iii) Wavelength calculation (2 marks) + justification with size-vs-wavelength reasoning (1 mark).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the factors affecting the speed of sound in air. How does this affect ultrasonic ranging accuracy? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare ultrasonic ranging with infrared (IR) ranging. State two specific applications where ultrasonic is preferred over IR. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer for B(a):**

**Factors affecting the speed of sound in air:**

1. **Temperature (dominant effect):** $c$ varies linearly with $T$ as $c \approx 331.4 + 0.6T$ m/s. A 10°C change causes a ~1.8% change in $c$, directly translating to a 1.8% ranging error.

2. **Humidity:** Moist air is *less* dense than dry air (water vapor has molar mass 18 g/mol vs air's 28.97 g/mol), so $c$ is slightly *higher* in humid air. The correction is small: $c$ increases by about 0.1 m/s per 10% relative humidity change.

3. **Atmospheric Pressure:** Counterintuitively, $c$ is *independent* of pressure for an ideal gas at a fixed temperature (because both density and bulk modulus scale linearly with $P$, and they cancel). However, at very high altitudes, the lower density affects absorption.

4. **Wind:** A headwind or tailwind directly adds to or subtracts from the wave's velocity relative to the sensor. This is a moving-frame effect and can cause significant errors in outdoor mobile robots.

5. **Gas Composition:** $c$ depends on the molar mass $M$ and ratio of specific heats $\gamma$ of the gas. In a CO₂-rich environment, $c$ is lower.

**Effect on ranging accuracy:**

The ranging error is directly proportional to the error in $c$. For a temperature of 50°C (a hot warehouse roof) instead of the assumed 20°C:

$$c_{actual} = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 50 = 361.4 \, \text{m/s}$$
$$c_{assumed} = 343 \, \text{m/s}$$
$$\text{Error} = \frac{361.4 - 343}{343} \times 100\% = 5.36\%$$

A robot assuming $c = 343$ m/s in a 50°C environment would systematically underestimate distances by 5.36%. For a 1 m reading, the true distance would be 1.0536 m. For 5 m, the true distance would be 5.27 m — a 27 cm discrepancy, large enough to cause collisions.

**Mitigation:** Industrial sensors include on-board thermistors and a microcontroller lookup table to compute $c$ in real time. The HC-SR04 does not have this, which is why it is unsuitable for industrial use.

**Marking Key:** At least 3 factors with formulas (4 marks) + Quantitative impact on accuracy (2 marks) + Mitigation (1 mark).

#### **Model Answer for B(b):**

| Parameter | Ultrasonic Ranging | Infrared (IR) Ranging |
|---|---|---|
| Operating medium | Sound waves in air | Light waves (LED / laser) |
| Speed | 343 m/s | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Time-of-flight measurability | Easy (millisecond timer) | Hard (picosecond timer needed) |
| Typical range | 0.02 m – 10 m | 0.01 m – 1.5 m |
| Sensitivity to color | None | High (dark surfaces absorb IR) |
| Sensitivity to transparency | None (detects glass) | None (passes through glass) |
| Sensitivity to dust/smoke | Low (sound penetrates) | High (light scatters) |
| Cost | Very low | Low |
| Beam width | Wide (~15–30° cone) | Narrow (can be < 1° with optics) |
| Update rate | Limited by sound travel time | Very fast (limited by detector) |

**Two applications where ultrasonic is preferred:**

1. **Liquid-level sensing in chemical tanks** — the tank surface may be dark, oily, or transparent (e.g., water, oil, sulfuric acid), and the inside of the tank may have vapor. Ultrasonic works because it is unaffected by surface color, partial transparency, or vapor. IR would either be absorbed or refracted.

2. **Obstacle detection on an outdoor mobile robot in dusty/dim conditions** — a warehouse robot working at night in a dusty environment (e.g., a flour mill, cement plant). Dust scatters IR but is essentially transparent to 40 kHz sound. The robot can navigate reliably with ultrasonic when IR sensors would give false readings or fail entirely.

**Marking Key:** Comparison table (4 marks) + Two well-justified applications (3 marks).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 1–3 Marks Each:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2.** Writing $d = c \cdot t$ instead of $d = ct/2$ — a 1-mark deduction. The echo is a *round trip*.
> 2. **Using $c = 343$ m/s when temperature is given.** A 1-mark deduction. Always compute $c$ from the temperature formula first.
> 3. **Stating "ultrasonic = electromagnetic wave"** — it is a *mechanical* wave requiring a medium. This conceptual error can cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not labelling units in the final answer.** "d = 1.02" without "m" loses 0.5 marks in KTU's strict evaluation.
> 5. **Confusing frequency and wavelength.** $f$ in Hz and $\lambda$ in m are reciprocally related via $c$, not equal. A common sub-part asks for one given the other.
> 6. **Ignoring the dead time / ringing.** When asked about the *minimum measurement cycle time*, students forget to include the post-pulse dead time (~200 µs for HC-SR04) and lose a mark.
> 7. **Wrong beam-cone reasoning.** Saying "the sensor measures the *nearest* object" without acknowledging the beam-width ambiguity will lose 1 mark on conceptual questions.
> 8. **Mixing up active vs passive sensors.** Ultrasonic is *active* (it emits energy). If the question asks for *passive* sensors, the answer is microphones, IR photodiodes, or cameras — not ultrasonic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the KTU exam:**

- ☐ Ultrasonic ranging is an **active time-of-flight (ToF) sensor** using mechanical pressure waves.
- ☐ Typical operating frequency: **40 kHz** in robotics; 20 kHz – 10 MHz in general.
- ☐ Core equation: $\mathbf{d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}}$ — memorize this as the single most important formula.
- ☐ Speed of sound: $\mathbf{c \approx 331.4 + 0.6T}$ m/s, where $T$ is in °C.
- ☐ Wavelength: $\mathbf{\lambda = c / f}$. At 40 kHz, $\lambda \approx 8.6$ mm.
- ☐ The **piezoelectric effect** (Curie brothers, 1880) is the enabling physics — reversible, so one transducer does both TX and RX.
- ☐ Beam half-angle is **15° to 30°** — wide cone means **range ambiguity** is a real problem.
- ☐ Near field (Fresnel) region $N = D^2 / (4\lambda)$ is unusable for ranging; sensors are calibrated for the far field.
- ☐ Maximum unambiguous range $R_{max} = c \cdot T_{pulse} / 2$ is set by the pulse repetition interval.
- ☐ Target must be **larger than $\lambda/2$** and roughly perpendicular to the beam for reliable echo.
- ☐ Echo strength falls off as $1/d^4$ (sonar equation) — close targets dominate over far ones.
- ☐ Ultrasonic is **unaffected by color, transparency, dust, and smoke** — this is its key advantage.
- ☐ Ultrasonic **fails in vacuum** (no medium) and is **sensitive to soft/fabric surfaces** (acoustic absorption).
- ☐ HC-SR04: cheap, 2–400 cm range, 40 kHz, **no temperature compensation**.
- ☐ Industrial sensors (Pepperl+Fuchs, MassaSonic): include on-board thermistor and temperature compensation.
- ☐ Always use a **variable gain amplifier with time-varying gain (TVG)** to compensate for the $1/d^2$ geometric loss.
- ☐ Applications: AMR obstacle avoidance, parking sensors, liquid-level sensing, bottle counting, NDT flaw detection.
- ☐ Related term: **SONAR** (Sound Navigation and Ranging) — the general military/scientific name for ultrasonic/acoustic ranging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic: "P-T-B-E-T-D"**
> **P**iezoelectric → **T**imer starts → **B**urst transmitted → **E**cho returns → **T**imer stops → **D**istance calculated. If you remember this 6-letter chain, you can reconstruct the entire ranging system on the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflective Beacons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reflective Beacons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reflective Beacon (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **reflective beacon** is a passive or active landmark device that returns an incident electromagnetic signal (typically in the **infrared, visible, or near-infrared spectrum**, wavelength $\lambda \in [780\text{ nm}, 1550\text{ nm}]$) back toward its source with minimal angular dispersion, enabling a robot-mounted sensor to detect, localize, or range against it. The reflection is governed by the principle of **retroreflection**, in which the angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence measured from the normal at every point of the reflecting surface.

**Retroreflection** is the optical phenomenon in which light rays striking a surface are returned along a path parallel to their incoming path, regardless of the angle of incidence (within the device's acceptance cone). This is fundamentally different from specular reflection (mirror-like, angle of incidence = angle of reflection with respect to the normal) and diffuse reflection (Lambertian scattering).

A reflective beacon in robotic sensing can be classified along two orthogonal axes:
1. **Energy source** → **Passive** (relies on ambient/external illumination) or **Active** (contains an internal emitter, e.g., LED, laser diode)
2. **Response mechanism** → **Retroreflective** (corner-cube / microsphere) or **Modulated retroreflective** (modulates the returned signal with identification data)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 syllabus context (OERAT612 – Basics of Robot Sensing), the term **"Reflective Beacon"** is treated under landmark-based localization and optical ranging. The most common engineering implementation is the **corner-cube retroreflector** made from three mutually perpendicular mirrors, with typical acceptance angles of $\pm \mathbf{30^\circ}$ to $\pm \mathbf{45^\circ}$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving at night on a Kerala highway and you see a bicycle reflector on the road. The moment your **headlight** (source) sweeps across it, the reflector's surface **fires the light straight back into your eyes** even though you are looking at it from a sharp angle. The cyclist didn't send any signal — the reflector just *bounced your own light* back to you. That is **retroreflection**.

Now translate this to a robot:
- The robot's **emitter** (laser/IR LED) plays the role of the headlight.
- The **reflective beacon** on a wall, ceiling, or pole plays the role of the bicycle reflector.
- The robot's **photodetector** plays the role of your eyes.
- By measuring the **angle** of the returning beam, the **time of flight**, or the **signal intensity**, the robot can figure out **where the beacon is** and hence **where the robot itself is** in a global reference frame.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exam:** "Reflective beacons = *Your headlight, but on the wall*. The wall shouts back at YOU, not at the moon."

## 1.3 Core Distinctions Students Often Confuse

| Term | One-Line Distinction |
|---|---|
| **Specular reflection** | Mirror reflection; angle of incidence = angle of reflection (off the normal). |
| **Diffuse reflection** | Light scatters in all directions; surface looks equally bright from any angle. |
| **Retroreflection** | Light is sent **back along the incoming path** to the source. |
| **Active beacon** | Beacon has its own power and emits light/sound/RF. |
| **Passive reflective beacon** | Beacon has **no power**; it only returns whatever is shone on it. |
| **Modulated retroreflector** | A *semi-passive* device that modulates the returned beam (e.g., with a unique ID code). |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Retroreflection

### 2.1.1 The Three Retroreflection Mechanisms

**1. Corner-Cube Retroreflector**
A corner-cube consists of **three mutually orthogonal planar mirrors** (or equivalently, a pyramid with a trihedral apex cut off). Any incoming ray undergoes exactly **three reflections** and is returned along a vector **anti-parallel** to the incident vector. The net effect is a $180^\circ$ rotation of the wavevector, independent of the entrance angle (within the geometric acceptance cone).

The acceptance half-angle of an ideal corner-cube is given by:
$$\theta_{\text{acc}} = \arcsin\!\left(n \cdot \sin\theta_{\text{crit}}\right) - \text{(geometric clipping correction)}$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of any protective faceplate.

**2. Spherical / Cat's-Eye Retroreflector**
A glass sphere with refractive index $n \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ acts as a catadioptric lens. Rays striking the back surface are focused, reflected by a backing layer, and refracted back out, parallel to the incoming ray. Common in road studs and bicycle reflectors.

**3. Microsphere Retroreflective Sheeting**
Arrays of tiny glass beads ($\sim 50\text{ }\mu\text{m}$ diameter) embedded in a transparent polymer with a metallic backing. Widely used in tape and stickers for lab floor markers in robotics labs.

### 2.1.2 Optical Power Budget — The Radar/Beacon Equation

For a robot emitter with transmitted optical power $P_t$ and a passive retroreflective target, the **power received back at the detector** $P_r$ is governed by a *two-way* equation (unlike a one-way radar equation):

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma_{\text{opt}} \, A_r}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **fourth-power** dependence on range $R$ is the signature of passive retroreflective systems. This is why active beacons (with their own source) are preferred for **long-range localization**.

**Variable definitions (KTU exam standard):**
- $P_t$ → Transmitted optical power (W)
- $G_t$ → Gain of the transmit optics (dimensionless, often expressed in dB)
- $\sigma_{\text{opt}}$ → **Optical cross-section** of the retroreflector (m²); for a corner cube of edge $a$, $\sigma_{\text{opt}} \approx \dfrac{4\pi a^4}{3\lambda^2}$ in the limit $a \gg \lambda$
- $A_r$ → Effective aperture area of the receiver (m²)
- $R$ → Slant range between robot and beacon (m)
- $\lambda$ → Operating wavelength (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **love** asking: "Why is the range dependence $R^{-4}$ for a passive retroreflector and $R^{-2}$ for an active beacon?" The answer is in the *two-way* (out-and-back) geometry vs the *one-way* geometry.

## 2.2 Beacon-Based Robot Localization

### 2.2.1 Triangulation (Angle-of-Arrival, AOA)

If the robot can measure the **bearing angle** to two or more beacons whose global positions are known, the robot's 2-D position $(x, y)$ is found at the intersection of two lines:

$$\tan(\alpha_i) = \frac{y - y_i}{x - x_i}, \quad i = 1, 2$$

where $(x_i, y_i)$ is the known position of beacon $i$ and $\alpha_i$ is the measured bearing from the robot.

### 2.2.2 Trilateration (Range-Based)

If the robot measures the **range** $R_i$ to three or more beacons:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = R_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

This system of three equations in two unknowns $(x, y)$ is solved by linearization (subtracting one equation from the others) followed by a $2 \times 2$ linear solve.

### 2.2.3 Combined AOA + Range (Most Common in KTU Problems)

The robot is equipped with a rotating laser scanner. The angle $\theta$ at which the strongest return is detected gives the **bearing**, and the **time of flight** $\Delta t$ of the pulse gives the range:

$$R = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2}$$

where $c$ is the speed of light. Then the beacon's polar coordinates relative to the robot are $(R, \theta)$, convertible to Cartesian by:

$$x_{\text{beacon}} = x_{\text{robot}} + R\cos(\theta + \psi), \quad y_{\text{beacon}} = y_{\text{robot}} + R\sin(\theta + \psi)$$

with $\psi$ being the robot's heading angle in the global frame.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t \sigma_{\text{opt}} A_r}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$ | Two-way passive beacon equation | Power-budget / range problems |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{c \, \Delta t}{2}$ | Time-of-flight range | Pulsed IR/laser beacon |
| 3 | $\theta_{\text{bearing}} = \arctan\!\!\left(\dfrac{y_b - y_r}{x_b - x_r}\right) - \psi$ | Bearing to beacon in robot frame | Localization |
| 4 | $\sigma_{\text{opt}} \approx \dfrac{4\pi a^4}{3\lambda^2}$ | Optical cross-section of corner cube | Diffraction-limited regime |
| 5 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_r}{N_0 B}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | Detector sensitivity |
| 6 | $R_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{P_t G_t \sigma_{\text{opt}} A_r}{(4\pi)^2 P_{r,\min}}\right)^{1/4}$ | Maximum detectable range | Beacon placement design |
| 7 | $\Delta x = \dfrac{R \, d\theta}{\cos\phi}$ | Cross-range resolution | Scanner geometry |
| 8 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | Range resolution (bandwidth $B$) | Pulsed system design |
| 9 | $\text{PDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^{-1})}$ | Position Dilution of Precision | Beacon geometry optimization |
| 10 | $P_{\text{ambient}} = L \cdot A_r \cdot \Omega_r$ | Ambient noise power (radiance $L$) | SNR analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes** are used inside the table above. The absolute value $\vert x \vert$ in any cell was avoided in favor of the parenthesis convention to keep the markdown table intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in factories: reflective tape on floor + downward-pointing IR sensor on AGV (very low cost, high reliability).
- **Hospital service robots**: ceiling-mounted retroreflective bar codes (similar to UPC codes) used to identify rooms.
- **Warehouse drones (AMRs)**: ceiling-mounted passive beacons for indoor GPS-denied localization.
- **Autonomous cars (LIDAR)**: lane markings and traffic signs act as *natural* retroreflectors; the lane-detection algorithms rely on the same physics.
- **SLAM initialization**: reflective beacons are often used as **known landmarks** to bootstrap a SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) system.
