## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage-Divider Output Voltage of an LDR Interface

> **Problem Context:** A photoresistor is connected in a voltage divider with a reference resistor $R_{\text{ref}} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ across a supply $V_{cc} = 5 \text{ V}$. The LDR has a dark resistance of $1 \text{ M}\Omega$ and a light resistance (at 1000 lux) of $500 \ \Omega$. Calculate (a) the output voltage in total darkness, and (b) the output voltage under 1000 lux illumination.

**Step 1 — Recall the standard voltage divider equation.**

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{\text{LDR}}}{R_{\text{LDR}} + R_{\text{ref}}}$$

**Part (a) — Dark condition:**

**Step 2 — Substitute $R_{\text{LDR}} = R_{\text{Dark}} = 1 \times 10^6 \ \Omega$.**

$$V_{\text{out, dark}} = 5 \cdot \frac{1 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^6 + 10 \times 10^3}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator.**

$$1 \times 10^6 + 10 \times 10^3 = 1.01 \times 10^6 \ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio and final voltage.**

$$V_{\text{out, dark}} = 5 \cdot \frac{1.0}{1.01} = 5 \cdot 0.9901 = 4.951 \text{ V}$$

> **Interpretation:** In darkness, almost the full supply appears across the LDR — the ADC will read a near-maximum value, which the firmware can interpret as "ambient is dark."

**Part (b) — Illuminated condition:**

**Step 5 — Substitute $R_{\text{LDR}} = R_{\text{Light}} = 500 \ \Omega$.**

$$V_{\text{out, light}} = 5 \cdot \frac{500}{500 + 10 \times 10^3}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the denominator.**

$$500 + 10{,}000 = 10{,}500 \ \Omega$$

**Step 7 — Compute the ratio and final voltage.**

$$V_{\text{out, light}} = 5 \cdot \frac{500}{10{,}500} = 5 \cdot 0.04762 = 0.238 \text{ V}$$

> **Interpretation:** Under bright illumination, the LDR resistance collapses and almost the full supply drops across the reference resistor — the ADC reads a near-zero value, interpretable as "ambient is bright." The signal swing of nearly **4.71 V** is more than adequate for a 10-bit ADC on a 5 V reference.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity from a Resistance–Illuminance Curve

> **Problem Context:** A photoresistor datasheet provides the following two operating points: $R_1 = 9 \ \text{k}\Omega$ at $E_1 = 100$ lux, and $R_2 = 900 \ \Omega$ at $E_2 = 1000$ lux. Determine the device's sensitivity constant $k$ in S/lux, assuming the linear conductance model $G = G_0 + kE$ is valid over this range.

**Step 1 — Convert the two resistance readings to conductance.**

$$G_1 = \frac{1}{R_1} = \frac{1}{9000} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S}$$

$$G_2 = \frac{1}{R_2} = \frac{1}{900} = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear conductance model at both points.**

$$G_1 = G_0 + k \cdot E_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.111 \times 10^{-4} = G_0 + 100k$$

$$G_2 = G_0 + k \cdot E_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.111 \times 10^{-3} = G_0 + 1000k$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the first equation from the second to eliminate $G_0$.**

$$G_2 - G_1 = k \cdot (E_2 - E_1)$$

**Step 4 — Insert the numerical values.**

$$1.111 \times 10^{-3} - 1.111 \times 10^{-4} = k \cdot (1000 - 100)$$

$$1.000 \times 10^{-3} = k \cdot 900$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $k$.**

$$k = \frac{1.000 \times 10^{-3}}{900} = 1.111 \times 10^{-6} \text{ S/lux}$$

**Step 6 — Back-substitute to find $G_0$.**

$$G_0 = G_1 - 100k = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} - 100 \times 1.111 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$G_0 = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} - 1.111 \times 10^{-4} = 0 \text{ S}$$

> **Interpretation:** In this specific datasheet curve, the dark conductance is effectively zero (i.e., the dark resistance is essentially infinite compared to illuminated values), and the device is purely **ohmic-linearly photoconductive** over the operating range — an ideal scenario for linear light measurement.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Photoresistor Trade-offs

| Property | Photoresistor (CdS) | Photodiode | Phototransistor |
|----------|---------------------|------------|-----------------|
| Output type | Resistive (passive) | Current source (active) | Current source (active) |
| Sensitivity $S$ | 100 – 1000 | High | Very high |
| Response time | Slow (10 – 100 ms) | Fast (ns – µs) | Medium (µs) |
| Linearity | Poor (logarithmic) | Excellent | Moderate |
| Power requirement | None (passive) | Requires reverse bias | Requires bias |
| Cost | **Very low** | Low | Low |
| Robotics use case | Ambient light, line following | Encoders, rangefinding | Object detection, comms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Photoresistor Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Light Source] --> B[Photoresistor Active Surface]
    B --> C[Change in Resistance]
    C --> D[Voltage Divider Network]
    D --> E[Analog Signal Conditioning]
    E --> F[ADC of Microcontroller]
    F --> G[Robot Control Algorithm]
    G --> H[Actuator Response]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000000
    style G fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000000
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Photoresistor Working Principle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Photon strikes CdS lattice] --> P2{Is photon energy greater than bandgap Eg}
    P2 -- No --> P3[Photon passes through unabsorbed]
    P3 --> P1
    P2 -- Yes --> P4[Electron excited from valence band to conduction band]
    P4 --> P5[Electron hole pair generated in semiconductor]
    P5 --> P6[Free carriers increase material conductivity]
    P6 --> P7[Resistance of photoresistor decreases]
    P7 --> P8[Voltage divider output changes]
    P8 --> P9[Microcontroller reads analog voltage]
    P9 --> P10[Robot decision: dark or bright]

    style P1 fill:#FFDEAD,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
    style P2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
    style P3 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000000
    style P4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style P5 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style P6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style P7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style P8 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    style P9 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000000
    style P10 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Voltage-Divider Interface Schematic (Mermaid Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[VCC 5V Supply] --- RREF[Reference Resistor Rref 10k]
    RREF --- NODE[Output Node Vout to ADC]
    NODE --- RLDR[Photoresistor LDR]
    RLDR --- GND[Ground 0V]

    style VCC fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style RREF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    style NODE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style RLDR fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style GND fill:#696969,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.4 Line-Follower Robot Sensor Pair (Differential Sensing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LF[White Floor Surface with Black Line] --> LDR1[Left LDR Sensor]
    LF --> LDR2[Right LDR Sensor]
    LDR1 --> CMP1[Comparator or ADC Channel 1]
    LDR2 --> CMP2[Comparator or ADC Channel 2]
    CMP1 --> MCU[Microcontroller Decision Unit]
    CMP2 --> MCU
    MCU --> M1[Left Motor Driver]
    MCU --> M2[Right Motor Driver]
    M1 --> ROBOT[Robot Chassis Movement]
    M2 --> ROBOT

    style LF fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style LDR1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style LDR2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style CMP1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    style CMP2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    style MCU fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000000
    style M1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style M2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style ROBOT fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a photoresistor. List any two semiconductor materials commonly used in its fabrication and state one typical robotics application.**

**Model Answer:**

> A photoresistor, also called a Light Dependent Resistor (LDR), is a passive two-terminal photosensitive device whose electrical resistance decreases as the intensity of incident light on its active surface increases.

**Commonly used semiconductor materials:**
1. **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)** — peak sensitivity in the green region (~540 nm).
2. **Cadmium Selenide (CdSe)** — peak sensitivity in the red region (~720 nm).

**Robotic application:** Photoresistors are widely used in **line-following robots**, where a differential pair of LDRs compares reflected light from the floor to detect and track a dark path on a bright surface. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for materials, 1 for application)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between the internal and external photoelectric effects. Which one governs the operation of a photoresistor?**

**Model Answer:**

> | Feature | External Photoelectric Effect | Internal Photoelectric Effect |
> |---------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
> | Electron ejection | Electrons are emitted *out* of the material into vacuum/air | Electrons remain *within* the material, moving between energy bands |
> | Photon energy requirement | Very high (work function level, several eV) | Modest (equal to bandgap, typically 1–3 eV) |
> | Typical devices | Photo-emissive tubes, photomultipliers | Photoresistors, photodiodes, phototransistors, solar cells |
> | Vacuum required? | Yes | No |
>
> The **internal photoelectric effect** governs the operation of a photoresistor. Incident photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ excite valence-band electrons into the conduction band, generating mobile electron-hole pairs that increase the conductivity of the semiconductor and thereby reduce its electrical resistance. *(3 marks: 1 for distinction table, 1 for correct identification, 1 for justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Always state the **threshold condition** $h\nu \geq E_g$ explicitly before substituting — students commonly skip this and lose 1 mark.
> - **Unit conversions are NOT optional.** Mixing eV and joules without conversion is the single most common error. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for inconsistent units.
> - For voltage-divider problems, **draw the circuit** (or reference it clearly) — KTU examiners award at least 1 mark for a labelled diagram.
> - Do **not** confuse "sensitivity" with "sensitivity constant $k$" — they are *different* quantities. Sensitivity $S = R_{\text{Dark}}/R_{\text{Light}}$ is dimensionless; $k$ has units of S/lux.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1]`

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the construction and working principle of a photoresistor. Discuss the role of bandgap energy in determining its spectral response. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A photoresistor consists of a thin layer of polycrystalline photoconductive semiconductor — most commonly **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)** — deposited on a ceramic substrate. The active track is laid in a **zig-zag (serpentine) pattern** between two metal electrodes. This geometric arrangement maximizes the surface area exposed to light within a small package. The whole assembly is encapsulated in a transparent resin or glass window to protect the semiconductor from humidity and mechanical damage while admitting photons.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. In the **dark state**, the semiconductor has very few free charge carriers (only those generated thermally), so its resistance is extremely high (megaohms).
2. When **light of suitable wavelength** strikes the active surface, photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed.
3. Each absorbed photon **liberates an electron** from the valence band to the conduction band, creating an electron-hole pair.
4. These additional free carriers **enhance the conductivity** of the material, **reducing the resistance** to a low value (hundreds of ohms in bright light).
5. The change in resistance is detected by connecting the LDR in a **voltage divider** and reading the resulting analog voltage with a microcontroller's ADC.

**Role of Bandgap Energy (2 marks):**
The **bandgap energy $E_g$** is the minimum photon energy required to liberate a valence electron. Photons with energy below $E_g$ pass through the material without being absorbed and therefore cannot generate carriers. The **threshold wavelength** $\lambda_c$ is given by:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

For CdS with $E_g = 2.42$ eV, $\lambda_c \approx 513$ nm. Hence CdS photoresistors are most sensitive to blue-green light and are essentially blind to red and infrared radiation. Different materials (CdSe, PbS) are selected when sensitivity to other spectral regions is required.

---

**(b) A photoresistor is connected in a voltage divider with a 22 kΩ reference resistor across a 5 V supply. The dark resistance of the LDR is 2 MΩ, and at an illuminance of 500 lux its resistance drops to 1.1 kΩ. Calculate: (i) the output voltage in darkness, (ii) the output voltage at 500 lux, and (iii) the photosensitivity of the device. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Equation:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{\text{LDR}}}{R_{\text{LDR}} + R_{\text{ref}}}$$

**(i) Output voltage in darkness (2 marks):**

$$V_{\text{out, dark}} = 5 \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6 + 22 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_{\text{out, dark}} = 5 \cdot \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{2{,}022{,}000} = 5 \cdot 0.9891 = 4.946 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Output voltage at 500 lux (2 marks):**

$$V_{\text{out, light}} = 5 \cdot \frac{1.1 \times 10^3}{1.1 \times 10^3 + 22 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_{\text{out, light}} = 5 \cdot \frac{1{,}100}{23{,}100} = 5 \cdot 0.04762 = 0.238 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Photosensitivity (3 marks):**

$$S = \frac{R_{\text{Dark}}}{R_{\text{Light}}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{1.1 \times 10^3} = 1818.18$$

**Final Answer Summary:** $V_{\text{out, dark}} = 4.946 \text{ V}$, $V_{\text{out, light}} = 0.238 \text{ V}$, $S \approx 1818$. *(The voltage swing of ~4.7 V is more than adequate for a 5 V ADC.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2]`

**(a) With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the application of photoresistive sensors in a differential light-tracking robotic system. Explain why a differential pair is preferred over a single photoresistor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Light Source e.g. Sun] --> L1[LDR 1 Left]
    SUN --> L2[LDR 2 Right]
    L1 --> DIV1[Voltage Divider 1]
    L2 --> DIV2[Voltage Divider 2]
    DIV1 --> ADC1[ADC Channel Left]
    DIV2 --> ADC2[ADC Channel Right]
    ADC1 --> CTRL[Motor Control Algorithm]
    ADC2 --> CTRL
    CTRL --> M_LEFT[Left Servo Motor]
    CTRL --> M_RIGHT[Right Servo Motor]
    M_LEFT --> PANEL[Solar Panel Orientation]
    M_RIGHT --> PANEL

    style SUN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style L1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style L2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style DIV1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    style DIV2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    style ADC1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000000
    style ADC2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000000
    style CTRL fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000000
    style M_LEFT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style M_RIGHT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style PANEL fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
```

**Working Description (2 marks):**
Two photoresistors (LDR 1 on the left, LDR 2 on the right) are mounted on the robot's payload (e.g., a solar panel) such that a **light-deflecting septum** casts an identical shadow on both when the panel is perfectly aligned with the light source. Each LDR is in its own voltage divider, producing two analog voltages $V_{\text{out,1}}$ and $V_{\text{out,2}}$. The microcontroller computes the **difference** $\Delta V = V_{\text{out,1}} - V_{\text{out,2}}$ and drives two servo motors to rotate the panel until $\Delta V = 0$, at which point the panel is normal to the light source.

**Why a Differential Pair? (2 marks):**
1. **Common-mode rejection of ambient fluctuations:** A single LDR cannot distinguish between "the sun moved" and "a cloud passed by." A differential pair responds only to *directional* changes, cancelling out uniform ambient variations.
2. **Improved accuracy and zero-point stability:** The null point ($\Delta V = 0$) is well-defined and stable, eliminating the need for absolute calibration of each sensor.
3. **Higher sensitivity to small angular errors:** The slope of $\Delta V$ versus angle is steeper than the slope of a single channel versus angle, giving finer pointing resolution.

---

**(b) The datasheet of a CdS photoresistor provides the following calibration data: at 10 lux the resistance is 9 kΩ and at 100 lux the resistance is 900 Ω. Assuming the device obeys the linear conductance model $G = G_0 + kE$, determine (i) the sensitivity constant $k$, (ii) the dark conductance $G_0$, and (iii) the expected resistance at 250 lux. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert resistances to conductances (1 mark):**

$$G_{10} = \frac{1}{9000} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S}$$

$$G_{100} = \frac{1}{900} = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S}$$

**Step 2 — Set up the two-equation system using the linear model (1 mark):**

$$1.111 \times 10^{-4} = G_0 + 10k \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$1.111 \times 10^{-3} = G_0 + 100k \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract (i) from (ii) to find $k$ (2 marks):**

$$(1.111 \times 10^{-3}) - (1.111 \times 10^{-4}) = (100 - 10)k$$

$$1.000 \times 10^{-3} = 90k$$

$$k = 1.111 \times 10^{-5} \text{ S/lux}$$

**Step 4 — Back-substitute to find $G_0$ (1 mark):**

$$G_0 = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} - 10 \times 1.111 \times 10^{-5} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} - 1.111 \times 10^{-4} = 0 \text{ S}$$

**Step 5 — Predict the resistance at 250 lux (2 marks):**

First, find conductance:

$$G_{250} = G_0 + k \cdot 250 = 0 + 1.111 \times 10^{-5} \times 250 = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S}$$

Then, convert back to resistance:

$$R_{250} = \frac{1}{G_{250}} = \frac{1}{2.778 \times 10^{-3}} = 360 \ \Omega$$

**Final Answer Summary:** $k = 1.111 \times 10^{-5}$ S/lux, $G_0 = 0$ S, $R_{250} \approx 360 \ \Omega$.

> **Engineering Insight:** Because $G_0 = 0$, this device is *ideal* in the linear region — its resistance scales as $1/E$. A robotic light-meter built around this sensor can use a simple reciprocal-calculation routine in firmware, avoiding the need for lookup tables.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Photoresistive Devices**

- **Definition:** A **photoresistor (LDR)** is a passive, two-terminal device whose **resistance decreases** as incident light intensity **increases**.
- **Operating principle:** **Internal photoelectric effect** — photons with $h\nu \geq E_g$ generate electron-hole pairs in a photoconductive semiconductor.
- **Most common material:** **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)** with $E_g \approx 2.42$ eV, peaking at $\lambda \approx 540$ nm (green).
- **Other materials:** **CdSe** (red-sensitive, ~720 nm), **PbS** (infrared, ~2 µm).
- **Threshold wavelength formula:** $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$ — *always* state this before substituting values.
- **Conductance model:** $G = G_0 + k \cdot E$ — linear in illuminance.
- **Resistance model:** $R = \dfrac{1}{G_0 + k \cdot E}$ — hyperbolic in illuminance.
- **Photosensitivity (figure of merit):** $S = \dfrac{R_{\text{Dark}}}{R_{\text{Light}}}$, typically **100 to 1000** for CdS.
- **Voltage-divider interface:** $V_{\text{out}} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{\text{LDR}}}{R_{\text{LDR}} + R_{\text{ref}}}$ — *always* draw the circuit.
- **Dark resistance range:** **1 MΩ to 100 MΩ**.
- **Light resistance (at 1000 lux):** **few hundred Ω to few kΩ**.
- **Response time:** Slow — $\tau_r \approx 5$–$100$ ms, $\tau_f \approx 10$–$500$ ms. **Cannot be used for high-speed optical comms.**
- **Spectral range:** Visible (400–700 nm) for CdS/CdSe; near-IR (1–3 µm) for PbS.
- **Spectral response is non-uniform** — devices are *blind* to wavelengths longer than $\lambda_c$.
- **Non-linearity:** Resistance is logarithmic-like over a wide illuminance range — the linear conductance model is an approximation valid only over limited regions.
- **Polarization:** A photoresistor is **non-polarized** — it can be connected either way in a circuit.
- **Hysteresis & memory effect:** Long-term light exposure slightly alters the resistance — calibrate periodically in precision robotic instruments.
- **Temperature dependence:** Resistance varies with temperature even at constant illumination — important in outdoor robotics.
- **Comparison corner:** Photoresistor (slow, passive, cheap) vs. Photodiode (fast, active, linear) vs. Phototransistor (sensitive, active, moderate speed) — choose based on the **bandwidth, cost, and signal-conditioning** requirements of the robotic subsystem.
- **Robotic applications:** Line-following, light-seeking, solar tracking, automatic lighting, flame detection, simple optical encoders, twilight detectors for AGV safety systems.
- **Always convert units:** $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J — this conversion **must** appear in numerical solutions involving photon energy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photodiodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Photodiodes # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Photodiode (Official KTU 2024 Definition):** > A **photodiode** is a solid-state, reverse-biased PN-junction semiconductor device that converts incident optical radiation (photons) into an electrical current through the **internal photoelectric effect**. The magnitude of the generated photocurrent is directly proportional to the incident optical power, making it a fundamental **light-to-electrical transduction element** in robotic vision, LIDAR, and proximity-sensing systems. The photodiode is one of the most widely deployed photonic sensors in robotics due to its **fast response time (nanosecond range)**, **high sensitivity**, **compact form factor**, and **compatibility with standard CMOS fabrication processes**. ### 1.1.1 Core Photodiode Symbol & Terminal Convention | Terminal | Symbol | Function | |----------|--------|----------| | Anode (A) | + terminal | Connected to lower potential (N-side for PN) | | Cathode (K) | − terminal | Connected to higher potential | | Optical Window | — | Top-surface entry point for incident photons | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: "The Rain Gauge on a Tilted Roof"** > Imagine a sloped rooftop with a measuring bucket (the **depletion region**). Raindrops (representing **photons**) fall on the roof. The number of raindrops collected is **counted** by a flow meter (representing the **photocurrent**). When the rain is heavy (bright light), the meter reads high. When the rain is light (dim light), the meter reads low. Importantly, the roof is angled such that even small drips are funneled efficiently into the bucket — this is exactly the role of the **depletion region** in a photodiode: it captures and converts incoming photons into measurable electron flow. This analogy captures three crucial engineering truths: 1. **Proportionality**: Output current ∝ light intensity (linear transduction) 2. **Spectral Selectivity**: Each "raindrop size" (photon wavelength) is filtered by the **bandgap energy (Eg)** of the semiconductor 3. **Speed**: Electron transit through the depletion region is extremely fast — like water flowing down a slope (≈ nanoseconds) ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Material Parameters The following constants are **mandatory** for KTU numerical problems: | Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit | |----------|--------|-------|------| | Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s | | Speed of light | $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s | | Electron charge | $q$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C | | Boltzmann constant | $k$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K | | Silicon bandgap (300 K) | $E_g^{Si}$ | **1.12** | eV | | Germanium bandgap (300 K) | $E_g^{Ge}$ | **0.67** | eV | | GaAs bandgap (300 K) | $E_g^{GaAs}$ | **1.42** | eV | > [!TIP] > **Cut-off Wavelength Reminder:** A photodiode can only detect photons whose energy is ≥ bandgap energy: $\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$. Photons below this threshold (longer wavelengths) pass through unabsorbed — a critical limit for **spectral range** in robotic vision. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step The photodiode operates on the **internal photoelectric effect**, governed by three sequential physical processes: ### Step 1: Photon Absorption - An incident photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ strikes the semiconductor lattice. - If $E_{ph} \geq E_g$ (bandgap energy), the photon is absorbed and excites a **valence-band electron** into the **conduction band**, generating an **electron–hole pair (EHP)**. ### Step 2: Carrier Separation (Drift in Depletion Region) - The reverse-bias voltage widens the **depletion region** (also called the **space-charge region**). - The strong built-in electric field $\mathcal{E}$ within this region **sweeps** electrons toward the N-side and holes toward the P-side *before* they can recombine. - This swept charge constitutes the **primary photocurrent**. ### Step 3: Carrier Collection (Diffusion Outside Depletion) - EHPs generated in the neutral P and N regions contribute a slower, **diffusion-based** photocurrent component. - These carriers must diffuse to the depletion edge before being collected. - This is why **minimizing neutral-region thickness** and **maximizing depletion width** improves speed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Reverse Bias?** > Reverse bias is mandatory because: > 1. It **widens the depletion region**, increasing the absorption volume. > 2. It provides a **strong drift field** for fast carrier collection. > 3. It **suppresses majority-carrier (dark) current flow** in the forward direction. > 4. It keeps the device in a **high-impedance, low-noise** regime. ## 2.2 Modes of Operation | Mode | Bias Condition | Speed | Linearity | Noise | Application | |------|----------------|-------|-----------|-------|-------------| | **Photovoltaic** | Zero bias (V = 0) | Slow | Excellent | Very low | Solar cells, precision light meters | | **Photoconductive** | Reverse bias | **Fast** | Good | Higher | Robotic light sensors, encoders, LIDAR | > [!WARNING] > In **photoconductive mode**, the dark current is non-zero and must be subtracted during signal conditioning. In **photovoltaic mode**, the device behaves as a current source in parallel with a forward-biased diode (the classic solar-cell model). ## 2.3 Equivalent Circuit Model (KTU High-Yield) A photodiode can be modeled as: $$I_{total} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right) - I_{shunt}$$ Where: - $I_{ph}$ = generated photocurrent (proportional to optical power) - $I_0$ = reverse saturation (dark) current - $I_{shunt}$ = leakage current through shunt resistance ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Photon energy | $E_{ph} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | J or eV | Foundation formula | | 2 | Cut-off wavelength | $\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$ | m | Longest detectable λ | | 3 | Photocurrent | $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$ | A | R is responsivity | | 4 | **Responsivity** | $R = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | A/W | **Most important formula** | | 5 | Quantum efficiency | $\eta = \frac{N_{e^{-}}}{N_{ph}} = \frac{R \cdot hc}{q\lambda}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | ≤ 100% (ideally) | | 6 | Photon flux | $\Phi = \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu}$ | photons/s | Used in η calculations | | 7 | Rise time | $t_r \approx \frac{0.35}{BW}$ | s | −3 dB bandwidth relation | | 8 | Bandwidth limit | $BW = \frac{1}{2\pi R_L C_j}$ | Hz | $C_j$ = junction capacitance | | 9 | Noise Equivalent Power | $NEP = \frac{V_n}{R_v} = \frac{i_n}{R}$ | W/√Hz | Min. detectable power | | 10 | Detectivity | $D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A}}{NEP}$ | cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W | Normalized figure of merit | | 11 | Dark current | $I_d = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right)$ | A | Schottky diode equation | | 12 | Multiplication (APD) | $M = \frac{I_{ph}^{APD}}{I_{ph}^{primary}}$ | dimensionless | Avalanche gain | > [!CAUTION] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **luminous flux (lumens)** with **radiant flux (watts)**. Robotic sensing always uses **radiometric** (W) units, not photometric (lm). KTU examiners frequently include a 1-mark trap on this distinction. ## 2.5 Real-World Robotic Applications | Robotic Subsystem | Photodiode Role | Key Property Used | |-------------------|-----------------|-------------------| | **Line-following robot** | Detects reflective contrast | Fast response, high sensitivity | | **LIDAR / Time-of-Flight** | Receives pulsed laser echoes | Nanosecond rise time | | **Proximity sensor** | Detects IR reflection | Spectral response in IR band | | **Optical encoder** | Reads wheel rotation slots | High bandwidth | | **3D Vision (Structured Light)** | Receives projected pattern | Spatial resolution / array form | | **Solar-powered robots** | Energy harvesting | High efficiency (PV mode) | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Cut-off Wavelength from Bandgap Energy > **Objective:** Derive the maximum wavelength ($\lambda_c$) a photodiode can detect, given the semiconductor bandgap. ### Step 1: Threshold Condition For a photon to be absorbed, its energy must equal or exceed the bandgap: $$E_{ph} \geq E_g$$ ### Step 2: Express Photon Energy Using the Planck–Einstein relation: $$E_{ph} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute and Solve for λ Setting $E_{ph} = E_g$ (the threshold case) and solving for the longest detectable wavelength: $$\frac{hc}{\lambda_c} = E_g$$ $$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate for Silicon (E_g = 1.12 eV) Convert $E_g$ to Joules: $E_g = 1.12 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 1.794 \times 10^{-19}$ J $$\lambda_c^{Si} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{1.794 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$\lambda_c^{Si} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.794 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_c^{Si} \approx 1.108 \; \mu m}$$ > **Physical Interpretation:** Silicon photodiodes are sensitive to the **visible spectrum (0.4–0.7 μm) and near-IR up to ~1.1 μm**. This is why they cannot detect thermal-IR (8–14 μm) — GaAs or InGaAs sensors must be used instead. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Responsivity from Quantum Efficiency > **Objective:** Establish the relationship between the **quantum efficiency (η)** and the **responsivity (R)**. ### Step 1: Define Quantum Efficiency The number of collected electrons per incident photon: $$\eta = \frac{\text{electrons/sec collected}}{\text{photons/sec incident}} = \frac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{opt}/(h\nu)}$$ ### Step 2: Rearrange for Photocurrent Multiply both sides by $I_{ph}$ definition: $$I_{ph} = \eta \cdot q \cdot \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu}$$ ### Step 3: Recognize that $P_{opt}/(h\nu) = \Phi$ (photon flux) But more usefully, express $P_{opt}$ in terms of wavelength using $P = E \cdot \Phi$: $$I_{ph} = \eta \cdot q \cdot \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$ ### Step 4: Compare with $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$ Equating the two expressions for $I_{ph}$: $$R \cdot P_{opt} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} \cdot P_{opt}$$ $$\boxed{R = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}}$$ > **Physical Interpretation:** Responsivity is **linearly proportional to wavelength (λ)** for an ideal photodiode. Therefore, a Si photodiode has higher R in the red (0.7 μm) than in the blue (0.4 μm), even though photon energy is lower in the red. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) > **Problem:** A silicon PIN photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 80% at $\lambda = 900$ nm. The incident optical power is 50 μW. Compute the photocurrent and the responsivity. ### Given: - $\eta = 0.80$ - $\lambda = 900 \text{ nm} = 9 \times 10^{-7}$ m - $P_{opt} = 50 \; \mu W = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ W - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s - $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s - $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C ### Step 1: Compute Responsivity $$R = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} = \frac{(0.80)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(9 \times 10^{-7})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}$$ Numerator: $$(0.80)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(9 \times 10^{-7}) = 1.1534 \times 10^{-25}$$ Denominator: $$(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8}) = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$$ $$R = \frac{1.1534 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.5802 \; A/W$$ ### Step 2: Compute Photocurrent $$I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = (0.5802 \; A/W)(5 \times 10^{-5} \; W)$$ $$\boxed{I_{ph} = 2.901 \times 10^{-5} \; A = 29.01 \; \mu A}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Noise Equivalent Power (NEP) NEP is defined as the **incident optical power** required to produce a **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of unity** in a **1 Hz bandwidth**. ### Step 1: Definition by Signal = Noise Condition At NEP, the photocurrent equals the noise current: $$I_{ph} = i_n \quad \text{(in 1 Hz BW)}$$ ### Step 2: Express Photocurrent in Optical Power Using $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$: $$R \cdot P_{NEP} = i_n \quad \text{(at 1 Hz BW)}$$ ### Step 3: Solve for NEP $$\boxed{NEP = \frac{i_n}{R}} \quad \text{(W / √Hz)}$$ > **Interpretation:** Lower NEP = more sensitive detector. High-performance Si PIN photodiodes achieve NEP ≈ $10^{-14}$ W/√Hz. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Key Trade-offs in Photodiode Selection | Parameter | PIN Photodiode | Avalanche Photodiode (APD) | Schottky Photodiode | Phototransistor | |-----------|---------------|---------------------------|---------------------|-----------------| | **Internal Gain** | None (M = 1) | High (M = 10–1000) | None | Very high (β = 100+) | | **Speed** | Fast (ns) | Moderate (sub-ns at high V) | Very fast (ps) | Slow (μs) | | **Noise** | Low | Higher (excess noise factor) | Low | High | | **Bias Voltage** | Low (5–30 V) | High (100–400 V) | Low | Low (V_CC) | | **Sensitivity** | Moderate | **Very high** | Moderate | Very high | | **Cost** | Low | High | Moderate | Very low | | **Best for Robots** | Line sensors, encoders | LIDAR, faint signals | High-speed pulse detection | Low-cost proximity | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Photodiode Operational Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Light Source
Photon Flux Phi] --> B[Optical Window
Anti-Reflection Coating] B --> C[PN Junction
Depletion Region] C --> D[Reverse Bias Supply
V_R 5V to 30V] D --> E[Photocurrent I_ph
Proportional to P_opt] E --> F[Transimpedance Amplifier
TIA Stage] F --> G[Signal Conditioning
Filter and ADC] G --> H[Microcontroller
Robot Decision Logic] H --> I[Robot Actuator
Motor Servo] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Photodiode Internal Structure (PIN) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PRegion["P+ Region (Heavily Doped P)"] P1[Anode Contact] end subgraph Intrinsic["Intrinsic Region (Depletion Zone)"] I1[Wide Space Charge Region] I2[High Electric Field Drift] I3[Primary Photon Absorption] end subgraph NRegion["N Region (Lightly Doped N)"] N1[Cathode Contact] N2[Reverse Bias Applied Here] end P1 --> I1 I1 --> I2 I2 --> I3 I3 --> N1 N2 --> I1 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Robotic Light-Sensing System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input["PHYSICAL INPUT LAYER"] L1[Incident Light Photons] L2[Target Object Reflectance] end subgraph Sensor["PHOTODIODE SENSOR LAYER"] S1[Photodiode PN Junction] S2[Responsivity R Conversion] S3[Dark Current Subtraction] end subgraph Signal["SIGNAL PROCESSING LAYER"] P1[Transimpedance Amplifier] P2[Low-Pass Anti-Alias Filter] P3[12-bit ADC Sampler] end subgraph Decision["ROBOT CONTROL LAYER"] D1[Threshold Comparator] D2[Obstacle Avoidance Algorithm] D3[Motor Driver PWM] end L1 --> S1 L2 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 ``` ## 4.4 Photodiode Bias Configurations (Mermaid Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PV["PHOTOVOLTAIC MODE (Zero Bias)"] PV1[Photodiode Symbol Anode to GND] PV2[Cathode to Amplifier Positive Input] PV3[Output: Open Circuit Voltage V_oc] end subgraph PC["PHOTOCONDUCTIVE MODE (Reverse Bias)"] PC1[Photodiode Anode to GND] PC2[Reverse Bias V_R Applied to Cathode] PC3[Output: Photocurrent via TIA] end PV --> Q{Application?} Q -->|Precision| PV Q -->|Speed| PC ``` > [!TIP] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In **photovoltaic mode**, the photodiode generates a small open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ (logarithmic with light intensity) — ideal for light meters. In **photoconductive mode**, the output is a **linear current** — preferred in robotic vision systems. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Define a photodiode. With a neat sketch, explain its principle of operation.** **Model Answer:** A photodiode is a reverse-biased PN-junction semiconductor device that converts incident light into an electrical current via the **internal photoelectric effect**. **Operation:** 1. Photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strike the depletion region. 2. They generate **electron-hole pairs (EHPs)**. 3. The strong reverse-bias electric field sweeps electrons toward the N-side and holes toward the P-side. 4. The resulting current is proportional to the incident optical power: $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$. **Sketch (verbal):** A P-N junction with reverse-bias battery, terminals marked Anode (P) and Cathode (N), incident photons shown entering from the top with an optical window. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q: Define responsivity and quantum efficiency of a photodiode. State their SI units.** **Model Answer:** | Quantity | Definition | Formula | Unit | |----------|------------|---------|------| | **Responsivity (R)** | Ratio of output photocurrent to incident optical power | $R = I_{ph}/P_{opt}$ | **A/W** | | **Quantum Efficiency (η)** | Ratio of collected electrons to incident photons | $\eta = (I_{ph}/q)/(P_{opt}/h\nu)$ | **dimensionless (0–1)** | **Relationship:** $R = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** **Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a PIN photodiode. Why is the intrinsic layer introduced? **(7 Marks)** **Q: (b)** A silicon PIN photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 65% at 850 nm. The incident optical power is 100 μW. Calculate: (i) The responsivity of the photodiode. (ii) The output photocurrent. (iii) The number of photons incident per second. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — PIN Photodiode Construction and Working **Construction:** A PIN photodiode consists of three regions: 1. **P+ region** (heavily doped P-type, very thin) 2. **Intrinsic (I) region** (lightly doped or pure semiconductor, wide) 3. **N region** (lightly doped N-type) The intrinsic region is sandwiched between the P and N regions, hence the name **P-I-N**. **Working:** - Under reverse bias, the entire intrinsic region becomes **fully depleted** of charge carriers. - This creates a **wide, uniform electric field** that spans the I-region. - Photons enter through the anti-reflection-coated top window and are predominantly absorbed in the **wide intrinsic region**. - The generated EHPs are **swiftly separated by the strong drift field**, giving fast response (nanosecond regime). - The P+ and N regions are thin to minimize diffusion delay. **Why Intrinsic Layer?** 1. **Increases depletion width** → higher absorption efficiency, especially for longer-wavelength (red/IR) photons that penetrate deeper. 2. **Reduces junction capacitance** $C_j \propto 1/W$ → increases bandwidth and speed. 3. **Lowers transit time** of carriers across the depletion region. 4. **Improves quantum efficiency** by capturing more photons before they exit the device. --- #### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical **Given:** - $\eta = 0.65$ - $\lambda = 850$ nm $= 8.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m - $P_{opt} = 100 \; \mu W = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ W **Part (i): Responsivity** $$R = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} = \frac{(0.65)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(8.5 \times 10^{-7})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}$$ Numerator: $(0.65)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(8.5 \times 10^{-7}) = 8.851 \times 10^{-26}$ Denominator: $(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8}) = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$ $$R = \frac{8.851 \times 10^{-26}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.4452 \; A/W$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 0.445 \; A/W}$$ **Part (ii): Photocurrent** $$I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = (0.4452 \; A/W)(1 \times 10^{-4} \; W)$$ $$\boxed{I_{ph} = 4.452 \times 10^{-5} \; A = 44.52 \; \mu A}$$ **Part (iii): Photons per Second** Photon energy: $$E_{ph} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{8.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.339 \times 10^{-19} \; J$$ Photon flux: $$\Phi = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{ph}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{2.339 \times 10^{-19}} = 4.275 \times 10^{14} \; \text{photons/s}$$ $$\boxed{\Phi \approx 4.28 \times 10^{14} \; \text{photons/second}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3]** **Q: (a)** Explain the working of an Avalanche Photodiode (APD) with a neat diagram. How does it differ from a PIN photodiode? **(7 Marks)** **Q: (b)** A photodiode has a responsivity of 0.5 A/W at 600 nm. If the incident light intensity is 2 mW/cm² on a sensor area of 4 mm², compute: (i) The optical power incident on the active area. (ii) The generated photocurrent. (iii) The quantum efficiency at this wavelength. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — APD **Working of APD:** An Avalanche Photodiode operates under **high reverse bias** (close to the breakdown voltage). The construction is similar to a PIN, but with an additional **multiplication region** where impact ionization occurs. **Sequence of events:** 1. Photon enters and is absorbed in the absorption region, generating a primary EHP. 2. The photogenerated carrier (usually the electron) is accelerated by the high field in the multiplication region. 3. It gains sufficient kinetic energy to **ionize lattice atoms** on collision, creating a **secondary EHP**. 4. These secondary carriers, in turn, ionize further atoms, producing a **self-sustaining avalanche**. 5. The total photocurrent is amplified by a multiplication factor $M$ (typically 10 to 1000): $$I_{ph}^{APD} = M \cdot I_{ph}^{primary}$$ **Diagram (verbal):** Shows P+ / Intrinsic / P / N+ layers, with the multiplication (avalanche) zone highlighted, and a high reverse bias supply. **Differences from PIN:** | Feature | PIN | APD | |---------|-----|-----| | Internal gain | None (M = 1) | High (M = 10–1000) | | Reverse bias | Moderate (5–30 V) | High (100–400 V) | | Sensitivity | Moderate | Very high | | Noise | Low | Higher (excess noise factor) | | Speed | Very fast | Slightly slower (due to avalanche build-up) | | Application | General sensing | LIDAR, faint-signal detection | --- #### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical **Given:** - $R = 0.5$ A/W - $\lambda = 600$ nm $= 6 \times 10^{-7}$ m - Light intensity $I_{opt} = 2$ mW/cm² $= 0.2$ W/m² - Active area $A = 4$ mm² $= 4 \times 10^{-6}$ m² **Part (i): Optical Power** $$P_{opt} = I_{opt} \times A = (0.2 \; W/m^2)(4 \times 10^{-6} \; m^2)$$ $$\boxed{P_{opt} = 8 \times 10^{-7} \; W = 0.8 \; \mu W}$$ **Part (ii): Photocurrent** $$I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = (0.5 \; A/W)(8 \times 10^{-7} \; W)$$ $$\boxed{I_{ph} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \; A = 0.4 \; \mu A}$$ **Part (iii): Quantum Efficiency** Rearranging $R = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$: $$\eta = \frac{R \cdot hc}{q \lambda} = \frac{(0.5)(1.9878 \times 10^{-25})}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(6 \times 10^{-7})}$$ $$\eta = \frac{9.939 \times 10^{-26}}{9.612 \times 10^{-26}} = 1.034$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Critical Sign Convention:** Quantum efficiency **cannot exceed 1.0 (100%)** in physical reality. If your calculation gives $\eta > 1$, the given data is **physically inconsistent** (likely a textbook error). In such cases, write: *"η ≈ 1.034 indicates the device operates beyond ideal quantum limit; practical η must be capped at 1.0."* and proceed. **Do not silently skip this — KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark for not flagging the inconsistency.** > > **Other Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. Mixing up **eV and Joules** for $E_g$ when computing $\lambda_c$. > 2. Forgetting to convert **mm² to m²** in area-based questions. > 3. Using **forward-bias** equations (Shockley) instead of the photodiode's reverse-bias photocurrent model. > 4. Confusing **spectral responsivity** (A/W at a specific λ) with **broadband responsivity**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** ### Core Concepts - [x] A photodiode is a **reverse-biased PN junction** that converts photons into photocurrent. - [x] Operates on the **internal photoelectric effect** ($h\nu \geq E_g$). - [x] Two modes: **Photovoltaic (zero bias)** and **Photoconductive (reverse bias)**. - [x] Photoconductive mode is **faster**; photovoltaic mode has **lower noise**. ### Top 5 Must-Memorize Formulas 1. $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$ → **cut-off wavelength** 2. $R = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ → **responsivity** 3. $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$ → **photocurrent** 4. $\eta = \dfrac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{opt}/h\nu}$ → **quantum efficiency** 5. $NEP = \dfrac{i_n}{R}$ → **noise equivalent power** ### Photodiode Family — Key Distinctions - **PN photodiode**: simple, slow - **PIN photodiode**: intrinsic layer → fast, high responsivity - **APD (Avalanche)**: internal gain M → high sensitivity, high bias - **Schottky photodiode**: metal-semiconductor junction → ultra-fast (ps) - **Phototransistor**: very high gain, slow, cheap ### Material vs. Spectral Range | Material | $E_g$ (eV) | $\lambda_c$ (μm) | Spectral Band | |----------|------------|------------------|---------------| | Silicon (Si) | 1.12 | **1.11** | Visible + near-IR | | Germanium (Ge) | 0.67 | **1.85** | Near-IR | | GaAs | 1.42 | **0.87** | Visible + near-IR | | InGaAs | 0.75 | **1.65** | Telecom (1300–1550 nm) | | HgCdTe | tunable | 2–14 | Thermal IR | ### Robotic Engineering Highlights - **Line-following robots** → Si PIN photodiode, IR LED pair, contrast detection. - **LIDAR** → APD or Si PIN, sub-ns pulse detection. - **3D ToF cameras** → arrayed photodiodes, modulated light source. - **Solar-powered robots** → PV mode, Si solar cells (large area). ### Final KTU Tips - Always include **units** in numerical answers. - Always show the **formula substitution step** explicitly. - Memorize the values of $h, c, q$ to 3 significant figures. - Practice at least 3 numerical problems from the past 3 KTU papers. - Draw **neat, labeled circuit diagrams** with reverse-bias polarity clearly marked.

topics.title: Phototransistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phototransistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **phototransistor** is a semiconductor light-sensitive optoelectronic device that converts incident optical (photon) energy into an amplified electrical current at its output terminals. Structurally, it is a bipolar junction transistor (BJT — typically NPN) in which the base region is exposed to incident light through a transparent window in the encapsulation. The photons striking the base–collector depletion region generate electron–hole pairs, which act as a photogenerated base current. This current is then amplified by the transistor's inherent current gain ($\beta$ or $h_{FE}$), producing a collector current that is typically **100 to 1000 times larger** than what a similarly sized photodiode would produce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A phototransistor is functionally equivalent to a **photodiode + transistor amplifier** integrated into a single semiconductor die. The transistor action provides inherent internal gain, making phototransistors ideal for low-light detection applications in robotic sensing systems.

### 1.2 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Wavelength of peak spectral response ($\lambda_p$):** typically **800 nm – 900 nm** (near-infrared region)
- **Rise time / Fall time ($t_r, t_f$):** typically **2 µs – 50 µs** (slower than photodiodes due to base–collector capacitance)
- **Dark current ($I_D$):** typically **1 nA – 100 nA** at $V_{CE} = 10\,\text{V}$
- **Collector–Emitter Breakdown Voltage ($BV_{CEO}$):** typically **20 V – 50 V**
- **Responsivity ($\mathcal{R}$):** typically **0.1 A/W – 0.5 A/W** at $\lambda_p$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Light-Controlled Water Tap"**
>
> Imagine a regular garden water tap (the transistor's base–emitter junction) that controls a large industrial water pipe (the collector–emitter path). Normally, a small twist of the handle (base current $I_B$) releases a torrent of water (collector current $I_C$).
>
> In a **phototransistor**, we have **removed the handle entirely** and replaced it with a **sunlight sensor**. The brighter the light falling on the sensor, the more "twist" is automatically applied to the tap, and the more current flows between collector and emitter. No external base wire is needed — light itself becomes the control input. This is why phototransistors are so widely used in robotic line-following and proximity sensors: they are essentially "self-modulating" current sources driven by photons.

### 1.4 Distinction from a Photodiode

| Feature | Photodiode | Phototransistor |
|---|---|---|
| Internal gain | **Unity (×1)** | **$\beta$ (×100 to ×1000)** |
| Response speed | Fast (ns) | Slower (µs) |
| Output signal | Small (µA) | Large (mA) |
| External circuit | Needs op-amp | Direct digital interface |
| Linearity | Highly linear | Moderately linear |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Photon Incidence:** Light of wavelength $\lambda$ (with photon energy $E_p = hc/\lambda$) strikes the exposed base–collector depletion region of the NPN transistor.
2. **Photogeneration:** If $E_p \geq E_g$ (the semiconductor bandgap, e.g., **1.12 eV for Si**), photons are absorbed and create **electron–hole pairs**.
3. **Carrier Separation:** The strong electric field in the reverse-biased base–collector junction sweeps electrons toward the collector and holes toward the base.
4. **Photocurrent at Base–Collector:** The separated carriers form a primary photocurrent $I_{ph}$ proportional to the incident optical power $P_{in}$.
5. **Transistor Amplification:** The photogenerated holes accumulating in the base act as a virtual **base current** $I_{B,ph}$. By BJT action, the collector current is amplified:
   $$I_C = \beta \cdot I_{B,ph} + I_{CEO}$$
6. **Output:** A large, easily measurable collector current $I_C$ appears at the output terminal, suitable for direct interfacing with a microcontroller ADC or comparator.

### 2.2 Modes of Operation

- **Active (Linear) Mode:** Collector–base junction reverse-biased; emitter–base junction forward-biased by photocarriers. Used for analog light measurement.
- **Switching (Saturation) Mode:** $I_C$ sufficient to drive the transistor into saturation. Used for digital light/dark detection (e.g., object presence sensors in robotics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the base terminal usually left unconnected (floating)?**
> In most practical phototransistor circuits, the base pin is left **open-circuited**. This is because ambient temperature can cause leakage current $I_{CBO}$ that would otherwise be multiplied by $\beta$ and corrupt the signal. Floating the base minimizes noise pickup, although some designs add a base–emitter resistor (typically **1 MΩ – 10 MΩ**) to set a defined bias and improve switching speed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I_{ph}$ | Primary photocurrent | $I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi_\lambda$ | A |
| $I_C$ | Output collector current | $I_C = (\beta + 1)(I_{ph} + I_{CB0})$ | A |
| $\mathcal{R}$ | Responsivity | $\mathcal{R} = I_{ph} / P_{in}$ | A/W |
| $\eta$ | Quantum efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{e^- \text{ collected}}}{N_{ph \text{ incident}}}$ | dimensionless |
| $I_{CEO}$ | Dark (leakage) current | $I_{CEO} = (\beta + 1) I_{CBO}$ | A |
| $\lambda_c$ | Cutoff wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}$ | µm |
| $S$ | Normalized sensitivity | $S = \mathcal{R}/\mathcal{R}_{max}$ | dimensionless |
| $f_c$ | Cutoff frequency | $f_c \approx 1 / (2\pi \tau_r)$ | Hz |
| $\Phi_\lambda$ | Photon flux | $\Phi_\lambda = P_{in} \lambda / (hc)$ | photons/s |
| $V_{CE(sat)}$ | Saturation voltage | typically **0.2 V – 0.4 V** | V |

> **Note on Table Notation:** Vertical bars in any absolute-value expression in the formulas above (e.g., $\vert V_{CE} \vert$) are rendered using the $\vert$ command to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In robotics, phototransistors are deployed in:
- **Line-following robots** (IR reflectance sensing on a white/black surface)
- **Edge-detection systems** (preventing a mobile robot from falling off a table)
- **Proximity / object-presence sensors** (using modulated IR LED + phototransistor pairs)
- **Optical encoders** (rotational speed measurement on motor shafts)
- **Sun-tracking solar panels** (dual-phototransistor differential sensing)

The internal gain $\beta$ is a critical advantage: it eliminates the need for an external amplifier stage, reducing PCB area, BOM cost, and noise pickup — three crucial factors in compact robotic designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Collector Current from Incident Optical Power

**Given:**
- Incident optical power on the active area: $P_{in}$ (in watts)
- Wavelength of incident light: $\lambda$ (in metres)
- Quantum efficiency of the base–collector junction: $\eta$ (dimensionless, $0 < \eta < 1$)
- Transistor current gain: $\beta$
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}$
- Speed of light: $c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$
- Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$

**Step 1 — Energy of a single photon:**
The energy carried by one photon of wavelength $\lambda$ is given by the Planck–Einstein relation:
$$E_p = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Number of photons per second (photon flux):**
If the total incident optical power is $P_{in}$, the number of photons striking the device per second is the total energy divided by the energy per photon:
$$\Phi_\lambda = \frac{P_{in}}{E_p} = \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 3 — Number of electron–hole pairs generated:**
Only a fraction $\eta$ of the incident photons successfully generate collectable electron–hole pairs. Therefore:
$$N_{e-h} = \eta \cdot \Phi_\lambda = \frac{\eta \, P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 4 — Primary photocurrent at the base–collector junction:**
Each electron–hole pair contributes one elementary charge $q$ per carrier transit. Thus the primary photocurrent is:
$$I_{ph} = q \cdot N_{e-h} = \frac{q \eta \lambda P_{in}}{hc}$$

This is the well-known **photodiode-style** primary photocurrent equation. It is the current generated *before* transistor amplification.

**Step 5 — BJT amplification (active region):**
The photogenerated current $I_{ph}$ flows into the base, acting as a virtual base current. The collector current is then amplified by the current gain:
$$I_C = \beta \cdot I_{ph} + I_{CEO}$$

Substituting Step 4 into this BJT relation:
$$I_C = \frac{\beta \, q \eta \lambda P_{in}}{hc} + I_{CEO}$$

**Step 6 — Simplified engineering form:**
Since $\beta \gg 1$ and $I_{CEO} \ll \beta I_{ph}$ under typical illumination, the dominant output current is:
$$\boxed{I_C \approx \frac{\beta \, q \eta \lambda P_{in}}{hc}}$$

This equation demonstrates the central engineering advantage of a phototransistor: **the output current scales linearly with both the incident optical power and the transistor gain.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cutoff Wavelength for a Silicon Phototransistor

**Given:** Silicon bandgap energy $E_g = 1.12\,\text{eV}$.

**Step 1 — Bandgap to wavelength conversion:**
For a photon to be absorbed, its energy must satisfy $E_p \geq E_g$. Equating the photon energy to the bandgap:
$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_c} = E_g$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the cutoff wavelength:**
$$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3 — Insert numerical values in electron-volt units:**
Using the standard conversion $hc = 1.24\,\text{eV·µm}$:
$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24\,\text{eV·µm}}{1.12\,\text{eV}} = 1.107\,\text{µm} \approx 1.1\,\text{µm}$$

**Interpretation:** A silicon phototransistor cannot detect photons with wavelength longer than approximately **1.1 µm** (1100 nm), because such photons lack sufficient energy to bridge the silicon bandgap. This is why Si phototransistors are insensitive to far-infrared thermal radiation (e.g., human body emission peaks near 10 µm).

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A Si phototransistor has quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.6$, current gain $\beta = 200$, and is illuminated by monochromatic light of $\lambda = 900\,\text{nm}$ with $P_{in} = 100\,\text{µW}$. The dark current is $I_{CEO} = 50\,\text{nA}$. Compute:
(a) the primary photocurrent $I_{ph}$,
(b) the output collector current $I_C$,
(c) the responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ of the phototransistor.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**
$$I_{ph} = \frac{q \eta \lambda P_{in}}{hc}$$
$$= \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.6)(900 \times 10^{-9})(100 \times 10^{-6})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^{8})}$$

Numerator:
$$(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.6)(900 \times 10^{-9})(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 8.65 \times 10^{-30}\,\text{C·m·W/m·m}$$

Denominator:
$$(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^{8}) = 1.988 \times 10^{-25}\,\text{J·m}$$

$$I_{ph} = \frac{8.65 \times 10^{-30}}{1.988 \times 10^{-25}} = 4.35 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{A} \approx 43.5\,\text{µA}$$

**Part (b):**
$$I_C = \beta I_{ph} + I_{CEO} = (200)(43.5\,\text{µA}) + 50\,\text{nA} = 8.7\,\text{mA} + 0.05\,\text{µA} \approx 8.7\,\text{mA}$$

**Part (c):**
$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_C}{P_{in}} = \frac{8.7 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 87\,\text{A/W}$$

This responsivity is roughly **200× higher** than a comparable photodiode ($\mathcal{R} \approx 0.43\,\text{A/W}$ at 900 nm), confirming the **internal gain advantage** of the phototransistor topology.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Phototransistor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Photon Flux Phi_lambda] --> B[Transparent Window Lens]
    B --> C[Base-Collector Depletion Region]
    C --> D[Photogenerated Electron-Hole Pairs]
    D --> E[Primary Photocurrent I_ph]
    E --> F[BJT Amplification Stage gain beta]
    F --> G[Amplified Collector Current I_C]
    G --> H[Output to Microcontroller ADC or Comparator]
    I[Base Pin Floating or High-Value Resistor] -.-> F
    J[Supply V_CC via Load Resistor R_L] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The horizontal arrows (`-->`) trace the **forward signal path** from incident photons to amplified output current.
- The dotted arrow (`-.->`) represents the **optional base bias** path (left floating in most practical designs).
- The vertical supply feed (`J --> G`) shows the external bias arrangement required to operate the BJT in the active region.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Typical Biasing & Signal Conditioning Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph bias[Biasing Network]
        V1[DC Supply V_CC 5V] --> R1[Load Resistor R_L 10k ohm]
        R1 --> node1[Collector Node]
        node1 --> Q1[NPN Phototransistor]
        Q1 --> G1[Ground 0V]
    end
    subgraph out[Signal Conditioning]
        node1 --> C1[Coupling Capacitor optional]
        C1 --> CMP[Comparator LM393]
        CMP --> MCU[Microcontroller GPIO Digital Input]
    end
    subgraph light[Optical Input]
        LED[IR LED Emitter 940nm] -->|Reflected Light| Q1
    end
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The phototransistor `Q1` is configured in **common-emitter mode** (emitter grounded, collector tied to $V_{CC}$ through a load resistor $R_L$).
- When light strikes the device, $I_C$ rises, the voltage drop across $R_L$ increases, and the collector node voltage falls — producing an **active-low** digital output.
- A comparator (e.g., LM393) can be used to threshold the analog signal into a clean digital logic level for the robot's microcontroller.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: Phototransistor Characteristics Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Phototransistor] --> P1[Electrical Characteristics]
    P --> P2[Optical Characteristics]
    P --> P3[Dynamic Characteristics]
    P1 --> P1a[Dark Current I_CEO]
    P1 --> P1b[Breakdown Voltage BV_CEO]
    P1 --> P1c[Saturation Voltage V_CEsat]
    P2 --> P2a[Spectral Response curve]
    P2 --> P2b[Responsivity R A per W]
    P2 --> P2c[Cutoff Wavelength lambda_c]
    P3 --> P3a[Rise Time t_r]
    P3 --> P3b[Fall Time t_f]
    P3 --> P3c[Cutoff Frequency f_c]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a phototransistor. Mention any two advantages it has over a photodiode.**

**Model Answer:**

A phototransistor is a light-sensitive semiconductor device that combines the function of a photodiode with the amplifying action of a bipolar junction transistor. Incident light generates a primary photocurrent in the base–collector junction, which is then amplified by the transistor's current gain $\beta$ to produce a much larger collector current.

**Two advantages over a photodiode:**
1. **Internal current gain** ($\beta = 100$ to $1000$) provides output currents in the **mA range**, eliminating the need for an external op-amp stage.
2. **Higher signal-to-noise ratio** at low light levels because the noise contributed by the BJT is divided by $\beta$ when referred to the input, making it suitable for detecting weak optical signals in robotic proximity sensors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the cutoff wavelength in a phototransistor? Calculate the cutoff wavelength for a germanium phototransistor ($E_g = 0.67\,\text{eV}$).**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:** The cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$ is the longest wavelength of light that the phototransistor can detect. Photons with $\lambda > \lambda_c$ do not have enough energy to generate electron–hole pairs, so the device becomes insensitive beyond this limit.

**Calculation:**
$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}\,\text{µm} = \frac{1.24}{0.67}\,\text{µm} = 1.85\,\text{µm}$$

Thus, a Ge phototransistor is sensitive out to **1.85 µm**, making it useful for near-infrared detection.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a phototransistor. Discuss the role of the base region being left floating in most practical designs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon phototransistor ($\eta = 0.7$, $\beta = 150$) is illuminated by light of wavelength 850 nm and optical power 50 µW. Determine:
  (i) the primary photocurrent $I_{ph}$,
  (ii) the output collector current $I_C$, and
  (iii) the device responsivity $\mathcal{R}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

A phototransistor is constructed as an NPN bipolar junction transistor (BJT) encapsulated in a transparent package that exposes the **base–collector depletion region** to incident light. The construction consists of:
- A heavily doped **N⁺ emitter** at the top
- A thin **P-type base** in the middle (with the base lead usually left open)
- A wide **N-type collector** at the bottom, with the largest depletion region
- A **transparent plastic or glass window** in the package to admit light

**Working Principle:**
1. Photons of energy $E_p \geq E_g$ enter through the transparent window and penetrate the base–collector depletion region.
2. Each absorbed photon creates an electron–hole pair.
3. The reverse-bias electric field across the base–collector junction sweeps electrons toward the collector and holes toward the base.
4. The accumulated holes constitute a **photogenerated base current** $I_{B,ph}$.
5. Standard BJT action amplifies this: $I_C = \beta \cdot I_{B,ph} + I_{CEO}$.

**Why the base is left floating:**
- It minimises external noise pickup at the high-impedance base node.
- It prevents the multiplication of temperature-induced leakage current $I_{CBO}$ by $\beta$ at the output (which would degrade dark-current performance).
- A small base–emitter resistor (1–10 MΩ) may optionally be added to define bias and improve switching speed, but the floating configuration is preferred for low-frequency light sensing in robotics.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $\eta = 0.7$, $\beta = 150$, $\lambda = 850\,\text{nm} = 850 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}$, $P_{in} = 50\,\text{µW} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{W}$.

**(i) Primary photocurrent:**
$$I_{ph} = \frac{q \eta \lambda P_{in}}{hc}$$
$$= \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.7)(850 \times 10^{-9})(50 \times 10^{-6})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^{8})}$$

Numerator $= 4.766 \times 10^{-30}$, Denominator $= 1.988 \times 10^{-25}$
$$I_{ph} = 2.40 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{A} = 24.0\,\text{µA}$$

**(ii) Output collector current:**
$$I_C = \beta I_{ph} + I_{CEO} \approx (150)(24.0\,\text{µA}) = 3.6\,\text{mA}$$

**(iii) Responsivity:**
$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_C}{P_{in}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 72\,\text{A/W}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice — Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare phototransistors with photodiodes. Explain why phototransistors are preferred in low-light robotic sensing applications despite their slower response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the spectral response curve of a typical silicon phototransistor and identify the peak response region. Justify why these devices are commonly used with **940 nm IR LEDs** in robotic proximity and line-following modules. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Parameter | Photodiode | Phototransistor |
|---|---|---|
| Internal gain | **×1 (unity)** | **×100 to ×1000** |
| Response time | nanoseconds | microseconds |
| Output current (typical) | µA | mA |
| External amplification | Required | Not required |
| Linearity | Excellent | Moderate |
| Power consumption | Low | Slightly higher |
| Cost per unit | Lower | Slightly higher |

**Why preferred in low-light robotic sensing:**
- The **internal current gain $\beta$** amplifies weak photocurrents to easily detectable levels, eliminating the need for an external op-amp that would otherwise introduce additional noise.
- The **higher signal-to-noise ratio** at low light levels makes the device robust against ambient interference.
- **Direct digital interfacing** with microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino, STM32) is possible without intermediate analog stages — simplifying PCB design.
- The slower response (microseconds) is acceptable for robotic applications like line-following, where the relevant event timescales are typically **10 ms or longer**.

**Model Solution to (b):**

The spectral response of a silicon phototransistor peaks in the **near-infrared region**, typically at **800–900 nm**, with a sharp cutoff near **1100 nm** (corresponding to the Si bandgap of 1.12 eV).

**Why 940 nm IR LEDs are commonly used:**
1. **940 nm falls within the high-responsivity region** of Si phototransistors, ensuring maximum detection sensitivity.
2. **940 nm is invisible to the human eye** and most cameras, so the sensor is immune to visible-light interference from indoor lighting or sunlight.
3. **940 nm is well-matched to the spectral peak** of cheap, mass-produced IR LEDs, making the overall emitter–detector pair cost-effective for educational and hobbyist robotic platforms.
4. **Longer wavelengths (e.g., 1550 nm)** would be invisible to silicon — hence Si phototransistors and 940 nm emitters form a natural technological pairing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> Students commonly lose marks on phototransistor problems for the following reasons:
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $\beta$:** Many students compute only the primary photocurrent $I_{ph}$ and present it as the final answer, omitting the transistor amplification step. Remember: $I_C = \beta \cdot I_{ph}$ in the active region.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $\lambda$:** When using $\lambda$ in the $I_{ph} = q \eta \lambda P_{in}/hc$ formula, students must convert **nm to metres** (e.g., 850 nm $= 850 \times 10^{-9}$ m) — failure to do so yields answers off by a factor of $10^9$.
> 3. **Confusing responsivity definitions:** Some textbooks define responsivity with respect to the primary photocurrent (photodiode-style), while others with respect to the amplified collector current (phototransistor-style). KTU 2024 expects the **amplified** form unless explicitly stated.
> 4. **Cutting off the cutoff-wavelength derivation:** Always show the substitution $hc = 1.24\,\text{eV·µm}$ for full credit on numerical problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phototransistor = Photodiode + BJT amplifier**, integrated on a single silicon die.
- The **base is left floating** in most designs to minimise noise and dark current.
- **Peak spectral response** lies at **800–900 nm**; cutoff at **~1100 nm** for Si.
- **Output current** $I_C \approx \beta \cdot q \eta \lambda P_{in}/hc$ — linear in both $P_{in}$ and $\beta$.
- **Responsivity** is **100×–1000× higher** than a comparable photodiode due to internal gain.
- **Trade-off:** High gain comes at the cost of **slower response** (µs vs. ns).
- **Saturation voltage** $V_{CE(sat)} \approx$ **0.2 V – 0.4 V** must be accounted for in low-voltage designs.
- **Cutoff wavelength formula:** $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g$ (with $E_g$ in eV, $\lambda_c$ in µm).
- **Primary applications in robotics:** line-following, edge detection, proximity, optical encoders, sun tracking.
- **Dark current** $I_{CEO} = (\beta + 1) I_{CBO}$ is temperature-sensitive and is the principal noise floor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Range Sensor (LIDAR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Range Sensor (LIDAR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LIDAR (Light Detection And Ranging)** is an active, electro-optical remote sensing technology that determines the range (distance) to a target object by illuminating it with pulsed or modulated laser light and measuring the reflected return signal. In the context of robotics, a **Laser Range Sensor (LIDAR)** is an active proximity/distance sensor that emits a narrow beam of coherent monochromatic laser radiation (typically in the **Near-Infrared (NIR)** band, **905 nm** or **1550 nm**) and analyses the back-scattered photons to produce a 1-D, 2-D, or 3-D spatial map of the surrounding environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The 2024 scheme categorises LIDAR under **Active Range Sensors**. Unlike passive sensors (e.g., cameras) that rely on ambient illumination, LIDAR is *self-illuminating* and therefore operates reliably in complete darkness, fog mitigation permitting, and provides direct metric (SI-unit) distance measurements — a property known as **absolute ranging**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of LIDAR as **a bat's echolocation, but using light instead of sound, and a million times faster**.

| Aspect | Bat's Echolocation | Robot's LIDAR |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Acoustic chirp (sound) | Photon pulse (laser) |
| Speed | ~ 343 m/s | **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Timing device | Biological auditory cortex | High-precision timer / ADC |
| Output | Mental image of cave | Polar coordinate map (r, θ) |

When you shout at a distant cliff and hear the echo, your brain unconsciously calculates distance using the round-trip delay. LIDAR does the exact same thing: it sends out a packet of light, waits for the "echo," and converts that tiny time delay into a precise millimetre-accurate distance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Standard automotive LIDAR wavelengths: **905 nm** (Si-based detectors) and **1550 nm** (eye-safe, InGaAs detectors)
- Typical pulse duration: **5 – 20 ns**
- Maximum range (automotive): **100 – 250 m**
- Angular resolution: **0.1° – 1°**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two wavelengths?**
> The **905 nm** band uses cheaper Silicon (Si) avalanche photodiodes (APDs) but is more dangerous to the human retina. The **1550 nm** band is absorbed by the eye's cornea before reaching the retina, making it intrinsically **eye-safe** at higher pulse powers, which is why long-range autonomous vehicles (e.g., Waymo, Velodyne) prefer it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Taxonomy — Three Core Ranging Principles

LIDAR systems recover distance using one of three fundamental physical principles. The KTU examiner expects you to identify *which* principle is being used from a given schematic.

### 2.1.1 Principle A — Pulsed Time-of-Flight (TOF)

**Why it works:** Light travels at a *finite, known* speed. If we launch a 5-ns pulse at a wall 1.5 m away, it returns after exactly 10 ns.

**How it works (bullet logic):**
1. Laser driver triggers a photodiode → emits an optical pulse.
2. A high-speed **Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC)** or **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** starts a counter.
3. The photodetector (APD or SPAD) registers the returned photon burst.
4. The counter stops. The elapsed time $\Delta t$ is the *round-trip* time.
5. Distance is half the round-trip distance (the light travelled forward *and* backward).

### 2.1.2 Principle B — Phase-Shift (Amplitude Modulated CW)

**Why it works:** If the emitted beam's intensity is modulated sinusoidally at a known frequency $f_{mod}$, the reflected wave arrives with a phase lag $\varphi$. The lag encodes the distance.

**How it works:**
1. A CW laser diode is intensity-modulated (AM) at $f_{mod}$ (typically 1 – 100 MHz).
2. The detector mixes the returning beam with the local oscillator.
3. A phase-meter extracts the phase difference $\varphi \in [0, 2\pi)$.
4. Ambiguity arises when distance exceeds $c / (2 f_{mod})$ — solved by toggling multiple modulation frequencies.

### 2.1.3 Principle C — Laser Triangulation

**Why it works:** Classic geometry. Two known points (laser source + detector) and a known baseline create a triangle whose base angles change with target distance.

**How it works:**
1. A line-laser projects a stripe onto the target.
2. A CMOS/CCD camera views the stripe from a fixed baseline $b$.
3. The stripe's image position $x'$ on the sensor shifts with distance $R$.

This method is **short-range** (mm to ~10 m) but extremely precise (µm-level). Common in industrial bin-picking and smartphone face-ID.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used below (NOT the bare `\vert` inside table cells) so the KTU Markdown renderer does not break the column boundaries.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TOF Range | $R = \dfrac{c \, \Delta t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light (m/s), $\Delta t$ = round-trip time (s) | Pulsed LIDAR |
| 2 | Phase-Shift Range | $R = \dfrac{c \, \varphi}{4 \pi \, f_{mod}}$ | $\varphi$ = phase lag (rad), $f_{mod}$ = modulation freq. (Hz) | CW AM LIDAR |
| 3 | Max Unambiguous Phase Range | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, f_{mod}}$ | Limit before phase wraps by $2\pi$ | CW LIDAR |
| 4 | Triangulation Range | $R = \dfrac{f \, b}{x'}$ | $f$ = focal length (m), $b$ = baseline (m), $x'$ = image shift (m) | Short-range |
| 5 | Laser Pulse Energy | $E_p = P_{peak} \cdot \tau_{pulse}$ | $P_{peak}$ (W), $\tau_{pulse}$ (s) | Eye-safety check |
| 6 | SNR (LIDAR equation) | $SNR = \dfrac{P_r}{NEP \cdot \sqrt{BW}}$ | $P_r$ = received power, $NEP$ = noise-equivalent power, $BW$ = bandwidth | Performance |
| 7 | Photon Counting Rate | $\dot{N} = \dfrac{P_r \cdot \eta}{h \nu}$ | $\eta$ = quantum efficiency, $h \nu$ = photon energy (J) | SPAD detectors |
| 8 | Beam Divergence | $\theta_{div} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi \, w_0}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength, $w_0$ = beam waist | Spot size |

**Real-world engineering utility:** These eight equations cover 100% of KTU numerical problems on LIDAR. Memorise the first three — they appear in nearly every past paper.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pulsed Time-of-Flight Range Equation

We start from the kinematic definition of speed.

**Given:**
- $c$ = speed of light in the propagation medium (≈ vacuum value for air, since refractive index $n_{air} \approx 1.0003$).
- $\Delta t$ = measured round-trip time between emission and detection.

**Assumption:** The medium is homogeneous and non-dispersive at the operating wavelength.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Definition of speed:} \quad & c = \frac{\text{total optical path length}}{\text{time elapsed}} \\[4pt]
\text{For a round-trip to target at range } R: \quad & c = \frac{2R}{\Delta t} \\[4pt]
\text{Rearranging for } R: \quad & 2R = c \, \Delta t \\[4pt]
\text{Therefore:} \quad & R = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual conversion logic:** The factor of 2 appears because light traverses the distance $R$ *twice* — once outward to the target and once back to the detector. Without halving, the computed range would be twice the true range.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Phase-Shift Range Equation

**Given:** An intensity-modulated wave of frequency $f_{mod}$, returning with phase lag $\varphi$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Modulation wavelength in medium:} \quad & \lambda_{mod} = \frac{c}{f_{mod}} \\[4pt]
