# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define a hydraulic actuator. List any four advantages it offers over electric and pneumatic actuators for robotic applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *A hydraulic actuator is a fluid-power device that converts the pressure energy of an incompressible hydraulic fluid into mechanical work (linear or rotary) to actuate a robotic joint or end-effector.*

**Four advantages:**

1. **Very high force / power density** — a 1 kg hydraulic actuator can produce 10–20 kN of force, far exceeding electric motors. This enables *heavy-payload* industrial manipulators (>100 kg) and legged robots.
2. **Self-lubricating and overload-tolerant** — oil lubricates internal components, giving long service life in dirty environments (mining, construction).
3. **Inherent overload protection** — a relief valve limits maximum pressure, protecting the robot from shock loads.
4. **Safe in explosive / submerged environments** — no sparking, works underwater (ROV arms) and in flammable atmospheres (oil-rig robots).
5. **Stiff positioning and good damping** — high bulk modulus gives good position holding without brake.

*(Any four well-explained advantages — 3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for advantages.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the working of a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with a neat sketch. Write the expression for extension and retraction force.**

**Model Answer:**

> A double-acting cylinder has two ports — *cap-end* (A) and *rod-end* (B) — separated by a piston connected to a rod. Pressurised oil from the DCV enters **port A** and pushes the piston **outward** (extension); oil from the **B** side returns to tank. To retract, the DCV is reversed: oil enters B and returns from A.

**Force expressions:**

$$
F_{ext} = P_b \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = P_b \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

**Asymmetry ratio:**

$$
\frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}
$$

A *neat sketch* must show pump, 4/2 or 4/3 DCV, cylinder, tank, and labelled ports — 1 mark for the sketch and 2 marks for the equations and explanation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Module in the End-Semester Exam has *internal choice*. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from a module. Each question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, describe the components of a hydraulic actuation system used in a robotic manipulator. Explain the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The hydraulic actuation system for a robotic manipulator consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Prime Mover (Electric Motor / IC Engine):** Supplies the input mechanical power. In a stationary industrial robot this is a 3-phase induction motor; in a mobile mining robot it may be a diesel engine.

2. **Hydraulic Pump (Fixed or Variable Displacement):** Converts mechanical power into hydraulic power. The *fixed-displacement* pump gives constant flow (cheaper, open-loop control); the *variable-displacement* swash-plate pump enables energy-efficient servo control.

3. **Reservoir / Tank:** Stores the hydraulic fluid, allows air to separate, and dissipates heat through its large surface area.

4. **Filters (suction, return, pressure line):** Maintain ISO 4406 cleanliness class 16/14/11 or better to prevent valve spool jamming — critical in dusty robotic cells.

5. **Pressure Relief Valve:** A normally-closed valve that opens when system pressure exceeds a preset value, protecting actuators and lines from over-pressure.

6. **Directional Control Valve (DCV):** A 4/3 solenoid- or pilot-operated valve routes flow to one side of the cylinder while exhausting the other side. Centre conditions: *tandem* (all ports blocked, lock), *open-centre* (P to T, pump unloads), *closed-centre* (all ports blocked, pump pressurised).

7. **Flow Control Valve / Servo Valve:** Regulates actuator speed (open-loop) or closes the inner hydraulic loop (proportional/servo) to achieve precise joint position.

8. **Actuator (Cylinder or Motor):** The final stage that produces linear or rotary motion at the robot joint.

9. **Sensors (LVDT, encoder, pressure transmitter, load cell):** Provide real-time feedback to the controller for closed-loop operation.

10. **Electronic Controller (PLC / motion controller / DSP):** Compares desired and actual joint positions and generates valve commands.

**Function in robotics:** Together, these components form a *closed-loop servo-hydraulic* drive capable of producing high joint torques with position accuracy of order 0.1 mm — used in heavy-payload welding robots and forestry manipulators.

*(Mark split: 3 for block diagram, 3 for component listing, 1 for role-in-robotics. Total 7.)*

#### (b) A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 45 mm. It is supplied with oil at 200 bar. Calculate: (i) extension force, (ii) retraction force, (iii) force asymmetry ratio, and (iv) cylinder extension velocity if the pump flow is 25 L/min. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Bore $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- Rod $d = 45 \text{ mm} = 0.045 \text{ m}$
- Supply pressure $P_b = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- Pump flow $Q = 25 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Assume rod-side returned to tank ($P_a = 0$).

**Step 1 — Areas**

$$
A_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 50.27 \text{ cm}^2
$$

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08^2 - 0.045^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0064 - 0.002025)}{4}
$$

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi \times 0.004375}{4} = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 34.36 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Extension force**

$$
F_{ext} = P_b \cdot A_b = 2 \times 10^7 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 1.005 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 100.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Retraction force**

$$
F_{ret} = P_b \cdot A_a = 2 \times 10^7 \times 3.436 \times 10^{-3} = 6.872 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \approx 68.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Asymmetry ratio**

$$
\frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \frac{A_b}{A_a} = \frac{50.27}{34.36} = 1.463
$$

**Step 5 — Extension velocity**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_b} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0829 \text{ m/s} \approx 83 \text{ mm/s}
$$

*(Mark split: 1 for area calculation, 2 for forces, 1 for ratio, 1 for velocity, 2 for unit conversion discipline. Total 7.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]

#### (a) With the help of neat diagrams, explain the construction and working of an axial-piston hydraulic motor. Derive the expressions for theoretical torque and power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** An axial-piston hydraulic motor consists of (i) a **cylinder block** with $z$ (typically 7 or 9) axial bores, (ii) **pistons** fitted in the bores, (iii) a **swash plate** (or cam ring) inclined at angle $\gamma$ to the axis, (iv) a **valve plate** with kidney-shaped ports for inlet and outlet, and (v) a **drive shaft** coupled to the cylinder block.

**Working:** Pressurised oil enters via the inlet port, fills the bores whose pistons are on the high-pressure side of the swash plate, and pushes the pistons against the inclined swash plate. The axial thrust on each piston is resolved into a tangential force on the swash plate; this tangential force creates a torque on the cylinder block, which rotates the output shaft. Oil from the bores on the low-pressure side is pushed back to tank via the outlet kidney port.

**Derivation of theoretical torque:**

Let $z$ = number of pistons, $A_p$ = piston area, $R$ = piston-circle radius, $\gamma$ = swash-plate angle.

Axial thrust on a piston at angular position $\theta$:

$$
F_i = P \cdot A_p
$$

The perpendicular (tangential) component producing torque is $F_i \tan \gamma$ on the swash plate, and the moment arm is $R$, so the torque from one piston is:

$$
T_i = P \cdot A_p \cdot R \cdot \tan\gamma \cdot \sin\theta_i
$$

Summing over all pistons ($z$ equally spaced, $\theta_i = 2\pi i / z$):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{z} \sin\theta_i = 0
$$

But averaging over a full revolution, the effective torque is the *mean* contribution:

$$
\boxed{T_{th} = \frac{P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}}
$$

where the **displacement per revolution** is:

$$
D_m = z \cdot A_p \cdot R \cdot \tan\gamma
$$

**Theoretical power:**

$$
\boxed{P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = P \cdot Q_{th} = P \cdot D_m \cdot n}
$$

*(Mark split: 2 for construction, 2 for working with diagram, 2 for torque derivation, 1 for power expression. Total 7.)*

#### (b) A hydraulic robot joint uses an axial-piston motor with displacement $D_m = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$. The motor is supplied with oil at $\Delta P = 180 \text{ bar}$ and $Q = 50 \text{ L/min}$. Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.95$ and mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.91$. Determine: (i) motor speed in rpm, (ii) theoretical and actual torque, (iii) shaft power, and (iv) overall efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D_m = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 60 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $\Delta P = 180 \text{ bar} = 18 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $Q = 50 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\eta_v = 0.95$, $\eta_m = 0.91$

**(i) Motor speed:**

$$
n = \frac{Q \cdot \eta_v}{D_m} = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.95}{60 \times 10^{-6}} = 13.19 \text{ rev/s} = 791.6 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Torque:**

Theoretical:

$$
T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi} = \frac{18 \times 10^6 \times 60 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi} = 171.9 \text{ N·m}
$$

Actual (using $\eta_m$):

$$
T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 171.9 \times 0.91 = 156.4 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iii) Shaft power:**

$$
P_{shaft} = T_{act} \cdot 2\pi n = 156.4 \times 2\pi \times 13.19 = 12.95 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Overall efficiency:**

Input hydraulic power:

$$
P_h = \Delta P \cdot Q = 18 \times 10^6 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-4} = 15.0 \text{ kW}
$$

Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_h} = \frac{12.95}{15.0} = 0.863
$$

Cross-check: $\eta_{overall} \approx \eta_v \eta_m = 0.95 \times 0.91 = 0.8645$ ✔

*(Mark split: 1.5 for speed, 1.5 for torque, 2 for power, 2 for efficiency and unit conversion. Total 7.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes observed in KTU valuation of Hydraulic Actuator problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bar to Pa.** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, *not* $10^6$. A wrong unit prefix here can shift the answer by an order of magnitude — automatic 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Using $D^2/4$ for both extension and retraction.** Extension uses *bore area*; retraction uses *annulus area* $\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you state the wrong area in a free-body sketch.
> 3. **Not stating the rod-side pressure assumption.** Always say "rod side connected to tank, $P_a \approx 0$ (gauge)" — otherwise the derivation is incomplete (–1 mark).
> 4. **Confusing volumetric and mechanical efficiency.** $\eta_v$ affects *speed and flow*; $\eta_m$ affects *torque*. Mixing them up → wrong $n$ and $T$ → 0 marks in sub-question.
> 5. **Ignoring the sketch requirement.** Part (a) of any 7-mark question carries **at least 1–2 marks** for a *labelled, neat diagram*. A textual description alone is not enough.
> 6. **Forgetting the 14-mark question internal choice.** Pick Question A or B, not both.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Hydraulic actuator = fluid-power device that converts pressurised-oil energy into linear or rotary mechanical work for robot joints.
- ✅ Working fluid is **incompressible** (bulk modulus $\beta_e \approx 1.4\text{–}1.7$ GPa) — this is what gives high force density.
- ✅ **Pascal's law** is the foundation: $F = P \times A$.
- ✅ Linear actuators: single-acting, double-acting, telescopic. Rotary: gear, vane, axial-piston, radial-piston.
- ✅ **Extension force** uses bore area $\pi D^2/4$; **retraction force** uses annulus area $\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$.
- ✅ Force-asymmetry ratio: $F_{ext}/F_{ret} = D^2 / (D^2 - d^2)$.
- ✅ Cylinder velocity: $v = Q / A$ (extension slower than retraction because $A_b > A_a$ for same $Q$).
- ✅ Motor torque: $T = \Delta P \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_m / 2\pi$. Motor speed: $n = Q \eta_v / D_m$.
- ✅ Overall efficiency: $\eta = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ (typically 0.80–0.95 for servo-piston motors).
- ✅ Hydraulic resonance: $\omega_h = \sqrt{\beta_e A^2 / (M V_t)}$ — sets the upper limit of joint bandwidth (5–20 Hz typical).
- ✅ **Standard industrial pressure:** 210 bar (21 MPa). Maximum robotics test: 350 bar.
- ✅ Hydraulic robots are chosen for: **heavy payload, hazardous/submerged, overload-tolerant, stiff** applications.
- ✅ Standard 4/3 DCV centre conditions to remember: *tandem-centre (all blocked)*, *open-centre (P→T unloads pump)*, *closed-centre (pressurised with no flow)*.
- ✅ Bulk modulus of oil limits dynamic response — KTU loves asking why hydraulic robots are slower in dynamic motion than electric ones.
- ✅ Always show units, free-body, and a labelled diagram in long-answer questions.
- ✅ Applications to remember: shipyard welding, mining LHD, ROV arms, Atlas biped, aerospace FBW, Brokk demolition, forestry.
- ✅ KTU exam weighting: Module 4 typically contributes 1 × 14-mark question (with internal choice) in the End-Semester Exam and 1–2 short questions in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Actuating Systems: Electric Motors as Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electrical Actuating Systems: Electric Motors as Actuators ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is an Electrical Actuating System? In the context of robotics, an **Electrical Actuating System** is the sub-system that converts **electrical energy** supplied by a controller, battery, or power supply into **controlled mechanical motion** (rotational or linear) at the robot's joints or end-effector. The principal device performing this conversion is the **Electric Motor**, which therefore acts as the *muscle* of every robotic manipulator, mobile base, gripper, and humanoid limb. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** An *actuator* is a device that produces motion in response to a control signal. When the energy source is electricity, the actuator is termed an *electrical actuator*, and when implemented using electromagnetic energy conversion principles, it is realized as an **electric motor**. ### 1.2 Classification Snapshot (Module 4 Lens) Electric motors relevant to robot sensing/actuation are conventionally classified by: | Criterion | Families | |---|---| | Supply type | **DC Motors**, **AC Motors** | | Commutation | **Brushed**, **Brushless (BLDC)** | | Control philosophy | **Stepper Motors** (open-loop), **Servo Motors** (closed-loop) | | Motion output | **Rotary**, **Linear** | | Robot application | Joint drive, wheel drive, gripper drive, tool drive | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Muscle Metaphor" Imagine a human arm lifting a cup of coffee. The brain sends tiny electrical pulses through nerves, telling muscle fibres how hard to pull. In a robot: - The **controller (microcontroller / PLC)** plays the role of the **brain**. - The **encoder / sensor** plays the role of the **nerve** (feedback). - The **electric motor** plays the role of the **muscle**, generating torque. - The **gear train / harmonic drive** is the **tendon**, converting speed into strength. - The **H-bridge driver** is the **synapse**, directing energy flow. Just as you cannot pick up a 10 kg dumbbell with a single muscle fibre, a robot cannot move a heavy payload with a tiny motor. Hence, **motor sizing** (torque, speed, power) is a foundational design step in robotics. ### 1.4 Why Electric Motors Dominate Robotics > [!NOTE] > - **Precision**: Stepper and servo motors can resolve positions to fractions of a degree (0.036° or better). > - **Controllability**: Torque and speed are smoothly variable via voltage / current / PWM. > - **Cleanliness**: No oil leakage (unlike hydraulics) and suitable for clean rooms. > - **Efficiency**: Modern BLDC motors exceed **90 %** efficiency, compared to ~30–40 % for pneumatic systems. > - **Sensor Integration**: Naturally compatible with optical encoders, resolvers, Hall sensors, and tachogenerators — a key reason for placement inside the *Robot Sensing* syllabus. ### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standard Values) - **Stall Torque (T_s)**: Maximum torque at zero speed (motor shaft locked). Typical small DC motor: **0.5 N·m to 5 N·m**. - **No-Load Speed (ω_0)**: Rotational speed with zero load. Units: **rad/s** or **RPM**. - **Rated Voltage (V_rated)**: Typical hobby DC: **6 V, 12 V, 24 V**; industrial servo: **48 V – 400 V**. - **Torque Constant (K_t)**: Units **N·m/A**. - **Back-EMF Constant (K_e)**: Units **V·s/rad** (equal to K_t in SI units). - **Electrical Time Constant (τ_e)** and **Mechanical Time Constant (τ_m)**. - **Step Resolution (Stepper)**: e.g., **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **0.72°** (500 steps/rev). ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Brushed DC Motor — Workhorse of Robotics A brushed DC motor consists of a **stator** (permanent magnet producing radial field B), a **rotor/armature** (coil winding on iron core), a **commutator** (split-ring), and **brushes** (carbon contacts). When current *I* flows through the armature placed in field *B*, the Lorentz force **F = BIL** acts on each conductor, producing torque. **Operating Principle (4 Step Logic):** 1. **Current Injection** — The DC source drives current *I* through the brushes → commutator → armature coils. 2. **Force Generation** — Each conductor of length *L* in magnetic flux density *B* experiences force F = BIL. For an armature with effective radius *r*, the resulting torque is T = F × r, summed over all active conductors. 3. **Commutation** — As the rotor turns, the commutator reverses current direction every 180° mechanically, ensuring unidirectional torque. 4. **Back-EMF** — Rotation in the magnetic field induces a counter-voltage E = K_e · ω that opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's Law), limiting current and hence torque at high speed. ### 2.2 The Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor — Modern Industrial Standard A BLDC motor **inverts** the construction: the **permanent magnets** are on the **rotor**, while the **windings** are on the **stator**. Commutation is performed *electronically* using a 3-phase inverter and **Hall-effect sensors** or **sensorless back-EMF zero-crossing detection**. > [!TIP] > **Why BLDC for robots?** No brush wear → longer life (10 000+ hours), higher power density, lower electrical noise (important for sensitive robot sensors), and excellent heat dissipation (stator is on the outside, easy to mount on a heatsink). ### 2.3 The Stepper Motor — Open-Loop Precision A stepper motor rotates in **discrete angular steps** synchronized with input pulses. It is the motor of choice when *open-loop* positioning is acceptable (3D printers, CNC, low-cost pick-and-place robots). - **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper**: Rotor is a permanent magnet; stator has salient poles. Step angle typically **90°** (4 steps/rev) — too coarse for most robotics, so rarely used alone. - **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper**: Rotor is a soft-iron gear-shaped piece. Stator energised phases pull rotor to minimum-reluctance position. Step angles down to **15°**. - **Hybrid Stepper (HB)**: Combines PM and VR principles. Most common in robotics. Standard step angle **1.8°** (200 steps/rev), or with microstepping **0.1125°** (3200 steps/rev). **Step Angle Formula:** $$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$$ where **N_s** = number of stator pole pairs, **N_r** = number of rotor teeth. ### 2.4 The Servo Motor — Closed-Loop Precision A *servo motor* is a complete **closed-loop positioning system** comprising: 1. A DC or BLDC motor (the *plant*). 2. A **gearbox** (torque multiplier). 3. A **position sensor** — typically a **potentiometer** for hobby RC servos or an **optical encoder / resolver** for industrial servos. 4. An **internal controller** (PID loop) that compares commanded position vs measured position and drives the motor accordingly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hobby RC Servo Command Standard (PWM):** > - Frequency: **50 Hz** (period 20 ms). > - Pulse width range: **1.0 ms (0°) → 1.5 ms (90°) → 2.0 ms (180°)**. > - Supply voltage: typically **4.8 V – 6 V**, with separate power and signal lines. > Industrial servos use fieldbus protocols (EtherCAT, CANopen, RS-485) instead of PWM. ### 2.5 The AC Induction & Synchronous Motor - **Induction Motor**: Rotor current is *induced* by stator rotating field; rugged, cheap, used in heavy robotic bases. - **Synchronous Motor**: Rotor magnet (PM or DC-excited) locks with stator rotating field; constant speed ω_s = 120·f/P where **f** = supply frequency, **P** = pole pairs. Used in high-precision CNC and direct-drive robot arms (KUKA, ABB). ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the variable definitions carefully.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Back-EMF | $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ | V | K_e in V·s/rad | | 2 | Electromagnetic Torque | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | N·m | K_t in N·m/A | | 3 | Motor Constant Equivalence | $K_t = K_e$ (SI units) | — | True when using rad/s | | 4 | Armature Voltage Eqn | $V = E + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ | V | KVL around armature | | 5 | Steady-State Armature Current | $I_a = \dfrac{V - K_e \omega}{R_a}$ | A | dI_a/dt = 0 | | 6 | No-Load Speed | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e}$ | rad/s | At T = 0, E ≈ V | | 7 | Stall Torque | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t V}{R_a}$ | N·m | At ω = 0, E = 0 | | 8 | Mechanical Power Output | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | W | Output shaft power | | 9 | Electrical Power Input | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_a$ | W | DC supply power | | 10 | Motor Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{T \omega}{V I_a}$ | unitless | Always < 1 | | 11 | Power Dissipated (Heat) | $P_{heat} = I_a^2 R_a$ | W | Loss in armature | | 12 | Mechanical Time Constant | $\tau_m = \dfrac{J R_a}{K_t K_e}$ | s | J = total inertia at shaft | | 13 | Electrical Time Constant | $\tau_e = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ | s | Armature RL time | | 14 | Stepper Step Angle | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{N_s N_r}$ | deg | HB stepper typical 1.8° | | 15 | Stepper Holding Torque | $T_h = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} \cdot I^2$ | N·m | Variable reluctance model | | 16 | Stepper Max Slew Rate | $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{V}{L \cdot dI/dt_{max}}$ | rad/s | Limited by inductance | | 17 | Servo PWM Duty (50 Hz) | $D = \dfrac{t_p}{20\,ms}$ | — | 1.0–2.0 ms pulse | | 18 | AC Synchronous Speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM | P = number of poles | | 19 | Slip (Induction) | $s = \dfrac{\omega_s - \omega_r}{\omega_s}$ | — | 0 (no-load) to 1 (stall) | | 20 | Torque of Induction Motor | $T = \dfrac{K s R_r}{(R_r/s)^2 + X_{lr}^2} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s}$ | N·m | Simplified Kramers eqn | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial 6-DOF Robot Arms (e.g., KUKA KR 1000)**: Use high-torque BLDC servo motors with harmonic drive gearboxes and absolute encoders for ±0.05 mm repeatability. - **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)**: Use frameless BLDC torque motors for *force feedback* (haptics) — the motor acts both as actuator and sensor. - **Mobile Robots / AGVs**: Use BLDC hub motors for direct-drive wheels, with optical encoders for odometry (sensing) and PID speed control (actuation). - **Hobby Robotics (Arduino)**: Use RC servos for joints and small DC gear motors for wheels. - **3D Printers**: Hybrid stepper motors with microstepping drivers (A4988, TMC2209) for sub-millimetre accuracy. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the DC Motor Governing Equation We begin with the equivalent circuit of an armature-controlled DC motor. The armature is modelled as a resistor R_a in series with an inductor L_a, and a back-EMF source E = K_e · ω opposing the supply. **Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature loop.** Going around the loop, the sum of voltage drops equals the supply: $$V = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E$$ **Reasoning:** The supply voltage V must overcome the resistive drop (I_a R_a), the inductive drop (L_a dI_a/dt during transients), and the back-EMF E which appears as a voltage rise opposing current. **Step 2 — Write the mechanical equation (Newton's 2nd law for rotation).** The electromagnetic torque T_e generated by the motor acts on the rotor and load inertia J, with viscous damping coefficient B: $$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega = T_e - T_L$$ **Reasoning:** Net torque (T_e − T_load − B·ω) accelerates the inertia J. The viscous term B·ω models bearing friction, windage, and any load friction that scales with speed. **Step 3 — Couple electrical to mechanical via motor constants.** The motor converts current into torque: $$T_e = K_t I_a$$ The rotation induces a counter-voltage: $$E = K_e \omega$$ **Step 4 — Derive the open-loop transfer function Ω(s)/V(s).** Take the Laplace transform of both equations with zero initial conditions: $$V(s) = I_a(s)(R_a + L_a s) + K_e \Omega(s)$$ $$J s \Omega(s) + B \Omega(s) = K_t I_a(s) - T_L(s)$$ Solve the first equation for I_a(s): $$I_a(s) = \frac{V(s) - K_e \Omega(s)}{R_a + L_a s}$$ Substitute into the second equation: $$(J s + B) \Omega(s) = K_t \cdot \frac{V(s) - K_e \Omega(s)}{R_a + L_a s} - T_L(s)$$ Multiply both sides by (R_a + L_a s): $$(J s + B)(R_a + L_a s) \Omega(s) = K_t V(s) - K_t K_e \Omega(s) - (R_a + L_a s) T_L(s)$$ Group the Ω(s) terms on the left: $$\left[(J s + B)(R_a + L_a s) + K_t K_e \right] \Omega(s) = K_t V(s) - (R_a + L_a s) T_L(s)$$ Therefore the **forward transfer function** from voltage to speed is: $$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_e}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify by ignoring armature inductance (good for low-frequency analysis).** If L_a is small (typical small DC motor: L_a ≈ 0.1 mH), then for frequencies well below 1/τ_e we set L_a ≈ 0: $$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V(s)} \approx \frac{K_t}{R_a (J s + B) + K_t K_e} = \frac{K_t / (R_a J)}{s + (B R_a + K_t K_e)/(R_a J)}$$ This is a **first-order system** with steady-state gain K = K_t / (B R_a + K_t K_e) and time constant τ_m = R_a J / (B R_a + K_t K_e). **Step 6 — Solve the no-load speed (T_L = 0, steady state, dω/dt = 0).** In steady state dI_a/dt = 0 and dω/dt = 0. From the mechanical equation: $$0 = K_t I_a - T_L \Rightarrow I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t}$$ For no load T_L = 0 → I_a = 0. Then from the electrical equation: $$V = 0 \cdot R_a + 0 + K_e \omega_0 \Rightarrow \omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **No-load speed is set purely by supply voltage and back-EMF constant.** This is why a higher V_drive gives a higher top speed. **Step 7 — Solve the stall torque (ω = 0, T = T_stall).** At stall ω = 0 → E = K_e · 0 = 0. The full supply voltage drops across the armature: $$V = I_a R_a \Rightarrow I_a = \frac{V}{R_a}$$ The torque is: $$T_{stall} = K_t I_a = \frac{K_t V}{R_a}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Stall current is large.** A 12 V motor with R_a = 1 Ω draws 12 A when stalled. Always include a current limit in the drive electronics to prevent winding burnout. ### 3.2 Derivation of Stepper Motor Step Angle (Hybrid Type) **Given:** A hybrid stepper has N_s = 50 stator teeth-pole pairs per phase (i.e., 50 salient poles around the stator circumference interacting with the rotor) and N_r = 50 rotor teeth. The fundamental step in degrees is the angle through which the rotor must rotate for the magnetic circuit to repeat its alignment pattern. When one phase is energised, the rotor turns to align with the nearest pair of stator poles. The angle between consecutive alignments is: $$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{2500} = 0.144°$$ This corresponds to **2500 steps per revolution**, which is the *electrical* resolution. In practice, microstepping subdivides each step into 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, or even 1/256 fractional steps using sinusoidal current shaping, achieving effective resolutions of **0.018° (20 000 steps/rev)** or finer. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Motor > [!NOTE] > **Use the matrix below as a decision support tool for KTU design problems.** | Application | Recommended Motor | Control Scheme | Position Feedback? | |---|---|---|---| | Mobile robot wheel (small) | Brushed DC + Gearbox | PWM via H-Bridge | Optional optical encoder | | Mobile robot wheel (premium) | BLDC Hub Motor | FOC / 6-Step | Hall sensors or sensorless | | Robot arm joint (low-cost) | RC Servo | Internal PID | Built-in pot | | Robot arm joint (industrial) | BLDC Servo + Harmonic Drive | Field-oriented control | Absolute multi-turn encoder | | 3D Printer / Pick-Place | Hybrid Stepper | Open-loop + microstepping | Optional (closed-loop stepper if needed) | | Heavy CNC / Direct Drive | AC Synchronous / Torque Motor | Vector control | Resolver / Sin-Cos encoder | | Surgical / Haptic robot | Frameless BLDC Torque Motor | Force-impedance control | High-res encoder + torque sensor | ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style Extended) **Problem:** A brushed DC motor has the following ratings: V = 24 V, R_a = 1.2 Ω, K_e = 0.05 V·s/rad, K_t = 0.05 N·m/A, B = 0, J = 5 × 10⁻⁵ kg·m², no load. Find (a) the no-load speed, (b) the stall torque, (c) the steady-state current when load torque is 0.15 N·m, and (d) the steady-state speed under that load. **Step (a) — No-load speed.** $$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e} = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480 \text{ rad/s} \approx 4586 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step (b) — Stall torque.** $$T_{stall} = \frac{K_t V}{R_a} = \frac{0.05 \times 24}{1.2} = 1.0 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step (c) — Current under load.** $$I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t} = \frac{0.15}{0.05} = 3.0 \text{ A}$$ **Step (d) — Speed under load.** Use V = K_e ω + I_a R_a: $$\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e} = \frac{24 - (3.0)(1.2)}{0.05} = \frac{24 - 3.6}{0.05} = \frac{20.4}{0.05} = 408 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\omega \approx 3895 \text{ RPM}$$ **Verification (Power Balance):** Output power = T·ω = 0.15 × 408 = 61.2 W. Input power = V·I = 24 × 3 = 72 W. Efficiency = 61.2/72 ≈ 85 %. ✓ (Plausible for a small DC motor at moderate load.) ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robot Motor Drive System The Mermaid diagram below maps the complete signal and power flow of a closed-loop servo drive for a robot joint, integrating sensing (Module 1–3) with the actuation focus of Module 4. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Robot Controller
Trajectory Planner] -->|Reference Position θ_ref| B[Outer Loop
PID Position Controller] B -->|Velocity Command ω_ref| C[Inner Loop
PID Velocity Controller] C -->|PWM Duty Cycle| D[H-Bridge
3-Phase Inverter] D -->|PWM Phase Currents| E[BLDC Motor
Stator Windings] E -->|Mechanical Torque| F[Gearbox
Harmonic Drive] F -->|Reduced Speed /
Increased Torque| G[Robot Joint
Output Link] G -->|Back-Driven Motion| H[Optical Encoder
Resolver / Hall Sensors] H -->|Position θ_meas| B H -->|Velocity ω_meas| C H -->|Current Feedback| D I[Power Supply
24-48 V DC Bus] --> D J[Current Sense
Shunt / LEM Module] --> D K[Temperature Sensor
NTC / PTC] --> C L[Emergency Stop
Safety Relay] --> D ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Follow the outer feedback loop — the controller commands a position, the encoder reports the actual position, the PID computes a correction, and the inverter drives the motor. The *current* and *temperature* feedback paths are inner loops that protect the motor from overload. This is the canonical architecture used in industrial robot arms. ### 4.2 DC Motor Equivalent Circuit with Field-Oriented Components ```mermaid flowchart LR Vsrc[Supply V] -->|Positive Terminal| Ra[Armature Resistance R_a] Ra --> La[Armature Inductance L_a] La --> Eb[Back-EMF Source
E = K_e x omega] Eb -->|Negative Terminal| Vsrc M1[Mechanical Load
Inertia J + Damping B] -.->|Coupled| Eb ``` > [!TIP] > The back-EMF source is shown as a *dependent voltage source* whose value rises with shaft speed. In simulation tools (PSpice, MATLAB/Simulink), it is implemented as a controlled voltage source: `E = Ke * omega`. ### 4.3 Stepper Motor Microstepping Drive Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB MCU[Microcontroller
Step + Direction Pins] -->|STEP pulse| DRV[A4988 / TMC2209
Microstepping Driver IC] MCU -->|DIR signal| DRV PSU[DC Supply
8-35 V] -->|VMOT| DRV DRV -->|Phase A Current| PA[Coil A
Bipolar Winding] DRV -->|Phase B Current| PB[Coil B
Bipolar Winding] PA --> MOT[Hybrid Stepper
1.8 deg / 200 steps] PB --> MOT MOT --> SHAFT[Output Shaft] SHAFT --> ENC[Optional Optical Encoder
for Closed-Loop] ENC -.->|Position Feedback| MCU ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why microstepping matters in robotics:** Pure full-step operation causes resonance and 25 % torque ripple. 1/16 microstepping with sinusoidal current shaping reduces ripple to < 5 %, smooth enough for camera gimbals and pick-and-place arms. ### 4.4 Servo Motor Internal Block Diagram (RC Type) ```mermaid flowchart LR PWM[External PWM
50 Hz, 1-2 ms] --> IC[Internal Controller
Mini-PCB with Op-Amps] POT[Potentiometer
Shaft Position Sensor] --> IC IC -->|Bidirectional Drive| HB[H-Bridge
Transistor Pair] HB -->|Forward / Reverse| M[DC Motor
with Gearbox] M -->|Shaft Rotation| GEAR[Gear Train
Plastic / Metal] GEAR -->|Reduced Speed| OUT[Output Spline
Positioned 0-180 deg] OUT --> POT PSU2[External 5-6 V Supply] --> HB ``` > [!NOTE] > The potentiometer forms the **mechanical feedback element** — as the output spline rotates, the wiper voltage changes, the controller compares it to the PWM reference, and drives the motor until they match. This is a *position servo* in its purest form. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B] *Define an electric actuator. Why are DC motors preferred over AC induction motors in small mobile robots?* **Model Answer (Key Points):** An electric actuator is a device that converts electrical energy into controlled mechanical motion, typically using an electric motor as the prime mover. **DC motors are preferred in small mobile robots** because: 1. They run directly from **battery DC** (3.7 V – 24 V) without needing an inverter. 2. They have **simple speed-torque control** via PWM through an H-bridge. 3. They are **lightweight, cheap, and easily integrated** with encoders. 4. AC induction motors require AC supply and three-phase inverters, which are bulky and power-hungry — unsuitable for battery-powered platforms. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (–1 mark):** Writing only "DC is better" without specifying the *robot context* (battery power, weight, simplicity) loses marks. Always tie your answer to the application. #### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] *Explain the working principle of a stepper motor. What is the significance of microstepping in robotic positioning applications?* **Model Answer (Key Points):** A stepper motor rotates in **discrete angular steps** synchronized with input pulses. Each pulse energises a stator phase sequence, attracting the rotor by **variable reluctance** (VR type) or **permanent magnet alignment** (PM/HB type). The rotor's angular position after N pulses is precisely N × θ_step, giving **open-loop position control** without feedback. **Significance of Microstepping:** - **Smooth motion**: Interpolates between full steps using sinusoidal current shaping (e.g., 1/16 microstepping yields 3200 steps/rev from a 1.8° motor). - **Reduced resonance and vibration** — critical for camera gimbals and surgical robots. - **Higher resolution** — fine increments of **0.05625°** (1/32) or even **0.007°** (1/256). - **Lower torque ripple** (< 5 % at high microstepping levels). ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam – June 2024** **(a)** Derive the voltage equation and torque equation of a DC motor. Show that the no-load speed is V/K_e and the stall torque is K_t·V/R_a. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DC motor has V = 48 V, R_a = 0.8 Ω, K_e = 0.12 V·s/rad, K_t = 0.12 N·m/A, B = 0. Find: (i) No-load speed in RPM, (ii) Stall current and stall torque, (iii) Speed and efficiency when load torque is 2 N·m. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution (a):** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. The derivation proceeds as: **Voltage equation (KVL):** $$V = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + K_e \omega$$ **Torque equation (Newton's 2nd law, rotation):** $$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega = K_t I_a - T_L$$ **No-load speed:** At no load T_L = 0, B = 0, dω/dt = 0 ⇒ I_a → 0 ⇒ V = K_e · ω_0 ⇒ **ω_0 = V/K_e**. **Stall torque:** At stall ω = 0 ⇒ E = 0 ⇒ I_a = V/R_a ⇒ **T_stall = K_t · V / R_a**. ##### **Model Solution (b):** **(i) No-load speed:** $$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e} = \frac{48}{0.12} = 400 \text{ rad/s}$$ Converting to RPM: $$N_0 = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 400 \cdot 9.5493 = 3819.7 \text{ RPM}$$ **(ii) Stall current and torque:** $$I_{stall} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{48}{0.8} = 60 \text{ A}$$ $$T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.12 \times 60 = 7.2 \text{ N·m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Warning:** Writing *stall current = 60 A* without comment loses a mark. Always add: "This is dangerously high; a current limit / fuse is mandatory in the driver circuit." (–1 mark if omitted.) **(iii) Speed and efficiency at T_L = 2 N·m:** Steady-state armature current: $$I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t} = \frac{2}{0.12} = 16.667 \text{ A}$$ Speed from the voltage equation: $$V = I_a R_a + K_e \omega \Rightarrow \omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e} = \frac{48 - (16.667)(0.8)}{0.12} = \frac{48 - 13.33}{0.12} = \frac{34.67}{0.12} = 288.9 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\omega \approx 2759 \text{ RPM}$$ Output power: $$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 2 \times 288.9 = 577.8 \text{ W}$$ Input power: $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_a = 48 \times 16.667 = 800 \text{ W}$$ Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{577.8}{800} = 0.722 = 72.2\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often compute efficiency as 1 − I_a² R_a / (V I_a). That gives the *electrical* efficiency, missing the *mechanical* losses (friction, windage). With B = 0 here they coincide, but in general list both terms explicitly to gain full credit. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a brushless DC (BLDC) motor. Discuss its advantages over brushed DC motors in robotic applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hybrid stepper motor has 50 stator pole teeth per phase and 50 rotor teeth. Calculate (i) the full-step angle, (ii) the resolution in steps per revolution, (iii) the effective step angle with 1/16 microstepping, and (iv) the shaft position after 1830 input pulses. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution (a):** **Construction:** A BLDC motor has a **permanent-magnet rotor** (typically 2 to 8 pole pairs of rare-earth NdFeB magnets) and a **stator** with three-phase concentrated windings (U, V, W) embedded in laminated silicon-steel slots. The rotor position is sensed by **3 Hall-effect sensors** spaced 120° electrical apart, or estimated sensorlessly from back-EMF zero-crossings. **Working:** The controller energises exactly **two of the three phases** at any time in a six-step sequence (UV → UW → VW → VU → WU → WV). Current flowing through the energised phases creates a stator field that *pulls* the nearest rotor magnet pole toward it. As the rotor aligns, the Hall sensors signal the controller to switch to the next phase, advancing rotation by **60° electrical** (= 360°/N for an N-pole rotor). **Advantages over Brushed DC in Robotics:** 1. **No brush wear** — operational life **10 000+ hours** vs. 1000–2000 hours for brushed. 2. **Higher efficiency** (90 %+) → longer battery life in mobile robots. 3. **Lower electrical noise** (no brush arcing) — critical when using sensitive robot sensors (IMU, vision). 4. **Better heat dissipation** — stator windings are on the outside casing, which can be clamped to a heatsink. 5. **Higher power-to-weight ratio** — superior torque density for humanoid and aerial robots. 6. **Higher speed capability** — no mechanical commutation limit. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Warning:** Listing advantages without *quantifying* them loses marks. Use **numbers** (hours, percentages, ratios) wherever possible. ##### **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** N_s = 50, N_r = 50. **(i) Full-step angle:** $$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{2500} = 0.144°$$ **(ii) Resolution in steps per revolution:** $$\text{Steps/rev} = N_s \cdot N_r = 2500 \text{ steps/rev}$$ **(iii) Effective step angle with 1/16 microstepping:** $$\theta_{micro} = \frac{\theta_{step}}{16} = \frac{0.144°}{16} = 0.009°$$ **(iv) Shaft position after 1830 input pulses (full-step mode):** $$\theta_{shaft} = 1830 \times 0.144° = 263.52°$$ Equivalent in revolutions: $$\text{Revolutions} = \frac{1830}{2500} = 0.732 \text{ rev}$$ Rotor position mod 360°: **263.52°** from the electrical reference. If 1/16 microstepping were used: $$\theta_{micro\text{-}shaft} = 1830 \times 0.009° = 16.47°$$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall (–2 marks):** Many students confuse *full steps* with *microsteps* and mix units. Always state clearly which mode is being assumed, and convert RPM ↔ rad/s correctly using 1 rev = 2π rad. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Definition:** An electric motor converts electrical energy → mechanical (rotary) energy via electromagnetic induction; in robotics it is the *actuator muscle*. - [x] **DC Motor Equation:** $V = I_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + K_e \omega$ and $T = K_t I_a$. In SI units **K_e = K_t**. - [x] **No-Load Speed:** $\omega_0 = V / K_e$ — independent of load. - [x] **Stall Torque:** $T_{stall} = K_t V / R_a$ — never operate a motor continuously at stall (overheating!). - [x] **BLDC Advantages:** Long life, high efficiency, low noise, better heat dissipation — but needs electronic commutation via Hall sensors or sensorless algorithms. - [x] **Stepper Motor Step Angle:** $\theta_{step} = 360° / (N_s N_r)$ for hybrid type. **Microstepping** (1/8, 1/16, 1/32) smooths motion and reduces resonance. - [x] **Servo Motor:** Closed-loop *positioning* device combining a motor, gearbox, position sensor (pot/encoder), and PID controller. Hobby RC servo uses **50 Hz PWM**, 1.0–2.0 ms pulse width for 0°–180°. - [x] **H-Bridge:** 4-switch topology for bidirectional DC motor drive; pulse-width-modulated (PWM) on the high-side switches controls speed. - [x] **Time Constants:** Mechanical $\tau_m = J R_a / (K_t K_e)$; Electrical $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$. These determine how fast a motor responds. - [x] **Robot Motor Selection Matrix:** Hobby → RC Servo / DC Gear Motor; Industrial → BLDC + Harmonic Drive + Encoder; Precision Open-Loop → Stepper; Heavy / Direct-Drive → AC Synchronous. - [x] **Typical Ratings (Memorize):** 6 V / 12 V / 24 V / 48 V supply; 1.8° (200 step) hybrid stepper; 50 Hz / 1.0–2.0 ms servo PWM. - [x] **Common Pitfall:** Confusing *RPM* with *rad/s* (multiply RPM by 2π/60 to get rad/s). Always carry units in your calculations. - [x] **Always add a flyback diode** across an inductive DC motor to protect the driver from back-EMF spikes when switching off.

topics.title: Overview of Electric Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Electric Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Motor:** An electric motor is an **electromechanical energy conversion device** that transforms **electrical energy** (supplied from a DC source or AC mains) into **rotational mechanical energy** (shaft work) based on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** and the **Lorentz force law** — i.e., the force experienced by a current-carrying conductor placed inside an external magnetic field.

Mathematically, the instantaneous force experienced by a single conductor of length $L$ carrying current $I$ inside a uniform magnetic flux density $B$ is given by the Lorentz force equation:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the direction of current flow and the magnetic field vector. When $\theta = 90^{\circ}$, the force becomes maximum and is given by $F_{max} = BIL$.

### 1.2 Key Components of an Electric Motor

| S.No | Component | Function |
|:----:|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | **Stator** | Stationary part that produces the main magnetic field (either via permanent magnets or field windings) |
| 2 | **Rotor / Armature** | Rotating part that carries the armature conductors where torque-producing current flows |
| 3 | **Commutator** | Mechanical rectifier (in DC motors) that reverses current direction every half rotation to maintain unidirectional torque |
| 4 | **Brushes** | Carbon/graphite contacts that transfer current between the stationary external circuit and the rotating commutator |
| 5 | **Field Windings** | Coils wound around pole shoes that produce the working flux $\Phi$ when energized |
| 6 | **Shaft & Bearings** | Mechanical output transmission and low-friction rotor support |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Wheel with an Electromagnet:**
> Imagine a water wheel. Now, instead of letting falling water spin it, we replace the water with a **stream of tiny magnets pushing other magnets** arranged on paddles around a wheel. Every time a paddle-magnet is pushed by the stator-magnet, the wheel rotates a bit. To keep the wheel spinning in one direction (instead of stalling at the alignment point), we **flip the polarity of the paddles just before they reach the magnetic neutral** — this flipping is precisely what the **commutator + brushes** do in a real DC motor.

A simpler analogy for a beginner: **Think of an electric motor as a "reverse generator."** A generator converts motion into electricity; a motor converts electricity into motion using the very same physics — magnetic fields interacting with current-carrying coils.

### 1.4 Relevance to Robotics (KTU Course Context)

In robotic systems, electric motors are the **primary actuators** — they convert the controller's electrical command signals into the **physical joint motion** required for locomotion, manipulation, and gripping. The most common robotic actuator types are:

- **DC Servo Motors** — used for closed-loop position/velocity control
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors** — high-efficiency drives for robotic arms
- **Stepper Motors** — open-loop precise position control in pick-and-place robots
- **AC Servo Motors** — industrial robots requiring high torque at low speeds

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Electric Motors

Electric motors are broadly classified based on the **type of supply**, **commutation method**, and **operating principle**:

**A. Based on Power Supply Type**

1. **DC Motors**
   - Brushed DC Motor (BDC)
   - Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)
   - Servo Motor (DC servo)
2. **AC Motors**
   - Induction Motor (Asynchronous) — Squirrel cage, Wound rotor
   - Synchronous Motor
3. **Special-Purpose Motors**
   - Stepper Motor (Variable Reluctance / Permanent Magnet / Hybrid)
   - Servo Motor (AC servo)

**B. Based on Rotor-Field Relationship**

- **Synchronous Motors:** Rotor speed $N_s$ is exactly equal to the rotating stator field speed.
- **Asynchronous (Induction) Motors:** Rotor speed $N_r$ is always less than the synchronous speed $N_s$ — defined by slip $s$.

### 2.2 Fundamental Operating Principles

The operation of every rotating electric motor is governed by **two universal physical laws**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law 1 — Lorentz Force Law (Force on a conductor):**
> A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a mechanical force.
> $$\vec{F} = I(\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law 2 — Faraday's Law of Induction (Back EMF generation):**
> When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, an EMF is induced that opposes the cause of motion (Lenz's law). This is called **Back EMF** ($E_b$).

The interplay between these two laws — the applied voltage $V$ overcoming the back EMF $E_b$ to push armature current $I_a$ which produces torque $T$ against the load — is the **heart of motor operation**.

### 2.3 Working Principle — DC Motor (Step-by-Step)

1. The field windings (or permanent magnets) on the stator produce a uniform magnetic flux $\Phi$ per pole.
2. The armature conductors on the rotor carry a current $I_a$ supplied via brushes and commutator.
3. Each conductor experiences a tangential Lorentz force $F = BIL$, producing a torque $T$ about the rotor shaft.
4. As the rotor rotates, the conductors cut the magnetic flux, inducing a **back EMF** $E_b$ opposing the applied voltage $V$.
5. The commutator periodically reverses the current direction so that torque remains unidirectional.

### 2.4 Working Principle — AC Induction Motor

1. A three-phase stator winding is fed with a balanced three-phase AC supply.
2. The three-phase currents produce a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ (where $f$ = supply frequency, $P$ = number of poles).
3. The RMF cuts the stationary rotor bars (or shorted rotor windings), inducing a rotor current by **Faraday's induction law**.
4. By **Lenz's law**, the rotor current creates its own magnetic field that opposes the relative motion, dragging the rotor along with the RMF.
5. The rotor always lags the synchronous field — this lag is quantified by **slip** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$.

### 2.5 Working Principle — Stepper Motor

1. Stepper motors rotate in **discrete angular steps** (typically $1.8^{\circ}$, $0.9^{\circ}$, or even $0.36^{\circ}$).
2. Each electrical pulse applied to a specific stator phase causes the rotor to move one fixed step.
3. No feedback sensor is needed in open-loop operation — the motor position is determined by counting pulses.
4. Types: **Variable Reluctance (VR)**, **Permanent Magnet (PM)**, and **Hybrid**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Examinations**

| S.No | Formula | Description | Units |
|:----:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $E_b = k \Phi \omega$ | Back EMF in terms of angular velocity | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ | Back EMF in terms of RPM (N) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $T = k \Phi I_a$ | Developed electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ | Torque in terms of machine constants | N·m |
| 5 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | DC motor voltage equation | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ | Speed equation of a DC motor | RPM |
| 7 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of AC motor | RPM |
| 8 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | dimensionless (or pu) |
| 9 | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | Rotor speed of induction motor | RPM |
| 10 | $P_{developed} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical power developed | Watts (W) |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}$ | Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_{steps}}$ | Step angle of stepper motor | degrees ($^{\circ}$) |

Where:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in **Webers (Wb)**
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths ($A = 2$ for wave winding, $A = P$ for lap winding)
- $N$ = Speed in **RPM**
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity in **rad/s**
- $k$ = Machine constant (Back EMF constant = Torque constant in SI units)
- $f$ = Supply frequency in **Hz**

### 2.7 Engineering Utility in Robotic Systems

In modern robotic arms and mobile platforms, motors are embedded inside **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors)** and **BLDC motors** for their:

- **High torque-to-inertia ratio** — required for fast acceleration of robot joints
- **Precise positioning** — essential for trajectory tracking
- **Encoder compatibility** — for closed-loop servo control
- **High efficiency (>90%)** — important for battery-powered autonomous robots

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Back EMF Equation of a DC Motor

**Objective:** Derive an expression for the back EMF $E_b$ induced in a DC motor armature.

**Assumptions:**
- Each armature conductor cuts the magnetic flux $\Phi$ once per pole-pair per revolution.
- The flux is uniformly distributed in the air gap.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let:
- $P$ = total number of poles
- $\Phi$ = useful flux per pole in Webers
- $N$ = armature speed in RPM
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature winding

The number of armature conductors connected in **series per parallel path** is $\dfrac{Z}{A}$.

In **one revolution**, each conductor cuts a flux equal to:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P \cdot \Phi$$

The **time taken for one revolution** (period) is:

$$T_{rev} = \dfrac{60}{N} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$

Therefore, the **rate of flux cutting per conductor** (which is the EMF induced per conductor by Faraday's law) is:

$$\text{EMF per conductor} = \dfrac{P \Phi}{T_{rev}} = \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60}$$

Since there are $\dfrac{Z}{A}$ conductors in series per parallel path, and the total EMF induced in the armature equals the sum of EMFs in series:

$$\boxed{E_b = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A} \quad \text{[Volts]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Form in SI Units:**
> $$E_b = k \Phi \omega \quad \text{where} \quad \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \, \text{rad/s}$$
> Hence the relationship: $k = \dfrac{P \, Z}{2 \pi A}$ (machine constant).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Equation of a DC Motor

**Objective:** Derive the expression for the electromagnetic torque developed by a DC motor armature.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Consider the power balance. The **electrical power converted into mechanical form** is equal to the product of back EMF and armature current:

$$P_{converted} = E_b \cdot I_a$$

But also by the fundamental power-torque relationship:

$$P_{converted} = T \cdot \omega$$

where $T$ is the developed torque and $\omega$ is the angular velocity.

Equating both expressions:

$$T \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$$

Substitute $E_b = k \Phi \omega$:

$$T \cdot \omega = (k \Phi \omega) \cdot I_a$$

Cancelling $\omega$ from both sides (assuming non-zero speed):

$$\boxed{T = k \, \Phi \, I_a \quad \text{[Newton-metres]}}$$

Substituting $k = \dfrac{P Z}{2 \pi A}$, we obtain the **expanded torque equation**:

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2 \pi \, A} \quad \text{[N·m]}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed Equation and Speed Regulation

**Starting Point — Armature Voltage Equation:**

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the DC motor armature circuit:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

where $V$ = applied terminal voltage, $E_b$ = back EMF, $I_a$ = armature current, $R_a$ = armature resistance.

Substituting $E_b = k \Phi N$ (solving the back EMF equation for $N$):

$$V = k \Phi N + I_a R_a$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi} \quad \text{[RPM]}}$$

**Speed Regulation:** Defined as the change in speed from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load speed.

$$\% \, \text{Regulation} = \dfrac{N_{no\text{-}load} - N_{full\text{-}load}}{N_{full\text{-}load}} \times 100$$

At **no-load**: $I_a \approx 0$, so $N_0 \approx \dfrac{V}{k \Phi}$ (ideal no-load speed)

At **full-load**: $I_a = I_{FL}$, so $N_{FL} = \dfrac{V - I_{FL} R_a}{k \Phi}$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors arranged in a wave winding ($A=2$). The armature resistance is $0.5 \, \Omega$, the flux per pole is $30 \, \text{mWb}$, and the motor runs at 1000 RPM. Calculate:
> (a) The back EMF induced
> (b) The armature current
> (c) The developed electromagnetic torque

**Given:**
- $P = 4$
- $V = 250 \, \text{V}$
- $Z = 800$
- $A = 2$ (wave winding)
- $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- $\Phi = 30 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Wb}$
- $N = 1000 \, \text{RPM}$

**Solution:**

**(a) Back EMF:**

Using the derived formula:

$$E_b = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A} = \dfrac{4 \times (30 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1000 \times 800}{60 \times 2}$$

$$E_b = \dfrac{4 \times 0.030 \times 1000 \times 800}{120} = \dfrac{96000}{120}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 200 \, \text{V}}$$

**(b) Armature Current:**

From KVL: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$

$$I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \dfrac{250 - 200}{0.5} = \dfrac{50}{0.5}$$

$$\boxed{I_a = 100 \, \text{A}}$$

**(c) Developed Torque:**

Using the expanded torque equation:

$$T = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2 \pi \, A} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.030 \times 800 \times 100}{2 \pi \times 2}$$

$$T = \dfrac{9600}{4 \pi} = \dfrac{9600}{12.566}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 763.94 \, \text{N·m}}$$

**Verification using $P_{mech} = T\omega$:**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} \approx 104.72 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 200 \times 100 = 20000 \, \text{W}$$

$$T = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\omega} = \dfrac{20000}{104.72} \approx 763.94 \, \text{N·m} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.5 Synchronous Speed & Slip Derivation

For a **3-phase AC induction motor**, the rotating magnetic field is produced by three balanced stator windings spatially displaced by $120^{\circ}$ and fed by a 3-phase supply. The RMF completes **one full revolution in $\dfrac{2}{P}$ of the AC cycle** (where $P$ = total poles), since each pole pair corresponds to half an electrical cycle.

**Derivation:**

Let $f$ = supply frequency in Hz, $P$ = number of poles.

Electrical cycles per minute $= 60 f$.

Electrical cycles per mechanical revolution $= \dfrac{P}{2}$.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{N_s = \dfrac{60 \times 2 \times f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P} \quad \text{[RPM]}}$$

**Slip Calculation:**

$$\boxed{s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

The rotor frequency (frequency of induced rotor current) is:

$$f_r = s \cdot f$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Motor Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Electric Motors"] --> B["DC Motors"]
    A --> C["AC Motors"]
    A --> D["Special Purpose Motors"]

    B --> B1["Brushed DC Motor BDC"]
    B --> B2["Brushless DC Motor BLDC"]
    B --> B3["DC Servo Motor"]

    C --> C1["Induction Motor"]
    C --> C2["Synchronous Motor"]
    C1 --> C1a["Squirrel Cage Rotor"]
    C1 --> C1b["Wound Rotor"]
    C2 --> C2a["Salient Pole"]
    C2 --> C2b["Non Salient Pole"]

    D --> D1["Stepper Motor"]
    D --> D2["AC Servo Motor"]
    D1 --> D1a["Variable Reluctance VR"]
    D1 --> D1b["Permanent Magnet PM"]
    D1 --> D1c["Hybrid Stepper"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of an Electric Motor Drive System (Robotics-Oriented)

```mermaid
graph LR
    PWR["DC Power Supply or AC Mains"] --> CTRL["Motor Controller or Driver Circuit"]
    SENSE["Position Sensor Encoder or Resolver"] --> FB["Feedback Signal Path"]
    FB --> CTRL
    CMD["Command Input from Microcontroller or PLC"] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DRV["Power Amplifier Stage H-Bridge or Inverter"]
    DRV --> MOT["Electric Motor DC Stepper Servo BLDC"]
    MOT --> LOAD["Mechanical Load Robotic Joint or Wheel"]
    MOT -. Shaft Coupled .-> SENSE
    LOAD --> APP["Robotic Application Arm Gripper Wheel"]
```

### 4.3 Internal Architecture of a Brushed DC Motor

```mermaid
graph TB
    STATOR["STATOR Permanent Magnet or Field Winding Produces Main Flux Phi"] --> AIRGAP["AIR GAP Region of Energy Conversion"]
    ROTOR["ROTOR Armature Core with Slots"] --> WIND["Armature Conductors Carry Current I A"]
    WIND --> AIRGAP
    AIRGAP --> FORCE["Lorentz Force F equals B I L Acts Tangentially"]
    FORCE --> TORQUE["Net Electromagnetic Torque T equals k Phi I A"]
    TORQUE --> SHAFT["Mechanical Output Shaft RPM equals N"]
    COMM["Commutator and Brush Assembly"] --> WIND
    WIND -. Induces .-> EMF["Back EMF E B equals k Phi Omega Opposes Applied Voltage V"]
```

### 4.4 Working Sequence of a DC Motor (Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1 Apply DC Voltage V to Armature Terminals"] --> S2["Step 2 Current I A Flows Through Armature Conductors"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Each Conductor in Magnetic Field Experiences Lorentz Force F equals B I L"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Cumulative Force Creates Net Torque T equals k Phi I A"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Rotor Begins Rotating at Angular Velocity Omega"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 Conductors Cut Flux Inducing Back EMF E B equals k Phi Omega"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7 Net Armature Current Reduces to I A equals V Minus E B divided by R A"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8 Steady State Reached Motor Runs at Constant Speed N"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above illustrate the **functional architecture** of the motor, controller, and feedback loop rather than physical geometry. For detailed mechanical cross-sections, refer to standard KTU textbooks on Electrical Machines.
