## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an electric motor. With a neat block diagram, explain its functional role as an actuator in a robotic system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **electric motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy based on the principle of Lorentz force — i.e., a current-carrying conductor placed inside a magnetic field experiences a mechanical force $F = BIL$.

In a robotic system, the motor serves as the **actuator** — it receives a low-power control signal from the microcontroller/PLC, and produces the necessary **torque and angular displacement** at the robot joints. A typical robotic motor drive system consists of:

- **Controller** → generates PWM signals
- **Driver/Power Amplifier** → boosts the signal to motor-rated power
- **Motor** → produces mechanical torque
- **Sensor (Encoder/Resolver)** → provides position feedback for closed-loop control

The motor's shaft is mechanically coupled to the robotic link, which translates the rotation into the required joint motion. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for block diagram and explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a stepper motor and a servo motor. Mention two specific applications of each in robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|:--------|:--------------|:------------|
| Control type | Open-loop (no feedback needed) | Closed-loop (with encoder feedback) |
| Motion | Discrete steps (e.g., $1.8^{\circ}$) | Continuous rotation with precise positioning |
| Holding torque | High at standstill | Depends on feedback controller |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (due to encoder + controller) |
| Accuracy | Lower (may miss steps under overload) | Very high (no missed steps) |

**Stepper motor applications in robotics:**
1. 3D printer extruder and axis drives
2. Pick-and-place robot arm positioning

**Servo motor applications in robotics:**
1. Robotic arm joint control (e.g., 6-DOF industrial robots)
2. CNC machine tool feed drives

*(3 marks — 2 for comparison table, 1 for applications)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **back EMF equation** of a DC motor, stating clearly all assumptions used. Show that the EMF constant $k$ in the expression $E_b = k\Phi\omega$ equals $\dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 4-pole DC series motor has 1200 armature conductors arranged in simplex wave winding. The armature and series field resistances are $0.3 \, \Omega$ and $0.1 \, \Omega$ respectively. The flux per pole at a certain load is $25 \, \text{mWb}$. If the motor takes 50 A from the supply, calculate:
- (i) The back EMF
- (ii) The developed torque
- (iii) The speed in RPM **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation of Back EMF Equation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The armature is star-connected with $Z$ total conductors
2. Each conductor cuts flux $\Phi$ under each pole as it rotates
3. There are $A$ parallel paths in the armature winding
4. Flux distribution in the air gap is uniform
5. The motor is operating in steady-state at speed $N$ RPM

**Derivation:**

Let $N$ = armature speed in RPM, $\Phi$ = flux per pole, $P$ = number of poles, $Z$ = total conductors, $A$ = parallel paths.

In **one revolution**, each conductor passes under **$P$ poles**, hence cuts flux:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P \cdot \Phi$$

Time taken for one revolution:

$$T_{rev} = \dfrac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

Average EMF induced per conductor (rate of flux cutting):

$$\varepsilon_{conductor} = \dfrac{P \Phi}{T_{rev}} = \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60} \text{ V}$$

Conductors in series per parallel path $= \dfrac{Z}{A}$

Total back EMF:

$$E_b = \dfrac{Z}{A} \cdot \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60} = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$

Converting $N$ to $\omega$ (rad/s): $N = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2 \pi}$

Substituting:

$$E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi \cdot 60 \omega \cdot Z}{60 \cdot 2\pi \cdot A} = \dfrac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A} \cdot \omega$$

Comparing with $E_b = k \Phi \omega$:

$$\boxed{k = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}}$$

**(7 marks — 3 for derivation, 2 for assumption statement, 2 for $k$ derivation)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 220 \, \text{V}$
- $P = 4$
- $Z = 1200$
- $A = 2$ (simplex wave)
- $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.1 \, \Omega$
- $\Phi = 25 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Wb}$
- $I_a = 50 \, \text{A}$

**(i) Back EMF:**

By KVL:

$$E_b = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

$$E_b = 220 - 50 \times (0.3 + 0.1) = 220 - 50 \times 0.4 = 220 - 20$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 200 \, \text{V}}$$

**(ii) Developed Torque:**

$$T = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = \dfrac{4 \times (25 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1200 \times 50}{2 \pi \times 2}$$

$$T = \dfrac{4 \times 0.025 \times 1200 \times 50}{4\pi} = \dfrac{6000}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 477.46 \, \text{N·m}}$$

**(iii) Speed in RPM:**

From the back EMF equation:

$$N = \dfrac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z} = \dfrac{200 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 0.025 \times 1200}$$

$$N = \dfrac{24000}{120}$$

$$\boxed{N = 200 \, \text{RPM}}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for EMF, 3 for torque with formula, 2 for speed)**

---

**Question B [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle** of a three-phase **squirrel cage induction motor** with the help of a neat diagram. Define **synchronous speed**, **slip**, and **rotor frequency**, and derive the relation $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole induction motor is supplied from a 50 Hz, 415 V line. At full-load, the rotor runs at 960 RPM. Calculate:
- (i) Synchronous speed
- (ii) Slip at full-load (in %)
- (iii) Frequency of rotor induced current
- (iv) Comment on the rotor resistance and reactance referred to stator frequency **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of Squirrel Cage Induction Motor:**

**Construction:**

- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with three-phase distributed windings placed in slots, separated by $120^{\circ}$ in space.
- **Rotor:** Laminated cylindrical core with **aluminum or copper bars** embedded in semi-closed slots, all bars short-circuited at both ends by **end rings** — resembling a "squirrel cage."
- No external electrical connection to rotor (hence no brushes, no commutator — robust and maintenance-free).

**Working Principle:**

1. A balanced 3-phase AC supply to the stator winding produces a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** that rotates at synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$.
2. This RMF cuts the stationary rotor bars.
3. By **Faraday's law**, EMF is induced in the rotor bars.
4. Since the rotor bars are short-circuited through end rings, **rotor current flows**.
5. By **Lenz's law**, the rotor current-carrying conductors placed in the RMF experience a Lorentz force.
6. The cumulative force produces a torque that drags the rotor in the direction of the RMF.
7. Rotor never reaches $N_s$ (otherwise no relative motion → no induced EMF → no torque). Hence rotor speed $N_r < N_s$.

**Derivation of $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$:**

The stator is wound for $P$ poles. The RMF travels a distance of **one pair of poles (i.e., $\dfrac{P}{2}$ electrical cycles)** per mechanical revolution.

In one **second**, the supply completes $f$ electrical cycles.

Therefore, the RMF completes:

$$\text{Revolutions per second} = \dfrac{f}{P/2} = \dfrac{2f}{P}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_s = \dfrac{2f}{P} \times 60 = \dfrac{120 f}{P} \text{ RPM}$$

**(7 marks — 2 construction, 3 working, 2 derivation)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P = 6$
- $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$
- $N_r = 960 \, \text{RPM}$
- $V_L = 415 \, \text{V}$

**(i) Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = \dfrac{6000}{6}$$

$$\boxed{N_s = 1000 \, \text{RPM}}$$

**(ii) Slip at Full Load:**

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \dfrac{1000 - 960}{1000} = \dfrac{40}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{s = 0.04 = 4\%}$$

**(iii) Frequency of Rotor Current:**

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50$$

$$\boxed{f_r = 2 \, \text{Hz}}$$

**(iv) Comment on Rotor Resistance & Reactance:**

The rotor resistance $R_2$ remains **independent of slip** (it depends only on the conductor material and temperature). However, the rotor reactance $X_2$ at slip frequency is:

$$X_2 = 2 \pi f_r L = 2 \pi (s \cdot f) L = s \cdot X_2^{stator\text{-}freq}$$

where $X_2^{stator\text{-}freq} = 2 \pi f L$ is the rotor reactance referred to stator frequency.

Therefore, at full load ($s = 0.04$), the rotor reactance is **very small** ($4\%$ of standstill reactance), making the rotor circuit predominantly **resistive** at operating slip. This is by design — it ensures **high starting torque** and stable running performance.

**(7 marks — 1 each for sub-parts, 3 for explanation)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $A$ values:** Students frequently write $A = P$ for all DC motors. **Correction:** $A = 2$ for **wave winding**; $A = P$ for **lap winding**. The problem statement must be read carefully.
>
> 2. **Mixing units in torque equation:** When $N$ is in RPM and $T$ must be in N·m, use $T = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$. The version with $60$ in the denominator gives N·m only if $N$ is in RPS — avoid this confusion.
>
> 3. **Forgetting series field resistance in KVL:** In **series motors**, KVL must include both $R_a$ and $R_{se}$: $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$. Marks are cut if only $R_a$ is considered.
>
> 4. **Back EMF vs Applied Voltage sign:** $E_b$ always **opposes** $V$. Writing $V + E_b = I_a R_a$ instead of $V - E_b = I_a R_a$ is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Missing SI units in final answers:** Always state $N$ in RPM, $T$ in N·m, $E_b$ in Volts, $\Phi$ in Webers.
>
> 6. **Synchronous speed unit slip:** $N_s$ and $N_r$ must be in the **same unit (RPM)** before computing slip — do not mix rad/s and RPM.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Overview of Electric Motors**
>
> ✅ Electric motor converts **electrical energy → mechanical energy** via Lorentz force and Faraday's induction law.
>
> ✅ Key components: **Stator, Rotor (Armature), Commutator, Brushes, Field Windings**.
>
> ✅ **DC Motor:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$; $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$; $T = k \Phi I_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$.
>
> ✅ **Induction Motor:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$; $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$; $f_r = sf$.
>
> ✅ **Stepper Motor:** Discrete step motion; $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\text{No. of steps}}$; open-loop control.
>
> ✅ **Servo Motor:** Closed-loop with encoder/resolver; high precision for robotic joints.
>
> ✅ **Robotic Relevance:** BLDC and PMSM dominate modern robot joints due to high torque density, efficiency, and precise control.
>
> ✅ **EMF constant $k$** in SI units satisfies $E_b = k \Phi \omega$ and $T = k \Phi I_a$ (numerically equal).
>
> ✅ **Unit Constants to Memorize:** $\Phi$ in Wb, $T$ in N·m, $N$ in RPM, $\omega$ in rad/s.
>
> ✅ **Slip Range:** Induction motor operates with $0 < s < 1$ (motoring mode); $s > 1$ = braking; $s < 0$ = generating mode.
>
> ✅ **Commutator** is the heart of a brushed DC motor — reverses current to maintain unidirectional torque.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid-State Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solid-State Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solid-State Switch (SSS)** is a semiconductor-based electronic switching device that controls the flow of electrical current between two terminals in an ON (conducting) or OFF (non-conducting) state **without any mechanical movement of physical contacts**. In the KTU 2024 Robotics context, solid-state switches form the fundamental interface between low-power digital/analog sensing circuits and higher-power actuators, motors, and signal-conditioning stages within a robot's perception and actuation chain.

These devices exploit the **non-linear electrical behaviour of doped semiconductor junctions** (PN junctions in BJTs, metal-oxide gate structures in MOSFETs, four-layer PNPN latches in thyristors, or magneto-sensitive carriers in Hall plates) to achieve switching action at speeds typically between **1 µs and 100 ns**, with operational lifespans exceeding **10⁹ switching cycles** — orders of magnitude beyond electromechanical relays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Solid-state switches are treated as the *enabling hardware primitives* for converting sensor signals (light, magnetic field, temperature, position) into clean, noise-immune binary or proportional electrical outputs usable by robot controllers (PLC, microcontroller, DSP).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Electronic Tap":**
> Imagine a water tap in your kitchen. A *mechanical* tap uses a metal valve that physically rotates and slides to open/close water flow. A *solid-state switch* is like a **smart electronic faucet** that uses an invisible magnetic field, a beam of light, or a tiny voltage pulse to start or stop the flow — there are **no moving metal parts**, no wear-and-tear, and no "drips" (contact bounce). The water either flows cleanly or is shut off completely, and the command is given electronically (touch sensor, light sensor, magnetic tag, etc.) — which is exactly how robots achieve clean, silent, ultra-fast switching.

### 1.3 Classification at a Glance

Solid-state switches used in robotic sensing/control are classified by their **control mechanism** and **terminal structure**:

- **Voltage-Controlled (Voltage-Driven):** MOSFET, IGBT, JFET — gate signal controls conduction.
- **Current-Controlled:** BJT, Darlington pair, photo-transistor — base/light current controls conduction.
- **Triggered Latch (Bi-Stable):** SCR, TRIAC, GTO thyristor — once triggered, they stay ON until current falls below holding value.
- **Field-Activated:** Hall-effect switches, magnetoresistive (MR/GMR/TMR) switches, Reed-replacement ICs.
- **Optically-Activated:** Phototransistors, photodiodes, optotriacs, solid-state relays (SSRs).
- **Integrated Smart Switches:** Hall latches, smart power switches, motor-driver ICs with embedded MOSFETs.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:** The **band-gap voltage of silicon** at 300 K is approximately **1.12 eV**, which sets the fundamental threshold (~0.6–0.7 V) for conduction in all silicon-based solid-state switches discussed in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principles of Major Solid-State Switch Families

#### A. Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) as a Switch

- The BJT operates in two regions for switching: **Cut-off (OFF state)** and **Saturation (ON state)**.
- **Cut-off:** $V_{BE} < 0.7$ V, so $I_B \approx 0$, $I_C \approx 0$, $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}$.
- **Saturation:** $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V, $V_{CE} \approx V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V, and $I_C \approx V_{CC}/R_C$.
- Logic-level microcontrollers (3.3 V / 5 V) can directly drive the base via a current-limiting resistor.

#### B. MOSFET as a Switch

- **Enhancement-mode N-channel MOSFET** is the workhorse of digital switching.
- Gate-source threshold voltage $V_{GS(th)}$ (typically **1 V to 4 V**) must be exceeded.
- In the **triode (linear) region** as a switch: $V_{DS} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$, where $R_{DS(on)}$ is typically **10 mΩ to 100 mΩ** for power MOSFETs.
- The gate is **insulated** — essentially no steady-state gate current, making it ideal for direct microcontroller interfacing.

#### C. Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR / Thyristor)

- A four-layer (PNPN) latching device — once a gate pulse triggers conduction, it remains ON even if the gate signal is removed, provided the anode current stays above the **holding current $I_H$**.
- Used in **AC power control** and **DC latching circuits** in robotics (e.g., emergency-stop latches, brake engage circuits).

#### D. TRIAC (Bidirectional Thyristor)

- Essentially two SCRs connected in anti-parallel inside one package — conducts in **both half-cycles** of an AC waveform.
- Phase-angle firing allows proportional control of AC loads — directly applicable to robot AC servo motor drives and lamp/indicator dimming.

#### E. Hall-Effect Switch

- A solid-state sensor-switch that toggles its output when the **magnetic flux density B** at its sensing face crosses a defined threshold (e.g., **B_OP ≈ 30 mT**, **B_RP ≈ 20 mT** — built-in hysteresis).
- Used in robotic joint encoders, gripper open/close feedback, wheel-rotation sensing, and proximity detection of ferrous parts.

#### F. Solid-State Relay (SSR)

- A packaged module containing an **input-side LED (opto-isolator)**, an **output-side triac/MOSFET**, and a galvanic isolation barrier (**isolation voltage typically 2.5 kV to 4 kV RMS**).
- Combines the load-handling capability of a relay with the no-contact, no-bounce, silent operation of semiconductors.

### 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

- **Why use solid-state over mechanical?** Robotic environments have high vibration, dust, and millions of switching cycles. A mechanical relay has contacts that arc, wear, and bounce. A solid-state switch has **zero contact bounce**, **sub-microsecond response**, and **no arc**.
- **How is ON/OFF achieved?** By manipulating the energy-band structure of the semiconductor using an external field (electric or magnetic) or photon flux, thereby creating or destroying a conductive channel.
- **Why are MOSFETs preferred in modern robots?** Lower drive power (CMOS compatible), lower on-state loss ($I^2 R_{DS(on)}$), higher switching frequency (kHz to MHz), and easier PWM integration for DC motor speed control.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE**, especially for Part-B (14-mark) derivations on transistor/MOSFET switching circuits and Hall-effect sensor problems.

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Operating Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BJT DC Current Gain | $I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$ | $\beta$ (unitless, 50–400), $I_C$, $I_B$ in A | Active region |
| 2 | BJT Switch Saturation | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C}$ | $V_{CC}$ in V, $R_C$ in $\Omega$ | Saturation |
| 3 | Base Drive Resistor | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_B}$ | $V_{in}$ control voltage, $V_{BE(sat)} \approx 0.7$ V | Saturation |
| 4 | MOSFET Saturation Current | $I_D = \dfrac{k}{2}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | $k$ process transconductance, $V_{th}$ threshold | Saturation |
| 5 | MOSFET ON-State Loss | $P_{cond} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | $I_D$ drain current, $R_{DS(on)}$ in $\Omega$ | Triode / ohmic |
| 6 | Switching Power Loss | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$ | $f_{sw}$ switching frequency in Hz | Hard switching |
| 7 | Hall-Effect Output Voltage | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$ | $R_H$ Hall coefficient, $I$ control current, $B$ flux density, $t$ plate thickness | Linear region |
| 8 | SCR Holding Condition | $I_A \ge I_H$ | $I_A$ anode current, $I_H$ holding current (mA range) | Latched ON |
| 9 | TRIAC Phase Control | $V_{out,rms} = V_m \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | $\alpha$ firing delay angle in rad | AC line |
| 10 | Thermal Junction Rise | $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$ | $\theta_{JA}$ junction-to-ambient thermal resistance (°C/W) | Steady state |
| 11 | Switching Times (BJT) | $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$, $t_{off} = t_s + t_f$ | $t_d$ delay, $t_r$ rise, $t_s$ storage, $t_f$ fall (s) | Transient |
| 12 | SSR Isolation Voltage | $V_{iso} \ge 2.5 \text{ kV}_{RMS}$ | Typical datasheet rating | Safety standard |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

Solid-state switches are the **silent workhorses** behind virtually every modern robotic system:

- **Industrial Robotic Arms (e.g., FANUC, KUKA):** IGBT modules drive the three-phase servo inverters; Hall-effect ICs provide rotor-position feedback.
- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** MOSFET H-bridges drive DC traction wheels; solid-state relays switch battery-pack contactors.
- **Medical / Surgical Robots:** Optically-coupled SSRs provide patient-isolated switching of high-voltage imaging systems.
- **Aerospace Robotics (Drone Swarms):** GaN-based solid-state switches operate at MHz frequencies for ultra-light motor control.
- **Agricultural Robots:** Sealed Hall switches survive dust and moisture in tractor-mounted manipulators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: BJT as a Switch — Designing the Base Resistor

**Problem Statement:** Design the base resistor $R_B$ of an NPN BJT switch used to drive a robot solenoid coil. Given: $V_{CC} = 12$ V, solenoid coil resistance $R_C = 200$ $\Omega$, BJT DC gain $\beta = 100$, microcontroller output $V_{in} = 5$ V, $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7$ V, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2$ V.

**Step 1 — Find the required collector saturation current.**
The coil must receive full current when the BJT is ON. Use Ohm's law across the coil, accounting for the BJT's small saturation drop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{C(sat)} &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C} \\[6pt]
I_{C(sat)} &= \frac{12 \text{ V} - 0.2 \text{ V}}{200 \ \Omega} \\[6pt]
I_{C(sat)} &= \frac{11.8}{200} \\[6pt]
I_{C(sat)} &= 0.059 \text{ A} = 59 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The collector-emitter junction drops 0.2 V when saturated, leaving 11.8 V across the 200 Ω coil. The current is simply Ohm's law.

**Step 2 — Find the minimum base current needed to reach saturation.**
Apply the BJT current relation $I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$ in the **active (edge of saturation)** boundary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B(min)} &= \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta} \\[6pt]
I_{B(min)} &= \frac{59 \text{ mA}}{100} \\[6pt]
I_{B(min)} &= 0.59 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* $\beta = I_C / I_B$ is rearranged to find the base current that supplies exactly 59 mA of collector current. Below this, the BJT is in the active region and acts as an amplifier (bad for a switch).

**Step 3 — Apply a forced overdrive factor (ODF) of 2 for reliable saturation.**
Industrial-grade designs use an ODF (typically 1.5 to 5) to guarantee saturation across temperature and $\beta$ variation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B &= ODF \cdot I_{B(min)} \\[6pt]
I_B &= 2 \times 0.59 \text{ mA} \\[6pt]
I_B &= 1.18 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $R_B$ from the input loop KVL.**
The base-emitter junction drops 0.7 V; the microcontroller outputs 5 V, so 4.3 V appears across $R_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B &= \frac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_B} \\[6pt]
R_B &= \frac{5 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V}}{1.18 \text{ mA}} \\[6pt]
R_B &= \frac{4.3}{1.18 \times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
R_B &= 3644 \ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Choose the next lower standard E12 value.**
Select **$R_B = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$** (E12 standard) to keep the BJT in saturation under all conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Using too small an $R_B$ wastes microcontroller I/O current. Using too large an $R_B$ may leave the BJT in the active region, dissipating excess power as heat ($P = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$) — a common KTU exam pitfall.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: MOSFET Power Dissipation in a Robotic Motor Drive

**Problem Statement:** A robot's DC gear motor is driven by an N-channel MOSFET. Given: motor draws $I_D = 4$ A at 24 V, MOSFET $R_{DS(on)} = 50$ m$\Omega$, switching frequency $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz, rise time $t_r = 50$ ns, fall time $t_f = 50$ ns.

**Step 1 — Conduction loss (steady-state ON period).**
The MOSFET behaves like a small resistor in the ON state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} \\[6pt]
P_{cond} &= (4)^2 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \\[6pt]
P_{cond} &= 16 \times 0.05 \\[6pt]
P_{cond} &= 0.8 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* $4^2 = 16$; multiply by 0.05 Ω to get 0.8 W of I²R heating.

**Step 2 — Switching loss (transitions).**
Each ON/OFF transition dissipates $\frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D$ over the rise/fall time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DS} \cdot I_D \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{sw} \\[6pt]
P_{sw} &= 0.5 \times 24 \times 4 \times (50 + 50) \times 10^{-9} \times 20 \times 10^{3} \\[6pt]
P_{sw} &= 0.5 \times 24 \times 4 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} \times 20\,000 \\[6pt]
P_{sw} &= 0.5 \times 24 \times 4 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \\[6pt]
P_{sw} &= 0.096 \text{ W} = 96 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Combine the rise and fall times (100 ns total). Multiply by switching frequency to get the duty-cycle fraction spent switching. Multiply by ½·V·I to get average switching loss per cycle.

**Step 3 — Total MOSFET dissipation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= P_{cond} + P_{sw} \\[6pt]
P_{total} &= 0.8 + 0.096 \\[6pt]
P_{total} &= 0.896 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify junction temperature stays within safe limits.**
Assume ambient temperature $T_A = 50$ °C and the MOSFET is mounted on a small heatsink with $\theta_{JA} = 60$ °C/W:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= T_A + P_{total} \cdot \theta_{JA} \\[6pt]
T_J &= 50 + 0.896 \times 60 \\[6pt]
T_J &= 50 + 53.76 \\[6pt]
T_J &= 103.76 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since typical silicon MOSFETs are rated for $T_{J(max)} = 150$ °C, the design is **thermally safe**, but margin is thin — a KTU exam may ask to redesign the heatsink or reduce switching frequency.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Solid-State Switch

| Switch Type | Best for | Switching Speed | Drive Requirement | Typical Robotic Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BJT | Low-frequency DC, cost-sensitive | ~1 µs | Continuous base current ~1 mA | Relay replacement, LED/solenoid driver |
| Power MOSFET | DC motor PWM, fast switching | ~10–100 ns | CMOS gate, ~10 nC charge | Wheel-driver H-bridge, gripper motor |
| IGBT | High-voltage AC/DC motor | ~100 ns – 1 µs | Gate driver, 10–20 V | Servo inverter in robotic arm |
| SCR / Thyristor | AC mains, latching | ~1 µs | Short gate pulse, then auto-latch | Brake release, emergency stop latch |
| TRIAC | AC phase control | ~10 µs | Isolated gate pulse | AC lamp/indicator dimming |
| Hall-Effect Switch | Magnetic field sensing | ~1 µs | 3.3–24 V supply | Joint position, wheel RPM sensor |
| SSR (Triac output) | AC load isolation | ~1 ms (zero-cross) | LED current ~5–20 mA | Mains-powered heater, conveyor motor |
| SSR (MOSFET output) | DC load isolation | ~100 µs | LED current | Battery-pack contactor, DC pump |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Solid-State Switch Subsystem in a Robot

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates how a solid-state switch acts as the **bridge between the sensing layer and the actuation layer** of a robot, including isolation, signal conditioning, and feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sensIn[Robot Sensor Input]:::in --> opAmp[Signal Conditioner OpAmp]:::proc
    opAmp --> mcu[Microcontroller GPIO]:::proc
    mcu --> driverIC[Gate Driver or Base Resistor]:::proc
    driverIC --> sss[Core Solid State Switch MOSFET IGBT TRIAC]:::power
    sss --> load[Robot Actuator Motor Solenoid Lamp]:::out
    load --> fbSns[Feedback Sensor Hall Encoder]:::in
    fbSns --> mcu
    isoBarrier[Galvanic Isolation Optocoupler]:::iso -.-> driverIC
    heatsink[Thermal Management Heatsink]:::thermal --- sss

    classDef in fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef power fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#4a148c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef iso fill:#f57f17,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef thermal fill:#616161,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

*Interpretation:* The robot's raw sensor signal (left) is conditioned and fed to a microcontroller. The MCU's low-power logic output drives a solid-state switch (the red power block), which in turn energises an actuator. Feedback from the actuator returns to the MCU, closing the loop. The orange optocoupler provides galvanic isolation for safety, and the grey thermal block ensures the switch stays within its SOA (Safe Operating Area).

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Hall-Effect Switch Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    magField[External Magnetic Field B]:::env --> hallPlate[Hall Sensing Plate]:::sens
    hallPlate --> diffAmp[Differential Amplifier]:::proc
    diffAmp --> schmidt[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]:::proc
    schmidt --> hyst[Hysteresis Block BOP BRP]:::proc
    hyst --> outStage[Open Drain or Push Pull Output]:::out
    vReg[Voltage Regulator 3V3 to 24V]:::pwr -.-> hallPlate
    vReg -.-> diffAmp
    vReg -.-> schmidt

    classDef env fill:#37474f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sens fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#00695c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#4a148c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef pwr fill:#bf360c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

*Interpretation:* A magnetic field induces the Hall voltage in the blue plate. The signal is amplified, fed through a Schmitt trigger for noise immunity, processed through a hysteresis block (defining the operate and release points $B_{OP}$ and $B_{RP}$), and finally presented as a clean logic-level output to the robot controller.

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting a Solid-State Switch in a Robotic Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose Solid State Switch]:::dec --> q1{Load type?}:::dec
    q1 -- DC low voltage --> q2{Current level?}:::dec
    q1 -- DC high voltage --> igbt[IGBT]:::out
    q1 -- AC mains --> triac[TRIAC or AC SSR]:::out
    q2 -- under 5A --> mosfet[Logic Level MOSFET]:::out
    q2 -- over 5A --> pmosfet[Power MOSFET with Driver]:::out
    q2 -- very small signals --> bjt[BJT or Phototransistor]:::out
    start -.-> q3{Is input a magnetic field?}:::dec
    q3 -- yes --> hall[Hall Effect Switch]:::out
    q3 -- no light --> opt[Optocoupler or Phototransistor]:::out

    classDef dec fill:#263238,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a solid-state switch and list any four advantages it offers over an electromechanical switch in robotic applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A solid-state switch is a semiconductor device that performs ON/OFF switching of electrical circuits **without any moving mechanical parts**, by exploiting the electrical properties of doped silicon, magnetic-field-sensitive materials, or optically-coupled junctions.

Four advantages (any four, ½ mark each + ½ mark for definition):

1. **No contact bounce** — produces a clean, noise-free transition ideal for digital controllers.
2. **High switching speed** — from microseconds (SCR) down to nanoseconds (MOSFET), enabling high-frequency PWM.
3. **Long operational life** — no arc, no wear, >10⁹ switching cycles versus ~10⁵ for mechanical relays.
4. **Immunity to shock, vibration, dust, and corrosive environments** — critical in industrial and field robots.
5. **Galvanic isolation via optical coupling** — built-in safety for high-voltage motor control.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the working principle of a Hall-effect switch. State two applications in robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a current-carrying semiconductor plate is exposed to a perpendicular magnetic field **B**, the moving charge carriers experience a **Lorentz force** that deflects them to one side of the plate, producing a small transverse **Hall voltage** $V_H$. (1½ marks)

In a Hall-effect switch IC, this tiny voltage is amplified, passed through a Schmitt trigger and a hysteresis comparator, and used to drive an open-drain or push-pull transistor output. The output toggles when B exceeds the **operate point $B_{OP}$** and resets only when B falls below the **release point $B_{RP}$**, providing clean, bounce-free switching. (1 mark)

Two applications (¼ mark each):
- Brushless DC (BLDC) motor rotor-position sensing in robotic joints.
- Proximity detection of ferrous gear teeth for wheel-rotation feedback in mobile robots.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** (either Question A OR Question B). Solve whichever best matches your preparation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **BJT as a solid-state switch** in the cut-off and saturation regions. Derive the expression for the **base resistor $R_B$** required to drive a relay coil of 240 Ω from a 12 V supply using a BJT with $\beta = 120$, microcontroller output $V_{in} = 5$ V, $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7$ V, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2$ V. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a **solid-state relay (SSR)**. Compare it with an **electromechanical relay (EMR)** in a tabular form with at least six parameters. State two applications of SSRs in industrial robotic systems. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Operation in cut-off region (OFF state):** When $V_{in} = 0$ V, the base-emitter junction is unbiased ($V_{BE} < 0.7$ V), so $I_B = 0$, $I_C = 0$, and the full supply voltage appears across the BJT: $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC} = 12$ V. The relay coil sees no current and remains de-energised. (1½ marks)

**Operation in saturation region (ON state):** When $V_{in} = 5$ V is applied through $R_B$, the base current saturates the BJT. Now $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2$ V, and the collector current is limited only by the relay coil resistance. (1½ marks)

**Numerical Design of $R_B$:**

Step 1 — Collector saturation current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{C(sat)} &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C} = \frac{12 - 0.2}{240} = 49.2 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Minimum base current (edge of saturation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B(min)} &= \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta} = \frac{49.2 \text{ mA}}{120} = 0.41 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Forced overdrive (using ODF = 2 for safe saturation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B &= 2 \times 0.41 \text{ mA} = 0.82 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Base resistor from KVL:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B &= \frac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_B} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{0.82 \text{ mA}} = 5244 \ \Omega \approx 5.1 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (E12 standard)}
\end{aligned}
$$

(4 marks for numerical work)

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**SSR Construction and Working (3 marks):**
An SSR consists of three isolated sections: (1) an **input LED** (typically an infrared GaAs diode), (2) a **photo-sensitive receiving element** (phototriac, phototransistor, or photovoltaic stack), and (3) a **power output switch** (triac for AC loads or MOSFET pair for DC loads). The LED is driven by the low-voltage control signal; the emitted light triggers the output switch, providing **galvanic isolation of 2.5 kV to 4 kV RMS** between input and output.

**Comparison Table — SSR vs EMR (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Solid-State Relay (SSR) | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) |
|---|---|---|
| Switching speed | 0.1 – 10 ms | 5 – 15 ms |
| Contact bounce | None (zero) | Significant (1–5 ms) |
| Mechanical wear | None (no moving parts) | High (contacts erode) |
| Operational life | >10⁹ cycles | 10⁵ – 10⁶ cycles |
| Acoustic noise | Silent | Audible click |
| Isolation | Optical (2.5–4 kV) | Magnetic/galvanic (1–2 kV) |
| On-state voltage drop | 1–2 V (triac) | ~0 V (closed contact) |
| Leakage current in OFF | 1–10 mA | ~0 A |
| EMI/RFI generation | Low (zero-cross types) | Moderate (contact arcing) |
| Cost per channel | Higher | Lower |

**Two Applications in Industrial Robots (1 mark):**
1. Switching AC mains to conveyor motors and pump actuators in assembly-line robots.
2. Isolated control of high-power soldering/heater elements in PCB-assembly robotic cells.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a circuit diagram, describe the operation of a **MOSFET as a solid-state switch** in a robotic DC motor driver. A robot's wheel motor is controlled by an N-channel enhancement MOSFET with $R_{DS(on)} = 40$ m$\Omega$, $V_{GS} = 10$ V, and motor current $I_D = 5$ A. Calculate the **(i)** conduction loss and **(ii)** junction temperature if the ambient temperature is 45 °C and the thermal resistance of the heatsink is 50 °C/W. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **TRIAC-based solid-state switch** for AC load control in a robot's auxiliary system. With the help of a phasor/waveform diagram, derive the expression for the RMS output voltage in terms of the firing angle $\alpha$. List two advantages of TRIAC over SCR. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**MOSFET as a switch (3 marks):** The enhancement-mode N-channel MOSFET is OFF when $V_{GS} < V_{GS(th)}$ and ON (in the triode/ohmic region) when $V_{GS} \gg V_{GS(th)}$. The gate being insulated, no continuous current is drawn from the microcontroller. When ON, the drain-source channel behaves as a small resistor $R_{DS(on)}$, allowing the motor current to flow with minimal loss. A flyback diode is placed across the motor to handle inductive kickback.

**Numerical (4 marks):**

(i) Conduction loss:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} = (5)^2 \times 40 \times 10^{-3} = 25 \times 0.04 = 1.0 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) Junction temperature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} = 45 + 1.0 \times 50 = 45 + 50 = 95 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T_{J(max)}$ for silicon MOSFETs is 150 °C, the design has a healthy 55 °C margin.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**TRIAC Working (2 marks):** A TRIAC is a bidirectional thyristor equivalent to two SCRs in anti-parallel. It can conduct in both half-cycles of an AC waveform. A small gate pulse applied at a controlled **firing angle $\alpha$** after each zero-crossing triggers conduction until the next zero-crossing, naturally commutating the device. This allows proportional AC power control — directly applicable to robot heater modules, indicator dimmers, and AC fan drives.

**RMS Output Voltage Derivation (3 marks):**

The TRIAC conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$ in the positive half-cycle (and similarly in the negative half). For a sinusoidal input $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out,rms}^2 &= \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\[6pt]
V_{out,rms}^2 &= \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) \\[6pt]
V_{out,rms}^2 &= \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\omega t) - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} \\[6pt]
V_{out,rms}^2 &= \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right] \\[6pt]
V_{out,rms} &= V_m \sqrt{ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\alpha}{2\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4\pi} }
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator of the bracketed term by 2 to match standard textbook form:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out,rms} = V_m \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}\,}
$$

**Two Advantages of TRIAC over SCR (2 marks):**
1. **Bidirectional conduction** — controls both half-cycles with a single device, eliminating the need for a rectifier bridge.
2. **Simpler trigger circuit** — one gate pulse per AC cycle suffices (in phase-control mode) versus the complex synchronized firing required for two anti-parallel SCRs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE for this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ODF (overdrive factor):** Many students compute $R_B$ using the bare $I_{B(min)}$. Examiners expect an ODF of 1.5–2 for a *robust industrial* design. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong region in MOSFET:** Writing $I_D = \frac{k}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ (the **saturation** region formula) when the question asks for a *switch* (which uses the **triode/ohmic** region). The switch model is $V_{DS} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.
> 3. **Ignoring $V_{CE(sat)}$ or $V_{BE(sat)}$:** Always subtract the junction drops in KVL — examiners explicitly test whether you remember the **0.7 V** and **0.2 V** standard drops.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in $P_{sw}$:** Mixing nanoseconds with kilohertz without explicit conversion. Always show: $50 \text{ ns} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ s.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark subparts:** KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** purely for a *correctly labelled* circuit diagram. Never skip it.
> 6. **Hall-effect answer without the hysteresis $B_{OP}$ / $B_{RP}$ values:** A complete answer must mention hysteresis to avoid chatter near the threshold.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Solid-state switch = no moving parts**, exploits semiconductor junction physics for ON/OFF action.
- **BJT** is **current-controlled**; **MOSFET** is **voltage-controlled** (gate-isolated, CMOS friendly).
- **Saturation** = BJT ON (V_CE ≈ 0.2 V); **Cut-off** = BJT OFF (I_C ≈ 0).
- **R_DS(on)** is the key MOSFET figure of merit — lower is better for high-current motor drives.
- **SCR latches ON**; **TRIAC latches ON bidirectionally**; both are reset only when current falls below the holding value.
- **Hall-effect switch** outputs logic HIGH/LOW when magnetic flux density crosses $B_{OP}$ (operate) or $B_{RP}$ (release).
- **Solid-State Relay (SSR)** = input LED + opto-isolator + output triac/MOSFET, providing 2.5–4 kV galvanic isolation.
- **Key formulas to memorise:**
  * $I_C = \beta I_B$ and $R_B = (V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)})/I_B$ for BJT switches.
  * $P_{cond} = I_D^2 R_{DS(on)}$ for MOSFET conduction loss.
  * $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_r + t_f) f_{sw}$ for MOSFET switching loss.
  * $T_J = T_A + P_D \theta_{JA}$ for thermal verification.
  * $V_{out,rms} = V_m \sqrt{1 - \alpha/\pi + \sin 2\alpha / (2\pi)}$ for TRIAC phase control.
- **Standard silicon constants:** $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7$ V, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2$ V, $V_{GS(th)} \approx 1$–$4$ V, $V_H \propto B$.
- **Always** check the Safe Operating Area (SOA) and junction temperature for power switches in robot motor drivers.
- **No contact bounce, sub-microsecond speed, >10⁹ cycle life, silent operation** are the four pillars justifying solid-state switches in modern robotics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solenoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solenoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **solenoid** is an electromechanical transducer consisting of a helical (cylindrical) coil of insulated wire, typically wound around a movable ferromagnetic core (plunger or armature). When energized by an electric current, it generates a concentrated, uniform magnetic field along its axis, producing a measurable mechanical force (linear thrust or rotary torque) on the plunger. In the context of **robot sensing and actuation**, solenoids are classified as *actuator-class transducers* that act as the bridge between an electronic control signal (low-power DC pulse) and physical mechanical motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OERAT612 Module 4):**
> "A solenoid is a static electromagnetic actuator that converts electrical energy into linear or rotary mechanical displacement via the magnetic force generated by current-carrying coils, widely used in robotic valves, grippers, relays, and sensor pre-conditioning stages."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a hollow plastic drinking straw with many loops of thin copper wire wrapped tightly around it — that is, mechanically, a **solenoid coil**. Now, insert a soft iron nail (the *plunger*) inside the straw so it can slide freely in and out. The moment you connect the wire ends to a battery:

- Current flows through the loops.
- Each loop behaves like a tiny magnet, and all loops line up like soldiers.
- The collective magnetic pull *sucks* the iron nail *into* the coil with a noticeable "click" and a thump.
- Disconnect the battery, and a small internal **spring** pushes the nail back to its original position.

This "magnetic suck-and-push" is the heart of every solenoid. In a robot, this simple suck-and-push action is used to flip a gripper open/closed, open a pneumatic valve to admit compressed air, or trigger a limit switch to report "object detected."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Vacuum permeability: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m (T·m/A)**
> - Relative permeability of soft iron: **μᵣ ≈ 2000 – 6000** (non-linear)
> - Coil resistance is typically **5 Ω to 100 Ω** for low-voltage robotic solenoids.
> - Operating voltages for robotics: **5 V, 12 V, 24 V DC**.

## 1.3 Classification of Solenoids

| Type | Motion Mode | Typical Robotics Use |
|---|---|---|
| Linear (Pull-type) | Plunger pulled into coil | Pneumatic valve actuation |
| Linear (Push-type) | Plunger pushed out via internal core | Gripper finger extension |
| Rotary Solenoid | Limited-angle rotational step (e.g., 25°, 45°, 90°) | Selector valves, camera tilt |
| Latching (Bistable) | Holds position without continuous current | Battery-powered mobile robots |
| Proportional Solenoid | Stroke proportional to current | Hydraulic servo valves |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

A solenoid operates in a five-stage causal chain:

1. **Electrical Input Stage** — A DC voltage source (V) drives a current (I) through a coil of N turns. The current obeys the standard RL circuit transient: $I(t) = \dfrac{V}{R}\left(1 - e^{-Rt/L}\right)$.
2. **Magnetomotive Force (MMF) Generation** — Each turn contributes an MMF, and the total MMF is $\mathcal{F} = N \cdot I$, measured in **At (ampere-turns)**.
3. **Magnetic Flux Channeling** — The MMF drives magnetic flux (Φ) through the low-reluctance ferromagnetic core and the air gap: $\Phi = \dfrac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}_{total}}$, where reluctance $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$.
4. **Force Production** — The flux crossing the air gap pulls the plunger deeper into the coil, because the system *minimizes total reluctance* by closing the air gap. The force is given by **Maxwell's Stress / Virtual Work** method.
5. **Mechanical Output** — The plunger displacement (x) drives a mechanical load (valve stem, gripper linkage, switch contact).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Solenoid Force:**
> The ferromagnetic plunger is *attracted* into the coil simply because the magnetic field prefers to travel through iron (low reluctance) rather than air (high reluctance). The system always seeks the *lowest energy state* — i.e., minimum reluctance, minimum air-gap length, maximum inductance.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are board-exam ready. Memorize the right-hand column for guaranteed marks in Part A derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnetic field inside a long solenoid | $B = \mu_0 n I$ | Tesla (T) | $n = N/l$ is turns per unit length |
| 2 | Magnetic flux linkage | $\lambda = N\Phi = LI$ | Weber (Wb) | $L$ is inductance |
| 3 | Inductance of solenoid | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{l}$ | Henry (H) | Assumes closed magnetic path |
| 4 | Reluctance of magnetic path | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | At/Wb (H⁻¹) | Analogue of electrical resistance |
| 5 | MMF | $\mathcal{F} = NI$ | Ampere-turns (At) | Analogue of EMF |
| 6 | Force on plunger (energy method) | $F_x = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{dx}$ | Newton (N) | Virtual work principle |
| 7 | Force on plunger (field method) | $F_x = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ | Newton (N) | Maxwell stress on air gap |
| 8 | Energy stored in magnetic field | $W = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2 = \dfrac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot A l$ | Joule (J) | Energy density $\times$ volume |
| 9 | Pull-in / Hold current | $I_{hold} = \dfrac{F \cdot 2 \mu_0}{N^2 A} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\mu_r - 1}$ | A (Amps) | Threshold for plunger motion |
| 10 | Time constant of solenoid coil | $\tau = L/R$ | seconds (s) | Determines response speed |
| 11 | Stroke / Pull-in distance | $x_{max} \approx 0.1 l$ to $0.3 l$ | metres (m) | Practical mechanical limit |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $B = \mu_0 N I$ for the field inside a solenoid. The correct form is $B = \mu_0 n I$ where $n = N/l$. Dropping the per-unit-length factor loses 1 mark.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern robotics, solenoids are the **workhorses of low-cost automation** because they are:
- **Cheap** (₹50 – ₹500 in India, $1 – $10 globally).
- **Fast** (response times of 5 – 30 ms).
- **Compact** (10 mm – 50 mm footprint).
- **Interfaceable** — directly controlled by microcontroller GPIO pins (via MOSFET drivers).

Industrial robotics arms often have **pneumatic muscles** powered by solenoid valve banks (5/2, 3/2 directional control valves). In mobile robots (AGVs, AMRs), latching solenoids save battery power by holding state without continuous current. In sensor pre-conditioning, solenoid-actuated shutters protect LiDAR and camera lenses in outdoor robots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Magnetic Field Inside an Ideal Long Solenoid (Using Ampere's Law)

**Statement:** To derive the axial magnetic flux density $B$ inside an ideal infinitely long solenoid carrying a steady current $I$, with $n$ turns per unit length.

**Step 1 — Setup and Amperian Loop Selection:**
Consider a long solenoid with $n$ turns per metre, radius $a$. Apply **Ampere's Circuital Law** in integral form:
$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enclosed}$$

Choose a rectangular Amperian loop (Amperian loop ABCDA) with one side of length $l$ *inside* the solenoid parallel to the axis, and the opposite side *outside*.

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Line Integral in Four Segments:**

Segment AB (inside, parallel to axis): $\int_A^B \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B \cdot l$

Segment BC (perpendicular, inside to outside): $\int_B^C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ (B is axial; path is radial)

Segment CD (outside the solenoid): $\int_C^D \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ (field outside a long solenoid ≈ 0)

Segment DA (perpendicular, outside to inside): $\int_D^A \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ (same reason as BC)

**Step 3 — Sum and Substitute:**
$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B \cdot l + 0 + 0 + 0 = B l$$

The enclosed current is $I_{enclosed} = n \cdot l \cdot I$ (since $n \cdot l$ turns each carrying $I$ are enclosed).

**Step 4 — Final Result:**
$$B l = \mu_0 \, n \, l \, I$$

$$\boxed{B = \mu_0 n I}$$

This shows that the magnetic field inside a long solenoid depends *only* on the turns-per-unit-length and the current — not on the radius. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inductance of a Solenoid (L = μ₀N²A/l)

**Step 1 — Total Flux Linkage:**
The flux through one turn is $\Phi = B \cdot A = \mu_0 n I \cdot A$.
For $N$ turns, total flux linkage is:
$$\lambda = N \Phi = N \cdot \mu_0 n I A = N \cdot \mu_0 \cdot \frac{N}{l} \cdot I \cdot A = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l} \cdot I$$

**Step 2 — Apply Definition of Inductance:**
Inductance is defined as flux linkage per unit current:
$$L = \frac{\lambda}{I} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$$

**Step 3 — Account for Ferromagnetic Core:**
With a soft-iron core of relative permeability $\mu_r$, the inductance becomes:
$$\boxed{L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{l}}$$

This explains why adding an iron core multiplies the inductance (and hence the force) by a factor of $\mu_r \approx 2000$–$6000$. ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Force on the Plunger (Energy / Virtual Work Method)

**Step 1 — Co-energy Concept:**
The magnetic co-energy of an inductor carrying current $I$ is:
$$W'_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} L(x) I^2$$

**Step 2 — Force from Virtual Displacement:**
If the plunger moves by a virtual displacement $dx$ in the direction of force $F$, the mechanical work done is $F \cdot dx$. Equating mechanical work to the change in co-energy at constant current:
$$F \, dx = dW'_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \, dL$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Force:**
$$\boxed{F_x = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute L(x) for a Plunger Geometry:**
For a plunger partially inserted by length $x$ into a coil of $N$ turns, cross-section $A$, and with effective air-gap function $g(x)$:
$$L(x) = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g(x)}$$

Differentiating:
$$\frac{dL}{dx} = -\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g(x)^2} \cdot \frac{dg}{dx}$$

**Step 5 — Final Force Expression:**
$$F_x = -\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2 \, g(x)^2} \cdot \frac{dg}{dx}$$

The negative sign confirms the force *reduces* the air gap (i.e., pulls the plunger in). ∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Stored in the Magnetic Field

**Step 1 — Energy Density Derivation:**
Energy density (energy per unit volume) in a magnetic field is:
$$u = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Step 2 — Total Energy in Solenoid Volume:**
The volume of the magnetic field region inside the solenoid is $V = A \cdot l$:
$$W = u \cdot V = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot A \cdot l$$

**Step 3 — Substitute B and Simplify:**
$$W = \frac{(\mu_0 n I)^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot A l = \frac{\mu_0 n^2 I^2 A l}{2}$$

Since $N = n l$ and $L = \mu_0 N^2 A / l$:
$$W = \frac{1}{2} \mu_0 n^2 I^2 A l = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l} I^2 = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 = \frac{B^2 A l}{2 \mu_0}}$$

This is the same as the energy stored in any inductor. ∎

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example Solved (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A robotic gripper uses a solenoid with $N = 500$ turns, length $l = 0.05$ m, radius $a = 0.01$ m, carrying $I = 2$ A. The plunger is soft iron with $\mu_r = 3000$. Find:
(a) Inductance $L$
(b) Energy stored $W$
(c) Force on the plunger (assume $dL/dx = 0.4$ H/m)

**Solution:**

(a) Cross-sectional area: $A = \pi a^2 = \pi (0.01)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{l} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(3000)(500)^2(3.1416 \times 10^{-4})}{0.05}$$

Let me compute the numerator:
$$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3000 = 3.7699 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$(500)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{5}$$
$$(3.7699 \times 10^{-3})(2.5 \times 10^{5}) = 942.48$$
$$942.48 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = 0.2961$$
$$L = \frac{0.2961}{0.05} = 5.92 \text{ H}$$

(b) Energy stored:
$$W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 = \frac{1}{2}(5.92)(2)^2 = 11.84 \text{ J}$$

(c) Force on plunger:
$$F_x = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{1}{2}(2)^2(0.4) = 0.8 \text{ N}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller GPIO Signal 5V DC] --> B[MOSFET Driver IRLZ44N]
    B --> C[Flyback Diode 1N4007 Protection]
    C --> D[Solenoid Coil N turns of copper wire]
    D --> E[Ferromagnetic Core Soft Iron Plunger]
    E --> F[Mechanical Output Stage]
    F --> G1[Pneumatic Valve]
    F --> G2[Robot Gripper Finger]
    F --> G3[Limit Switch Contact]
    D --> H[Return Spring Provides Reset Force]
    H --> E
    D --> I[Magnetic Flux Loop Closed Through Yoke]
    I --> D
```

## 4.2 Detailed Cross-Sectional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COIL_ASSEMBLY[COIL_ASSEMBLY]
        A1[DC Supply V] --> A2[Helical Coil N turns]
        A2 --> A3[Magnetic Field B along axis]
        A3 --> A4[Flux Density B = mu0 n I]
    end
    subgraph CORE_ASSEMBLY[CORE_ASSEMBLY]
        B1[Soft Iron Plunger] --> B2[Air Gap g x variable]
        B2 --> B3[Mechanical Force F pull]
        B3 --> B4[Plunger Displacement x]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_ASSEMBLY[OUTPUT_ASSEMBLY]
        C1[Linear Motion] --> C2[Valve Stem / Gripper Linkage]
        C2 --> C3[External Mechanical Load]
    end
    A4 --> B2
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
```

## 4.3 Force vs. Current Analytical Plot (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input: Current I in Amps] --> P2{Compute via Formula}
    P2 --> P3[F = 0.5 times I squared times dL by dx]
    P3 --> P4[Plot F on Y axis vs I on X axis]
    P4 --> P5[Parabolic curve passing through origin]
    P5 --> P6[At I = 0 F = 0]
    P5 --> P7[At I = 2A F approx 0.8 N]
    P5 --> P8[At I = 4A F approx 3.2 N]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams:** The first flowchart shows the *electrical-to-mechanical signal chain* in a robot-mounted solenoid. The second shows the *internal physics*: coil → field → air gap → force. The third emphasizes that **solenoid force scales with the SQUARE of current**, which is why PWM control at low duty cycles is the standard energy-saving technique in robotic solenoids.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a solenoid and list any two applications of solenoids in robotics.**

**Model Answer:**
A solenoid is an electromechanical device consisting of a helical coil of insulated wire wound around a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic plunger. When current flows through the coil, a magnetic field is established that exerts force on the plunger, producing linear or rotary motion.

**Two applications in robotics:**
1. **Pneumatic/Hydraulic valve actuation** in robotic arms — the solenoid energizes a directional control valve that admits compressed air to a cylinder.
2. **Gripper actuation** — a pull-type solenoid closes the gripper jaws to grasp a workpiece, and a return spring opens them on de-energization.

*(Acceptable: relay switching, camera shutter, conveyor diverters, door locks in mobile robots.)* [3 marks]

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the expression for the magnetic field inside a long solenoid. Define the term "ampere-turns."**

**Model Answer:**
The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is given by:
$$B = \mu_0 n I$$
where $n = N/l$ is the number of turns per unit length and $I$ is the current through the coil.

**Ampere-turns (At):** The product of the number of turns $N$ of a coil and the current $I$ flowing through it. It is a measure of the magnetomotive force (MMF) produced by the coil:
$$\mathcal{F} = N I \quad \text{[At]}$$

[3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the magnetic field inside a long solenoid using Ampere's circuital law. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solenoid of length 20 cm, radius 2 cm, and 1000 turns carries a current of 5 A. Calculate:
(i) the magnetic field $B$ inside the solenoid,
(ii) the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through each turn,
(iii) the total flux linkage $\lambda$,
(iv) the inductance $L$ of the solenoid. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of B inside a long solenoid

**Assumptions:**
1. The solenoid is *infinitely long* (so the field outside is zero and the field inside is uniform and parallel to the axis).
2. The coil is *tightly wound* with no gaps between turns.
3. The medium inside is *air* (or vacuum) with permeability $\mu_0$.

**Derivation:**
Consider a long solenoid with $n$ turns per metre. Apply Ampere's Circuital Law to a rectangular Amperian loop ABCDA with one side of length $l$ inside the solenoid parallel to the axis:
$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enclosed}$$

**Evaluating the line integral in four segments:**

Segment AB (inside, parallel to axis):
$$\int_A^B \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B l$$

Segment BC (inside, perpendicular to axis):
$$\int_B^C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$$

Segment CD (outside, parallel to axis):
$$\int_C^D \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0 \quad (\text{since } B_{outside} \approx 0 \text{ for long solenoid})$$

Segment DA (outside, perpendicular):
$$\int_D^A \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$$

**Summing up:**
$$B l = \mu_0 (n l) I$$

$$\boxed{B = \mu_0 n I}$$

This is independent of the radius of the solenoid. [7 marks]

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:**
- $l = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2$ m
- $a = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02$ m
- $N = 1000$ turns
- $I = 5$ A

**(i) Magnetic field $B$:**
Turns per unit length: $n = N/l = 1000/0.2 = 5000$ turns/m
$$B = \mu_0 n I = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(5000)(5) = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 25000$$

Let me compute this:
$$B = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-7} \times 25000 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} \text{ T} = 0.0314 \text{ T}$$

**(ii) Flux through each turn:**
Cross-sectional area: $A = \pi a^2 = \pi (0.02)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
$$\Phi = B A = 0.0314 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} = 3.95 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Wb}$$

**(iii) Total flux linkage:**
$$\lambda = N \Phi = 1000 \times 3.95 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0395 \text{ Wb}$$

**(iv) Inductance $L$:**
$$L = \frac{\lambda}{I} = \frac{0.0395}{5} = 7.9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H} = 7.9 \text{ mH}$$

[7 marks]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a linear solenoid with a neat sketch. Discuss its applications in robotic grippers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the energy method, derive the expression for the force on the plunger of a solenoid. A solenoid has $N = 800$ turns, length $l = 0.1$ m, cross-section $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², and is operated at $I = 1.5$ A. If the rate of change of inductance is $dL/dx = 0.5$ H/m, calculate the force on the plunger. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Applications

**Construction:**
A linear solenoid consists of the following components:
1. **Helical coil** — a cylindrical coil of insulated copper wire wound on a non-magnetic former (typically plastic or phenolic resin).
2. **Plunger (armature)** — a soft-iron cylindrical rod that can slide axially inside the coil.
3. **Yoke / frame** — a soft-iron outer frame that completes the magnetic circuit and reduces flux leakage.
4. **Stopper / end cap** — limits the plunger travel at the maximum stroke position.
5. **Return spring** — a helical compression spring that pushes the plunger back to its rest position when the coil is de-energized.
6. **Lead wires and terminals** — for electrical connection to the driver circuit.

**Working Principle:**
When DC current $I$ flows through the coil, the MMF $NI$ drives magnetic flux through the soft-iron plunger and the air gap between the plunger tip and the yoke. The magnetic field in the air gap exerts an attractive force on the plunger (Maxwell stress). The plunger moves into the coil against the return spring. When the coil is de-energized, the spring pushes the plunger back to its original position.

**Mathematical form:**
The force is given by:
$$F = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Applications in Robotic Grippers:**
1. **Pick-and-place grippers** — a pull-type solenoid closes the jaws to grip a workpiece, and a spring opens them on release. Used in SCARA and Cartesian robots.
2. **Soft-robotic fingers** — miniature solenoids embedded in elastomer fingers provide active bending for delicate object manipulation.
3. **Pneumatic gripper pilot valves** — solenoid-operated 3/2 valves control the compressed air supply to pneumatic gripper cylinders.
4. **Surgical/medical robots** — micro-solenoids actuate micro-grippers in minimally invasive surgical tools.
5. **End-effector tool changers** — solenoids lock and release gripper attachments in modular robot systems.

[7 marks]

#### Part (b) — Force Derivation and Numerical Calculation

**Derivation using the Energy Method:**

**Step 1:** The magnetic co-energy stored in an inductor carrying current $I$ is:
$$W'_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$

**Step 2:** If the plunger moves by a virtual displacement $dx$ in the direction of force $F$ at constant current, the mechanical work done equals the change in co-energy:
$$F \, dx = dW'_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \, dL$$

**Step 3:** Solving for the force:
$$\boxed{F_x = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx}}$$

**Step 4:** For a solenoid with an air gap, the inductance is:
$$L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g(x)}$$
where $g(x)$ is the effective air-gap length. Differentiating and substituting:
$$F_x = -\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A I^2}{2 g(x)^2} \cdot \frac{dg}{dx}$$

The negative sign indicates the force is *attractive* (pulls the plunger in to reduce the air gap).

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $N = 800$ turns
- $l = 0.1$ m
- $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $I = 1.5$ A
- $dL/dx = 0.5$ H/m

$$F_x = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{1}{2} \times (1.5)^2 \times 0.5$$

$$F_x = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.25 \times 0.5 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.125 = 0.5625 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_x \approx 0.56 \text{ N}}$$

[7 marks]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Solenoid questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the per-unit-length factor** — Writing $B = \mu_0 N I$ instead of $B = \mu_0 (N/l) I$ or $B = \mu_0 n I$. This is a 1-mark deduction that compounds across sub-parts.
> 2. **Unit mismatches** — Using cm or mm in calculations without converting to SI (metres). Always convert to SI first.
> 3. **Missing the assumptions** in the Ampere's law derivation. A long-solenoid derivation without stating "field outside ≈ 0" and "field inside is uniform" loses 2 marks outright.
> 4. **Sign convention error** in force derivation. The force is *attractive* (reduces air gap) and should carry a negative sign when derived via the $g(x)$ formulation.
> 5. **Confusing $B = \mu_0 n I$ (air core) with $B = \mu_0 \mu_r n I$ (with iron core).** Read the problem carefully — if $\mu_r$ is given, you must use it.
> 6. **Not drawing the Amperian loop** in the derivation. A labelled rectangular loop is worth 1 mark in the KTU answer script.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Solenoid = coil + ferromagnetic plunger** that converts electrical energy to linear/rotary motion.
- ⚡ **B inside long solenoid:** $B = \mu_0 n I$ where $n = N/l$.
- ⚡ **Inductance:** $L = \mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A / l$ — increases with $N^2$, $A$, $\mu_r$.
- ⚡ **Force on plunger:** $F = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{dx} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ — force ∝ $I^2$ (parabolic).
- ⚡ **Energy stored:** $W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 = \dfrac{B^2 A l}{2\mu_0}$.
- ⚡ **Time constant** of solenoid: $\tau = L/R$ — determines response speed (5–30 ms typical).
- ⚡ **Ampere's circuital law** is the derivation tool for $B$ inside a solenoid — always draw the Amperian loop.
- ⚡ **MMF (F) = NI** in At; **Reluctance (R) = l/(μ₀μᵣA)** in At/Wb; **Φ = F/R** in Wb.
- ⚡ **5 types of solenoids in robotics:** linear (pull), linear (push), rotary, latching, proportional.
- ⚡ **Real-world robotic uses:** pneumatic valves, grippers, relays, limit switches, latches in mobile robots, camera shutters.
- ⚡ **Energy saving tip:** Use *latching* or *PWM-driven* solenoids in battery-powered robots.
- ⚡ **Protection circuit:** Always include a **flyback diode** (1N4007 or similar) across the coil to protect the driving MOSFET/relay from back-EMF.
- ⚡ **Iron core multiplies $L$ and $F$ by a factor of $\mu_r$** (2000 – 6000) compared to air core.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Solenoids**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | OERAT612 — Basics of Robot Sensing*

topics.title: Piezoelectric Actuator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT612 - BASICS OF ROBOT SENSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Actuator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Piezoelectric Actuator** is a solid-state electromechanical transduction device that converts an applied electrical energy into a precisely controlled mechanical displacement (strain or force) by exploiting the **converse piezoelectric effect** exhibited by certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials (e.g., PZT, Quartz, PVDF, BaTiO₃). In the context of robotics, these actuators are widely used in micro-positioning stages, active vibration damping, and high-speed force-feedback systems because of their sub-micrometer resolution, bandwidth exceeding **10 kHz**, and lack of moving parts such as gears or bearings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The student must clearly distinguish between the **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** (mechanical stress → charge generation, used in sensors) and the **Converse Piezoelectric Effect** (applied electric field → mechanical strain, used in actuators). The same material can therefore act as both a sensor and an actuator — the foundational dual-role principle behind smart-material robotics.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Piezoelectricity** | Coupling between mechanical and electrical states in non-centrosymmetric crystals |
| **Direct Effect** | Stress (T) → Electric Displacement (D) |
| **Converse Effect** | Electric Field (E) → Mechanical Strain (S) |
| **Poling** | Pre-alignment of dipoles by a strong DC field to activate the effect |
| **Curie Temperature (T_c)** | Temperature above which the material loses its piezoelectric property |
| **Poling Direction** | The axis (3-direction) along which the ceramic is permanently polarized |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sponge Sandwich"**
> Imagine a microscopic sandwich made of crystal layers. When you apply a small voltage across the top and bottom plates, each crystal layer infinitesimally "squeezes" or "stretches" — like a wet sponge swelling when water enters. A single layer moves only a tiny amount (a fraction of a micrometer), but if you stack 100 such layers mechanically in series (electrically in parallel), the small motions add up, producing a usable mechanical stroke. This is precisely how a **piezoelectric stack actuator** works.

### 1.4 Intuitive Summary
- Apply voltage → crystal deforms → useful motion.
- Remove voltage → crystal returns to original shape.
- The deformation is *tiny* per layer, so **stacking** or **bimorph bending** is used to amplify it.
- Poling is the "memory step" that makes the deformation predictable and repeatable.
- Above **T_c** (Curie temperature), the sponge forgets — no more piezoelectric response.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Piezoelectric Actuator Works

1. **Material Selection:** A ferroelectric ceramic such as **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** is chosen because of its exceptionally high piezoelectric charge coefficient $d_{33} \approx 400 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{C/N}$.
2. **Poling Process:** The ceramic is heated just below its **Curie temperature** and subjected to a strong DC electric field (~3–5 kV/mm), permanently aligning the randomly oriented electric dipoles along the field direction.
3. **Electrical Excitation:** When a controlled voltage $V$ is applied across the electrodes, the aligned dipoles either align more (extension) or partially de-align (contraction) with the field, producing a microscopic strain.
4. **Strain Production:** The strain in the poling direction is given by $S_3 = d_{33} \cdot E_3$, where $E_3 = V/t$ and $t$ is the layer thickness.
5. **Mechanical Output:** Because the ceramic is mechanically constrained, the resulting free strain is converted into useful force, displacement, or vibration.
6. **Stacking for Amplification:** To multiply the displacement, multiple thin ceramic disks (each ~50–100 µm thick) are mechanically stacked with interleaved electrodes connected in parallel electrically.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why poling?** Without poling, dipoles are random, and the net strain cancels to zero. Poling creates a permanent bias.
- **Why thin layers?** A thin layer means low voltage can produce a high electric field ($E = V/t$).
- **Why stack?** Total displacement $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$, where $n$ is the number of layers — amplification by simple addition.
- **Why below Curie temperature?** Above $T_c$, the crystal becomes paraelectric (centrosymmetric), losing all piezoelectric activity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name / Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_3 = d_{33} \cdot E_3$ | **Converse effect** — strain from applied field | $S_3$: strain (m/m), $d_{33}$: piezo coefficient (m/V), $E_3$: field (V/m) |
| 2 | $D_3 = d_{33} \cdot T_3 + \varepsilon^{T}_{33} E_3$ | **Direct effect** — charge from stress | $D_3$: electric disp. (C/m²), $T_3$: stress (N/m²), $\varepsilon^{T}_{33}$: clamped permittivity (F/m) |
| 3 | $\Delta L_{free} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$ | Free displacement of a **stack actuator** | $n$: number of layers, $V$: applied voltage (V), $\Delta L$: displacement (m) |
| 4 | $E_3 = V / t$ | Electric field across one layer | $t$: layer thickness (m) |
| 5 | $F_{block} = d_{33} \cdot A \cdot Y^{E}_{33} \cdot E_3$ | **Blocked force** (max force at zero displacement) | $Y^{E}_{33}$: Young's modulus (N/m²), $A$: cross-section (m²), $F$: N |
| 6 | $C = n \cdot \varepsilon^{T}_{33} \cdot A / t$ | Capacitance of the stack | $C$: capacitance (F) |
| 7 | $\eta_{mech} = k^2_{33} / (1 + k^2_{33})$ | Mechanical coupling efficiency | $k_{33}$: electromechanical coupling factor |
| 8 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{Y^{E}_{33}}{\rho}}$ | Longitudinal resonance frequency | $L$: stack length (m), $\rho$: density (kg/m³) |
| 9 | $u_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2} Y^{E}_{33} S^2_{max}$ | Maximum elastic energy density | $u_{max}$: J/m³ |
| 10 | $S_{max} \approx 0.001$ to $0.002$ | Practical maximum strain for PZT | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The symbols $d_{33}$, $d_{31}$, and $d_{15}$ are *tensor* components. The **first subscript** indicates the direction of the *electric field* (or polarization), the **second** indicates the direction of the *resulting strain* (or stress). For an actuator poled along axis-3, the **useful longitudinal coefficient is $d_{33}$**, while $d_{31}$ governs the transverse contraction that limits stack design.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** High-speed nanopositioners, optical mirror tilting in laser systems, active tool alignment.
- **Automotive:** Diesel fuel injectors (common-rail piezo systems) — switching times under 100 µs.
- **Aerospace:** Adaptive wing morphing, vibration suppression in helicopter blades.
- **Medical:** Ultrasonic imaging transducers, micropumps for drug delivery.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Inkjet printer heads, camera autofocus modules, haptic feedback in touchscreens.
